## Trade River Segment and Straight Lake Segment (Atlas Map 3f)

**Summary:** The Trade River Segment and Straight Lake Segment covers approximately 7.9 miles in Polk & Burnett Counties (Atlas Map 3f). It comprises the Trade River Segment (4.3 mi, 150th St. to 280th Ave.), the Straight Lake Segment (3.6 mi, 280th Ave. to 100th St. (CTH-I)). **Trade River Segment—4.3 miles (3.9 IAT, 0.4 CR):** 150th St. to 280th Ave.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Trade River Segment icon]
**Trade River Segment—4.3 miles (3.9 IAT, 0.4 CR):** 150th St. to 280th Ave.

[Icon: Water icon]
From the Trade River and headwaters wetlands.

[Icon: Dog icon]
Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Camping and Picnic icon]
[Icon: Park icon]
At Coon Lake Park in Frederic, ~2 mi north of the Trail.

[Icon: Trail type icon]
Segment includes connecting route roadwalk. Portions overlap cross-country ski trails and logging/forest roads.

[Icon: ColdCache icon]
[Icon: Geocaching icon]
Two ColdCache sites on segment.

[Icon: Ski trail icon]
Trade River Ski Trail network.

[Icon: Hunting icon]
Portion of the segment crossing private land between 150th St. and the SIATA property east of 140th St. is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: Straight Lake Segment icon]
**Straight Lake Segment—3.6 miles:** 280th Ave. to 100th St. (CTH-I)

Note: The DNR bans glass containers in Straight Lake State Park (SLSP).

[Icon: Water icon]
From Straight Lake, Rainbow Lake and the Straight River.

At SLSP.

[Icon: Camping icon]
At several walk-in campsites ⛺ in SLSP and at two Dispersed Camping Areas DCA.

[Icon: Hunting icon]
Portion of segment crossing private land between IATA’s Frog Pond Preserve and CTH-I is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: Dog icon]

[Icon: Trail network icon]
SLSP trail network and access roads and a blue-blazed spur trail to the two DCAs.

![Map of Trade River and Straight Lake segments showing trail path, parking, and local roads](page_46_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Parking icon 1]
**1 150th St. Trail Access (45.63269, -92.46725):** From Frederic on STH-35/48, take STH-35/48 south 1.6 mi. At 150th St. turn left and continue south 0.3 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: Parking icon 2]
**2 280th Ave. Trail Access (45.61232, -92.42927): Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: Parking icon 3]
**3 100th St. (CTH-I) Trail Access (45.59533, -92.37674):** From Luck at the junction of STH-35 and STH-48, take STH-48 east 5.2 mi. At 110th St. (CTH-I) (becomes 100th St. (CTH-I)) turn left and go north 0.9 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) 140th St. (north parking area). (ii) 140th St. (south parking area). (iii) Straight Lake State Park (SLSP) picnic area on 120th St.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::polk-burnett-c.parent_text:
## Trade River Segment

**Summary:** The Trade River Segment: This segment highlights the Trade River and its headwaters area, where knobs of basalt bedrock are prominent features.

This segment highlights the Trade River and its headwaters area, where knobs of basalt bedrock are prominent features. During the pre-Cambrian era, 1.1 billion years ago, the Earth’s crust split across part of the North American continent. Known as the Midcontinent Rift, the split extended from Kansas to Lake Superior. Huge volumes of molten lava flowed to the surface and cooled, forming the basalt bedrock. For most of that distance, the basalt is beneath the sedimentary rock. But, from St. Croix Falls to Lake Superior, you’ll see exposed bedrock outcroppings.

Begin on 150th Street at the Trade River Ski Area parking area, which offers four kilometers of groomed ski trails in winter. Your route will briefly overlap the ski trail; if you hike here in winter, stay well off to the side of the groomed trail. Trailside benches provide places to rest and enjoy nature.

When you reach 140th Street, turn right and head south for 0.4 miles along this unmarked connecting route before departing it and continuing east on the segment’s blazed portion. You’ll cross a prairie, hike along the Trade River, and go through a wooded area. A notable waypoint is a big basalt rock dropped here by the Superior Lobe.

Because of impermeable bedrock, for the last half mile before the segment’s endpoint on 280th Avenue, the route navigates wetlands (and accompanying beaver dams), which make up the headwaters of the Trade River. The segment’s design incorporates the terrain’s uniqueness as it meanders next to, around, and over bedrock. In addition, an elevated, serpentine boardwalk offers passage across perpetually wet ground.

![Map of the Trade River Segment showing the trail route winding through the Straight River Wildlife Area and Straight Lake State Park, with waypoints like a "big basalt rock" and various road crossings including 140th Ave, 130th St, and 270th Ave.](page_47_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::polk-burnett-c.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::polk-burnett-c.parent_text:
## Straight Lake Segment

**Summary:** The Straight Lake Segment: The segment travels through the heart of the 3,000-acre Straight Lake State Park (SLSP), containing the headwaters of two river systems – the Straight River draining southeast and the Trade River draining northwest – as well as pristine Straight Lake.

The segment travels through the heart of the 3,000-acre Straight Lake State Park (SLSP), containing the headwaters of two river systems – the Straight River draining southeast and the Trade River draining northwest – as well as pristine Straight Lake. The landscape is hummocky with outcroppings of basalt bedrock. Because the bedrock is close to the surface, the terrain contains numerous ephemeral ponds, perched wetlands, and marshes.

The park, in a transition zone between the northern hardwood and prairie forests, contains an intermingling of species from both vegetative communities. You may notice an extensive block of oak forest approaching old-growth status. Black bears, red foxes, river otters, gray tree frogs, leopard frogs, and four-toed salamanders are common.

The segment also features the Alliance-owned Frog Pond Preserve. The 39-acre Preserve protects 3,500 feet of Straight River shoreline and its namesake, an ephemeral pond – characterized by its woodland location, small size, lack of connection to a permanent waterbody, and tendency to dry out by mid- to late summer. Frog Pond, located in the property’s southwest corner, likely originates from a pit previously containing buried ice.

From the Trail access on 280th Avenue, hike past the north shore of 107-acre Straight Lake, a shallow drainage lake with a small dam at its outlet to the Straight River. Shortly after crossing the dam, you’ll pass an access road to SLSP parking areas, other park facilities, and the park’s main entrance. Continue east through the Straight River tunnel channel, the finest example of a glacial tunnel channel in the Midwest. During the Ice Age, a subglacial river rapidly flowed as it exited beneath the receding glacier. The sediment collected was carried away from the glacial margin, leaving the channel intact.

Follow the Trail as it nears the eastern end of Rainbow Lake, where you can glimpse the lake through the trees. Soon after, you’ll cross a curving boardwalk protecting wetland habitat, leave SLSP, and enter the Alliance’s Frog Pond Preserve. Pass a blue-blazed spur trail that leads south to two separate Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Continue through the Preserve’s upland forest with its overstory of red oak and an understory of sugar maple, basswood, ironwood, and white birch. Finally, enjoy spectacular views of the Straight River tunnel channel valley as you continue to the segment’s endpoint on 100th Street (CTH-I), passing through a gate along the route.

### AREA SERVICES

**Frederic:** Restaurant, convenience store, camping, library, medical service. From 150th St. go north 1.6 mi.

**Straight Lake State Park (SLSP)**: Camping. On Trail. (715-483-3747, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/straightlake; 10 walk-in campsites available; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

**Luck:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. From 120th St. Trail access go west ~5 mi.

## Straight River Segment and Pine Lake Segment (Atlas Maps 3f, 4f)

**Summary:** The Straight River Segment and Pine Lake Segment covers approximately 6.3 miles in Polk & Burnett Counties (Atlas Map 3f, 4f). It comprises the Straight River Segment (3.4 mi, 270th Ave. to Round Lake Rd.), the Pine Lake Segment (2.9 mi, Round Lake Rd. to 70th St.). ---

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Straight River Segment icon] **Straight River Segment—3.4 miles**: 270th Ave. to Round Lake Rd.

* [Icon: Water icon] From the Straight River and Long Lake.
* [Icon: Trail overlap icon] Portions overlap a snowmobile trail, STH-48, a private gravel driveway and some logging/forest roads.

* [Icon: Camping icon] At Dispersed Camping Areas [Icon: DCA icon] on Long Lake and east of 95th St.
* [Icon: Loop trail icon] Two white-blazed loop trails: one along the east side of the Straight River and the other off 95th St. into the IATA’s Straight River Preserve. Also, a blue-blazed spur to a DCA.

* [Icon: Hunting season icon] The segment is closed during gun deer season.

* [Icon: Leashed dogs icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

---

[Icon: Pine Lake Segment icon] **Pine Lake Segment—2.9 miles**: Round Lake Rd. to 70th St.

* [Icon: No water icon] No reliable sources of water.
* [Icon: Trail overlap icon] Portions overlap snowmobile trails, logging/forest roads and 260th Ave.

* [Icon: Hunting season icon] Portion between Round Lake Rd. and STH-48 is closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: Loop trail icon] A white-blazed loop trail crossing a debris field.

* [Icon: Leashed dogs icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

**[Icon: 1 icon] 270th Ave.Trail Access (45.59690, -92.36606)**: From Luck at the junction of STH-35 and STH-48, take STH-48 east for 5.2 mi. At 110th St. (CTH-I) (which becomes 100th St.) turn left and go north 1.3 mi. At 270th Ave. turn right and go east 0.6 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

**[Icon: 2 icon] Round Lake Rd. Trail Access (45.57627, -92.32994)**: No Parking.

**[Icon: 3 icon] 70th St. Trail Access (45.59181, -92.30438)**: From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and STH-63, take STH-48 west 15.7 mi. At 260th Ave. turn right and go east 0.7 mi. At 70th St. turn left and go north 0.7 mi. From Frederic at the intersection of STH-35 and CTH-W, take CTH-W east 9.0 mi. At 60th St. (CTH-O) turn right and go south 3.0 mi and follow CTH-O to the west on 270th Ave. for 0.9 mi. At 70th St. turn left and go south 0.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::polk-burnett-c.parent_text:
## Straight River Segment

**Summary:** The Straight River Segment: The segment highlights the Straight River, a textbook glacial tunnel channel.

The segment highlights the Straight River, a textbook glacial tunnel channel. The area was completely ice-covered when the glacier’s edge was at the St. Croix Moraine. Water, under high pressure, flowed under the glacier through this channel to the ice margin. The Alliance-owned Straight River Preserve, a 58.26-acre property, protects about 3,500 feet of the river’s shoreline and about 1,000 feet of Long Lake’s shoreline.

![Yellow square icon] From the Trail Access at 270th Avenue, head south, passing through a dry kettle as the Trail makes its way toward the Straight River tunnel channel. After dropping down from the rim of the tunnel channel, meet a white-blazed loop trail on the left. It leads to an enormous white pine on private land and then

![Map of the Straight River and Pine Lake segments of the Ice Age Trail, showing trail paths, roads (STH-48, 270th Ave, 260th Ave, Round Lake Rd), lakes (Long Lake, Johnson Lake, Dahl Lake, Little Ward Lake, Pine Lake, Rhinstad Lake), and points of interest like Dispersed Camping Areas (DCA) and State Ice Age Trail Areas (SIATA).](page_50_image_1_v2.jpg)

continues along the east side of the Straight River to STH-48. The white-blazed trail is at river level and offers excellent viewing of eagles and nesting Canada geese, sandhill cranes, and trumpeter swans.

Back at the intersection with the white-blazed trail, continue south. Here, the Trail goes up and down a small kame, crosses the Straight River, and switch-backs up an esker separating the Straight River from Long Lake. Shortly after reaching the top of the esker, you’ll encounter a junction with a blue-blazed spur trail leading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) on Long Lake for long-distance, multiday hikers.

Continue along the esker above the shore of Long Lake before reaching a private gravel drive and several private cabins. You can see the Straight River to the east and Long Lake to the west. Follow the gravel drive southeast to its intersection with STH-48.

Head east for 0.3 miles along the southern shoulder of STH-48. After about 900 feet, 95th Street heads south. For a short side trip, follow 95th Street to enter another section of the Straight River Preserve (east side of the road). A white-blazed loop trail leads to a wooded hilltop known as Moe’s Mountain and a DCA for long-distance, multiday hikers.

Back on STH-48, cross the Straight River. On the north side of STH-48 is the southern access to the white-blazed loop trail encountered earlier in the segment. Just after crossing the Straight River, the Trail departs from STH-48. Head southeast through a pine grove back toward the shore of the Straight River. Hug the river’s edge for nearly half a mile before turning east, climbing a hill, and traversing field edges to the segment’s endpoint on Round Lake Road.

**Pine Lake Segment**

This segment highlights State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA) property. Except for a larger wetland along the north side of 260th Avenue, most of the western and southern parts of the SIATA contain hummocks composed of sand and pebbles deposited by meltwater

from the Superior Lobe of the Laurentide ice sheet, extensively collapsed outwash plains from buried melting ice or small isolated wetlands. The eastern part of the property hosts a pitted outwash plain.

From the Trail access on Round Lake Road, head east, passing through
wooded and recently logged areas. After 0.5 miles, reach a gated cattle field.
The Trail continues into the pasture. You may enter it through the gate if it is unlocked (please make sure to close it) or use fence stiles on the adjoining fences. Once in the field, hug its edge before heading east into the forest over another fence stile. From here, the segment occasionally shares a snowmobile trail; pay close attention to blazes and directional arrows on posts as the Trail continues east and then heads north toward STH-48.

Cross STH-48 with caution and continue as the Trail bends to the northwest and then north along a field edge to 260th Avenue. Turn right and head east for 0.2 miles on 260th Avenue before going off-road and heading north again. Between 260th Avenue and the segment endpoint at 70th Street, the Trail crosses a SIATA containing a white-blazed loop trail. For a side trip, the loop’s eastern side offers further exploration of the glacial features mentioned above. Otherwise, if you continue straight ahead, you’ll head north and east to the segment’s endpoint on 70th Street.

### AREA SERVICES

| Town       | Service Information                                                             |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Frederic   | Restaurant, convenience store, camping, library, medical service. From From 270th Ave. Trail access go north \~10 mi. |
| Luck       | Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. From From STH-48 Trail access go west \~7 mi.       |
| Cumberland | Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. Area information for Cumberland Chamber of Commerce (715-822-3378, cumberlandchamberwi.com). From From 70th St. Trail access go east \~17 mi.     |

JANET WOOD

![Straight River Segment: Red milkweed attracts a giant swallowtail.](page_51_image_1_v2.jpg)

Straight River Segment: Red milkweed attracts a giant swallowtail.

![Map of the McKenzie Creek Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through the McKenzie Creek State Wildlife Area and Polk County Forest, with landmarks like Clam Falls Flowage, Dinger Lake, and McKenzie Lake.](page_52_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::polk-burnett-c.parent_text:
## McKenzie Creek Segment

**Summary:** The McKenzie Creek Segment is a 9.4-mile trail in Polk & Burnett Counties, running from 270th Ave. (CTH-O) to 50th St. (CTH-O). This wild and scenic segment highlights the steep and hilly topography of the 5,497-acre McKenzie Creek State Wildlife Area, established in 1945.

(Atlas Maps 4f, 5f)

### SNAPSHOT

**9.4 miles:** 270th Ave. (CTH-O) to 50th St. (CTH-O)

*   From McKenzie Creek, the Clam River, Dinger Lake, McKenzie Lake and other smaller streams/creeks.
*   County forest land west of CTH-O only. Primitive camping is not permitted in the State Wildlife Area.
*   In Clam Falls, ~2 mi north of the 60th St. parking area.
*   Portion of segment crossing private land between 60th St. parking area and 50th St. (CTH-O) is closed during gun deer season.
*   Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.
*   Portions overlap snowmobile trails and logging/forest roads.
*   Intersects a few side trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

**① 270th Ave. (CTH-O) Trail Access (45.59676, -92.29654):** From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and STH-63, take STH-48 west 10.0 mi. At 40th St. turn right and go north 2.0 mi. At 270th Ave. turn left and go west 2.5 mi. From Frederic at the intersection of STH-35 and 300th Ave. (CTH-W), take 300th Ave. (CTH-W) east 9.0 mi. At 60th St. (CTH-O) turn right and go south 3.0 mi and follow CTH-O to the west on 270th Ave. for 0.6 mi. Parking: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **50th St. (CTH-O) Trail Access (45.67614, -92.25801):** From *Cumberland* at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63, take STH-48 west for 8.0 mi. At 10th St. (CTH-E) turn right and go north, west then north 4.7 mi. *Note: When CTH-E turns west then north, it becomes 15th St.* At 300th Ave. (CTH-W) turn left and go west 4.0 mi. At 50th St. (CTH-O) turn right and go north 2.5 mi. From *Frederic* at the intersection of STH-35 and 300th Ave. (CTH-W), take 300th Ave. (CTH-W) east 10.0 mi. At 50th St. (CTH-O) turn left and go north 2.5 mi.
**Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) McKenzie Lake boat launch on 280th Ave. (ii) 300th Ave. (CTH-W) (iii) Clam River parking area on 60th St. A short spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

This wild and scenic segment highlights the steep and hilly topography of the 5,497-acre McKenzie Creek State Wildlife Area, established in 1945. The property provides watershed protection and access to four trout streams and six lakes. Growing near the lakes and in the bogs are tamarack, alder, and black spruce, with occasional white spruce and balsam fir. Seasonally, frogs are abundant. The remainder of the forest is primarily red oak, aspen, and pine, with some maple, ironwood, basswood, hickory, and elm. The forest continues to re-establish itself after years of logging and fire. Growing beneath the forest canopy are a wide variety of ferns, wildflowers like yellow lady’s slipper, wild geranium, Solomon’s seal, trillium, and an assortment of fungi along with wild strawberries and raspberries.

From the Trail access on 270th Avenue (CTH-O), head north, enter a northern hardwood forest, and follow a logging road down a slope. After leaving the logging road, head east to a single-track trail, following the ups and downs of the area’s kettle topography. After you pass a Leopold bench overlooking an ephemeral wetland, climb a slope to catch a glimpse of McKenzie Lake through the woods.

Continuing through forest and meadow, the Trail skirts the shore of spring-fed McKenzie Lake and crosses a gravel road leading to a boat ramp. The serene, undeveloped kettle lake is a prime example of an end product of the area’s glacial activity.

North of McKenzie Lake, cut through an area of towering white pines, cross McKenzie Creek, and then climb a ridge overlooking it. As you traverse rolling, ridgeline topography, you may notice a change in tree species. This area is home to undeveloped Margaret Lake and Tula Lake – both accessible from a trail west of where the segment crosses 300th Avenue (CTH-W).

North of 300th Avenue (CTH-W), the Trail shares a sandy logging road through an older logging harvest area. Pay close attention to signage as the Trail intersects other logging roads and a few side trails. Soon you’ll meet McKenzie Creek again; follow the creek north as it winds along McKenzie Creek’s tunnel channel. The creek is a pristine Class I trout stream with a naturally sustaining wild trout population. From a slight rise atop a hummock, enjoy the views of the creek below and the tunnel channel’s valley.

Eventually, you’ll cross a wooden bridge spanning a small tributary of McKenzie Creek and climb out of the tunnel channel. Follow the west shoreline of Dinger lake, one of the area’s many remote kettle lakes, and notice how the elevation drops. As the Trail curves to the north, enjoy the views across the lake.

Continue through the forest and cross the scenic Clam River, a Class III trout stream, on a long boardwalk and bridge.

Almost immediately after crossing Clam River, take a sharp right turn to stay on the route. (Continuing straight will lead you to the 60th Street parking area.) The Trail continues on a well-marked and maintained single-track trail through northern hardwood forest. It crosses a snowmobile trail, cuts through a section of private lands, and enters a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA). Finally, you’ll cross another small ephemeral stream (often dry) before reaching the segment’s terminus on 50th Street (CTH-O).

### AREA SERVICES

**Frederic:** Restaurant, convenience store, camping, library, medical service. From 300th Ave. (CTH-W) Trail access go west ~13 mi.
**Cumberland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. Area information for Cumberland Chamber of Commerce (715-822-3378, cumberlandchamberwi.com). From 300th Ave. (CTH-W) Trail access go east and south ~20 mi.

**Clam Falls:** Restaurant, camping. From the Clam River Trail access on 60th St. go north ~2 mi.

> *Hiking solo affords you the pleasure of being able to laugh at your own jokes and the silly inner workings of your mind. I am often entertained by the inner dialogues, thoughts, feelings, and dramas my mind stirs up, but when I was able to clear the mind by focusing on the present moment, the sights, smells, and sounds brought me to a place of bliss in the beauty of Wisconsin’s forests.*

> DUSTIN BROCKWAY, THOUSAND-MILER

![A wooden boardwalk bridge with railings curves through a lush green forest with ferns and trees.](page_55_image_1_v2.jpg)

JOHN WERNING

*McKenzie Creek Segment: An undulating bridge offers passage over a small tributary.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::polk-burnett-c.parent_text:
## Indian Creek Segment (Atlas Map 5f)

**Summary:** The Indian Creek Segment is a 5.4-mile trail in Polk & Burnett Counties (Atlas Map 5f), running from 50th St. (CTH-O) to 15th St. (CTH-E). This remote segment passes through thickly forested and hummocky areas of a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA), Polk County Forest, and private land.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3 hiker] **5.4 miles**: 50th St. (CTH-O) to 15th St. (CTH-E)

[Icon: water drop] From the headwaters of Indian Creek and other small intermittent streams/creeks.

[Icon: camping] Primitive camping on county forest lands.

[Icon: no hunting] Portion of the segment crossing private land west of 30th St. is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: dog on leash] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: snowmobile/road] Portions overlap snowmobile trails and logging/forest roads.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **50th St. (CTH-O) Trail Access (45.67614, -92.25801)**: From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63, take STH-48 west for 8.0 mi. At 10th St. (CTH-E) turn right and go north, west then north 4.7 mi. **Note**: When CTH-E turns west then north, it becomes 15th St. At 300th Ave. (CTH-W) turn left and go west 4.0 mi. At 50th St. (CTH-O) turn right and go north 2.5 mi. From Frederic at the intersection of STH-35 and 300th Ave. (CTH-W), take 300th Ave. (CTH-W) east 10.0 mi. At 50th St. (CTH-O) turn left and go north 2.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

② **15th St. (CTH-E) Trail Access (45.67297, -92.18594)**: From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63, take STH-48 west for 8.0 mi. At 10th St. (CTH-E) turn right and go north, west then north 7.4 mi. From Frederic at the intersection of STH-35 and 300th Ave. (CTH-W), take 300th Ave. (CTH-W) east 14.0 mi. At 10th St. (CTH-E) turn left and go north, west, then north 2.7 mi. **Note**: When CTH-E turns west then north, it becomes 15th St. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: 30th St.

### THE HIKE

This remote segment passes through thickly forested and hummocky areas of a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA), Polk County Forest, and private land. However, most of the segment is on public land – its forest is managed through select cutting to maintain extensive stands of hardwoods.

From the Trail access on 50th Street (CTH-O), head east through mostly softwood lowlands, crossing numerous creek beds on generally level terrain. This Trail section can be wet in spring and during rainy weather. Thick berry bushes, many with edible berries, are common in late summer, as are a variety of mushrooms.

Then travel through a high-relief hummocky area created when sand and gravel, carried by under-the-ice rivers and streams, were left behind after the ice sheets melted. These hummocks will challenge you with steep climbs and descents and provide lovely views of the surrounding forest.

The 30th Street crossing is the segment’s approximate midpoint and the start of a trek through more open woods and a few rocky creek beds. After a short climb, the Trail follows a forested ridge above a small, picturesque lily-filled lake, the headwaters of Indian Creek.

On the final mile of the segment, follow a narrow hand-carved trail and cross a short bridge and an open meadow dotted with young white pines to the eastern Trail access on 15th Street (CTH-E).

![Map of the Indian Creek Segment of the Ice Age National Scenic Trail, showing the trail route through Polk County Forest, with connections to McKenzie Creek Segment and Sand Creek Segment.](page_57_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Frederic:** Restaurant, convenience store, camping, library, medical service. From 50th St. (CTH-O) access go north and west Trail ~12.5 mi.

**Cumberland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. Area information for Cumberland Chamber of Commerce (715-822-3378, cumberlandchamberwi.com). From 15th St. (CTH-E) Trail access go south and east ~15.5 mi.

**Clam Falls:** Restaurant, camping. From 50th St. (CTH-O) Trail access go north and west ~3 mi.

![Photograph of a white trillium flower blooming in a lush green forest setting.](page_57_image_2_v2.jpg)
KRIS VAN HANDEL

*Indian Creek Segment: Trillium, a spring ephemeral, blooms trailside.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::polk-burnett-c.parent_text:
## Sand Creek Segment (Atlas Maps 5f, 6f)

**Summary:** The Sand Creek Segment is a 6.0-mile trail in Polk & Burnett Counties (Atlas Map 5f, 6f), running from 15th St. (CTH-E) Northern Trail Access to Lake 32 Rd.. This beautiful, remote segment crosses public land: the county forests of Polk and Burnett counties established in the early-to-mid 1930s.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage icon] **6.0 miles (5.7 IAT, 0.3 CR):** 15th St. (CTH-E) Northern Trail Access to Lake 32 Rd.

[Icon: water drop icon] From Sand Creek and some smaller streams/creeks and wetland areas.

[Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands.

[Icon: hunting icon] The portion of the segment crossing private land between 15th St. (CTH-E) and Polk County Forest boundary is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: roadwalk icon] Segment includes a short connecting route roadwalk. Small portions overlap snowmobile trails and gravel/dirt roads; a significant portion overlaps logging roads.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] **15th St. (CTH-E) Northern Trail Access (45.67297, -92.18594):** From Frederic at the intersection of STH-35 and 300th Ave. (CTH-W), take 300th Ave. (CTH-W) east 14.0 mi. At 10th St. (CTH-E) turn left and go north, west, then north 2.7 mi. From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63, take STH-48 west for 8.0 mi. At 10th St. (CTH-E) turn right and go north, west then north for 7.4 mi. Note: When CTH-E turns west then north, it becomes 15th St. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2 icon] **Lake 32 Rd. Trail Access (45.63999, -92.11874):** From Frederic at the intersection of STH-35 and 300th Ave (CTH-W), take 300th Ave. (CTH-W) east 14.0 mi. At 10th St. (CTH-E) turn right and go south 1.0 mi. At 290th St. turn left and go east 1.0 mi. At Polk Barron St. turn left and go north 0.5 mi. At 29 ½ Ave., turn right and go east 1.5 mi. At 1 ½ St. turn left and go north 0.5 mi. At 30th Ave. turn right and go east 0.2 mi to junction with Lake 32 Rd. From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63, take STH-48 west for 5.0 mi. At 2nd St. turn right and go north 1.6 mi. At 26th Ave. turn left and go west 1.0 mi. At 1st St. turn right and go north 1.5 mi. At 27 ½ Ave. turn right and go east 0.5 mi. At 1 ½ St. turn left and go north 2.5 mi. At 30th Ave. turn right and go east 0.2 mi to the junction with Lake 32 Rd. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

### THE HIKE

This beautiful, remote segment crosses public land: the county forests of Polk and Burnett counties established in the early-to-mid 1930s. After decades of radically harvesting trees, lumber companies sold the cutover land to white settlers. These new landowners attempted, but failed, to turn it into farmland. Unable to pay their taxes, they abandoned their property. County governments, relying on property taxes to build roads and schools, quickly became cash strapped. The state’s newly created Forest Crop Law program allowed counties to acquire tax-delinquent land and establish county forests – many acres, hand-planted – for future timber-sales revenue.

From the 15th Street (CTH-E) northern Trail access and parking area, walk 0.3 miles south on 15th Street (CTH-E). Here the segment heads east, off-road, and crosses private land for half a mile. It then enters Polk County Forest lands and bends to follow the Sand Creek tunnel channel over hummocky sand and gravel. Sand Creek flows through the bottom of the tunnel channel in a valley that extends to the town of Cumberland and includes several large lakes.

Just southeast of the Polk/Burnett county line is a bridge over Sand Creek, a favorite resting and gathering spot for hikers. The creek is home to a wide

![Map of the Indian Creek Segment and Timberland Hills Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through Polk, Burnett, and Barron Counties, including Sand Creek State Fishery Area and various county forests.](page_59_image_1_v2.jpg)

variety of fish, including largemouth bass, smallmouth bass, and walleye. Shortly after the creek crossing, hook up with a dirt forest road as it goes south and southeast. Turn left off the road and meander along old logging roads through the woods. Pay close attention to signage, as these logging roads can become overgrown.

You’ll cross in front of an old overgrown beaver dam resulting in a wet or flooded Trail in the spring or rainy periods. Follow the south side of a wetland before reaching the intersection of 1½ Street and 30th Avenue. Head east on 30th Avenue to the segment’s endpoint, where 30th Avenue meets Lake 32 Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Frederic:** Restaurant, convenience store, camping, library, medical service. From 15th St. (CTH-E) Trail access go north and west ~18 mi.

**Cumberland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. Area information for Cumberland Chamber of Commerce (715-822-3378, cumberlandchamberwi.com). From Lake 32 Rd. Trail access go east 12.3 mi.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Washburn and Barron Counties, showing the Timberland Hills, Grassy Lake, and Bear Lake segments. The map includes local roads, towns like Shell Lake, Barronett, Haugen, Cumberland, Rice Lake, and Barron, and a legend for trail types and land ownership.](page_60_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Barron, Washburn, & Rusk Counties

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Barron, Washburn, and Rusk Counties. The map highlights four trail segments: Tuscobia Segment, Hemlock Creek Segment, Northern Blue Hills Segment, and Southern Blue Hills Segment. The trail route is marked in red, passing through the town of Birchwood and near Weyerhaeuser. Key features include the Tuscobia State Trail, Rusk County Forest, and various highways such as 48, 53, 8, and 40. Nearby towns like Cameron, Chetek, and Bruce are also shown. An inset map of Wisconsin indicates the location of these counties within the state. The Ice Age Trail Alliance website, iceagetrail.org, is listed on the map.](page_61_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::barron-washbur.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::barron-washbur.parent_text:
# Barron & Washburn Counties

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Barron and Washburn counties](page_62_image_2_v2.jpg)

Atlas Maps 6f–10f; Databook pages 8–10

**Total miles**: 53.6 (Trail 43.0; Connecting Route 10.6)

The Ice Age Trail’s route through Barron and Washburn counties weaves across three different landscapes. To the west and north is the Superior Lobe end moraine, a lake-studded zone dominated by steep kettle topography. To the east is the Blue Hills, a 1.6-billion-year-old quartzite range. Between the two is an outwash plain created by meltwater from the Chippewa and Superior glacial lobes.

The Ice Age Trail enjoys passage across many acres of public land: Barron and Washburn County Forests are filled with lakes, streams, wetlands, and wildlife. These once vast forests attracted the interest of businessmen in a newly formed and rapidly developing United States. These enterprising speculators considered Wisconsin timberlands a good investment based on the profitable lumber industry of western New York. And, the westward push of white settlers moving to the treeless prairies of Indiana and Illinois created a demand for lumber for building homes, barns, and fences.

From 1860 to 1910, these forests provided the raw material for a thriving but short-sighted lumber industry. After a few decades, lumber companies sold the cutover land to European immigrants and American pioneers. These new landowners attempted but failed to turn their property into farmland. Unable to pay their taxes, they abandoned it. The state’s newly created Forest Crop Law program allowed counties to acquire tax-delinquent land and establish county forests – many acres, hand-planted – for future timber-sales revenue, wildlife habitat, and today’s recreation.

Primitive camping is allowed on Washburn and Barron County Forest land. Please camp at least 200 feet from trails and waterways.

The Ice Age Trail also shares the Tuscobia State Trail, a multiuse rail-trail across the outwash plain.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Superior Lobe Chapter formed in 2001 and meets regularly in the Rice Lake area. The Chapter maintains local Ice Age Trail segments and sponsors seasonal outings, including snowshoe hikes and birdwatching walks. The Chapter also offers the Superior Lobetrotter award to hikers completing all Ice Age Trail segments from Lake 32 Road in Burnett County to CTH-F in Rusk

County. Visit IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards to begin your Superior Lobetrotter adventure. To learn more about the Superior Lobe Chapter, visit IceAgeTrail.org/superior-lobe-chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

* **Barron County Visitor Information**: visitbarroncounty.com
* **Barron County Forest Office**: 715-537-6296, barroncountywi.gov
* **Washburn County Forest Office**: 715-635-4490, co.washburn.wi.us/departments/forestry
* **Washburn County Tourism**: washburncounty.org
* **Tuscobia State Trail (TST)**: On Trail, 715-415-0297, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/name/tuscobia

DAVE CALIEBE

![Grassy Lake Segment: Sunset reflections.](page_63_image_1_v2.jpg)

Grassy Lake Segment: Sunset reflections.

## Timberland Hills Segment (Atlas Map 6f)

**Summary:** The Timberland Hills Segment is a 10.9-mile trail in Barron & Washburn Counties (Atlas Map 6f), running from Lake 32 Rd. to Leach Lake Rd.. This segment traverses the hummocky topography of the Superior Lobe's St. Croix Moraine as it follows ski and snowshoe trails on hilly terrain through the Timberland West and Timberland ski areas.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiker icon] [Icon: 32] **10.9 miles**: Lake 32 Rd. to Leach Lake Rd.

[Icon: water icon] From some small lakes and small intermittent streams/creeks.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not allowed on groomed ski trails in winter.**

[Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands.

[Icon: trail type icon] Small portions overlap logging/ forest roads and a gravel road. Large portions overlap ski and snowshoe trails. Hike to the side of groomed ski trails in winter. Ski-area trails are open to horseback riding during the non-ski season.

[Icon: toilet icon] Two portables available near the crossings of CTH-H and Boyd Ln.

[Icon: ski/snowshoe icon] Ski/snowshoe network trails are open to hiking.

[Icon: hunting icon] Portions of segment crossing private land (i) from CTH-H to the Timberland Hills ski area western boundary and (ii) Timberland Hills ski area eastern boundary to Leach Lake Rd. are closed during gun deer season.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **Lake 32 Rd. Trail Access (45.63999, -92.11874):** From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63, take STH-48 west for 5.0 mi. At 2nd St. turn right and go north 1.6 mi. At 26th Ave. turn left and go west 1.0 mi. At 1st St. turn right and go north 1.5 mi. At 27½ Ave. turn right and go east 0.5 mi. At 17½ St. turn left and go north 2.5 mi. At 30th Ave. turn right and go east 0.2 mi to the junction with Lake 32 Rd. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

② **Leach Lake Rd. Trail Access (45.66111, -92.01683):** From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63, take STH-63 north for 8.3 mi. At Brickyard Rd. turn left and go west 0.9 mi. At Leach Lake Rd. turn right and go north then west then north 0.7 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Timberland West Ski Area parking area on 3rd St. (CTH-H) (ii) Timberland Hills Ski Area main parking area on Boyd Ln.

### THE HIKE

This segment traverses the hummocky topography of the Superior Lobe's St. Croix Moraine as it follows ski and snowshoe trails on hilly terrain through the Timberland West and Timberland ski areas. It also takes advantage of public land, especially the Burnett County Forest, which was established in 1932, with 29,800 tax-delinquent acres enrolled in the Forest Crop Law program.

Much of the early planting was done by hand using Riverside Civilian Conservation Corps labor. Today, the mature, productive forest covers 111,000 acres, rich in species diversity. The county manages the forest for recreational opportunities, wildlife habitat, watershed protection, and county revenue through selective timber harvests.

![Map of Timberland Hills Segment showing Lake 32, Sand Creek Segment, and Timberland West Ski Area](page_64_image_1_v2.jpg)

From the Trail Access on Lake 32 Road, head south on the Waterman Lakes Snowshoe Trail on a logging road, then east on a narrower snowshoe

Trail. The segment weaves through mixed hardwood forests and crosses an old beaver dam between two wetlands.

After the Trail transitions to the wide, grassy path of the Timberland West ski trails, cross CTH-H about 500 feet north of the Timberland West parking area. Continue east for about a quarter mile on Skinner Road, a gravel road skirting a small, unnamed lake. (If you arrive where the road ends at two driveways, you’ve walked too far.) Leave the road, following the southern edge of an open field before heading northeast across it and re-entering the woods.

Enter Burnett County Forest lands at a signed junction and connect with the wide, mowed trails of the Timberland Hills ski area. There are several intersecting ski trails, so look for, and pay close attention to, yellow blazes and Ice Age Trail directional arrows. The route through the ski area is quite hilly, often steep, and may have wet areas at the bottoms of the hills. Continue north to the ski area’s main parking lot and winter warming hut off Boyd Lane.

From Boyd Lane, generally continue north, then east through a stand of white pine and over a bridge. Expect the Trail to ascend and frequently descend as it goes in and out of two tunnel channels, one of which flowed from the area of the present-day South Fork of the Clam River and the other from Leach Lake.

At the east side of the ski area, leave the ski trail to follow a narrow track alongside some wetlands and beaver ponds. Sit for a while on a Leopold bench

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Timberland Hills Ski Area and Burnett County Forest, showing trail segments, distances, and connecting routes.](page_65_image_1_v2.jpg)

2.5-mi CR to Grassy Lake Segment. South, east then south on Leach Lake Rd., east on Brickyard Rd., south on Old Hwy 63, east on Pershing Rd.

overlooking a scenic pond to the south. After going through a blueberry patch and down a steep hill, you’ll meet a grassy road near a log fish carving. Follow the grassy road for about a quarter mile, paying close attention to signage, as there are intersecting roads and a right turn. Leave the road, head left, and enter a brushy area (site of a clearcut). Skirt a pond’s shoreline, climb the side of a steep ravine, and meander through the woods to the segment’s terminus on Leach Lake Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Timberland Hills and Timberland West ski areas:** Lodging, camping. On Trail. (1821 Boyd Rd., Barronett, winter trail conditions: timberlandhills.com); Rental lodging at Burnett County Camper Cabin. Accessible by foot. Nearest parking at Boyd Ln. parking area. (Rental reservations: burnettcounty.com/1148/Camper-Cabin). Camping (primitive) on Burnett County Forest land.

**Barronett:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store. From Leach Lake Rd. Trail access go south ~2.5 mi.

**Cumberland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. Area information for Cumberland Chamber of Commerce (715-822-3378, cumberlandchamberwi.com). From Leach Lake Rd. Trail access go south ~10 mi.

**Shell Lake:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. Area information for Shell Lake Chamber of Commerce (715-468-7679, visitshelllake.com). From Leach Lake Rd. Trail access go north ~8.5 mi.

> *Thanks to the Trail, I can now say some of my favorite things in life are being in absolute silence, listening to my footsteps shuffling along the path, and watching the fresh-fallen leaves dance on the road as I follow my sister to our parking spot. Camping in the Northwoods, the darkness of the tree bark against the autumn colors, the aroma of pine forests, the haunting wind in the trees, a single-track tree-lined view with a yellow blaze in sight (which is always your friend).*
>
> NANCY “TWEEDLE DEE” LEHMAN, THOUSAND-MILER

![A scenic view of a trail through a forest with vibrant autumn foliage in shades of red, orange, and yellow under a clear blue sky.](page_67_image_1_v2.jpg)

BARB LEETZOW

*Timberland Hills Segment: Autumn’s full glory.*

**Barron & Washburn Counties**

## Grassy Lake Segment (Atlas Map 7f)

**Summary:** The Grassy Lake Segment is a 8.5-mile trail in Barron & Washburn Counties (Atlas Map 7f), running from Pershing Rd. to 30th Ave.. Like the Timberland Hills Segment, the remote Grassy Lake Segment continues through the hummocky topography of the Superior Lobe’s St. Croix Moraine and skirts beaver-inhabited lakes and wetlands, mostly following logging roads.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: A2] [Icon: 4] **8.5 miles**: Pershing Rd. to 30th Ave.

[Icon: water drop icon] From several small lakes, streams/creeks and other wetland areas.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: campground icon] At nearby private campground [Icon: A].

Nearly the full segment is on logging/forest roads, portions of which are open to snowmobiling. A portion overlaps primitive Shingle Camp Fire Ln., which is open to cars and ATVs.

[Icon: primitive camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **Pershing Rd. Trail Access (45.65388, -91.97662)**: From Cumberland at the intersection of STH-48 and USH-63 take USH-63 north for 8.3 mi. At Brickyard Rd. turn right and go east 0.1 mi. At Old Hwy. 63 (unsigned) turn right and go south 0.1 mi. At Pershing Rd. (unsigned) turn left and go east 0.7 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **30th Ave. Trail Access (45.63890, -91.87814)**: From Haugen at the intersection of CTH-SS and CTH-V, take CTH-V west, south and west 3.1 mi.. Where CTH-V curves south for a second time, continue straight west on 27th Ave. 0.3 mi. At 13 ¾-16<sup>th</sup> St., turn right and go north 4.0 mi. At 30th Ave. turn left and go west 0.1 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: Lehman Lake Rd.

### THE HIKE

Like the Timberland Hills Segment, the remote Grassy Lake Segment continues through the hummocky topography of the Superior Lobe’s St. Croix Moraine and skirts beaver-inhabited lakes and wetlands, mostly following logging roads.

It also crosses portions of the 149,000-acre Washburn County Forest, established in 1930.

*Note: Ice Age Trail signage can be knocked down by animals or concealed by foliage. Portions of the segment may also be wet or flooded, so wear appropriate gear. No footbridges exist on the segment, but you may find yourself crossing active beaver dams as you navigate wet areas.*

![Map of Grassy Lake Segment showing Washburn County Forest and Grassy Lake](page_68_image_1_v2.jpg)

From the Trail access on Pershing Road, head north, go around a gate, and pass within 800 feet of Grassy Lake’s western edge. Head north, then east to Lehman Lake Road (gated) through generally forested terrain and past some small lakes and wetlands.

The segment enters the Washburn County Forest Welsh Lake Unit east of Lehman Lake Road. For the first 0.6 miles, you’ll share the route with primitive Shingle Camp Fire Lane, which is open to car and ATV traffic. After 0.6 miles, leave Shingle Camp Fire Lane, following a logging road to the right. Almost immediately, you’ll veer to the left and head up a hill, navigate some berms, and then rejoin the logging road. Turn left at the next logging road junction (about 0.6 miles after leaving the Fire Lane). It soon transitions to a wide, active logging road. In about 250 feet, turn left again, following the wide

road first right and then left for approximately 0.5 miles. Again, the Trail briefly jogs left, then turns right, leaves the logging road, and transitions back to a grassy path, meandering through the woods and past wetland areas.

Continue through more wetland areas and some hummocky terrain to the segment’s terminus on 30th Avenue (gated).

### AREA SERVICES

**Barronett:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store. From Pershing Rd. Trail access go west 0.7 mi. At Old Hwy 63 turn right and go north 0.1 mi. At Brickyard Rd. turn left and go west 0.1 mi. At USH-63 turn left and go south 1.5 mi.

**Cumberland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From Pershing Rd. Trail access go west and south ~9 mi. Area information for Cumberland Chamber of Commerce (715-822-3378, cumberlandchamberwi.com).

**Shell Lake:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From Pershing Rd. Trail access, go west 0.7 mi. At Old Hwy. 63, turn right and go north 0.1 mi. At Brickyard Rd. turn left and go west 0.1 mi. At USH-63 turn right and go north 6.9 mi. Area information for Shell Lake Chamber of Commerce (715-468-7679, visitshelllake.com).

**Sarona:** Camping. From 30th Ave. Trail access go north ~8 mi.

**Haugen:** Restaurant, grocery store, camping. From 30th Ave. Trail access go south and east 7.3 mi. .

![Map of the Bear Lake Segment of the Ice Age Trail in Washburn and Barron Counties. The map shows the trail route through Washburn County Forest, passing by Lehman Lake, Johnson Lake, and a beaver dam. It includes mileage markers (0.6, 0.9, 1.7, 2.6), road names like Pershing Rd, Fire Ln, and 30th Ave, and a scale bar. A text box in the lower left corner provides directions: "2.5-mi CR to Timberland Hills Segment. West on Pershing Rd., north on Old Hwy 63, west on Brickyard Rd., north, west then north on Leach Lake Rd."](page_69_image_1_v2.jpg)

## Bear Lake Segment (Atlas Map 8f)

**Summary:** The Bear Lake Segment is a 5.4-mile trail in Barron & Washburn Counties (Atlas Map 8f), running from 30th Ave. to 16th St. (CTH-VV). This segment continues from the Grassy Lake Segment through the hummocky topography of the Superior Lobe’s St. Croix Moraine, the outer edge of which is about 1.5 miles southeast of the segment’s endpoint on 16th Street (CTH-VV).

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Hiking icon]
② ③ **5.4 miles:** 30th Ave. to 16th St. (CTH-VV)

[Icon: Water icon]
Crooked Lake and other small lakes, intermittent streams/creeks and wetland areas.

[Icon: Dog on leash icon]
Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Primitive camping icon]
Primitive camping on county forest lands.

[Icon: Multi-use trail icon]
Small portions overlap a bike trail, gravel roads and logging/forest roads.

[Icon: Campground icon]
At two nearby private campgrounds.

[Icon: Scout camp icon]
Scout camp trail network.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Parking icon 1]
**① 30th Ave. Trail Access (45.63890, -91.87814):** From Haugen at the intersection of CTH-SS and CTH-V, take CTH-V west, south and west 3.1 mi.. Where CTH-V curves south for a second time, continue straight west on 27th Ave. for 0.3 mi. At 13¾-16th St., turn right and go north 4.0 mi. At 30th Ave. turn left and go west 0.1 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: Parking icon 2]
**② 16th St. (CTH-VV) Trail Access (45.61860, -91.82714):** From Haugen at the intersection of CTH-SS and CTH-V, take CTH-V west 0.2 mi. At Norvin Ave. turn right and go north 0.2 mi. At 5th St. (CTH-VV), which first becomes 28th Ave. and then becomes 16th St., turn left and go west then north 2.7 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** 13¾ - 16th St.

### THE HIKE

This segment continues from the Grassy Lake Segment through the hummocky topography of the Superior Lobe’s St. Croix Moraine, the outer edge of which is about 1.5 miles southeast of the segment’s endpoint on 16th Street (CTH-VV). It visits several lakes and scenic wetlands and also meanders through portions of the 16,000-acre Barron County Forest, established in 1940.

From the Trail access on 30th Avenue, head east on the road for 0.1 miles to an intersection with Barron County’s 13¾ - 16th Street (a road whose name changes to Shallow Lake Road a short distance north, at the Barron/ Washburn county line). Turn south at that intersection and follow 13¾ - 16th Street (a Rustic Road) for 0.1 miles to where the segment leaves the road and continues east through a mix of forest and marsh on Barron County Forest land. You may catch a glimpse of Bear Lake through the woods in the distance to the east as you cross the hummocky terrain, especially during the leaf-off season.

As the segment nears Crooked Lake’s southern shore, you will pass two benches overlooking the lake. From here you leave Barron County Forest land and enter the Boy Scouts’ L.E. Phillips Scout Reservation. *Note: Please stay on the Trail crossing through the Scout property and do not explore or use their facilities.* Then, cross a cattail-lined wetland on a 16-foot puncheon and 190-foot boardwalk. This area is a haven for northern waterfowl and dragonflies and often features a nightly chorus of frogs.

From this area, continue east and then northeast, passing the camp’s Bass Lake beach before reaching the segment’s endpoint on 16th Street (CTH-VV).

![Map of the Grassy Lake Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through Bear Lake State Wildlife Area, Barron County Forest, and L.E. Phillips Scout Reservation, with connections to the Tuscobia Segment.](page_71_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Barronett:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store. From the 30th Ave. Trail access go west ~6 mi.

**Sarona:** Camping. From the 30th Ave. Trail access, take Shallow Lake Rd. north 2.2 mi.

**Haugen:** Restaurant, grocery store, camping. From 16th St. (CTH-VV) go east and south 2.9 mi.

**Rice Lake:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the 16th St. (CTH-VV) Trail access go east and south ~11 mi.

*You learn a lot when long-distance hiking. For example, just how much your body loves (read: needs) the flow of endorphins when you hit 15 miles hiked at a single crack. Chief among the lessons is to "Slow. It. Down." We live life at such a frenzied pace. Yet, how often do we take the time to stop, reflect, and discern? Hiking the Trail provides a needed break and space and time to contemplate. And through this process, we achieve discernment.*

*STEPHEN "SHERPA AND SHAMBLES" LACNIAK, THOUSAND-MILER*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::barron-washbur.parent_text:
## Tuscobia Segment (Atlas Maps 8f, 9f, 10f)

**Summary:** The Tuscobia Segment is a 11.2-mile trail in Barron & Washburn Counties (Atlas Map 8f, 9f, 10f), running from CTH-SS to Featherstone Dr. at Loch Lomond Rd. (28¾ St.). This segment, featuring glacial erratics, several small kettle lakes, views across an outwash plain, and a tunnel channel, follows a portion of the multiuse Tuscobia State Trail (TST) and a short portion of the historic Blueberry Line railway.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiker icon] [Icon: 1] **11.2 miles**: CTH-SS to Featherstone Dr. at Loch Lomond Rd. (28¾ St.)

* [Icon: water tap] At the Brill baseball field and park.
* [Icon: ColdCache] Three ColdCache sites on segment.
* [Icon: water drop] From several small lakes, the Brill River and other small intermittent streams/creeks.
* [Icon: dog] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: tent] At Chain Lake/Twin Lake [Icon: iA] walk-in campsite.
* [Icon: dashed line] Portions of the TST are open to biking, horseback riding, ATVs, and snowmobiling.
* [Icon: tent/RV] At nearby Waldo Carlson County Park, Doolittle Park and private campgrounds [Icon: A].
* [Icon: trail intersection] The western end of the segment intersects the Wild Rivers State Trail. The TST continues east from Balsam Lake Rd. A blue-blazed spur trail leads from the eastern end of the segment to the western edge of Birchwood.
* [Icon: picnic table] At Waldo Carlson County Park.
* [Icon: parking] At the CTH-SS parking area, the Brill baseball field and park and Waldo Carlson County Park.
* [Icon: wheelchair] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **CTH-SS Trail Access (45.57228, -91.76016)**: From Rice Lake at the intersection of STH-48 and CTH-SS, take CTH-SS north 4.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **Featherstone Dr. at Loch Lomond Rd. (28¾ St.) Trail Access (45.64443, -91.56716)**: From Birchwood at the intersection of Main St. and STH-48, take STH-48 west 1.0 mi. At Loch Lomond Rd. (28¾ St.) turn left and go south 0.1 mi to the intersection with gravel Featherstone Dr. **Parking**: Roadside.

**Additional Parking**: (i) 21st St. roadside parking. (ii) Town of Brill parking area. (iii) 26th St. roadside parking. (iv) Big Fish Wayside parking area in Birchwood on STH-48 at Vance St.

![Map of Tuscobia Segment showing trail path, mileage, and nearby landmarks like Tuscobia Lake and Bear Lake Segment connection.](page_72_image_1_v2.jpg)

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment near Brill, showing the Tuscobia State Trail, Barron County Forest, and various lakes including Round Lake, Chain Lake, and Goose Lake.](page_73_image_1_v2.jpg)

### THE HIKE

This segment, featuring glacial erratics, several small kettle lakes, views across an outwash plain, and a tunnel channel, follows a portion of the multiuse Tuscobia State Trail (TST) and a short portion of the historic Blueberry Line railway.

In 1966, the TST became the second abandoned rail line in the state converted for recreational use. The Omaha Railway’s Park Falls Line, built in 1902, ran from Tuscobia, north of Rice Lake, to Park Falls, Wis. At peak operation, 1 passenger, 1 freight, and 11 logging trains traveled each way each day – 26 trains passing a given point, offering access to travel and vital support to the local economy.

In 1965, after declines in the logging and railroad industries and the automobile’s rise, the line was abandoned. Hulda Hilfiker, a local resident, persuaded neighbors and the State of Wisconsin to convert the railroad grade to a recreational trail instead of dividing the land among farmers. Now, the portion of the TST shared with the Ice Age Trail is open for multiple forms of recreation.

Between its Trail access parking area on CTH-SS and the town of Brill, the segment links the terminal moraines of the Superior and Chippewa lobes, crossing their outwash plain. The Trail maintains a level grade with one exception: an area about a mile east of CTH-SS. Here it dips into a valley created

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment near Birchwood, showing Balsam Lake, Red Cedar Lake, Tuscobia State Trail, and the town of Birchwood. A text box provides directions: "2.4-mi CR to Hemlock Creek Segment. South on Loch Lomond Rd. (28 3/4 St.), southwest on Tagalong Ave (28 11/16 St.), south on Earn St. (28 11/16 St.) west on Loch Lomond Ave. (28 1/8 Ave.), south on Finohorn Rd. (28 11/16 St.)."](page_74_image_1_v2.jpg)

– once the site of a 358-foot-long railroad trestle – and crosses scenic Tuscobia Creek flowing south into Tuscobia Lake.

Upon reaching Brill, the segment crosses onto the Chippewa Moraine. Between Brill and Angus, you will pass numerous erratics and small kettle lakes.

Soon after crossing CTH-T you will enter Washburn County, where ATV use is allowed on the TST; caution is advised. The segment parallels the ATV portion of the TST until joining it at the bridge over “The Narrows.” This structure connects Balsam Lake and Red Cedar Lake, which are part of a tunnel channel of the Chippewa Moraine. A bench overlooks Balsam Lake on the north side of the TST.

Once over the bridge, depart the TST at Balsam Lake Road and head south across STH-48. Use caution when crossing the highway. From here, turn east and follow part of the historic Blueberry Line railway, paralleling Featherstone Drive, STH-48 and the TST. The Blueberry Line ceased passenger service to Birchwood in 1936, and in 2011 part of the railbed was refurbished and converted to a footpath. The segment ends where Featherstone Drive meets Loch Lomond Road (28<sup>3</sup>/<sub>4</sub> Street).

A blue-blazed spur trail continues northeast to the western edge of Birchwood. This spur ends at the “Big Fish Wayside” parking area. Here, Alliance volunteers and workers from local Dobiehill Timber engineered a 14<sup>th</sup>-century-style timber-framed structure that acts as a depot for hikers arriving at, or departing from, Birchwood.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Indian Mounds Park:** From the western terminus of the Tuscobia Segment, take CTH-SS 4.2 mi southeast to Rice Lake, turn east on North St. and merge onto Lakeshore Dr. (1015 Lakeshore Dr., Rice Lake).

Twelve conical mounds remain of a former 51-mound group, open to public foot traffic. A historical sign 100 yd farther south marks the location of the Bayfield Indian Trail. Several metal sculptures along Lakeshore Dr., near the park, provide information about the area’s history and Indigenous people.

### AREA SERVICES

**Haugen:** Restaurant, grocery store, camping. From the CTH-SS Trail access go ~2.5 mi north on CTH-SS.

**Rice Lake:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-SS Trail access go 4.5 mi south on CTH-SS. Area info from the Rice Lake Area Chamber of Commerce (715-234-2126 or 877-234-2126, ricelakechamber.org).

**Brill:** Restaurant, camping. On Trail.

**Chain Lake/Twin Lake Walk-in Campsite:** From the Trail crossing with CTH-V (28<sup>th</sup> Ave.), go east 1.2 mi on CTH-V. Walk-in campsite (0.1 mi south); first come, first served. No facilities. (parks.co.barron.wi.us/parks/camping/chain_laketwin_lake_primitive).

**Mikana:** Restaurant, grocery store, lodging, camping. From the crossing at CTH-T go 3.0 mi south on CTH-T/STH-48. Camping at Waldo Carlson Park & Campground (715-354-3353 or 715-418-1850 and off season, 715-537-6419, parks.co.barron.wi.us/parks/camping/waldo_carlson_park/).

**Birchwood:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping. From Loch Lomond Rd. (28¾ St.) and STH-48 go 1.0 mi northeast on STH-48. Area info available from the Birchwood Area Chamber of Commerce (birchwoodwi.com).

![A view of Red Cedar Lake from the Trail.](page_75_image_1_v2.jpg)

DON ERICKSON

Tuscobia Segment: A view of Red Cedar Lake from the Trail.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::barron-washbur.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::barron-washbur.parent_text:
## Hemlock Creek Segment (Atlas Map 10f)

**Summary:** The Hemlock Creek Segment is a 7.0-mile trail in Barron & Washburn Counties (Atlas Map 10f), running from Finohorn Rd. (28<sup>11</sup>/<sub>16</sub> St.) to S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F. This segment highlights the western edge of the Blue Hills and a loop around Hemlock Creek.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3] **7.0 miles**: Finohorn Rd. (28<sup>11</sup>/<sub>16</sub> St.) to S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F

* [Icon: potable water, picnic, and hiker icons] At the Murphy Flowage Picnic Area and Murphy Flowage Campground.
* [Icon: water drop icon] From Pigeon and Hemlock creeks and a few small intermittent streams and lakes/wetland areas.
* [Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands. Permit required on Rusk Co. Forest land.
* [Icon: campground icon] At Murphy Flowage Campground.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: snowshoe icon] Small portions overlap a snowshoe trail and logging/forest roads.
* [Icon: spur trail icon] A blue-blazed spur trail and a white-blazed loop south of Hemlock Creek.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. [Icon: 1] **Finohorn Rd. (28<sup>11</sup>/<sub>16</sub> St.) Trail Access (45.61148, -91.56690)**: From Birchwood at the intersection of Main St. and STH-48, take STH-48 west 1.0 mi. At Loch Lomond Rd. (28¾ St.) turn left and go south 2.3 mi then west 0.1 mi. At **Finohorn Rd. (28<sup>11</sup>/<sub>16</sub> St.)** turn left and go south 0.1 mi. Parking: Roadside.

2. [Icon: 2] **S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F Trail Access (45.56648, -91.50185)**: From Birchwood at the intersection of Main St. and STH-48, take STH-48 east 2.1 mi. At CTH-F turn right and go south 8.7 mi. Parking: Roadside on S. Bucks Lake Rd.

**Additional Parking**: (i) E. Lake Shore Dr. A 0.5-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT. (ii) Bolger Rd. (25½ Ave.) (iii) Murphy Flowage Picnic Area on CTH-F. Follow access road around to the picnic area.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights the western edge of the Blue Hills and a loop around Hemlock Creek. It crosses the Barron County Forest – 16,000 acres of public land. The topography of the forest and surrounding area formed as glaciers eroded hilltops and filled valleys. As a result, the terrain features mild elevation gains, and the forest undulates with gently rolling hills.

Head south from the trail access on Finohorn Road (28<sup>11</sup>/<sub>16</sub> Street), crossing Pigeon Creek and a gravel ATV trail. Continue over flat terrain for about 0.9 miles, where you’ll meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading south to East Lake Shore Drive. A little farther along is an abandoned blue heron rookery to the east, now home to nesting osprey. South of the rookery is a boardwalk; use caution and expect wet feet. An alternative is to walk on top of the beaver dam, paralleling the segment.

As the Trail travels toward Bolger Road, it ascends into Barron quartzite hills, the first taste of the Blue Hills, which are highlighted in greater depth along Rusk County’s segments. You’ll pass Firestorm Valley, the edge of the 1898 firestorm that burned almost 33,000 acres of forest. Watch for yellow blazes; the route intersects many grassy logging roads.

![Map of the Northern Blue Hills Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the route from Finohorn Rd. in Barron County to CTH-F in Rusk County. The map includes labels for Loch Lomond Ave., Pigeon Creek, Barron County Forest, Rusk County Forest, Bolger Rd., Hemlock Lake, Hemlock Creek, Murphy Flowage, and Murphy Flowage Picnic Area. A text box provides directions for a 2.4-mi CR to Tuscobia Segment: North on Finohorn Rd. (28¹¹/₁₆ St.), east on Loch Lomond Ave. (28¹/₈ Ave.), north on Earn St. (28¹¹/₁₆ Ave.), north on Tagalong Ave. (28¹¹/₁₆ St.), north on Loch Lomond Rd. (28³/₄ St.).](page_77_image_1_v2.jpg)

Soon after entering Rusk County, you’ll cross Bolger Road. South of it, the Trail drops steeply toward Hemlock Creek. Along the way, you’ll pass some small, quickly flowing creeks. After paralleling Hemlock Creek for half a mile, you’ll reach a junction with a white-blazed loop trail highlighting the creek’s southern banks. Continue east on the main route along the north side of Hemlock Creek. Several trailside benches present scenic views of the wetland below. After crossing the creek on an abandoned road bridge, enter the Murphy Flowage Picnic Area.

Continue south through a forested area, passing the east end of the white-blazed loop trail, and turn left at a primitive road. Follow it east to the segment’s terminus on CTH-F.

### AREA SERVICES

**Birchwood:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping. From the Finohorn Rd. (28¹¹/₁₆ St.) Trail access go north then east 3.6 mi.

**Murphy Flowage Recreation Area:** Camping. On Trail. Picnic area is on the west side of CTH-F on the Trail route. The campground is on the east side of CTH-F, 1.0 mi north of S. Bucks Lake Rd. Rusk County Forestry Department (715-532-2113, parks.ruskcounty.org/parks).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rusk-county::f.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rusk-county::f.parent_text:
# Rusk County

Atlas Maps 10f–14f; Databook pages 11–12

Total miles: 33.7 (Trail 16.9; Connecting Route 16.8)

![Map of Wisconsin with Rusk County highlighted in yellow](page_78_image_2_v2.jpg)

Two distinct landscapes dominate the Ice Age Trail route through Rusk County. The northern half of the county hosts a mountain range of quartzite bedrock older than the Appalachian Mountains and most of the Rocky Mountains. The 1.6-billion-year-old quartzite peaks, worn down over time, are called the Blue Hills for their many shades of blue, formed in early morning mist.

The Chippewa Lobe, flowing south and southwest, halted as it reached the Blue Hills. It left a legacy of scattered lakes offering abundant wildlife-enticing habitat. As a result, the southern half of Rusk County is a land of lakes, which extends into Chippewa County.

The once vast forests of Rusk County attracted the interest of businessmen in a newly formed and rapidly developing United States. These enterprising speculators considered Wisconsin timberlands a good investment based on the profitable lumber industry of western New York. And, the westward push of white settlers moving to the treeless prairies of Indiana and Illinois created a demand for lumber for building homes, barns, and fences.

From 1860 to 1910, these forests provided the raw material for a thriving but short-sighted lumber industry. After a few decades, lumber companies sold the cutover land to European immigrants and American pioneers. These new landowners attempted but failed to turn their property into farmland. Unable to pay their taxes, they abandoned it.

The State’s newly created Forest Crop Law program allowed counties to acquire tax-delinquent land and establish county forests – many acres, hand-planted – for future timber-sales revenue. By 1936, the Rusk County Forest consisted of 76,225 acres. Today, the county offers more than 88,000 acres of public lands full of logging trails and paths, including the Ice Age Trail, which enjoys passage across it.

. . .

Primitive camping is permitted at least 200 feet from trails and waterways.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Blue Hills Chapter was formed in 1992 after a reorganization of the Rusk County Chapter (begun in 1975). Adam Cahow (1929–2022), a University of

Wisconsin-Eau Claire professor, played an instrumental role in planning and constructing the Ice Age Trail through the county.

Today, the Chapter’s focus includes the layout and design of a new Ice Age Trail route through Rusk County, the Dreamer Route. It will feature numerous geologic wonders and improve the hiker experience in Rusk County in the coming years.

Learn more about the Chapter by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/blue-hills-chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Rusk County Tourism (and Visitor Center)**: 715-532-2642, ruskcountywi.com
**Rusk County Forestry and Parks Office**: 715-532-2113, ruskcounty.org

NANCY LEHMAN

![A photograph of a circular spiderweb catching sunlight in a forest, with tree trunks in the background and foreground.](page_79_image_1_v2.jpg)

Southern Blue Hills Segment: Sunlight highlights a carefully woven spiderweb.

## Northern Blue Hills Segment (Atlas Map 11f)

**Summary:** The Northern Blue Hills Segment is a 9.6-mile trail in Rusk County (Atlas Map 11f), running from S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F to CTH-F Southern Trail Access. This segment travels through Rusk County Forest land, highlighting the remote and rugged Blue Hills.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **9.6 miles**: S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F to CTH-F Southern Trail Access

*   [Icon: water and picnic icons] At the nearby Murphy Flowage Picnic Area and Murphy Flowage Campground.
*   [Icon: camping and recreation icons] At Murphy Flowage Campground [Icon: campground icon], at Audie Flowage-Perch Lake Recreation Area (see Area Services).

*   [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

*   [Icon: water source icon] From Devils Creek and other small streams/creeks and lakes/wetland areas.
*   [Icon: road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads – some frequently used by ATVs – and gravel S. Bucks Lake Rd.

*   [Icon: primitive camping icon] Primitive camping [Icon: permit icon] on county forest lands. Permit required.
*   [Icon: spur trail icon] A 0.3-mi blue-blazed spur trail to Stout Rd. parking area.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

**[Icon: parking 1 icon] ① S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F Trail Access (45.56648, -91.50185):** From Birchwood at the intersection of Main St. and STH-48, take STH-48 east 2.3 mi. At CTH-F turn right and go south 8.7 mi. **Parking:** Roadside on S. Bucks Lake Rd.

**[Icon: parking 2 icon] ② CTH-F Southern Trail Access (45.50087, -91.44574):** From Weyerhaeuser at the intersection of USH-8 and CTH-F (2nd St.), take CTH-F north then west 6.6 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Murphy Flowage Picnic Area on CTH-F. Follow access road around to the picnic area. (ii) Bucks Lake Rd. roadside parking. (iii) Stout Rd. A 0.3-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

This segment travels through Rusk County Forest land, highlighting the remote and rugged Blue Hills. Scenic streams and beautiful wetland areas are home to extensive beaver activity. The Trail skirts several large beaver lodges and crosses an active beaver dam.

The terrain requires you to pay close attention to Trail signage at intersecting logging/forest roads. In addition, after heavy rain, portions of the Trail may become muddy and rutted from ATV use, larger ponds may flood the Trail, and bridges can be slippery.

From the Trail access at the intersection of South Bucks Lake Road and CTH-F, briefly head east on South Bucks Lake Road before leaving the road and heading north into the woods. The Trail’s rocky tread travels over rolling terrain toward another intersection with South Bucks Lake Road. You’ll catch glimpses of Murphy Flowage through the trees as you cross a boardwalk.

At South Bucks Lake Road, turn left and briefly follow it before heading off-road to the south. Traverse low-relief hummocky terrain on logging/forest roads while passing through a mixed hardwood forest with several small ponds along the route. Cross over a beaver dam using caution; the tread is uneven and slippery.

After meeting the wide, grassy blue-blazed spur trail to the Stout Road parking area, continue southward through hardwood forest and rolling hummocky glacial topography. After reaching the outlet of a large wetland, follow a branch of Devils Creek through a scenic and peaceful shallow valley and cross a

![Map of the Hemlock Creek Segment showing Murphy Flowage Recreation Area, trail path, and connecting roads.](page_81_image_1_v2.jpg)

1.2-mi CR to Southern Blue Hills Segment. East and south on CTH-F. ↓

series of bridges. Next, climb a hill overlooking Devils Creek and descend the other side down to the creek. Cross the bridge spanning the water, and then ascend the bank on the opposite side. Follow the ridge through birch and pine forest before reaching the segment’s terminus at the CTH-F southern Trail access.

### AREA SERVICES

**Birchwood:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping. From S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F Trail access go north ~10.5 mi.

**Murphy Flowage Recreation Area:** Camping. On Trail. Picnic area is on the west side of CTH-F on the Trail route. The campground is on the east side of CTH-F, 1.0 mi north of S. Bucks Lake Rd. Rusk County Forestry Department (715-532-2113, parks.ruskcounty.org/parks). From S. Bucks Lake Rd. at CTH-F Trail access go north 0.7 mi.

**Audie Flowage-Perch Lake Recreation Area:** Camping. From S. Bucks Lake Rd. and CTH-F go east 4.8 mi on S. Bucks Lake Rd. For more information contact Rusk County Forestry Department (715-532-2113, parks.ruskcounty.org/parks).

**Weyerhaeuser:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging. From CTH-F southern Trail access go east and south 6.6 mi.
