## Southern Blue Hills Segment (Atlas Map 12f)

**Summary:** The Southern Blue Hills Segment is a 7.3-mile trail in Rusk County (Atlas Map 12f), running from Yuker Rd. at CTH-F to Old 14 Rd. (Bass Lake Rd). This remote and rugged segment highlights the Blue Hills as it traverses ridges, kettles, and ravines.

### SNAPSHOT

* [Icon: distance icon] [Icon: distance icon] **7.3 miles**: Yuker Rd. at CTH-F to Old 14 Rd. (Bass Lake Rd)
* [Icon: water icon] From North Lake, Moose Ear Creek and other small intermittent streams/ creeks and wetlands.
* [Icon: primitive camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest land and at a walk-in campsite on the south shore of North Lake. Permit required.
* [Icon: campground icon] At Murphy Flowage Campground [Icon: tent icon], Audie Flowage-Perch Lake Recreation Area (see Area Services).
* [Icon: hunting icon] The portion of the segment south of the Rusk County Forest boundary to Old 14 Rd. (Bass Lake Rd.) is closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: logging road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads and dirt access road.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

* [Icon: 1 icon] **Yuker Rd. at CTH-F Trail Access (45.48810, -91.43915):** From Weyerhaeuser at the intersection of USH-8 and CTH-F (2nd St.), take CTH-F north 5.4 mi to Yuker Rd., an unsigned primitive road heading west. **Parking**: Roadside on Yuker Rd.
* ![2 icon](page_82_image_8_v2.jpg) **Old 14 Rd. (Bass Lake Rd.) Trail Access (45.42196, -91.47601):** From Weyerhaeuser at the intersection of USH-8 and CTH-F (2nd St.), take CTH-F north 0.1 mi. At Railway Ave., which becomes Old 14 Rd. (Bass Lake Rd.), turn left and go west 1.0 mi. Angle left across the railroad tracks and continue west 1.9 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

### THE HIKE

This remote and rugged segment highlights the Blue Hills as it traverses ridges, kettles, and ravines. It enjoys passage across Rusk County Forest land. About two-thirds of the way through the segment, the south shore of North Lake is a perfect spot to watch birds, including loons.

During the spring and summer, dense vegetation hugs the Trail; pay close attention to Trail signage. In addition, low-lying areas can be very wet and muddy, especially during rainy periods.

From the Trail access at the intersection of CTH-F and Yuker Road (an unsigned primitive road used by fishermen and hunters accessing Rusk County Forest land), briefly head west on Yuker Road. Then, depart Yuker Road and head south on a logging/forest road. Soon you’ll split off to the right on a narrow tread. Within the first few miles, you’ll cross scenic Moose Ear Creek twice, first on a primitive wooden bridge and then on a log bridge.

As the route makes its way to North Lake, it follows or parallels forested ridges. Enjoy views of trailside kettles and down glacier-formed ravines on a mix of two-track foot-paths and logging roads. Enter a clearing and meet and briefly follow a dirt access road to the east before curving to the west and south of North Lake, a local fishing hole. At the lake’s south shore is a walk-in campsite with scenic views.

South of North Lake, the Trail soon comes to a gate and fence stile and enters private land. Cross the outlet/small stream created by a small, unnamed

lake. Weave in and out of the woods, skirting dairy pastures and crossing the small stream twice more on a combination of forest and farm roads and narrow tread. Be on the lookout for a unique wagon wheel/skull sculpture situated in a pasture at the landowner’s request. The sculpture was designed and installed by a small MSC crew led by a retired art professor.

Eventually, you’ll climb a ridge overlooking a lake and cross another fence stile. Reach a gravel farm road providing excellent views through an opening in the trees. Trek through another forested, hummocky area, cross a third fence stile, and cross a set of railroad tracks. Skirt another wetland/lake and a pine, oak, and birch forest with an undergrowth of ferns. Finally, arrive at the segment’s terminus on Old 14 Road (Bass Lake Road).

### AREA SERVICES

**Audie Flowage-Perch Lake Recreation Area:** Camping. From Yuker Rd. at CTH-F go north and east ~9 mi.

**Murphy Flowage Recreation Area:** Camping. On Trail. Picnic area is on the west side of CTH-F on the Trail route. The campground is on the east side of CTH-F, 1.0 mi north of S. Bucks Lake Rd. Rusk County Forestry Department (715-532-2113, parks.ruskcounty.org/parks). From Yuker Rd. at CTH-F go north ~10.5 mi.

**Birchwood:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping. From Yuker Rd. at CTH-F Trail access go north ~20 mi.

**Weyerhaeuser:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging. From Old 14 Rd. (Bass Lake Rd.) Trail access go east 3.0 mi.

![Map of the trail segment in Rusk County Forest. The trail starts at Yuker Rd. (point 1) and goes south to Old 14 Rd. (Bass Lake Rd.) (point 2). It passes through Rusk County Forest, crossing Moose Ear Creek and passing by North Lake and Pickerel Lakes. A connector route (CR) leads north to the Northern Blue Hills Segment, and another CR leads east to the Chippewa Moraine Segment. Distances in miles are marked along the trail.](page_83_image_1_v2.jpg)

![Map of the Chippewa Moraine Segment of the Ice Age Trail. The map shows the trail route in red passing through the Chippewa State Recreation Area. Nearby towns include New Auburn to the west, Bloomer to the south, and Chippewa Falls further south. Major highways shown are US-53 and WI-64, along with several county roads. A legend in the bottom left corner identifies the Ice Age Trail, connecting routes, other trails, county boundaries, and public or IATA land. A scale bar indicates a distance of 0 to 5 miles.](page_84_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Chippewa County

![A detailed map of the Ice Age Trail within Chippewa County, Wisconsin. The map highlights several trail segments: Harwood Lakes Segment, Firth Lake Segment, Chippewa River Segment, and Cornell Segment. It shows the trail's path relative to local landmarks such as Brunet Island State Park, Otter Lake County Park, and the towns of Holcombe, Cornell, Cadott, and Stanley. Major highways (27, 29, 64) and county roads are marked. An inset map of Wisconsin indicates the county's location in the state. The map also features a compass rose and the Ice Age Trail Alliance logo.](page_85_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Chippewa County

Atlas Maps 14f–19f; Databook pages 14–17

Total miles: 58.7 (Trail 23.3; Connecting Route 35.4)

![Map of Chippewa County showing the Ice Age Trail route](page_86_image_2_v2.jpg)

The Chippewa Lobe formed Chippewa County’s prominent glacial features, including the Chippewa Moraine. The terminal moraine stands above cultivated plains, with lakes and ponds set in a forested jumble of hills. Unlike the higher elevations of the interlobate Kettle Moraine in southeastern Wisconsin, gentler hills of “dead ice” moraine characterize the Chippewa Moraine.

As the ice ceased to move, fissures crisscrossed the glacier. Surface debris, transported by sliding ice and flowing meltwater, filled many cracks and formed sharp ridges after the ice melted. When these debris-covered blocks of ice melted, they created kettles and produced the area’s knob-and-swale or hummocky landscape. Ice-walled lake plains, flatter-topped areas formed by glacial lakebeds, are abundant in this area.

Because of the areas’ glacial significance, the Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area is a National Scientific Reserve Unit. Perched high atop an ice-walled lake plain within it is the David R. Obey Ice Age Interpretive Center with displays and exhibits describing the Wisconsin Glaciation. In addition to the Center, the area includes miles of hiking trails with interpretive signs. The Reserve and nearby Brunet Island State Park are waypoints on the Great Wisconsin Birding and Nature Trail.

The Ice Age Trail also passes through portions of the 34,500-acre Chippewa County Forest, once part of Wisconsin’s lumbering region that spanned the watersheds of the Black and Chippewa Rivers. The amount of pine harvested from the Black River Valley alone could have built a boardwalk nine feet wide and four inches thick around the world.

The Ice Age Trail also travels through Cornell, an Ice Age Trail Community. It is home to a truss company, a cabinetry company, and mill, which produces roofing felt. Adjacent to Brunet Island State Park, Cornell handily caters to the vacation state of mind. It welcomes hikers with ice cream and coffee shops, a brewery and a winery, and grocery and laundry services.

Trailside camping options abound in Chippewa County. The County Forest permits primitive camping at least 200 feet from waterways and trails. There are also a number of primitive campsites and Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance, multiday hikers, along the Ice Age Trail. Currently, these sites can be found along the Chippewa Moraine, Harwood Lakes, and Firth Lake segments.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Chippewa Moraine Chapter, formed in 1988, sponsors hikes, Trail improvement events, and presentations on glacial geology. The Chapter maintains a close relationship with the David R. Obey Ice Age Interpretive Center. Many Chapter events take place there, like the annual Parade of Colors hike in autumn.

The Chapter’s hiking award program, Chip Off the Old Moraine, incentivizes hikers by offering a patch and certificate to those who hike all Ice Age Trail miles in the county. Start your Chip Off the Old Moraine adventure by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/chippewa-moraine-chapter to learn more about the Chapter. You may also visit iatchippewa.org, which offers detailed parking information and Trail Dispatch newsletters. The Chapter also broadcasts Trail conditions and events via email and Twitter (twitter.com/iatachippewa).

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Chippewa County Tourism**: 715-723-0331, chippewacounty.com

**Chippewa County Facilities and Parks Division**: 715-726-7882, co.chippewa.wi.us/government/facilities-parks-division

**Chippewa County Land Conservation and Forest Management Division**: co.chippewa.wi.us/government/land-conservation-forest-management, 715-726-7920

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Cornell**: 715-239-3710, cityofcornell.com

![Landscape view of the Chippewa Moraine Segment with yellow flowers in the foreground and forested hills in the distance.](page_87_image_1_v2.jpg)

DAVID PEREZ

*Chippewa Moraine Segment: Distant hills shelter a sea of late-summer flowers.*

Chippewa County | 55

## Chippewa Moraine Segment (Atlas Map 15f)

**Summary:** The Chippewa Moraine Segment is a 7.9-mile trail in Chippewa County (Atlas Map 15f), running from 267th Ave. (Oak Ln.) to 167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.). This scenic segment begins by traveling through the North of North Shattuck Lake State Natural Area, a southern dry-mesic forest popular with many birds during the spring migration.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiker icon] [Icon: car icon] **7.9 miles**: 267th Ave. (Oak Ln.) to 167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.)

*Note: A re-route is planned immediately west of 160th St. (Town Line Rd.). Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

[Icon: water tap icon] At the Obey Interpretive Center.

[Icon: water drop icon] From numerous lakes.

[Icon: camping icons] [Icon: DCA icon] Primitive camping on portions of the Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area. A Dispersed Camping Area and two walk-in campsites near the Obey Interpretive Center.

[Icon: car and tent icon] A small private campground and a county park campground are located north of the western endpoint.

[Icon: restroom icon] Restrooms are available at the Obey Interpretive Center and at the two walk-in campsites (privy) just off the Trail. Please respect those who have reserved the sites.

[Icon: globe icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: road icon] Small portion overlaps 260th Ave. (Rattlesnake Hill Rd.).

[Icon: trail network icon] Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area trail network. Spur trails to walk-in campsites.

![Map of the Chippewa Moraine Segment within the Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area, showing the trail route passing by numerous lakes including Long Lake, Herde Lake, and South Shattuck Lake, and featuring the Obey Interpretive Center.](page_88_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **267th Ave. (Oak Ln.) Trail Access (45.23110, -91.43703):** From USH-53 take Exit 118 and follow CTH-M east for 7.0 mi. At STH-40 turn left and go north 0.7 mi. At 267th Ave. (Oak Ln.) turn right and go east 0.7 mi. **Parking:** Roadside, 300 ft west of Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.) Trail Access (45.21918, -91.34169):** From USH-53 take Exit 118 and follow CTH-M east for 12.6 mi. At 167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.) turn right and go south 0.9 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the parking area 0.2 mi south on Plummer Lake Rd.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Circle Hiking Trail parking area on CTH-M near western boundary of Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area. (No parking at CTH-M Trail access farther east.) (ii) David R. Obey Ice Age Interpretive Center off CTH-M. (iii) 260th Ave. (Rattlesnake Hill Rd.), 0.2 mi west of where the IAT heads off on the north side of the road. Parking area in the corner of a field. Overnight parking permitted.

### THE HIKE

This scenic segment begins by traveling through the North of North Shattuck Lake State Natural Area, a southern dry-mesic forest popular with many birds during the spring migration. Then, the Ice Age Trail winds through the Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area. This property offers unspoiled beauty: dramatic high-relief hummocky terrain and more than 20 kettle lakes. As the Trail approaches the segment’s east end, it crosses a large ice-walled lake plain and a carved channel just north of Plummer Lake.

During the Wisconsin Glaciation period, small lakes filled depressions in the ice surface. They became walled off by enormous ice blocks. Lakes often formed on debris-covered parts of the glacier; over time, fine sediment accumulated on the lakes’ floors. When the ice surrounding a lake melted, the accumulated sediment remained, forming a high, flat-topped “lake plain.” This segment passes several of these formations; often dish-shaped, these plateau-like areas have rich soil suitable for farming. The coarse material deposited near the ice block wall and the glacial lake’s shoreline created an elevated rim-like ridge around the lake plain. After a glacial lake drained, parts of the ice wall continued to melt. Water flowed across the now-dry lakebed, carving a channel as the ice wall continued to melt long after the original glacial lake was dry.

From the Trail access on 267th Avenue (Oak Lane), head south, and you’ll quickly meet a junction with a spur trail. It leads 825 feet to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. (This

![Map of the Chippewa Moraine Segment showing trail path, lakes, and parking areas.](page_89_image_1_v2.jpg)

campsite, with a fire ring, is located on Landmark Conservancy property near several small lakes.) As the Trail continues east, it enters the Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area, which hosts most of this segment. Soon, you’ll reach the northern tip of Horseshoe Lake – the first of two lakes along the segment bearing the same name.

On the way toward the southeastern corner of the lake, the Trail climbs a hill; during leaf-off, you can see five bays from the top. After descending the hill, you’ll meet the western junction of the Circle Trail. This junction is the first of several with other trails; signage is excellent throughout the State

Recreation Area’s trail network, making it easy to navigate. To stay on the Ice Age Trail, turn left here and continue east.

After you hike between North Shattuck Lake and the aptly named North of North Shattuck Lake, you’ll meet a spur trail on the right. It leads 750 feet to a walk-in campsite, one of two walk-in campsites, each with a privy and fire ring. (There is a small fee to stay at either site; check availability and pay at the David R. Obey Interpretive Center.) A short distance south of the campsite spur, you’ll meet the Dry Lake Trail and Mammoth Nature Trail; both branch off to the east. From this junction, continue southeast toward the Obey Interpretive Center.

From the Obey Interpretive Center, head north and wrap around the northern tip of a small lake, then continue southeast toward CTH-M. You’ll meet a 300-foot spur trail leading north to another walk-in campsite.

Cross CTH-M and continue south. After reaching the eastern junction with the Circle Trail, head east, passing another scenic cluster of lakes before crossing 144th Street (Ice Age Drive). As the segment makes its way in an easterly direction, it snakes past several lakes including Knickerbocker Lake, along the south shore; Dam Lake, around the southern tip; Little Beaver Lake and Pickerel Lake, passing south of the lakes; and the second Horseshoe Lake, skirting the north shore. Continuing, the Trail quickly crosses gravel 160th Street (Town Line Road).

Continue east across the ice-walled lake plain and carved channel described above. Intersect and briefly follow 260th Avenue (Rattlesnake Hill Road) and pass Plummer Lake before reaching the segment’s terminus at 167th Street (Plummer Lake Road).

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**David R. Obey Ice Age Interpretive Center:** On Trail. (13394 CTH-M, New Auburn, 715-967-2800, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/chipmoraine/naturecenter).

The Obey Interpretive Center has many hands-on and interactive activities, including activity books, short films and various displays about geologic, cultural and natural history. The Center has maps, restrooms, drinking water and helpful staff. Open hours vary; call or check website for details. Three loop trails start here and share parts of the Ice Age Trail: Circle Trail (4.5 mi), Dry Lake Trail (1.8 mi) and Mammoth Nature Trail (0.7 mi). These trails feature numerous interpretive signs described in *Hiking Field Trip Guide for Glacial Landforms*, available at the Center.

### AREA SERVICES

**Bloomer:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, library, medical services. From the 267th Ave. (Oak Ln.) Trail access, go west on 267th Ave. (Oak Ln.) 0.7 mi. At STH-40, turn left and go south 11.3 mi.

**David R. Obey Ice Age Interpretive Center:** On Trail. (13394 CTH-M, New Auburn, 715-967-2800, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/chipmoraine/naturecenter). Camping at the two walk-in campsites. Campers need to register at the Obey Interpretive Center before heading out.

**STH-40:** Restaurant. Just south of Morris-Erickson County Park on Long Lake and at 270th Ave.

**New Auburn:** Convenience store, camping. Convenience store at the intersection of STH-40 and CTH-M, 2.0 mi west of the Obey Interpretive Center. Camping at Morris Erickson County Park (715-726-7882, reservations.co.chippewa.wi.us/parks/camping/). Village of New Auburn, 6 mi west on CTH-M, has a convenience store.

[Icon: Trail City logo] **Cornell:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, post office, medical service. On Trail. Camping at Brunet Island State Park (reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Area info available at the City of Cornell (715-239-3710, cityofcornell.com). From the Obey Interpretive Center on CTH-M east ~19 mi.

![A group of people of various ages snowshoeing through a snowy, wooded landscape on the Chippewa Moraine Segment of the Ice Age Trail.](page_91_image_1_v2.jpg)
SHERRY JASPER

Chippewa Moraine Segment: A snowshoe hike gets all ages out to play.

## Harwood Lakes Segment (Atlas Maps 15f, 16f)

**Summary:** The Harwood Lakes Segment is a 6.2-mile trail in Chippewa County (Atlas Map 15f, 16f), running from 167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.) to CTH-E. Most of the segment traverses public land, the 34,500-acre Chippewa County Forest, located within the Chippewa Lobe’s terminal moraine.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage marker] 4 **6.2 miles**: 167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.) to CTH-E

[Icon: water drop icon] From numerous lakes, streams/creeks and wetland areas.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: camping icon] A walk-in campsite between the two Harwood Lakes, a Dispersed Camping Area near the west shore of Picnic Lake and primitive camping on county forest lands.

[Icon: dashed line icon] Portions overlap Plummer Lake Rd., the Nawakwa equestrian trail, and logging and forest roads.

[Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: crossed lines icon] Spur trail to walk-in campsite at Harwood Lakes, blue-blazed loop trail to the DCA and Girl Scout Camp Nawakwa trail network.

[Icon: hunting icon] A portion of the segment crossing private land between the eastern boundary of the Chippewa County Forest and CTH-E is closed during gun deer season.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking 1 icon] **167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.) Trail Access (45.21918, -91.34169)**: From USH-53 take Exit 118 and follow CTH-M east for 12.6 mi. At 167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.) turn right and go south 0.9 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at the parking area 0.2 mi south on Plummer Lake Rd.

[Icon: parking 2 icon] **CTH-E Trail Access (45.20902, -91.27325)**: From Cornell take STH-64 west across the Chippewa River. At CTH-CC turn right and go north 1.0 mi. At CTH-Z turn left and go west 5.0 mi. At CTH-E turn right and go north 3.0 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

**Additional Parking**: Deer Fly Trail. From CTH-M and Deer Fly Trail (gravel road), go south 2.6 mi on Deer Fly Trail to the parking area on west side of road.

### THE HIKE

Most of the segment traverses public land, the 34,500-acre Chippewa County Forest, located within the Chippewa Lobe’s terminal moraine. The rolling topography, dotted with pristine lakes and wetlands as well as beaver dams and a massive erratic, hosts oak, aspen, and hardwood trees. The county established the forest in 1939, enrolling tax-delinquent land into a County Forest reserve. Early pioneers had abandoned their homesteads, finding the depleted soils of the previously clear-cut land unsuitable for agriculture. Today, forest management includes selective timber harvest, recreational opportunities, wildlife habitat, and watershed protection.

From the Trail access on 167th Street (Plummer Lake Road) just south of the road’s intersection with 260th Avenue (Rattlesnake Hill Road), head south along the road for 0.2 miles across a narrow causeway between Plummer Lake and a wetland. Depart from the road and head east through the easternmost and relatively flat portion of the Chippewa Moraine State Recreation Area property. The remainder of the segment features dramatic, high-relief hummocky topography. Enter a second-growth forest on a wide path with a high canopy and diverse understory of ferns and woodland plants. *Note:*

![Map of the Chippewa Moraine Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route winding through the Chippewa County Forest past numerous lakes including Harwood Lakes, Mud Lake, Dorothy Lake, Bass Lake, and Picnic Lake, with mileage markers and points of interest like Baldy Mtn.](page_93_image_1_v2.jpg)

*Camping is not permitted between 167th Street (Plummer Lake Road) and the campsite between the two Harwood Lakes.*

Leave the State Recreation Area, enter Chippewa County Forest lands, and soon arrive at a bench with lovely views of the Harwood Lakes. About 200 feet east of it, a spur trail heads steeply downhill to a small, but picturesque, walk-in campsite (no privy) on the narrows between the two lakes.

In rolling topography, cross Mud Brook on a serpentine boardwalk/bridge with a bench overlooking Mud Brook Valley. Briefly follow the edge of the wetland, climb steeply to the top of a well-defined, hummocky ridge, and descend to follow the edge of a swamp. Along the north side of the Trail, you may detect ruts from when loggers cut trenches in the ground and filled them with water to create iced tracks for their logging sleds.

Climb out of the wet area and cross a distinctive county ATV trail. Then cross a bridge over a small stream, follow the edge of another wetland area, and soon arrive at Deer Fly Trail (a gravel road).

East of Deer Fly Trail, you’ll pass a massive glacial erratic just 40 feet off the Trail, then cross a footbridge at the base of a beaver dam that creates a flowage to the south. Next, continue to a bridge (called “Kim’s Crossing,” in memory of Kim Heidtke, who loved the Trail) spanning a small gulch.

Soon you’ll meet and briefly overlap an equestrian trail as you meander through the woods. Enjoy a rest on a Leopold bench with a panoramic view of a tamarack bog before dropping to a small, intermittent stream with a rock crossing. Immediately climb again and enjoy views of a small isolated pond. Shortly after, you’ll meet the western end of a marked loop trail. It leads to a Dispersed

Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers and has a fire pit and log benches.

Continuing on the Trail, you’ll pass the eastern end of the DCA’s loop trail and cross a bridge (on private property) spanning an inlet to Picnic Lake.

Enjoy many views of this pristine lake as you wrap around its eastern edge. Portions of the Trail along Picnic Lake can flood, requiring brief detours. Before reaching its terminus on CTH-E, the Trail skirts above a scenic hemlock-rimmed pond to the north.

### AREA SERVICES

**New Auburn**: See *Chippewa Moraine Segment, p. 56*. From the 167th St. (Plummer Lake Rd.) Trail access go north and west ~13 mi.

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Cornell**: *Cornell Segment, p. 66*. From the CTH-E Trail access go south and east 9.0 mi.

KRIS VAN HANDEL

![A hiker with a red backpack walks along a long, winding wooden boardwalk through a marshy area with tall yellow grass and bare trees in the background.](page_94_image_1_v2.jpg)

*Harwood Lakes Segment: A volunteer-built boardwalk keeps a hiker’s boots dry.*

## Firth Lake Segment and Chippewa River Segment (Atlas Map 16f)

**Summary:** The Firth Lake Segment and Chippewa River Segment covers approximately 10.3 miles in Chippewa County (Atlas Map 16f). It comprises the Firth Lake Segment (8.4 mi, 245th Ave. (Moonridge Trail) to CTH-CC.), the Chippewa River Segment (1.9 mi, CTH-CC to CTH-Z).

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Firth Lake Segment icon] **Firth Lake Segment—8.4 miles (5.9 IAT, 2.4 CR):** 245th Ave. (Moonridge Trail) to CTH-CC.

*Note: A Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) is planned north of 245th Ave. (Moonridge Trail) approximately 0.5-mi from the western end of the segment. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

* [Icon: Water icon] From Firth Lake and a few intermittent streams/creeks.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

* [Icon: Camping icon] Primitive camping [Icon: Primitive camping icon] on county forest lands and at two Dispersed Camping Areas [Icon: DCA icon].
* [Icon: Logging road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads.

* [Icon: ColdCache icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.
* [Icon: Spur trail icon] Three short blue-blazed spur trails; one to a parking area next to Firth Lake and two to DCAs.

[Icon: Chippewa River Segment icon] **Chippewa River Segment—1.9 miles (1.8 IAT, 0.1 CR):** CTH-CC to CTH-Z

* [Icon: Water icon] From the Chippewa River and Perch Lake.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

* [Icon: Camping icon] At nearby Brunet Island State Park [Icon: State Park icon] (see Area Services).
* [Icon: Road overlap icon] Small portion overlaps CTH-CC.

* [Icon: Amenities icons] At nearby Millyard Park, Brunet Island State Park and the Cornell Visitor Center. Picnic area and playground also available at Brunet Falls Park.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

**[Icon: 1] 245th Ave. (Moonridge Trail) Trail Access (45.20084, -91.25976):** From Cornell take STH-64 west across the Chippewa River. At CTH-CC turn right and go northwest 0.9 mi. At CTH-Z turn left and go southwest then west 5.0 mi. At CTH-E turn right and go north 2.0 mi. At 245th Ave. turn right and go 0.9 mi. **Parking:** Roadside. *Use caution as this is a narrow, curvy road.*

**[Icon: 2] CTH-CC Trail Access (45.18804, -91.17318): Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**[Icon: 3] CTH-Z Trail Access (45.17103, -91.17790):** From Cornell take STH-64 west across the Chippewa River. At CTH-CC turn right and go northwest 0.9 mi. At CTH-Z turn left and go west 0.1 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

**Additional Parking:** 250th Ave. parking area and boat launch. *Rough gravel drive, best accessed from the east (CTH-CC). A 0.2-mi access trail past the gate connects to the IAT.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::chippewa-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::chippewa-count.parent_text:
## Firth Lake Segment

**Summary:** The Firth Lake Segment: This scenic segment features Firth Lake, a shallow kettle lake situated in an outwash plain.

This scenic segment features Firth Lake, a shallow kettle lake situated in an outwash plain. Located within the Firth Lake State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA), the 52-acre lake, surrounded by a northern sedge meadow, is home to water lilies, cattails, swamp milkweed, beaver lodges, and loons.

The segment traverses the 34,500-acre Chippewa County Forest, a second-growth northern mesic forest of birch, red maple, ash, and balsam. Its understory contains woodland plants: ferns, clintonia, blue bead lily, bloodroot, Indian pipe, and red baneberry. Bridges, common throughout, were built by volunteers. It can be very wet in spring with snow melt and beaver activity in the many lakes and ponds. Rolling, high-relief hummocky topography characterizes the segment from its starting point to Firth Lake.

![Topographic map of the trail segment showing the route through Chippewa County Forest, including 245th Avenue (Moonridge Trail), 250th Avenue, and Spring Creek Flowage No. 1. A text box indicates a 1.8-mile connecting route to the Harwood Lakes Segment.](page_96_image_1_v2.jpg)

From the Trail access on 245th Avenue (Moonridge Trail), head north, then east to a bridge crossing a beaver dam. Enjoy the view from a nearby bench; look for trailing arbutus (an increasingly rare plant) on the north slope of the moraine. Cross another bridge and two boardwalks/bridges before again reaching 245th Avenue (Moonridge Trail) and crossing it.

Meet and follow logging roads for 0.4 miles before turning left onto single-track tread. Pass a scenic pond with a bench, and soon climb a rise overlooking a beaver dam and flowage.

Meander through hardwood forest and areas of aspen, briefly overlapping old forest roads. Then rock-hop across a small stream, climb out of the ravine, and travel beside the babbling water.

Continue east toward Firth Lake, crossing an ATV trail (follow it 50 feet to the right). Pass through a grooved-top ridge, a glacial feature characterized by two parallel ridges separated by a trench-like linear depression. Before you reach Firth Lake, a blue-blazed spur trail heads north to the 250th Avenue parking area and boat launch. Turn sharply south from the spur trail junction and climb a hill with a bench overlooking Firth Lake.

As the Trail leaves high-relief hummocky topography behind, you’ll skirt the southwest side of Firth Lake. Cautiously cross a 500-foot boardwalk over an old beaver dam. Continuing east into low-relief hummocky topography, cross another bridge before meeting the first of two short blue-blazed spur trails leading to Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance multiday hikers. You’ll intersect the second in short order.

Cross another bridge before re-entering Chippewa County Forest land. The Trail briefly (about 50 feet) overlaps a forest road before making a sharp 90-degree left turn. Head north as the Trail transitions from a single-track trail to a forest road on its way to 250th Avenue. Continue east on 250th Avenue, a connecting roadwalk, for 1.2 miles. At CTH-CC, turn right and go south 1.2 miles to the large CTH-CC Ice Age Trail parking area on the east side of the road.

**Chippewa River Segment**

This segment offers a view to the east of the confluence of the Fisher and Chippewa Rivers. It travels through a forest featuring stunning Chippewa River views from the edges of rolling bluffs, with braided, steep topography and vertical gains up to 70 feet. Several deep ravines transect the segment from west to east, and water drains seasonally to the river.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment in Chippewa County, showing Firth Lake, Krank Nature Preserve, Perch Lake, and the town of Cornell.](page_97_image_1_v2.jpg)

The forest cover includes various species of ash, oak, and maple, with an undergrowth of seasonal woodland flowers.

From the Trail access at the CTH-CC Ice Age Trail parking area, head south. After passing through a thin strip of land between the road and river, emerge onto CTH-CC and follow it south for 350 feet.

Head west off-road into Chippewa County Land Conservancy’s 598-acre Krank Nature Preserve. This property features upland mesic forests, tamarack bogs, alder thickets, and sedge meadows.

Climb a hill, turn south, and make your way to a deep ravine where you’ll cross a picturesque stream. Continue south through the woods, crossing a power line right-of-way before you reach Perch Lake. Bend south and east around the western shore of Perch Lake, then head southeast away from the lake to the segment’s endpoint on CTH-Z.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Cornell service icon] Cornell: See Cornell Segment, p. 66. From the CTH-Z Trail access, go northeast 0.1 mi. At CTH-CC turn right and go southeast 0.9 mi. At STH-64 turn left and go east 0.6 mi.

## Cornell Segment (Atlas Map 17f)

**Summary:** The Cornell Segment is a 1.5-mile trail in Chippewa County (Atlas Map 17f), running from Bridge St. (STH-64) to S. 8th St..

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **1.5 miles**: Bridge St. (STH-64) to S. 8th St.

![amenities icons](page_98_image_5_v2.jpg) At Mill Yard Park and at Jean Brunet City Park.

[Icon: leashed dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: no hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.

[Icon: wheelchair accessible icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

[Icon: camping icon] At Brunet Island State Park.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::chippewa-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::chippewa-count.parent_text:
## TRAIL ACCESS & PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] ① **Bridge St. (STH-64) Trail Access (45.16538, -91.15572)**: In Cornell, 0.1 mi east of Chippewa River. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access in Cornell Visitor Center parking area.

[Icon: parking icon 2] ② **S. 8th St. Trail Access (45.15849, -91.14179)**: From Cornell, at the intersection of STH-64 and S. 8th St., take S. 8th St. south 0.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access at Wisconsin Veteran Farm and Winery.

### THE HIKE

Cornell, an Ice Age Trail Community, is strategically located at the confluence of the scenic Chippewa and Fisher Rivers. Children play in tree-shaded parks, and ice cream shops line Main Street of this small midwestern town that shares history and name with New York State’s grand Ivy League University.

Cornell’s history begins July 2, 1862, with President Abraham Lincoln’s signing of the Morrill Land Grant College Act. This legislation promised states between 90,000 and 990,000 acres of federally owned western land (obtained from Indigenous people through treaty, cession, or seizure) depending on the size of their congressional delegations. This land, or the proceeds from its sale, was for establishing colleges dedicated to teaching agriculture and the mechanic arts (engineering).

Enter Ezra Cornell, philanthropist and one of the founders of Western Union Telegraph Company. He managed the land acquisition resulting from the Morrill Act for New York State’s land-grant college (now Cornell University), purchasing 500,000 acres of valuable northern Wisconsin timberland.

During Cornell’s land scouting trips to Wisconsin, he met and befriended Jean Brunet, a French noble who, at age 27, immigrated to America in 1818. Brunet established the first sawmill along the Chippewa River (now the site of Chippewa Falls) and, by 1843, had established an inn and trading post at Brunet Falls (now Cornell). He is considered the founding settler of Chippewa Falls and Cornell.

Eventually, Ezra Cornell willed the land, the falls, and the site of what would become the City of Cornell to Cornell University. Over time, profits from logging, lumber, and the lands’ eventual sale raised nearly $6 million for the university in what became a historic land speculation deal.

The segment travels along the northwestern edge of Mill Yard Park, a former pulpwood storage site. It now hosts restrooms, playground equipment, and a Visitors’ Center. It also features a 175-foot stacker that operated from 1912 until 1971, hoisting logs from the river to the sawmill. This homage to Wisconsin’s timber industry is rooted in Ezra Cornell’s

![Map of the Cornell segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through Cornell, Mill Yard Park, and near Brunet Island State Park.](page_99_image_1_v2.jpg)

efforts to build a mill and town near the falls. He allocated 2,000 acres of forest land for a community – a future sawmill’s labor force – and sketched a plat map. The City of Cornell remains a lumber town. It is home to a truss company, a cabinetry company, and the mill, which produces roofing felt.

From the Trail access at Bridge Street (STH-64), head north. Then, cut across Mill Yard Park, following its northwestern edge to the junction of Park Road and Thomas Street. Head east on Thomas Street for half a block to its junction with the Old Abe Trail. Head south on this paved path for one block before turning east on Main Street. Stop at one or more ice cream shops before arriving at North 5th Street at the southwest corner of Jean Brunet City Park. Head south on South 5th Street for three blocks and then east onto Bridge Street, where stops at the local brewery, diner, and coffee shop are encouraged. Bridge Street also boasts a grocery store and a 24-hour laundromat. At South 8th Street, head south to the Wisconsin Veteran Farm, LLC & Winery’s parking lot, the segment’s endpoint.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Ice Age Trail logo] **Cornell:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, post office, medical service. On Trail. Camping at Brunet Island State Park (reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Area info available at the City of Cornell (715-239-3710, cityofcornell.com).

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Taylor County, Wisconsin, highlighting the Lake Eleven Segment within the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest. The map includes an inset of Wisconsin showing Taylor County's location, labels for Pershing Wildlife Area, Gilman, Lublin, and various highways (73, 64) and county roads (D, E, M, H, F).](page_100_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.parent_text:
# Taylor County

![Map of Taylor County showing Ice Age Trail segments: Jerry Lake Segment, Mondeaux Esker Segment, Pine Line Segment, East Lake Segment, Rib Lake Segment, and Wood Lake Segment. The map also shows Timm's Hill (Wisconsin's Highest Point), Timm's Hill National Trail, Pine Line Trail, and various roads and towns including Medford, Westboro, Ogema, and Rib Lake. A legend indicates Ice Age Trail Community, Ice Age Trail, Connecting Route (unmarked), Other Trail, County Boundary, and Public or IATA Land.](page_101_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Taylor County

**Atlas maps 19f–26f; Databook pages 19–23**

*Total miles: 87.2 (Trail 65.9; Connecting Route 21.3)*

![Map of Taylor County showing the Ice Age Trail route](page_102_image_2_v2.jpg)

The Chippewa Lobe had the most significant influence on the present-day topography of Taylor County. It sculpted all but the county’s eastern edge (shaped by the Wisconsin Valley Lobe, as seen in the Wood Lake moraine) and the southern part of the county (shaped by earlier glaciations). Glaciers left behind a hilly terrain with kettle lakes, erratics, ice-walled lake plains, and eskers. Most of Taylor County lies within the end moraine zone near the recent glacier’s southernmost extent.

The Ice Age Trail travels through the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, highlighting the Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area, with the historic Mondeaux Lodge and Dam. The national forest is more than 850,000 acres and is rich in geologic and human history. Approximately 2,100 known archeological sites, containing more than half of the archeological sites recorded in northern Wisconsin’s 15 counties, are present in the forest. Sites range from 10,000-year-old prehistoric Indigenous people’s campsites to 20<sup>th</sup>-century homesteads.

The Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest enjoyed today grew up out of a stark and tragic landscape. Before logging began, hemlocks dominated the landscape along with white pine, yellow birch, and sugar maple. The lumberjack’s saw felled these old-growth trees. Initially, business interests preferred white pine, although they also prized red or Norway pine.

After depleting the forests, lumber companies sold the cutover land to European immigrants and American pioneers for farms and homesteads. However, the soil, better suited to growing trees, failed to support farming efforts, leading settlers to abandon their land. By 1928, the federal government purchased abandoned and tax-delinquent Northwoods land to establish a national forest. Soon, the Civilian Conservation Corps began planting trees.

Thanks to the repair efforts of many and today’s sustainable forestry practices, we can walk beneath a Northwoods tree canopy again, albeit a young Northwoods. Of Wisconsin’s more than 16 million acres of forest, less than .2% are more than 150 years old. Old-growth hemlocks, which used to be plentiful, can live up to 600 years.

In 1881, J.J. Kennedy, a Canadian-born entrepreneur of Scottish descent, determined the shores of Rib Lake, and its surrounding forests, to be a good location for a sawmill. It eventually became the Rib Lake Lumber Company (RLLC), which during its 60 years of operation processed over 1.4 billion board feet of lumber. The RLLC also had the distinction of being the last Wisconsin

sawmill hauling logs to its own mill on its own railroad when the last such log train load arrived at its mill on May 21, 1948.

In 1896, a local newspaper, the *Rib Lake Herald*, reported that the sawmill ran day and night. "Rib Lake is now one of the most hustling, busy bergs in the state... It seems that nothing can check the inevitable progress of our town. New buildings are everywhere growing up, and property is increasing in value, and settlers are pouring in to till the splendid agricultural land in the vicinity."

Today, Rib Lake, an Ice Age Trail Community, continues to offer vibrant small-town life. It attracts young families and retirees alike with its public library, quality school district, and low cost of living surrounded by natural beauty and numerous outdoor activities.

Primitive camping is allowed on Taylor County Forest lands and in the national forest. Please camp at least 200 feet from trails and waterways. The Rib Lake Segment includes a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA).

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The High Point Chapter, officially organized in 1986, is small but mighty. Its Chapter Co-Coordinators are legendary among those pursuing Thousand-Miler status for their willingness to provide shuttles. Chapter members dedicated to the vision of a continuous Ice Age Trail have sold or donated many acres of land to support the Ice Age Trail's passage through Taylor County.

Learn more about the Chapter by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/high-point-chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Taylor County Tourism Office & Medford Area Chamber of Commerce**: 715-748-4729, taylorcountytourism.com

**Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest Medford-Park Falls District Ranger**: 715-748-4875, fs.usda.gov/cnnf

**Taylor County Forestry & Recreation Department**: 715-748-1486, co.taylor.wi.us

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Rib Lake**: riblakewisconsin.com

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.parent_text:
## Lake Eleven Segment (Atlas Maps 20f, 21f, 22f)

**Summary:** The Lake Eleven Segment is a 15.6-mile trail in Taylor County (Atlas Map 20f, 21f, 22f), running from STH-64 to Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571). This varied segment in the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, with a distinct Northwoods feel, highlights the clear, deep kettle Lake Eleven and many other glacial features associated with the Perkinstown End Moraine.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Distance icon] [Icon: Hiking icon] **15.6 miles**: STH-64 to Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571)

[Icon: Water icon] From South Fork of the Yellow River, Lake Eleven, Beaver Creek and other small intermittent streams/creeks, lakes and wetland areas.

[Icon: ColdCache icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.

[Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Camping icon] Primitive camping on national forest lands.

[Icon: Walk-in campsite icon] Several walk-in campsites are located at Lake Eleven.

[Icon: Shared trail icon] Small portions overlap horseback and snowmobile trails and logging/forest roads.

[Icon: Campground icon] At Chippewa Campground ~9 mi west of CTH-M Trail access (see Area Services).

[Icon: Side trail icon] Short side trails lead to campsites around Lake Eleven.

![Map of Lake Eleven Segment within Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest showing trail path, access points, and local roads.](page_104_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **STH-64 Trail Access (45.11909, -90.70154):**
From Gilman take STH-64 east for 8.2 mi. From Medford at the intersection of STH-64 and STH-13, take STH-64 west 19.2 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

② **Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) Trail Access (45.21792, -90.58793):** From Medford at the intersection of STH-64 and STH-13, take STH-13 north 4.5 mi. At CTH-M turn left and go west 13.0 mi. At Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) turn right and go north 0.7 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

### THE HIKE

This varied segment in the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, with a distinct Northwoods feel, highlights the clear, deep kettle Lake Eleven and many other glacial features associated with the Perkinstown End Moraine.

In 2002, a tornado flattened the forest between STH-64 and FR-558, affecting the first 3.5 miles of the hike. Underbrush, rapidly growing raspberry bushes, and poplars replaced the area’s once towering pines and hardwoods. Due to lack of trees, yellow-blazed Carsonite posts mark the Trail route in this area. While the site is returning to its original state, rough footing makes navigating this section difficult.

The segment also passes through the Richter Lake Hemlocks State Natural Area. Highlighted here is a collapsed ice-walled lake plain with a northern mesic forest consisting mainly of sugar maples, white ash, basswood, and hemlock, along with some yellow birch and red maple. At the base of the lake plain, a small white cedar swamp progresses into a northern wet forest that surrounds a small kettle bog lake, which formed in an ice-block depression.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment showing Lake Eleven, Richter Lake Hemlocks State Natural Area, and surrounding features in the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest.](page_105_image_1_v2.jpg)

*Note: Throughout the segment, navigation is tricky. Signage is sparse, with long distances between blazes, and the Trail crosses old logging roads, ATV trails, and horse trails. Depending on the season, creek crossings can be treacherous with moving water after snow-melt or heavy rain. The Trail corridor also contains evidence of frequently used primitive camping spots.*

From the Trail access on STH-64, head north and east, and after 2.8 miles, skirt around a beaver dam; watch for signage and avoid crossing the actual structure. Before reaching FR-558, you’ll cross a small stream on a bridge built by MSC volunteers.

North of FR-558, continue northeast toward Lake Eleven on dramatic, high-relief, hummocky topography through a mix of conifers and hardwoods, offering a truly remote-feeling experience. This section highlights a classic ice-walled lake plain as it passes the south shore of a small pond roughly half a mile west of the Sawyer Avenue crossing. You’ll see beaver dams in ponds, creeks, bogs, and marshes. Boardwalks are well placed, but you will encounter bogs and wet crossings. This area is full of logging history; logging railroads penetrated the interior lands between the rivers around 1890.

Access a walk-in campsite on the south side of Lake Eleven by a short side trail about 100 feet west of a wooden bridge. The campsite, up the hill in a hemlock grove, is marked by a very large boulder. It has a stone fire ring, a log bench, and many good places for tents. As you pass the south side of Lake Eleven, a snowmobile trail leading west provides access to several walk-in campsites on the north shore of the lake that also offer benches and fire rings. Fishermen, equestrian groups, and hunters also use this area. Water for filtration is easily accessible.

As the segment makes its way to Perkinstown Avenue (FR-116), it passes through the Richter Lake Hemlocks State Natural Area, described above.

North of Perkinstown Avenue (FR-116), continue through rolling, forested terrain to CTH-M and then to a crossing of the South Fork of the Yellow River, which the Trail crosses again farther north, on the Jerry Lake Segment. About a mile after the river crossing, you’ll reach the segment’s endpoint at Sailor Creek Road (FR-571).

### AREA SERVICES

**Gilman:** Restaurant, grocery, convenience store, camping, library. From the STH-64 Trail access go west 8.0 mi.

**Medford:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. At the STH-13 wayside go south 12.6 mi on STH-13. For area info, contact the Medford Area Chamber of Commerce (715-748-4729). From the STH-64 Trail access go east 18.2 mi.

**Perkinstown:** Restaurant, convenience store. From CTH-M Trail access, go west ~3 mi.

**Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest Medford–Park Falls District Chippewa Campground:** Camping. From the CTH-M Trail access go west 7.8 mi on CTH-M. At Chippewa Rd. (FR-1417) go south 0.9 mi to the campground entrance (715-748-4875, fs.usda.gov/cnnf; reservations: 877-444-6777, recreation.gov).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.parent_text:
## Jerry Lake Segment (Atlas Maps 22f, 23f)

**Summary:** The Jerry Lake Segment is a 15.2-mile trail in Taylor County (Atlas Map 22f, 23f), running from Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) Southern Trail Access to CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.). This segment features Jerry Lake, the mile-long Hemlock Esker and crossings of the two forks of the Yellow River.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiking icon] **15.2 miles**: Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) Southern Trail Access to CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.)

* [Icon: water icon] From Jerry Lake, South and North Branches of the Yellow River, Sailor Creek and other small intermittent streams/creeks and lakes.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping [Icon: tent icon] on national forest lands and many walk-in campsites.
* Small portions overlap numbered forest roads and other primitive logging/forest roads.
* [Icon: toilet icon] Pit toilets at the walk-in campgrounds located at the northeast end of Jerry Lake and the southern end of the Chippewa Lobe Interpretive Loop.
* [Icon: trail icon] Partially shares the Chippewa Lobe Interpretive Loop, intersects and crosses the White Birch Trail and intersects parking area and road access spur trails.
* [Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] **1 Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) Southern Trail Access (45.21792, -90.58793)**: From Medford at the intersection of STH-64 and STH-13, take STH-13 north 4.5 mi. At CTH-M turn left and go west 13.0 mi. At Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) turn right and go north 0.7 mi.

**Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: parking icon 2] **2 CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.) Trail Access (45.29965, -90.47438)**: From Medford at the intersection of STH-64 and STH-13, take STH-13 north 4.6 mi. At CTH-M turn left and go west 7.2 mi. At CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.) turn right and go north 6.6 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) 0.3 mi north of Southern Trail access. A short spur trail leads to the IAT. (ii) Lake 19 Rd. (FR-108) roadside parking. (iii) White Birch Trail parking area on Mondeaux Ave. (FR-102). The White Birch Trail (Forest Trail 567) leads 1.1 mi south to the IAT. (iv) Mondeaux Ave (FR-102) just west of the North Fork of the Yellow River.

### THE HIKE

This segment features Jerry Lake, the mile-long Hemlock Esker and crossings of the two forks of the Yellow River. The segment traverses the Chippewa Moraine over crevasse fills and across several glacial ice-walled lake plains.

Besides traversing the Chequamegon National Forest, the segment also highlights the Lost Lake Esker State Natural Area. The Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources established it in 2007, protecting the complex forest and wetland communities associated with the Hemlock Esker and the glacial till surrounding it. You may notice stands of lowland swamp conifer dominated by white cedar and black ash. Tracts of mature hemlock-hardwood are also present.

From the southern Trail access on Sailor Creek Road (FR-571), angle north-west to the eastern shore of Jerry Lake. Pass a blue-blazed side trail to the east leading back to a parking area on the road. Farther north, pass a trailside walk-in campsite, featuring a stone fire ring on the lake’s northeast end. The walk-in site offers the best access point on the lake to get water; the receding

shoreline is marshy. An open-air pit toilet, uphill from the campsite, is hidden behind a tree.

From Jerry Lake, head north through a mixed-hardwood forest. Wildflowers carpet the forest floor mid-spring, including spring beauty, trillium, bloodroot, hepatica, and bellwort. After crossing Hay Meadow Road (FR-572), hike through a wetland area over several boardwalks. A Trail register and bench await. Then cross the South Fork of the Yellow River on a 67-foot-long bridge built by MSC volunteers.

After the bridge, turn northwest and follow the river for a while. Then, as the Trail heads away from the river, you’ll find a walk-in campsite to the east, on a ridge overlooking the water.

Continue north, away from the river, and soon the Trail will cross FR-576 twice. The first crossing is near the west end of the road, clearly designated as the “West” crossing. The second is designated the “East” crossing.

After the eastern crossing of FR-576, continue northeast and cross Sailor Creek on a 178-foot-long bridge/boardwalk built by MSC volunteers. A large blue-gray boulder

![Map of the trail segment through Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, showing Jerry Lake, South Fork Yellow River, Sailor Creek, and the Lake Eleven Segment.](page_108_image_1_v2.jpg)

![Map of the Mondeaux Esker Segment within the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, showing the Ice Age Trail, various forest roads, and the Ice Age Primitive Area.](page_109_image_1_v2.jpg)

![icon] marks a walk-in campsite on the west side of the Trail and the creek’s north side. Situated in a grove of hemlocks, it features a stone fire ring and a nice flat area with space for several tents.

After crossing the north end of Sailor Creek Road (FR-571), enter the Lost Lake Esker State Natural Area previously described. Then, make a sharp bend to the south and traverse Hemlock Esker for almost a mile, climbing 80 feet above the forest floor. After you descend it, turn northeast and wind through the hemlock-hardwood forest over hummocky terrain. Shortly after crossing an unnamed creek on a short boardwalk, a walk-in campsite, tucked under a hemlock canopy, is located to the east atop a rise. The area is flat with space for several tents.

After crossing Lake 19 Road (FR-108), follow the edge of a large bog before reaching the western junction with the Chippewa Lobe Interpretive Loop (read more below). A wooden sign marks it; blue diamond blazes mark the Loop trail. From this intersection, you may head south on the Loop trail to a walk-in campsite. The campsite, situated on high, dry ground, has benches and a stone fire ring. Just across the Trail from the campsite, a log path leads to a small bog lake which is not visible from the Trail.

Continue east from the junction with the Loop trail past marshes, bogs, and wetland areas. Here, portions of the Trail may be swampy during wet weather. After about a mile, you’ll reach a junction with the White Birch Trail (Forest Trail 567). If you head north it leads to a parking area on Mondeaux Avenue (FR-102); southeast leads to another access to the Chippewa Lobe Interpretive Loop.

Less than half a mile east of the junction with the White Birch Trail is the eastern junction with the Chippewa Lobe Interpretive Loop. Turn due north, and after 0.7 miles, reach a junction with Forest Trail 350, which leads directly north to Mondeaux Avenue (FR-102). To complete the segment, turn east at this junction. Almost immediately, you will reach a walk-in campsite with a stone fire ring and space for several tents, on the east side of the Trail and the west shore of a small unnamed lake not visible from the Trail. After passing the campsite, continue downhill and skirt the lake for several hundred yards. Water is more accessible here than from the steep, shrubby slope below the campsite.

Less than one mile east of the campsite you’ll meet a forest trail; turn north and soon reach Mondeaux Avenue (FR-102). Join the road to the east for 100 yards, crossing the North Fork of the Yellow River. A bench on the pedestrian bridge offers a spot to rest, listen to the water, and view wildlife. East of the river, dip back into the forest, cross Mondeaux Avenue (FR-102), and meander northeast to the segment’s endpoint on CTH-E. The last 1.6 miles, through birch forest with an undergrowth of ferns, are primarily flat and tend to get swampy after heavy rain and during the spring thaw.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.h2: SIDE TRAIL – CHIPPEWA LOBE INTERPRETIVE LOOP
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.parent_text:
## SIDE TRAIL – CHIPPEWA LOBE INTERPRETIVE LOOP

**6.1 miles:** Includes 4.6 miles of Loop trail plus 1.5 miles of Ice Age Trail to close the loop.

**Access by foot only:** (i) From the west, hike the Ice Age Trail 0.5 mi east from Lake 19 Rd. (FR-108) to a kiosk at the start of the Loop. (ii) From the north, hike the White Birch Trail (Forest Trail 567) south 1.1 mi, cross the Ice Age Trail and continue south another 0.5 mi to its intersection with the Chippewa Lobe Interpretive Loop. This is less scenic than the Ice Age Trail access. (iii) From the east, hike the Ice Age Trail 1.6 mi west of where the Ice Age Trail leaves FR-102 after crossing the North Fork of the Yellow River. A steel sign marks the trail junction.

The Chippewa Lobe Interpretive Loop, also called the “Blue Diamond Trail”, is a rugged route that circles a remote roadless area called the Ice Age Primitive Area. Six posts mark the miles oriented counterclockwise, starting from the Ice Age Trail’s western access to it at the kiosk. During wet weather, the loop may not be passable, as the Yellow River tributaries flow west to east through the entire section, with little structure at critical wet crossings. A walk-in campsite is located on the loop 0.1 mi south of its western intersection with the Ice Age Trail. A second secluded walk-in campsite is located at the southern end of the loop, 2.7 mi south of the Ice Age Trail’s western intersection and 1.9 mi south of the Ice Age Trail’s eastern intersection. Situated with pleasant views of the pond and

beaver dam, this exceptional site has a log bench, fire pit and small cast iron table made from an old fire grate. A pit toilet is hidden a few yards to the southeast.

### AREA SERVICES

**Gilman:** Restaurant, grocery, convenience store, camping, library. From the Sailor Creek Rd. (FR-571) Trail access go west ~18 mi.

**Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area:** Lodging, camping, seasonal concession stand. On Trail. Cabin rental (seasonal) at Mondeaux Dam Lodge (W7969 Park Rd., Westboro,. From the CTH-E Trail access go north and east ~4 mi.

**Medford:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. At the STH-13 wayside go south 12.6 mi on STH-13. For area info, contact the Medford Area Chamber of Commerce (715-748-4729). From the CTH-E Trail access go east and south ~22 mi.

![Jerry Lake Segment: Fall leaves carpet a boardwalk.](page_111_image_1_v2.jpg)

LISA SZELA

*Jerry Lake Segment: Fall leaves carpet a boardwalk.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.parent_text:
## Mondeaux Esker Segment (Atlas Map 23f)

**Summary:** The Mondeaux Esker Segment is a 11.7-mile trail in Taylor County (Atlas Map 23f), running from CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.) to Shady Dr.. This segment has two remote, rugged forested sections split by a trek through the popular Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area (MDRA).

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3] [Icon: 5 hiker] **11.7 miles**: CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.) to Shady Dr.

* [Icon: water tap and people icon] At MDRA lodge and campgrounds except that water is not available at Picnic Point campground.
* [Icon: water drop icon] From the glacial spring on MDRA Park Rd.. From the Mondeaux Flowage and other small streams/creeks and wetland areas.
* [Icon: hiker and tent icon] Primitive camping [Icon: tent icon] on national forest lands.
* [Icon: tent and triangle icon] At MDRA campgrounds [Icon: tent icon].
* [Icon: picnic table and hiker icon] Several areas within the MDRA including the lodge and Spearhead Point campground.
* [Icon: globe icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* ![dashed line icon](page_112_image_10_v2.jpg) Small portions through the MDRA and near Spearhead Point campground overlap roads or sidewalks. Other portions overlap logging/forest roads.
* ![crossing paths icon](page_112_image_8_v2.jpg) Near Mondeaux Dam the segment passes near the trail access for the Aldo Leopold Nature Trail.

![Map of the Mondeaux Esker Segment showing the trail route through the Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area, past the Mondeaux Flowage, and through the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest. Key points include Mondeaux Dam, Spearhead Point, West Point, and Picnic Point.](page_112_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.) Trail Access (45.29965, -90.47438):** From Medford at the intersection STH-64 and STH-13, take STH-13 north 4.6 mi. At CTH-M turn left and go west 7.2 mi. At CTH-E (Mondeaux Dr.) turn right and go north 6.6 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **Shady Dr. Trail Access (45.30747, -90.37270):** From Medford at the intersection of STH-64 and STH-13, take STH-13 north 11.1 mi. At N. Chelsea Ave. turn left and go west 0.2 mi. At Fischer Creek Rd. turn right and go north 1.0 mi. At Fawn Ave. turn left and go west 3.4 mi. **No Parking.**

**Additional Parking:** Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area

### THE HIKE

This segment has two remote, rugged forested sections split by a trek through the popular Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area (MDRA). The present-day Mondeaux Flowage occupies a seven-mile tunnel channel, which once gushed with meltwater beneath the Chippewa Lobe. The area’s well-known landforms — ice-walled lake plains, small kettle lakes, and forest-covered glacial knobs — resulted from stagnant glacial ice. Multiple eskers form the hogback ridges characteristic of the flowage area. The area is a waypoint on the Great Wisconsin Birding and Nature Trail. Birders should look for the state-threatened red-shouldered hawk and other species such as raven, pileated woodpecker, winter wren, hermit thrush, scarlet tanager, northern water-thrush, and other warblers. Watching and listening for the common loon along the flow is also rewarding. Pronounced glacial

![Map of the trail segment within Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, showing Rindt Rd., Shady Dr., and Fawn Ave. with trail access points 1 and 2.](page_113_image_1_v2.jpg)

topography and mature oak and maple forests with stands of hemlock, white pine, and white cedar characterize the section of Trail from CTH-E (Mondeaux Drive) to the Mondeaux Dam.

From the trail access on CTH-E (Mondeaux Drive), head east through the Mondeaux Hardwoods State Natural Area, traversing a large stand of hemlocks and briefly following an old logging road. After about 1.5 miles, you’ll reach a junction with a snowmobile trail (FR-100). Turn left, briefly following it, then turn sharply right and climb a hill. Continue to meander through the forest, eventually crossing Campers Road (FR-106) and hiking to the shore of the Mondeaux Flowage. Turn north, parallel the west side of the flowage, and then climb atop and follow the crest of the obvious Mondeaux Esker.

As you continue north, the Trail first crosses the West Point campground road (FR-1432) then meets and briefly follows

the road (FR-1434) leading to the Spearhead Point campground. At the intersection with the campground road (FR-1434), jump off the Trail for a side trip to a year-round glacial spring, with cold clear water available to the public. (Turn left on FR-1432, following it to Campers Road. Turn right, following it to Park Road, and turn left. The spring is on the west side of Park Road.)

Back on the Trail, pay close attention; signage is sparse as you approach Spearhead Point campground road (FR-1434) and continue heading north. After crossing the campground road near Spearhead Point and descending the Mondeaux esker, jump off the Trail for a second side trip. (The Aldo Leopold Commemorative Trail, a 1.2-mile lollipop nature trail, ascends to another esker. You can reach it by heading northwest from the esker steps across a parking lot to the trail access off Park Road.)

Back on the Trail, head north from the esker steps, follow the shoreline around the northern end of the flowage, and pass the facilities of the Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area (MDRA). Stop in at the historic Mondeaux Dam Lodge for a meal. Built by the Civilian Conservation Corps in 1938, the building is on the National Register of Historic Places. The MDRA also offers, in addition to several developed campgrounds around the Mondeaux Flowage, picnic shelters, a swimming beach, boat rentals, and a fishing pier.

The next portion of the segment, from the Mondeaux Dam to the segment’s end at the Shady Drive and Fawn Avenue intersection, can be exceptionally challenging. Tread can be rough, with roots, rocks, and wet areas. The Trail follows logging roads, parallels wetlands, and crosses creeks without bridges or on beaver dams. Some sections may require a wet ford due to beaver activity or seasonally changing water levels. Signage can be sparse, undergrowth can be thick, and cell phones may not work.

Cross the Mondeaux Dam and hike through the Lakeview Picnic Area (restrooms available) before heading south between the Eastwood Campground and the flowage. As the segment continues south along the flowage’s east side, you’ll traverse a young forest where undergrowth can make the Trail challenging to follow; pay close attention to signage.

Turn east, cross Mondeaux Drive (FR-104) and continue east into more damp/wet forest. After about a quarter mile, take a sharp right turn and head up a hill to an island of mature maples towering overhead with a clear understory. After descending the backside of the hill, you’ll drop into an extended wet section and soon cross a wetland on an old beaver dam. The tread can be uneven, so take care. Overgrown vegetation can cover yellow blazes and make the Trail difficult to follow in places. Continue east through wetlands and forests to its segment’s terminus at the intersection of Shady Drive and Fawn Avenue.

### AREA SERVICES

**Medford:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. At the STH-13 wayside go south 12.6 mi on STH-13. For area info, contact the Medford Area Chamber of Commerce (715-748-4729). From the CTH-E Trail access go south and east ~18 mi.

**Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest Medford Office:** Information (715-748-4875, www.fs.usda.gov/cnnf).

**Mondeaux Dam Recreation Area (MDRA):** Lodging, camping, seasonal concession stand. On Trail. Cabin rental (seasonal) at Mondeaux Dam Lodge (W7969 Park Rd., Westboro,

fs.usda.gov/detail/cnnf/recreation). Camping at Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest Campgrounds: Eastwood, Spearhead Point, Picnic Point (group), West Point (first come, first served) and nearby Twin Lakes (reservations: 877-444-6777, recreation.gov).

**Westboro:** Restaurant, library. At the STH-13 wayside go north 2.5 mi on STH-13. From the MDRA go east ~8.5 mi.

![A narrow dirt trail winds through a lush green forest with tall trees.](page_115_image_1_v2.jpg)

JEAN ADAMS

***Mondeaux Esker Segment:*** The Trail winds along the esker’s ridge.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.parent_text:
## Pine Line Segment and East Lake Segment (Atlas Maps 24f, 25f)

**Summary:** The Pine Line Segment and East Lake Segment covers approximately 7.4 miles in Taylor County (Atlas Map 24f, 25f). It comprises the Pine Line Segment (0.9 mi, Fischer Creek Rd. at Fawn Ave. to STH-13), the East Lake Segment (6.5 mi, STH-13 Wayside to CTH-D).

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Site icon] **Pine Line Segment—0.9 miles:** Fischer Creek Rd. at Fawn Ave. to STH-13

[Icon: Water and picnic icons] At Chelsea Lake County Park.

[Icon: Hunting icon] Segment is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: Dog on leash icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Camping icon] A walk-in campsite [Icon: Campsite icon].

[Icon: Rail-trail crossing icon] Crosses Pine-Line multiuse rail-trail at western end of segment.

![Map of Pine Line and East Lake Segments showing trail route, local roads like STH-13, Fawn Ave, and Gunnar Rd, and natural features like Chelsea Lake, Black Lake, and Skinner Lake.](page_116_image_1_v2.jpg)

[Icon: Ice Age Trail logo] **East Lake Segment—6.5 miles:** STH-13 Wayside to CTH-D

*Note: A reroute is planned immediately west of CTH-D. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

* [Icon: Water and restroom icons] At the STH-13 wayside.

* [Icon: Hunting icon] Portion of the segment crossing private land east of Beards Ln. is closed during gun deer season.

* [Icon: Water drop icon] From East Lake and a few other small lakes and intermittent streams/creeks.

* [Icon: Leashed dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

* [Icon: Camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands.

* [Icon: Multi-use trail icon] Portions overlap cross-country ski/bike trails and with logging/forest roads.

* [Icon: Skiing icon] Rib Lake Nordic Ski Trails network.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

* **① Fischer Creek Rd. at Fawn Ave. Trail Access (45.30864, -90.30394):** From Medford at the intersection of STH-64 and STH-13, take STH-13 north 13.0 mi. At Gunnar Rd. turn left and go west 0.5 mi. At Fischer Rd. turn left and go south 1.0 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Chelsea Lake County Park (see Area Services).

![Map of Rib Lake Segment and surrounding area including Rib Lake School Forest, Taylor County Forest, and Rib Lake Ski Trails.](page_117_image_1_v2.jpg)

* **② STH-13 Trail Access (45.31155, -90.29150): No Parking.** Instead, park at STH-13 Wayside.

* **③ STH-13 Wayside Trail Access (45.32073, -90.29059): Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

* **④ CTH-D Trail Access (45.34488, -90.21625):** From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take CTH-D north for 2.1 mi. **Parking:** 300 ft east of Trail Access at Rib Lake School Forest parking area.

## Pine Line Segment

**Summary:** The Pine Line Segment: This very short segment retains a remote, deep-woods feeling.

This very short segment retains a remote, deep-woods feeling.

From the Trail Access at the Fischer Creek Road and Fawn Avenue intersection, cross the Pine Line multiuse trail and follow the edge of a farm field east. Soon you’ll turn north and enter a wooded property owned by the Ice Age Trail Alliance. A small walk-in campsite, built by local Boy Scouts, is located in a wooded clearing west of the segment’s endpoint on STH-13.

To reach the East Lake Segment, head north on a connecting route on the shoulder of STH-13 for 0.6 miles to a wayside on the east side of the road where water and restrooms are available from late May to mid-September.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::taylor-county:.parent_text:
## East Lake Segment

**Summary:** The East Lake Segment: This remote, hilly and scenic segment highlights the headwaters of the Black River, historic logging sites, and Moose Mountain.

This remote, hilly and scenic segment highlights the headwaters of the Black River, historic logging sites, and Moose Mountain.

The segment travels through portions of the 17,688-acre Taylor County Forest. The first forest land acquisition occurred in 1937, when the Rib Lake Lumber Company deeded 3,360 acres to the county. The original forest had been composed of excellent stands of white pine, hemlock, northern hardwoods, and swamp conifers.

In today’s forest, mid- to late- successional northern hardwood forests are replacing the aspen, birch, oak, and jack pine trees planted in the 1940s through the 1970s. It is also sustainably managed for multiple uses and habitats. Taylor County’s aspen regeneration project follows the recommended practice for growing and reproducing aspen: clear-cutting all trees larger than two inches in diameter. Aspens regenerate through abundant root sprouts, or “suckers,” that develop after their trunks have been cut. Clear-cuts of 10 to 20 acres are best for wildlife and woodland uses.

From the Trail access at the STH-13 wayside, head east through hilly terrain and mixed hardwood forest, including many oaks. Look for signs noting points of interest or identifying trees. You’ll soon reach the headwaters of the Black River in a scenic hemlock grove. Farther on, enter a Taylor County aspen regeneration project area. Follow a swath cut through the trees before crossing Beards Lane.

Continue east and then northeast through a mix of Taylor County Forest and private lands, passing the shore of East Lake, a classic kettle lake, in dramatic high-relief hummocky topography. After passing East Lake, skirt a wetland and cross a bridge over an unnamed creek. Head northeast through forested but slightly less hilly terrain and eventually climb Moose Mountain. Here you may glimpse the surrounding area through the trees, especially during the leaf-off season. Erratics, scattered across Moose Mountain, range in size, shape, and color. Moose Mountain received its name from a Taylor County local in honor of a friend. Near the segment’s endpoint on CTH-D, you’ll encounter a locked gate where the Trail connects with and overlaps the Rib Lake Nordic Ski Trail network. Go around the gate and continue on to CTH-D, the segment’s endpoint.

### AREA SERVICES

**Chelsea Lake County Park**: From the Fawn Ave. and Fischer Creek Rd. intersection go south on Fischer Creek Rd. 0.5 mi. At the park’s gravel road, turn right and go west 0.3 mi to the county park.

**Westboro**: Restaurant, library. At the STH-13 wayside go north 2.5 mi on STH-13.

**Ogema**: Restaurant, lodging. At the STH-13 wayside go north 9.0 mi on STH-13.

**Medford**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. At the STH-13 wayside go south 12.6 mi on STH-13. For area info, contact the Medford Area Chamber of Commerce (715-748-4729).

[Icon: Taylor County logo] **Rib Lake**: See Rib Lake Segment, p. 87. From the CTH-D Trail access go south 2.0 mi.
