## Rib Lake Segment (Atlas Map 25f)

**Summary:** The Rib Lake Segment is a 5.5-mile trail in Taylor County (Atlas Map 25f), running from CTH-D to STH-102. This segment’s lengthy roadwalk is balanced by the wooded off-road section highlighting a steep-sided esker and several picturesque streams and lakes.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] [Icon: trail icon] **5.5 miles (2.7 IAT, 2.8 CR):** CTH-D to STH-102

*Note: New Ice Age Trail* will be open in October 2023 between CTH-D and Bear Ave. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

[Icon: water icon] At Rusch Preserve.

[Icon: water source icon] From Copper Creek, Sheep Ranch Creek and several lakes along the connecting route.

[Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping in areas in the IATA’s Marimor Preserve. Three walk-in campsites and Heilige Nacht Dispersed Camping Area in the Rusch Preserve.

[Icon: camping icon] At nearby private campground 4.0 mi south of the Trail.

[Icon: swimming icon] At South Harper Lake public beach (no facilities) on the west end of the lake.

[Icon: hunting icon] Portion of the segment crossing private land between CTH-C and STH-102 is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

Off-road sections overlap a short portion of the Timm’s Hill National Trail, cross-country ski trails and logging/forest roads.

[Icon: trail intersection icon] The connecting route portion passes an access point for the Timm’s Hill National Trail where it heads north from Rustic Road 1; off-road portion intersects a blue-blazed spur trail to a DCA and ski trails of the Rib Lake Nordic Ski Trails system.

[Icon: accessibility icon] Portions of this segment in the Rusch Preserve may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] **① CTH-D Trail Access (45.34488, -90.21625):** From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take CTH-D north for 2.1 mi. **Parking:** 300 ft east of Trail Access at Rib Lake School Forest parking area.

[Icon: parking icon 2] **② STH-102 Trail Access (45.36084, -90.14708):** From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take STH-102 east then north 5.5 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** Rib Lake Nordic Ski Trails accessible parking area on CTH-C and Rusch Preserve parking area 175 ft north on CTH-C.

### THE HIKE

This segment’s lengthy roadwalk is balanced by the wooded off-road section highlighting a steep-sided esker and several picturesque streams and lakes.

The segment borders the terminal moraines of the Wisconsin Valley Lobe (east) and the Chippewa Lobe (west). And the Trail highlights this glacial story in its travels across two Alliance-owned preserves. Locations across the Rusch Preserve reveal end moraine deposits, part of the Perkinstown Moraine, and pitted outwash areas. The Preserve also hosts Rib Lake Lumber Company Camp 6 site (1911–1914) and a historic logging ice road on/along Klostermann Creek. The creek, running the length of the property, provides a system of wetlands and high-quality northern hardwood forest. During 60 years of operation, the Rib Lake Lumber Company processed over 1.4 billion board feet of lumber.

The Trail also passes through the Marimor Preserve, which interprets the hummocky western end moraines left by the Wisconsin Valley Lobe. Pronounced MĀR-eh-mōr, it is short for “Marigold Moraine,” named for the marsh marigolds dominating the wetlands and ephemeral ponds in spring and the parallel end moraines oriented diagonally across

![Map showing the Ice Age Trail and Timm's Hill National Trail through the Rib Lake School Forest, Rusch Preserve, and Marimor Preserve, including segments like East Lake and Wood Lake.](page_120_image_1_v2.jpg)

the property. The word “moraine” is from the Old English “mor,” meaning hill. Modern geologists use the term “end moraine” to describe a glacially deposited ridge.

From the Trail access on CTH-D, follow a route parallel to the highway, duck into the woods, and then bear right and pass a flowing stream. Climb atop a steep-sided esker, then sharply descend it as the Trail follows and crosses a bridge over Sheep Ranch Creek. Wind through the forest to Harper Drive to begin a 2.8-mile connecting route road walk.

Hike north on Harper Drive for 1 mile. At Rustic Road 1 turn east and go 1.8 miles, past a swimming beach on South Harper Lake and an access point where the Timm’s Hill National Trail (timmshilltrail.com) heads north to Wisconsin’s highest point at 1951.5 feet. Continue to where both the Ice Age Trail and Timm’s Hill National Trail depart Rustic Road 1 and head south off-road into the Rusch Preserve.

Pass a Trail kiosk with a detailed map of the 72-acre Rusch Preserve and continue through wooded, low-relief terrain. Soon, you’ll reach a junction with a spur trail leading west to the Heilige Nacht Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. The site has two benches, a small “table,” and a fire ring. You may explore other parts of the Rusch Preserve, although watch carefully for private property markers and stay within the Preserve’s boundaries. Continue to the Rusch Preserve parking area, jog right then left, and quickly reach CTH-C. Turn south on CTH-C and hike a short distance to where the segment departs the road and continues east toward STH-102.

Briefly cut through an area of pine trees and a field before entering the woods and crossing St. Clair Creek on a curving boardwalk. Then, climb a hill and continue through alternating woods and fields. You’ll pass through

the Marimor Preserve and cross several logging roads before arriving at the segment’s terminus at STH-102.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Taylor County logo] T<sub>C</sub> **Rib Lake:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-D Trail access go 2.0 mi south on CTH-D. Lodging at Gasthaus B&B (715-427-5058) and Toad Hollow Loft (715-427-5676; airbnb.com/rooms/12104978). For area info, contact Village of Rib Lake (715-427-5404, riblakewisconsin.com).

**Medford:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. At the STH-13 wayside go south 12.6 mi on STH-13. For area info, contact the Medford Area Chamber of Commerce (715-748-4729). From CTH-D Trail access go south ~18 mi.

![Rib Lake Segment: A rock crew rests on the stone staircase they helped build.](page_121_image_1_v2.jpg)
DAVE CALIEBE

Rib Lake Segment: A rock crew rests on the stone staircase they helped build.

## Wood Lake Segment (Atlas Maps 25f, 26f)

**Summary:** The Wood Lake Segment is a 13.4-mile trail in Taylor County (Atlas Map 25f, 26f), running from STH-102 to Tower Rd.. This wild, remote segment highlights scenic Gus Johnson Creek, Wood Lake, wetlands and historic logging camp sites.

### SNAPSHOT

![3](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) ![5](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) **13.4 miles**: STH-102 to Tower Rd.

* ![water tap](page_122_image_16_v2.jpg)
* ![car/tent](page_122_image_16_v2.jpg)
* ![picnic table](page_122_image_16_v2.jpg) At Wood Lake County Park ![A.](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg)
* ![fishing](page_122_image_16_v2.jpg)
* ![hiking](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg)
* ![water drop](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) From Gus Johnson Creek, Wood Lake and other small streams/creeks, small lakes and wetland areas.

* ![hiker/tent](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) Primitive camping ![green dotted square](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) on county forest lands and on portions of the IATA’s Marimor Preserve.
* ![no hunting](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) Portions of the segment crossing private land east of STH-102 are closed during gun deer season.
* ![dog](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* ![road](page_122_image_13_v2.jpg) Portions overlap logging/forest roads.
* ![loop](page_122_image_1_v2.jpg) A white-blazed loop north of Wood Lake.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **STH-102 Trail Access (45.36084, -90.14708)**: From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take STH-102 east then north 5.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

② **Tower Rd. Trail Access (45.34059, -90.04003)**: From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take STH-102 east for 2.9 mi. Where STH-102 curves north, continue east on Wilderness Ave. for 5.9 mi. At Tower Rd. turn left and go north 0.5 mi. From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi to the junction with STH-107. Continue west on STH-64/107 for 6.6 mi. At CTH-M continue west for 10.9 mi. At Tower Rd. turn right and go north 9.0 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Bear Ave. (ii) Wood Lake County Park picnic area (see Area Services), (iii) North Loop Rd.

### THE HIKE

This wild, remote segment highlights scenic Gus Johnson Creek, Wood Lake, wetlands and historic logging camp sites.

It borders the terminal moraines of the Wisconsin Valley Lobe (east) and the Chippewa Lobe (west). The Trail interprets the hummocky Wood Lake Moraine, which contains the Alliance-owned Moraine Preserve. During leaf-off season, Moraine Preserve offers views to the northeast of an ice-walled lake plain. The area provides welcoming habitat for deer, black

![Map of Wood Lake Segment showing trail route, parking access points 1 and 2, IATA's Marimor Preserve, IATA's Moraine Preserve, Olson Lake, Wood Lake, and Historic Logging Camp 7 Site.](page_122_image_1_v2.jpg)

bears, wolves, grouse, and coyotes. In addition, its wetlands and lakes are home to eagles, sandhill cranes, loons, and beavers.

The segment introduces hikers to the impact of historic logging, logging camp life, and railroad operations through interpretive signs along the Wood Lake loop trail (a portion shared by the Ice Age Trail). The area continues to recover from the extensive logging of the early 1900s. At that time, logging camps, whose remains are found along the segment, dotted the forest landscape and were symbolic of the Northwoods way of life for European-Americans.

Wayfinding can present a challenge here. Expect difficult-to-traverse beaver dams, unbridged creeks, and seasonally flooded wetlands that occasionally swamp portions of the Trail.

From the Trail access on STH-102, enter the Alliance’s Marimor Preserve (described in the Rib Lake Segment) and hike through an area that embraces the western end moraines left by the Wisconsin Valley lobe. Climb one end moraine, drop to cross an intermittent stream at Six Stone Crossing, then follow the outer side of another end moraine.

Continue east through a mix of Taylor County Forest land, the Moraine Preserve, and private lands. Cross Bear Avenue for the first time (“western crossing”). Just south of it, follow a railroad right-of-way constructed in 1902, which ran between Rib Lake and Tomahawk. You’ll also pass a massive beaver dam that created a new lake. Soon you’ll reach the site of Rib Lake Lumber Company Logging Camp 7; a sign and historic photo mark the spot. Outlines

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Taylor County Forest, showing Wood Lake, various logging camp sites, and trail segments with mileage markers.](page_123_image_1_v2.jpg)

of its buildings are visible in the clearing on top of the hill southwest of the sign. The camp, where 125 lumberjacks cut hemlock and other hardwoods, operated from 1912 to 1913.

After you depart the Camp 7 site, leave the old railroad grade and wind through the forest to the second (“central”) crossing of Bear Avenue. From here you’ll travel north, traversing the scenic and steep-sided valley of Gus Johnson Creek, set between two ice-walled-lake plains and formed by water that gushed from under the ice at the outer edge of the Wood Lake Moraine. This segment has an incredibly intimate wilderness character, especially where it crosses Gus Johnson Creek. As you continue east of the creek, near the third crossing of Bear Avenue, the forest dramatically changes from hemlock, birch, and maple to maple, oak, and poplar.

Turn south and cross a bridge over an unnamed tributary of New Wood Creek. A bench overlooks a large beaver meadow created from a breached dam. Cross Cutoff Road and travel through spectacular high-relief hummocky topography, reaching the western junction of a 3.5-mile loop trail circling Wood Lake. The 67-acre lake is spring-fed with clear blue waters. The Ice Age Trail uses the western and southern portions of the loop, for 2.5 miles. The route passes through Wood Lake County Park’s swimming, picnicking, and camping areas, where water and restrooms are available seasonally. The white-blazed northern portion of the loop, known as Camp 4 Trail, highlights the historic Logging Camp 4 site, which was in operation from 1906 to 1910. A joint project of the Taylor County Forestry Department, Rib Lake Historical Society, Ice Age Trail Alliance, and the local High Point Chapter erected 20 signs identifying historical and geological sites on the Wood Lake loop trail. The signs include maps and photos focusing on the early logging history of the area.

From the eastern junction with the north part of the Wood Lake loop, head east and cross North Loop Road. Continue eastward through high-relief hummocky topography. You’ll cross from Taylor County into Lincoln County before reaching the segment’s endpoint on Tower Road.

### AREA SERVICES

*   [Icon: Trail Community logo] **Rib Lake**: See Rib Lake Segment, p. 87. From the STH-102 Trail access go south 5.5 mi.
*   **Medford**: See Pine Line Segment and East Lake Segment, p. 84. From the STH-102 Trail access go south ~22 mi.
*   **Wood Lake County Park**: Camping. On Trail. For information contact Taylor County Forestry and Recreation Department (715-748-1486). Camping is free Labor Day to Memorial Day. From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D take STH-102 east and north 4.0 mi. At Wood Lake Ave. turn right and go east 3.3 mi.
*   **Merrill**: See Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment, p. 105. From Tower Rd. Trail access go south and east ~30 mi.

![A scenic view of a lake in the Wood Lake Segment, with vibrant summer reflections of the sky and surrounding green forest on the calm water surface.](page_125_image_1_v2.jpg)
NANCY LEHMAN

*Wood Lake Segment: Vibrant summer reflections.*

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Lincoln County. The map shows several trail segments including the Timberland Wilderness Segment, Camp 27 Segment, New Wood Segment, Averill-Kelly Creek Segment, Turtle Rock Segment, and a portion of the Grandfather Falls Segment (labeled as Grandfa Seg). Key areas highlighted include the Lincoln County Forest, New Wood Wildlife Area, Bill Cross Rapids Wildlife Area, Camp New Wood County Park, and Council Grounds State Park. An inset map of Wisconsin shows the location of Lincoln County. The map also features the Ice Age Trail Alliance logo and website (iceagetrail.org), as well as various road markers (86, M, E, 64, 107) and the Taylor/Lincoln county boundary. A date code "041023" is visible in the bottom left corner of the map area.](page_126_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
# Lincoln County

![Map of Ice Age Trail segments in Lincoln County, Wisconsin. The map highlights the Harrison Hills Segment, Underdown Segment, and Grandfather Falls Segment. Key locations include Tomahawk, Alta Junction, Underdown Recreation Area, Tug Lake Recreation Area, and the city of Merrill, which is designated as an Ice Age Trail Community. The map also shows connecting routes, other trails, county boundaries, and public or IATA land, with major roads like Highway 51, 64, and 17 indicated.](page_127_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Lincoln County

Atlas Maps 26f–31f; Databook pages 25–29

Total miles: 65.8 (Trail 49.7; Connecting Route 16.1)

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Lincoln County](page_128_image_1_v2.jpg)

Lincoln County lies within the Northern Highlands, a once mountainous region that eroded to a rolling landscape long before the Ice Age. The Ice Age glaciers further scoured the higher bedrock hills and filled the valleys with glacial till and debris.

The Wisconsin Valley Lobe’s landforms created striking geological features, covering most of central and northern Lincoln County. Aerial views of the land reveal parallel moraine ridge “waves” formed by the receding glacier 15,000 to 18,000 years ago. As a result, a spectacular band of high-relief hummocky topography marks the maximum southern extent of this lobe.

Moraines of the advancing lobe bookend the Ice Age Trail – the Wood Lake Moraine on the west and the Harrison Moraine on the east. The Harrison Hills and Underdown segments are within the Harrison Moraine, where the elevation often varies by more than 200 feet. Deep kettles, fragmented ridges, irregular hummocks, and ice-walled lake plains mark this top-notch topography.

The Wisconsin River at Grandfather Falls provides an opportunity to see 1.8-billion-year-old exposed bedrock and large boulders smoothed by thousands of years of water flow. The river became a main artery for the lumber industry. By the mid-1850s, 5,000 raftsmen piloted 3,000 lumber rafts containing more than 100 million board feet of lumber down the Wisconsin every year.

Merrill, located along the scenic Wisconsin River at its confluence with the Prairie River, began humbly in 1843 with a trading post and one home. The community, called Jenny Bull Falls, changed its name to Merrill in 1881, in honor of S.S. Merrill, the general manager of the Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul, and Pacific Railroad. Merrill was a prosperous and thriving community at the center of the lumber industry and had electric streetcars installed in 1890, one of the first such installations in the world.

Today, Merrill is a proud Ice Age Trail Community. It boasts 14 parks spread across 1,200 acres of public land, earning its title as the “City of Parks.” A family-friendly community, it features a unique mix of small-town charm and four-season activities.

When enjoying Lincoln County Ice Age Trail segments, please keep in mind:

• Foresters use yellow paint markings throughout the county. These markers, which are irregular shaped and random, are NOT the Trail’s yellow blazes.

* Off-leash dogs may chase bears or risk attack from wolves. Keep dogs leashed.
* Do NOT hike with dogs during hunting season. Doing so endangers the use of private land the Trail crosses.
* For overnight parking locations, contact the Northwoods Chapter coordinator.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Northwoods Chapter, one of the oldest Alliance chapters, has a Trail segment adoption program, dividing Trail segments into manageable distances for volunteers to maintain. The Chapter has a rich history of working with youth groups and the Wisconsin Conservation Corps. It also supports the Alliance's MSC program and volunteers in building new Trail segments across the state and maintaining existing ones in the county.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/northwoods-chapter to learn more about the Chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Lincoln County Tourism Information:**
northcentralwisconsin.com/lincoln-county

**Lincoln County Forestry, Lands & Parks Department:** 715-539-1034,
co.lincoln.wi.us/forestry-land-and-parks

****Lincoln County Sheriff's Office:**** 715-536-6272; contact for overnight parking permission.

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Merrill:** merrillchamber.org/visit-merrill

![A hiker poses with a lush stand of Joe Pye Weed on the Camp 27 Segment.](page_129_image_1_v2.jpg)
ERIN JENSEN

*Camp 27 Segment: A hiker poses with a lush stand of Joe Pye Weed.*

## Timberland Wilderness Segment (Atlas Map 26f)

**Summary:** The Timberland Wilderness Segment is a 3.9-mile trail in Lincoln County (Atlas Map 26f), running from Tower Rd. Northern Trail Access to Tower Rd. Southern Trail Access. This segment passes through a privately owned, undeveloped 35,000-acre property featuring hardwood forests, ice-walled lake plains, ravines, and several small streams.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3] [Icon: 4] **3.9 miles**: Tower Rd. Northern Trail Access to Tower Rd. Southern Trail Access

[Icon: water source icon] From several small intermittent streams/creeks.
[Icon: logging road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads.

[Icon: leashed dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **1 Tower Rd. Northern Trail Access (45.34059, -90.04003)**: From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi to the junction with STH-107. Continue west on STH-64/107 for 6.6 mi. At CTH-M continue west for 10.9 mi. At Tower Rd. turn right and go north 9.0 mi. From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take STH-102 east for 2.9 mi. Where STH-102 curves north, continue east on Wilderness Ave. for 5.9 mi. At Tower Rd. turn left and go north 0.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **2 Tower Rd. Southern Trail Access (45.31017, -90.03202)**: From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi to the junction with STH-107. Continue west on STH-64/107 for 6.6 mi. At CTH-M continue west for 10.9 mi. At Tower Rd. turn right and go north 7.0 mi. From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take STH-102 east for 2.9 mi. Where STH-102 curves north, continue east on Wilderness Ave. for 5.9 mi. At Tower Rd. turn right and go south 1.7 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

### THE HIKE

This segment passes through a privately owned, undeveloped 35,000-acre property featuring hardwood forests, ice-walled lake plains, ravines, and several small streams. Timber production and harvest have not hindered the eastern timber wolf, black bear, bobcat, deer, red fox, and fisher populations. The region’s isolation and wildlife signs promote a true wilderness feeling. The state purchased a linear easement on this property to permanently protect the Ice Age Trail in 1999. In 2002, the state purchased a Forest Legacy Easement over the entire 35,000 acres preventing the land from being developed or subdivided.

From the Trail access, which is a point on Tower Road directly across from the Wood Lake Segment Trail access, head south. After a quarter mile, you’ll cross a gravel logging road, the first of four logging road crossings. A 21st-century logging operation used these roads. Before that, loggers last harvested the area in 1938. However, beavers are doing their own logging and changing the landscape as new wetlands form, impacting the Trail in some locations.

After crossing a second logging road, you’ll hike through an area with several trailside erratics and reach a large beaver dam with an expansive pond. Cross in front of and below the dam on a boardwalk. Continue through the forest, traversing the top of a crescent-shaped ridge before reaching an old railroad grade.

As the segment heads southeast, it follows the edge of an ice-walled lake plain. Peer through the forest to see creek-filled ravines and forested lowlands of mature hemlock, balsam fir, white cedar, and yellow birch. As the segment turns southwest, you’ll pass a Leopold bench dedicated to Herb Schotz, volunteer

and former coordinator of the Ice Age Trail Alliance’s Northwoods Chapter.

Cross a gully spanned by a bridge. Here steam engines moved timber via the old railroad grade to the main line south and east.

After crossing the third logging road (also an old railroad grade), follow the rolling ridges of two more ice-walled lake plains before crossing the final logging road and reaching the segment’s terminus on Tower Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Rib Lake:** See Rib Lake Segment, p. 87. From the Tower Rd. southern Trail access go south and west ~9 mi.

**Medford:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. At the STH-13 wayside go south 12.6 mi on STH-13. For area info, contact the Medford Area Chamber of Commerce (715-748-4729). From the Tower Rd. southern Trail access go south and west ~30 mi.

[Icon: T/C icon] **Merrill:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the Tower Rd. southern Trail access go south and east ~27.5 mi.

![Map of the trail segment showing Wood Lake Segment, Wilderness Ave, Tower Rd, and county boundaries.](page_131_image_1_v2.jpg)

> *Hiking the Ice Age Trail gave me the strength, courage, and determination I was lacking before. I feel I am a different person – a better person – and I love it. A motto I utilized numerous times along the Ice Age Trail: “Don’t limit my challenges, challenge my limits.”*

ROXANN “SUNNY ERRATIC” DUNST, THOUSAND- MILER

## Camp 27 Segment and New Wood Segment (Atlas Maps 26f, 27)

**Summary:** The Camp 27 Segment and New Wood Segment covers approximately 10.0 miles in Lincoln County (Atlas Map 26f, 27). It comprises the Camp 27 Segment (2.9 mi, Tower Rd. to unnamed logging road), the New Wood Segment (7.1 mi, Unnamed logging road to CTH-E). ---

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 5] **Camp 27 Segment—2.9 miles:** Tower Rd. to unnamed logging road

* [Icon: water icon] From the North Fork of the Copper River, a wetland area, and other small streams/creeks.
* [Icon: road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads.
* [Icon: spur trail icon] Short spur trail (gravel road) to the site of historic Camp 27 lumber camp.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs should be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. By law, dogs must be leashed April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.

---

[Icon: 2.5] **New Wood Segment—7.1 miles:** Unnamed logging road to CTH-E

*Note: A reroute is planned south of Conservation Ave. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

* [Icon: water icon] From the New Wood River, Camp Twentysix Creek and other small streams/creeks and wetland areas.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.
* [Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping on a narrow band of county forest.
* [Icon: road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads.
* [Icon: hunting icon] Portion of segment crossing private land between the eastern boundary of the Lincoln County Forest and CTH-E is closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: footpath icon] A number of hunter access footpaths on old forest roads.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **① Tower Rd. Trail Access (45.29520, -90.00360):** *From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi to the junction with STH-107. Continue west on STH-64/107 6.6 mi. At CTH-M continue west for 10.9 mi. At Tower Rd. turn right and go north 5.1 mi.*

![Map of Camp 27 and New Wood Segments showing trail path, parking areas, and geographic features like the North Fork Copper River and New Wood State Wildlife Area. 1.9-mi CR to Timberland Wilderness Segment. West and north on Tower Rd.](page_132_image_1_v2.jpg)

From Rib Lake at the intersection of STH-102 and CTH-D, take STH-102 east for 2.9 mi. Where STH-102 curves north, continue east on Wilderness Ave. for 5.9 mi. At Tower Rd., turn right and go south 3.5 mi to the Trail access on the east side of the road. **Parking: Roadside.**

[Icon: 2] **2 Unnamed Logging Road Trail Access (45.29751, -89.97287): Parking:** 0.3 mi northeast of Trail Access at end of unnamed logging road. *High clearance vehicle recommended.*

[Icon: 3] **3 CTH-E Trail Access (45.29676, -89.88912):** From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi. At STH-107 turn right and go north 13.0 mi. At CTH-E turn left across the Wisconsin River and go west then south 7.5 mi. **Parking: Adjacent to Trail Access.**

**Additional Parking:** (i) Conservation Ave. (ii) Additional parking areas farther west along Conservation Ave. near hunter access footpaths on old forest roads. These are maintained mostly during hunting season and provide access to the Ice Age Trail.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
## Camp 27 Segment

**Summary:** The Camp 27 Segment: Both the Camp 27 and New Wood segments traverse the New Wood State Wildlife Area.

### THE HIKE

Both the Camp 27 and New Wood segments traverse the New Wood State Wildlife Area. The area dates back to 1945 when the state purchased 960 acres of cutover and burned land for wildlife and waterfowl management and public hunting. Additional state purchases increased the State Wildlife Area to today’s 4,635 acres located in one of the most remote regions in the state. Since the early 1980s, timber wolf packs have returned and reestablished themselves in the area. In addition, the upland forest offers habitat for deer, ruffed grouse, snowshoe hare, hawks, bald eagles, and a variety of thrushes and warblers. Spring wildflowers are abundant.

The segment offers the chance to explore historic logging Camp 27, established in 1940 or 1941 by the Rib Lake Lumber Company of Delaware. Logs were transported by rail to the company’s sawmill in Rib Lake. At the time, hemlocks had grown so large that two people could barely reach around the trunks. Loggers felled the trees by hand using crosscut saws and axes. They worked in winter, icing the roads to transport the logs by sled. In some winters, as many as 150 men worked in Camp 27. Self-sufficient, the camp even had cattle and pigs. Unfortunately, by 1945 the area had been cut over and cleared. Little trace of Camp 27 remains, except for a small mound on the northern edge of the camp, probably a garbage dump site.

From the Trail access on Tower Road, head northeast. You’ll quickly reach a beaver dam more than 100 feet long. Use it to cross a large wetland. Then hike atop a narrow earthen embankment marked on each end with metal Trail markers. From here, the segment switches back and forth between narrow, tree-lined corridors and wide, grassy logging trails.

![Map of the Camp 27 and New Wood segments within the New Wood State Wildlife Area, showing trail paths, access points, and local landmarks like the Copper River and Big White Pines.](page_133_image_1_v2.jpg)

Farther along, cross the North Fork of the Copper River on a rocky creek bed about 20 feet wide. Depending on the snow melt or rain, this may be a relatively dry crossing or require a shallow ford. About a mile and a quarter past this crossing,

you’ll intersect a grass-covered logging road. For a side trip to logging Camp 27, described above, head north 100 yards on it.

From the intersection with the logging road to Camp 27, continue south, passing a small beaver dam. Cross a small stream on a footbridge and an ice-walled lake plain just shy of the segment’s terminus at an unnamed logging road.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
## New Wood Segment

**Summary:** The New Wood Segment: The State Wildlife Area through which some of this segment travels gets the name “New Wood” from the trees grown after the fires of the early 1930s burned through acres of land.

The State Wildlife Area through which some of this segment travels gets the name “New Wood” from the trees grown after the fires of the early 1930s burned through acres of land. In the preceding years, large pines were logged in the region from the 1850s through the 1890s, benefiting companies like the Stange & Kinzel Lumber Company and Stange Sash, Door, and Blind Factory of Merrill. By 1906, with the old-growth pines gone, A. H. Stange realized the untapped potential of hardwood and hemlock and acquired land that includes today’s Wildlife Area. In 1925, the Rib Lake Lumber Co. of Taylor County bought and held the land for the next 20 years.

From the Trail access on an unnamed logging road at the end of the Camp 27 Segment, head through a few swampy areas and marshland. Within a mile, you’ll cross the first of several old forest roads primarily used by hunters. As the segment continues eastward, you’ll traverse a classic esker and view trailside erratics. You’ll follow wide, grassy troads along the edges of open meadows and pass large, beautiful remnant pines and hemlock groves.

As you head north and cross Conservation Avenue, you’ll enter the State Wildlife Area and a one-mile-wide band of Lincoln County Forest, where primitive camping is permitted. As the trail curves to the south, you’ll reach a point along the New Wood River with three giant white pines that escaped the saw during the logging heyday, probably due to their small size at the time. Their canopy offers a scenic, peaceful resting spot. There is a bench and access to the New Wood River here.

Farther along, in a small grove of hemlocks, a river overlook features a Leopold bench where you can relax and enjoy the memorable view. Do not camp here; this spot and the remainder of the segment are on private land. Continue through hummocky glacial terrain and cross Camp Twentysix Creek on a narrow footbridge. Follow the flat, grassy railbed of a historic logging railway, where you may notice wolf scat. The railbed rises above the surrounding wetlands and takes the segment to its terminus on CTH-E.

### AREA SERVICES

* **Rib Lake**: See Rib Lake Segment, p. 87. From the Tower Rd. Trail access go west ~10 mi.
* **Medford**: See Pine Line Segment and East Lake Segment, p. 84. From the Tower Rd. Trail access go south and west ~27 mi.
* [Icon: Trail Community logo] **Merrill**: See Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment, p. 105. From the CTH-E Trail access go east then south ~23 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
## Averill-Kelly Creek Wilderness Segment (Atlas Maps 27f, 28f)

**Summary:** The Averill-Kelly Creek Wilderness Segment is a 4.7-mile trail in Lincoln County (Atlas Map 27f, 28f), running from CTH-E to Burma Rd.. This forested, remote segment, which is entirely on private lands, offers many opportunities for wildlife viewing.

### SNAPSHOT

<sup>2</sup> [Icon: 5] **4.7 miles**: CTH-E to Burma Rd.

[Icon: water drop icon] From the New Wood River, Averill Creek, Kelly Creek and a few smaller intermittent streams.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: hunting closure icon] The eastern portion of the segment is closed for hunting October 15 to December 31. A reroute is posted during this time. The entire segment is closed for the traditional 9-day gun deer season in November.

[Icon: logging road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **1 CTH-E Trail Access (45.28793, -89.88895)**: From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi. At STH-107 turn right and go north 13.0 mi. At CTH-E turn left, go across the Wisconsin River and go west then south for 8.1 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

[Icon: 2] **2 Burma Rd. Trail Access (45.30850, -89.82711)**: From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi. At STH-107 turn right and go north 13.0 mi. At CTH-E turn left across the Wisconsin River and go west 2.6 mi. At Burma Rd. turn left and go south 1.1 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park roadside at the Turtle Rock Segment Trail access sign 0.1 mi south on Burma Rd. **DO NOT** park in front of the private road with access gate on the west side of the road.

### THE HIKE

This forested, remote segment, which is entirely on private lands, offers many opportunities for wildlife viewing. Watch for signs of bear activity, wolf scat, and frogs at the three water crossings. After periods of heavy rain, the New Wood River and Averill-Kelly Creek swell, and currents increase, making these water crossings treacherous. For current conditions, check with the Northwoods Chapter coordinator. It is possible to bypass the segment at times of high water and during hunting season when it is closed. The alternative road walk is north and east on CTH-E, then south on Burma Road.

From the Trail access on CTH-E head east through an open canopy of aspen and birch trees regenerating after a timber harvest at the beginning of this millennium. In the first half mile, the area has been intermittently logged off, so pay close attention to the signage. Soon you’ll reach and then parallel the peaceful New Wood River before arriving at a place where you must ford it. The river is wide but generally only ankle- to knee-deep. However, water levels can reach hip-deep or higher. When water levels are high, it is best to take the alternate route (see above).

On the other side of this ford is an open grassy area at an unsigned trail junction. Continue east 300 feet and rock-hop across Averill Creek. After the creek crossing, traverse a sizeable clear-cut timber harvest area regenerating with pioneer plants such as birch, aspen, and cherry, along with blueberry and raspberry bushes, grasses, and shrubs.

![Map of the Turtle Rock Segment showing the trail path, Kelly Creek, Burma Rd., and nearby features like June Lake and New Wood River.](page_136_image_1_v2.jpg)

The segment uses portions of an old railroad grade as it makes its way to Kelly Creek, a trout stream. Cross the creek and turn left. The Trail narrows and heads for the segment’s endpoint on Burma Road. The section between Kelly Creek and Burma Road can be wet and swampy.

Please respect the landowners’ private property: stay on the Trail.

### AREA SERVICES

Tomahawk: See Turtle Rock and Grandfather Falls Segment, p. 105. From Burma Rd. go north, east then north ~17 mi.

[Icon: Trail Community logo] Merrill: See Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment, p. 105. From Burma Rd. go north, east then south ~18 mi.

> *I saw parts of the state I never would have and developed a much better appreciation of its beauty. For the first time ever, I saw porcupines in the wild, a beaver swimming in a river, and numerous bears (one with four cubs!). I loved the many lakes, too.*

> FIONA McTAVISH, THOUSAND-MILER

## Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment (Atlas Map 28f)

**Summary:** The Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment: 1.  **[Icon: 1] Burma Rd. Northern Trail Access (45.30850, -89.82711):** From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi.

### [Icon: 5] Turtle Rock Segment—5.1 miles: Burma Rd. Northern Trail Access to CTH-E

* [Icon: water icon] From the Wisconsin River and other small streams/creeks.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands.
* [Icon: road/trail icon] Segment includes brief roadwalk on Burma Rd. Portions overlap logging/forest roads and multiuse trails open to snowmobiling and cross-country skiing.
* [Icon: hunting season icon] Eastern portion of the segment crossing private land along and near the Wisconsin River is closed during gun deer season.

### [Icon: 3bk] Grandfather Falls Segment—4.0 miles: STH-107 Grandfather Falls Hydro Northern Parking Area to Eastern Terminus of out-and-back section through the Merrill School Forest

* [Icon: water icon] From the Wisconsin River and Ripley Creek.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: camping icon] Campground A at Camp New Wood County Park.
* [Icon: road/trail icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads and gravel access roads.
* [Icon: amenities icons] [Icon: amenities icons] [Icon: amenities icons] [Icon: amenities icons] [Icon: amenities icons] At Camp New Wood County Park. Also, portable toilet at STH-107 Grandfather Falls Hydro northern parking area.
* [Icon: spur trail icon] Unmarked spur trails off the northern portion along the Wisconsin River, the Merrill School Forest trail network and a spur trail crossing the Wisconsin Conservation Corps bridge over Ripley Creek.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1.  **[Icon: 1] Burma Rd. Northern Trail Access (45.30850, -89.82711):** From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west for 3.0 mi. At STH-107 turn right and go north 13.0 mi. At CTH-E turn left across the Wisconsin River and go west 2.6 mi. At Burma Rd. turn left and go south 1.1 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park roadside at the Turtle Rock Segment Trail access sign 0.1 mi south on Burma Rd. *DO NOT park in front of the private road with access gate on the west side of the road.*
2.  **[Icon: 2] CTH-E Trail Access (45.32314, -89.80090):** **Parking:** Across CTH-E from Trail Access.
3.  **[Icon: 3] STH-107 Grandfather Falls Hydro Northern Parking Area Trail Access (45.31417, -89.78310):** **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.
4.  **[Icon: 4] Eastern Terminus of out-and-back section through the Merrill School Forest (45.29271, -89.76845):** From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 west 3.0 mi. At STH-107 turn right and go north 10.2 mi to IAT crossing. The segment endpoint is 1.7 mi east of STH-107 in Merrill School Forest. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Merrill School Forest parking area outside of gated entrance or Camp New Wood County Park day use parking area.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Grandfather Falls Hydro southern parking areas on west side of STH-107. (ii) Wisconsin River canoe and tubing/float craft entry point parking area on west side of STH-107, a short distance south of Grandfather Falls Hydro southern parking area.

![Map of the Turtle Rock Segment area, showing the Wisconsin River, Lincoln County Forest, and connections to the Averill-Kelly Creek Wilderness and Underdown Segments.](page_138_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
## Turtle Rock Segment

**Summary:** The Turtle Rock Segment: This segment includes a diverse, beautiful, and very challenging trek along and near the banks of the Wisconsin River, where the ceremonial Turtle Rock is found.

This segment includes a diverse, beautiful, and very challenging trek along and near the banks of the Wisconsin River, where the ceremonial Turtle Rock is found.

It traverses a portion of the 100,843-acre Lincoln County Forest, established in 1935 with tax-delinquent land. It navigates thickets, isolated forests, and a rocky river shoreline, and it skirts a marsh. It may require a few hours to hike one way. On this lightly used segment, you must watch for blazes and directional arrows to stay on course — bears in the area are famous for knocking down signposts. The DNR maintains several wildlife and game clearings in the area, and the segment is a prime area for bird watching and wildlife viewing. Expect a sudden rise of grouse, curious forest hawks swooping overhead, and the noisy commotion of deer crashing through the undergrowth.

From the Trail access at the endpoint of the Averill-Kelly Creek Wilderness Segment on Burma Road, head south 0.1 miles. Leave Burma Road, head east, and within half a mile, you’ll reach a four-acre DNR wildlife clearing that is a good place for primitive camping. The segment’s first few miles follow wide forest roads through county forest land before transitioning to a narrower, winding path on private land. At times this section shares a snowmobile route.

Stay alert for a sharp left turn off the wider, multiuse trail, and head steeply down into the Wisconsin River valley. As the forest becomes dense near the

river, the tread can be slippery and uneven with forest debris. Use particular caution when climbing over rocks and crossing wet wooden planks. For more than a mile, the segment parallels the river through a scenic, rocky playground – you can even climb out on rocks to watch cascading river water. This side of the river offers the best view of Grandfather Falls. As you pass the west end of Grandfather Falls Dam you’ll see a bench overlooking the river near the start of the next segment. Look carefully between the Trail and the river to spot Turtle Rock, a substantial erratic with a “turtle head” protuberance jutting skyward amongst the forest’s rock gardens.

Curve west and parallel an unnamed creek emptying into the Wisconsin River. Climbing out of the lush Wisconsin River valley, you’ll find a wooden bench that sits 20 feet above the creek on a scenic bank. Near the Trail’s intersection with a forest road is a birch-tree-lined trail corridor.

Cut sharply to the north and quickly cross the creek you’ve been following with a boardwalk and rock hop in front of a beaver dam. Then you’ll arrive at an open area overlooking a beaver pond and wetland. The creek crossing can be tricky when the water is high. Shortly after passing through this area, follow a wide multiuse path to the segment’s endpoint on CTH-E.

*To reach the Grandfather Falls Segment, head east on a connecting route along lightly traveled CTH-E. After 1.3 miles, cross a bridge over the Wisconsin River to STH-107, turn right and hike south 0.8 miles along STH-107.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
## Grandfather Falls Segment

**Summary:** The Grandfather Falls Segment: In 1938, Wisconsin Public Service Corporation commenced a significant project – using the Wisconsin River’s 90-foot drop for power.

In 1938, Wisconsin Public Service Corporation commenced a significant project – using the Wisconsin River’s 90-foot drop for power. On the east side of the river, construction crews built a canal that diverts the water to two intake ports feeding penstocks. The penstocks carry water hundreds of yards downriver from the reservoir. At the bottom, a power plant converts the water into about 17 megawatts of electrical power. Until recent years, the penstocks were made of wood, but high maintenance costs led to replacing the wood with steel. You may walk through a section of wooden penstock displayed alongside the Trail.

Below the dam, you will view unique boulders and sculptured rock formations caused by thousands of years of cascading water. The sound is thunderous; this is the largest waterfall on the Wisconsin River. The rapids continue for about a mile, cascading through an enormous pre-Cambrian rock outcrop – geologic evidence of a former mountain range exposed by weathering and the more recent scraping of glaciers.

From the Trail access at the Grandfather Falls Hydro northern parking area on STH-107, head south past a red gate and cross a bridge over the reservoir’s inlet east of the Wisconsin River. Then head west on a gravel drive to the base of Grandfather Falls Dam and the original red brick hydroelectric plant. Next, you’ll enter the woods and travel south along the river, following a route used by Indigenous people, explorers, missionaries, and countless others over the centuries.

Continue south and then turn east to cross a grassy swath and the reservoir’s floodgate outlet on a bridge. The bridge is engineered to withstand floodwater and replaced several others that washed out over the years. Be mindful of the floodgate warning sirens – sounding once a week before the gates are opened.

Proceed on a short section north of the penstocks and south of the reservoir. Once past the northern end of the huge tubes, turn sharply south and parallel them. Toward the downstream end of the penstocks, continue around the east side of the existing hydroelectric plant before reaching a Wisconsin River canoe and tubing/floatcraft entry point and parking area. Continue south through the camping area of Camp New Wood County Park, the historic site of a 1935 Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) camp named Camp McCord. Then you’ll reach a junction with a spur trail; it heads south over the Wisconsin Conservation Corps bridge crossing Ripley Creek to the Camp New Wood County Park day use area entrance drive. At this junction, turn left and briefly head east along Ripley Creek to the STH-107 crossing.

Cross STH-107 to begin the 1.7-mile (one-way) out-and-back segment portion, which is necessary to cover the entire Ice Age Trail route. This stretch starts by descending and intermittently following a beautiful stretch of Ripley Creek on private Merrill School Forest property. Initially, students planted 2,000 conifer trees. After purchasing an additional 80 acres of land, 60,000 more trees were planted on the property.

The Trail climbs steeply to a meadow above the creek, where Trail conditions and markings may vary when overgrown. It also passes a private education center. This building is closed to the public. DO NOT stop to ask for water or restroom use.

The Trail’s endpoint is near the eastern boundary of the Merrill School Forest property. At the endpoint, turn around and return to the Trail’s crossing of STH-107.

### AREA SERVICES

**Camp New Wood County Park:** Camping. On Trail. Camping for a fee on a first come, first served basis. For info contact the Lincoln County Forestry, Lands & Parks Department (715-539-1034).

**Council Grounds State Park:** Camping. From Camp New Wood County Park on STH-107 go south 8.4 mi on STH-107 to the park entrance drive (715-536-8773, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/councilgrounds; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

[Icon: Trail City logo] **Merrill:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From Camp New Wood County Park go south 10.2 mi on STH-107. For area info contact the Merrill Area Chamber of Commerce (715-536-9474, merrillchamber.org).

**Tomahawk:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From Camp New Wood County Park go north ~17 mi on STH-107. For area info contact the Tomahawk Area Chamber of Commerce (715-453-5334, gototomahawk.com).

## Underdown Segment and Alta Junction Segment (Atlas Maps 29f, 30f)

**Summary:** The Underdown Segment and Alta Junction Segment: **[Icon: 4 3] Underdown Segment—6.3 miles: Horn Lake Rd. to Copper Lake Ave.**

### SNAPSHOT

**[Icon: 4 3] Underdown Segment—6.3 miles: Horn Lake Rd. to Copper Lake Ave.**

* [Icon: water drop] From Dog Lake and Mist Lake.

**[Icon: 3 2] Alta Junction Segment—1.2 miles: CTH-J Southern Trail Access to CTH-J Northern Trail Access**

* [Icon: water drop] From the North Branch of the Prairie River and a small intermittent creek.
* [Icon: dog] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. **[Icon: 1] Horn Lake Rd. Trail Access (45.30226, -89.63080):** From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and CTH-K, take CTH-K north 7.3 mi. At CTH-H turn right and go east 0.5 mi to gravel Horseshoe Lake Dr. As CTH-H turns north, continue east on Horseshoe Lake Dr. for 0.8 mi. At Horn Lake Rd. turn right and go south 1.2 mi. Parking: Roadside for two cars on the Trail access side of the road.
2. **[Icon: 2] Copper Lake Ave. Trail Access (45.33012, -89.56226):** Parking: Roadside.
3. **[Icon: 3] CTH-J Trail Southern Trail Access (45.33779, -89.54874):** No Parking: Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.2 mi north on CTH-J.
4. ****![4](page_141_image_15_v2.jpg) CTH-J Northern Trail Access** (45.35171, -89.55827):** From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take USH-51 north 13.0 mi to Irma. At CTH-J turn right and go east 5.1 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Alta Springs Rd. parking area. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Horn Lake. (ii) Prairie River SFA on CTH-J. (iii) Alta Springs Rd. roadside parking.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
## Underdown Segment

**Summary:** The Underdown Segment: This very hilly and forested segment passes through the popular Underdown Recreation Area and cuts through the primeval “Enchanted Forest,” a beautiful hemlock grove next to a wetland area.

This very hilly and forested segment passes through the popular Underdown Recreation Area and cuts through the primeval “Enchanted Forest,” a beautiful hemlock grove next to a wetland area. The segment’s topography is part of the Harrison Moraine of the Wisconsin Valley lobe. Glacial debris remnants in the form of hummocks separate deep kettles. Some kettles remain lakes, but most are filled with sphagnum peat. Thick forests, including frequent hemlock groves, white pine, mature maples, and white birch, conceal the high-relief hummocky terrain. You may not always see it, but you will feel the climbs and descents.

*Note: Single-track mountain bike trails and horse trails intertwine with the Ice Age Trail along this segment. Pay close attention at intersections.*

From the Trail access on Horn Lake Road, head east, then north. After about one mile, you’ll traverse a thick hemlock grove called the “Enchanted Forest,” where sphagnum moss bogs lie amid the hemlocks and towering white pines. Steep climbs up and down hummocks will greet you as the segment closes in on Mist Lake and reaches a Leopold bench at a high point above the lake. Farther east, another steep ascent leads to a primitive campsite halfway between Mist Lake and Dog Lake. After descending through a mature hardwood forest, you will encounter another hemlock grove wrapping around the northwest side of Dog Lake. On a slight rise facing the lake is a backpacking shelter with a fire ring, bench, and room for a few small tents.

After leaving Dog Lake, you’ll cross several small bridges and Loop Road (open to all vehicles, including ATVs) before entering a forest of mature white birch, home to nesting Cooper’s hawks. Continue past a Leopold bench, through an open forest and a previously logged area, and ascend to the segment’s high point. Volunteers have worked hard to make this section easy to traverse, with hand-built bench cuts carved into the hillside.

*To reach the Alta Junction Segment, head east for 0.7 miles on a connecting route along Copper Lake Avenue, then left on CTH-J, and head north for 0.5 miles.*

![Map of the Underdown Recreation Area within the Lincoln County Forest, showing the Ice Age Trail route passing by Horn Lake, Mist Lake, Dog Lake, and Lokemoen Lake, with various landmarks and connecting routes indicated.](page_142_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::lincoln-county.parent_text:
## Alta Junction Segment

**Summary:** The Alta Junction Segment: For much of its route, this short segment follows a historic railroad grade named for the historic junction of two rail lines at the village of Dunfield (now a ghost town).

For much of its route, this short segment follows a historic railroad grade named for the historic junction of two rail lines at the village of Dunfield (now a ghost town). The segment follows the east side of the North Branch of the Prairie River, an area known for the natural springs that bubble up from underground. The Alta Springs Bottling Company (late 1800s) and Pay Brothers Alta Springs Bottling Company (1930s) bottled and sold the water for medicinal purposes.

From the southern Trail access on CTH-J, cross hummocky terrain, cut through a parking area, and then follow a rail grade built in 1908. Old railroad ties are still visible, the remains of the Milwaukee Road network that mainly serviced the region’s logging industry. The rail lines served the land owned by Sigmund Heinemann and transported timber to the mill town of Heinemann until it burned down in 1910. Trains also hauled freight to the lumber mills in Merrill and Tomahawk until 1931. The segment ends when it again intersects CTH-J near Alta Springs Road.

![Map of the Alta Junction Segment showing the trail route along the North Branch Prairie River, with waypoints 2, 3, and 4, and connections to the Harrison Hills Segment.](page_143_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

*   **Underdown Recreation Area**: Camping. From the Copper Lake Ave. Trail access go west 1.1 mi. For info contact the Lincoln County Forestry, Lands & Parks Department (715-539-1034).
*   [Icon: Service Icon] **Merrill**: See Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment, p. 105. From the Copper Lake Ave. Trail access go west then south ~17 mi.
*   **Gleason**: See Harrison Hills Segment, p. 112. From the CTH-J southern Trail access go south then east ~6 mi.
*   **Tomahawk**: See Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment, p. 105. From the CTH-J northern Trail access go west then north 15.5 mi.

> *After a few days of hiking in near-constant rain... we stopped under some trees for a break. Raindrops were coming down fat and splashy. I noticed small puffball mushrooms on the forest floor. Each mushroom exhaled a puff of spores when a raindrop struck it.*
>
> LIZ LUSK, THOUSAND-MILER

## Harrison Hills Segment (Atlas Maps 30f, 31f)

**Summary:** The Harrison Hills Segment is a 14.5-mile trail in Lincoln County (Atlas Map 30f, 31f), running from CTH-J to First Lake Rd.. This beautiful, remote segment, which generally crosses public forest lands, features roller-coaster topography, plentiful primitive camping options, and long views from the top of Lookout Mountain.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 4 4] **14.5 miles**: CTH-J to First Lake Rd.

* [Icon: water drop icon] From the numerous trailside lakes, a trailside spring near the North Branch of the Prairie River and other small streams/creeks.
* [Icon: shelter icon] At an ATV shelter at the CTH-B Trail access.
* [Icon: dog on leash icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands. Walk-in shelter, walk-in campsites on an unnamed lake, on Chain Lake, and in a meadow.
* [Icon: logging road icon] Portions overlap logging/forest roads and two gravel roads.
* [Icon: group camping icon] At a walk-in campsite      on Bus Lake accessible from Turtle Lake Rd. Large enough for groups.
* [Icon: spur trail icon] Blue-blazed spur trail to Alta Springs Rd. parking area and a spur trail to walk-in campsite on Bus Lake.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

**① CTH-J Trail Access (45.35171, -89.55827)**: [Icon: 1] From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take USH-51 north 13.0 mi to Irma. At CTH-J turn right and go east 5.1 mi.
**No Parking.** Instead, park at the Alta Springs Rd. parking area. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

**② First Lake Rd. Trail Access (45.43370, -89.42516)**: [Icon: 2] From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 east 0.3 mi. At STH-17 turn left and go north-east for 21.9 mi. At First Lake Rd. turn left and go north then west 1.5 mi.
**Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Alta Springs Rd. roadside parking. (ii) Turtle Lake Rd. (iii) CTH-B (adjacent to the main Lincoln County ATV trail system parking area and building). Do not block ATV trail access.

![Map of Harrison Hills Segment showing trail path, lakes (Ebert, Bear, Bus, Camp, Burlock), and access points.](page_144_image_11_v2.jpg)

![Map of the Parrish Hills Segment showing various lakes including Lake Clara, Bass Lake, Long Lake, Seven Island Lake, Stewart Lake, Birch Lake, Snake Lake, Fox Lake, Bear Lake, Chain Lake, Camp Two Lake, Bennett Lake, Fish Lake, Lake Two, and Lake One. The map also shows Lookout Mountain, Moraine Lake, and various roads and trail markers.](page_145_image_1_v2.jpg)

### THE HIKE

This beautiful, remote segment, which generally crosses public forest lands, features roller-coaster topography, plentiful primitive camping options, and long views from the top of Lookout Mountain. It also supports a thriving wolf pack.

When the Wisconsin Valley glacial lobe advanced, there was most likely a period where the lobe surged forward, creating the area’s hilly terrain. Researchers have found that when today’s glaciers rapidly advance, their surfaces gather sediment, especially along the marginal edges. When the ice melts, it leaves behind deep kettles and high-relief hummocks. This effect on the landscape is evident as the segment traverses the Harrison Moraine, noted for its spectacular high-relief topography. The surrounding hummocky terrain, known as the Harrison Hills, includes many glacially deposited ridges and hills, such as Lookout Mountain, the fourth highest point on the Ice Age Trail at 1,920 feet above sea level. Views are awe-inspiring when leaves are off.

While usually easy to follow, the Trail corridor can be narrow in some areas. Watch carefully for Trail signage, especially at intersections and when on logging/forest roads; ongoing timber harvest can make navigation challenging.

Heading northwest from the Trail access at CTH-J, you’ll almost immediately meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading to the Alta Springs Road parking area. Then cross primitive Alta Springs Road. Soon you will reach Alta Springs, a natural spring, the site of two water bottling companies in the late 1800s and 1930s. Enjoy a nearby bench and view of the North Branch of the Prairie River. Follow the river briefly before heading northeast into a forested area dominated by maple, basswood, and oak. It also hosts a network of public ATV trails. Although the segment does not overlap them, ATVs are often seen or heard nearby.

Cross Beaver Trail Road and then Turtle Lake Road. You’ll soon meet a short spur trail leading to a three-sided camping shelter. Continue into a region filled with small, beautiful, undeveloped lakes, where it is common to see beavers swimming at dusk. Portions of the segment in this area may be wet or flooded in early spring or rainy periods. Heading south, wrap around the south end of Bus Lake and meet another spur trail leading north along the western edge of Bus Lake to a walk-in campsite. In another mile, as you head east, yet another short spur trail leads north to a campsite above an unnamed lake. Pass the shores of Duck Lake and a bench in memory of Herb Schotz, a pioneer in developing the Ice Age Trail in Lincoln county.

Travel across high-relief hummocky terrain for about three miles before reaching Lookout Mountain. There are several structures at the top, including two radio towers. Descend gradually east/northeast through a deciduous forest dominated by oak and maple trees. Pass an ATV parking area with vault toilets just before you cross CTH-B.

A popular fishing destination, the northeastern section of the segment passes many lakes, including picturesque Bear Lake. Two angler campsites are on Chain Lake. The first lies along a spur trail between the west and center lobes of the lake – it is approximately 100 feet off the Trail. The second is also reached by a spur trail, between the center and east lobes, and is approximately 400 feet off the Trail. Unfortunately, the second campsite is prone to flooding.

From Chain Lake, continue for about 2.0 miles, passing through a small hemlock stand. Next, climb up past another walk-in campsite in a meadow within sight of the Trail. It has a Leopold bench and a fire ring. From here, the segment quickly reaches its terminus on First Lake Road, right on the Lincoln/Langlade county line.

### AREA SERVICES

**Tomahawk:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-J Trail access go west then north 15.5 mi.

**Gleason:** Restaurant, convenience store, camping. From the CTH-J Trail access, take CTH-J east, south and east 5.6 mi to STH-17. At STH-17, turn left (northeast) and go 1.9 mi. From the CTH-B parking area, take CTH-B east 1.2 mi to STH-17. At STH-17 turn right (southwest) and go 10.0 mi.

**Harrison:** Restaurant. From the CTH-B Trail access parking area go west and north ~7 mi.

T<sub>C</sub> **Merrill:** See Turtle Rock Segment and Grandfather Falls Segment, p. 105. From the First Lake Rd. Trail access go east then south ~24 mi.

**Rhinelander:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the First Lake Rd. Trail access go east and north ~18 mi.

![A hiker with a backpack and trekking poles navigates a trail densely covered with exposed tree roots in a lush forest.](page_147_image_1_v2.jpg)

MATT HELMEID

Grandfather Falls Segment: A hiker navigates a rooted forest path.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segments in Langlade County, Wisconsin, showing the Parrish Hills Segment, Highland Lakes Segment, and Summit Moraine Segment. The map includes landmarks such as Summit Lake, Bogus Swamp, Veterans Memorial County Park, and the city of Antigo. County boundaries for Lincoln, Langlade, and Marathon are indicated, along with various highways including US-45 and State Highways 17, 64, 47, and 52.](page_148_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Langlade County

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Langlade County. The map shows the trail route through the county, highlighting the Lumbercamp Segment and the Kettlebowl Segment. It also shows the Peters Marsh Wildlife Area, the communities of Polar and Langlade, and various roads including Highways 52, 55, 64, and County Road A. A legend indicates the Ice Age Trail, connecting routes, other trails, county boundaries, and public or IATA land. An inset map shows Langlade County's location within Wisconsin. The Ice Age Trail Alliance website, iceagetrail.org, is also listed.](page_149_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Langlade County

*Atlas Maps 31f–39f; Databook pages 31–35*
Total miles: 84.1 (Trail 54.8; Connecting Route 29.2)

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Langlade County](page_150_image_2_v2.jpg)

The Wisconsin Valley, Langlade, and Green Bay lobes shaped the landscape of Langlade County. In places, one can imagine the violent movement of the glacial lobes as they carved and crafted the county’s landscape. These three glacial lobes deposited a large terminal moraine 250 feet high in places when the ice stopped and began to retreat 13,000 to 25,000 years ago. South and west of the end moraine is the broad outwash plain known as the Antigo Flats, named for the city of Antigo, which sits near its center. The town, formerly called Springbrook, was renamed Antigo, a derivation of an Ojibwe phrase *Nequi-Antigo-Seebeh*, which means “Where the river flows past evergreens.” Today it is a proud Ice Age Trail Community. The county seat of the “County of Trails,” Antigo welcomes hikers with its relaxing Northwoods atmosphere. Unwind in Antigo and enjoy shopping, history, and great food.

Langlade County is named for Charles de Langlade, son of a French man and an Ottawa woman, who was a leader in the region during the second half of the 1700s. The county’s glacial topography, kettle lakes, flowing rivers, and towering forests attracted an influx of timber estimators, land speculators, and homestead seekers in the United States’ westward expansion.

From 1860 to 1910, the forests provided the raw material for a thriving but short-sighted lumber industry. High demand and lack of forest management destroyed forests. Logging slash furthered the damage, creating ideal conditions for wildfires. After exhausting the timber supply, companies sold the clear-cut, burned-over land to European immigrants and American pioneers eager for a place to farm. But in just a few years, the soils gave out, and people left to seek their fortunes elsewhere.

The county forest program started in 1928 (the first of its kind in Wisconsin) after local voters approved the establishment of a unit on a large acreage of tax-delinquent, cutover land. Replanting began in earnest with the aid of Civilian Conservation Corps enrollees. Today, the county sustainably manages 130,877 acres for timber revenue, wildlife habitat, and public recreation.

. . .

When enjoying Langlade County Ice Age Trail segments, please keep in mind:

* Logging, a way of life in this area, constantly influences the Ice Age Trail. Before hiking, check Trail Alerts on the Alliance’s website, IceAgeTrail.org, for logging updates affecting wayfinding.

* The Chapter suggests using a compass and topographic map or FarOut, a mobile app featuring GPS-enabled Ice Age Trail maps.
* Yellow metal posts with and without Ice Age Trail signage signify the route through the county. They are used instead of the traditional wooden posts, which are often vandalized (by humans and bears) in the area.
* Primitive camping opportunities are plentiful along the Trail on county forest land. Hikers should set up their campsites at least 200 feet from trails and waterways. In addition, the Summit Moraine Segment now contains a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA).
* For overnight parking locations, contact the Langlade County Chapter Coordinator. And, as a courtesy, notify the county sheriff’s department of where you intend to park.
* Water sources are scarce along the Kettlebowl and Lumbercamp segments.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Langlade County Chapter formed in 1974. Within the first year, the Chapter had mapped and marked five Ice Age Trail segments. Chapter volunteers help maintain the Trail and promote the Trail with service club presentations, staffing information tables at community events, hosting guided hikes, and writing local news articles.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/langlade-county-chapter to learn more about the Chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

Langlade County Forestry Office: 715-627-6300, co.langlade.wi.us/departments/forestry-recreation-parks

Langlade County Sheriff Department: 715-627-6411; contact for overnight parking permission, co.langlade.wi.us

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] Ice Age Trail Community, Antigo: www.langladecounty.org

DAVE CALIEBE

![A wooden boardwalk winds through a lush, green forest with tall trees.](page_151_image_1_v2.jpg)

Summit Moraine Segment: A graceful boardwalk elevates a damp fall hike.

![Map of Parrish Hills Segment showing trail path, lakes (Two Island Lake, Lake Bennett, Lake Two, Lake One, Stevens Pond), roads (First Lake Rd., Pine Rd., Five Cent Rd.), and landmarks like Prairie River Fishery Area.](page_152_image_1_v2.jpg)
