iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.parent_text:
## Parrish Hills Segment

**Summary:** The Parrish Hills Segment is a 12.0-mile trail in Langlade County, running from First Lake Rd. western Trail access to CTH-T. This remote and rugged segment, the oldest in the county, was named after the town of Parrish, located in the northwestern corner of Langlade County.
(Atlas Maps 31f, 32f)

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Distance icon] **12.0 miles**: First Lake Rd. western Trail access to CTH-T

*Note: A reroute is planned immediately south of Five Cent Rd. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

[Icon: Water icon] At an ATV shelter north of Five Cent Rd. (see map icons).

[Icon: Camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands. Camping is not allowed in the immediate vicinity of the ATV shelter north of Five Cent Rd.

[Icon: Picnic icon] Townline Lake wayside.

[Icon: Restroom icon] At an ATV shelter north of Five Cent Rd. (see map icons) and the Townline Lake wayside.

[Icon: Hunting icon] Portion of segment on private land west of Pine Rd until Langlade County Forest is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: Dog on leash icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Shared trail icon] Portions overlap logging roads and ATV and snowmobile trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Parking icon 1] ① **First Lake Rd. Western Trail Access (45.43370, -89.42516)**: From Merrill at the intersection of USH-51 and STH-64, take STH-64 east 0.3 mi. At STH-17 turn left and go northeast for 21.9 mi. At First Lake Rd. turn left and go north then west 1.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: Parking icon 2] ② **CTH-T Trail Access (45.37570, -89.29477)**: From Antigo, at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64, take USH-45/47 north 16.6 mi to Summit Lake. At CTH-T turn left and go west 5.2 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access at Townline Lake wayside.

**Additional Parking**: Pine Rd. (Prairie River SFA), 0.1-mile south of where the IAT departs Pine Rd. to the east.

### THE HIKE

This remote and rugged segment, the oldest in the county, was named after the town of Parrish, located in the northwestern corner of Langlade County. Its namesake is also the Parrish End Moraine, a belt of moraine hills — boulders, sand, and gravel deposited by a massive ice sheet more than 10,000 years ago. The segment traverses the end moraine, winding its way southeast through hilly, hummocky terrain. It features the shoulder of Baldy Hill, a ford of the Prairie River, a scenic ridge with long views, several wetland areas, and scenic Townline Lake. Because area wetlands are extensive, the Trail winds through the landscape taking advantage of beaver dams and narrow, high ridges. Notable for the extent of evergreen forest (spruce, pine, fir, balsam, etc.) it traverses, the segment is hosted by a portion of the 130,877-acre Langlade County Forest.

Several characteristics make this segment potentially challenging to navigate. Wet crossings may require slight detours off-trail depending on water levels, precipitation, and beaver activity. Portions of the Trail may become overgrown, and foliage may cover

![Map of the Parrish Segment showing the trail route, mileage markers, and landmarks like Townline Lake and Highland Lakes Segment connection.](page_153_image_1_v2.jpg)

signage. Logging occurs periodically on the lands through which the segment passes. The Trail intersects or even overlaps logging roads, and ATV and snowmobile trails. The route frequently switches between these roads/trails and single-track tread. Pay close attention to signage and navigate carefully through intersections. Note: The logging roads between Pine Road and Townline Lake are ATV roads and are not maintained for general vehicular traffic. Go slowly and use caution when driving motor vehicles on these roads. Do not park on these roads; use pull-outs along the roads.

From the Trail access at the Lincoln/Langlade county line on First Lake Road, head east to a point where the segment leaves the road on single-track tread. Briefly head north, then east, through forested terrain, passing 1,831-foot Baldy Hill, and then meander to a crossing of STH-17. Use caution when crossing STH-17. In short order cross CTH-H – the highways parallel one another at this location. After crossing CTH-H, you’ll encounter a recently logged/clear-cut area. Hike east through the clear-cut, perpendicular to CTH-H. At the end of the clear-cut, turn right and head south along the edge of it. Continue over part of a glacial outwash fan from the Harrison Moraine. *Note: Portions of the segment here cross private and state lands where primitive camping is not permitted.*

After skirting south and east along a pine grove, meet and follow Pine Road south for a short distance. (There’s a parking area 0.1-mile south of where the segment departs Pine Road.) Then continue off-road southeast to a crossing (ford) of the Prairie River, which has a reasonably firm bottom with water levels typically below the knees. A rocky, vegetation-filled riverbed calls for appropriate footwear. You may sit to rest or change footwear on an old foundation near the ford.

East of the river, much of the route traverses high-relief hummocky topography, bringing you to a small wooden shelter in a grassy opening along a logging road. Cross the road and continue east. Soon, the Trail intersects another logging road and briefly follows it north. Depart the logging road and continue east until the Trail intersects yet another logging road. (For a side trip to an ATV shelter with drinking water, pit toilets, and a fire ring, follow this logging road south 0.3 miles.)

Cross the logging road to stay on the Trail and continue heading east. Soon, you’ll bend south to an intersection with primitive Five Cent Road. Turn right and follow it west for 0.1 miles before departing Five Cent Road and heading south on an old logging road. (For another side trip back to the ATV shelter: you can walk 0.2 miles farther west along Five Cent Road, then north on a logging road 0.2 miles to reach the ATV shelter.)

Back on Trail, follow the logging road, watching for signage. Continue winding along a series of ridges and valleys. You will intersect Nelson Firelane at a large “you-are-here” sign. Cross Nelson Firelane and eventually join snowmobile and ATV trails. Continue on and off these trails through extensive wetlands and logged clear-cut areas now becoming meadows. Stay alert for motorized traffic.

Follow the northeast edge of scenic Townline Lake along the base of a hill through a hemlock stand. Townline Lake is one of the county’s two trout lakes. Depending on the season, you might see loons. You may also note a stone bench, fire pit, and stairs built by the Civilian Conservation Corps in the 1930s just before the picnic area. The segment ends on CTH-T at the easternmost Townline Lake day-use picnic area. This spot features several picnic tables, grills, a fire ring, and a privy about 50 yards up the Trail into the woods. Another less developed day-use area is a short walk west along the road. Swimming is possible in both areas.

### AREA SERVICES

**Parrish:** Restaurant (tavern). From the STH-17 Trail access go north 0.1 mi.

**Rhinelander:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the STH-17 Trail access go north 15.5 mi. Area info available from the Rhinelander Chamber of Commerce (715-365-7464, explorerhinelander.com).

**Gleason:** Restaurant, convenience store, camping. From the STH-17 Trail access go south 10.5 mi.

**Merrill:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From STH-17 Trail access go south ~25 mi.

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Antigo:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service.

![A man and two women hiking through a river.](page_155_image_1_v2.jpg)
BRAD ALLEN

*Parrish Hills Segment: A smiling trio gamely fords the Prairie River.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.parent_text:
## Highland Lakes Segment (Atlas Maps 32f, 33f)

**Summary:** The Highland Lakes Segment is a 12.8-mile trail in Langlade County (Atlas Map 32f, 33f), running from CTH-T to CTH-B at snowmobile trail (old railroad grade).. This segment is named for the abundance of lakes located in the area that serve as the headwaters for the Hunting, Eau Claire, and Prairie rivers and features the Parrish Terminal Moraine.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage icon] **12.8 miles (8.2 IAT, 4.6 CR):** CTH-T to CTH-B at snowmobile trail (old railroad grade).

[Icon: water icon] From the West Branch of the Eau Claire River, Deep Wood, Alga, Susan and Alta lakes.

[Icon: park icon] At Townline Lake wayside and Summit Lake Park on USH-45 (see Area Services).

[Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping [Icon: dispersed camping icon] on county forest lands.

[Icon: hunting icon] Portion of segment crossing private land between two gates ((2.5 mi northwest of Kleever Rd.) is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

Segment includes a connecting route roadwalk. Significant portions overlap county forest logging roads open to logging trucks, snowmobile and ATV trails and Camp Susan’s entrance drive.

[Icon: nature trail icon] Camp Susan’s nature trail.

![wheelchair icon](page_156_image_5_v2.jpg) Portions of this segment along Camp Susan’s entrance drive may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **CTH-T Trail Access (45.37570, -89.29477):** From Antigo, at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64, take USH-45/47 north 16.6 mi to Summit Lake. At CTH-T turn left and go west 5.2 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access at Townline Lake wayside.

② **CTH-B Trail Access (45.34934, -89.17394):** From Antigo at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64, take USH-45/STH-47 north 12.1 mi. At Koepenick Rd. turn left (CTH-J turns to the right) and go west 0.8 mi. At CTH-B turn right and go north 1.0 mi to a snowmobile crossing. **No Parking.** Instead, park roadside along CTH-B at Camp Susan’s gated entrance drive, 0.1 mi north on CTH-B. Parking permitted on the side of the access road. Do not block the gate!

**Additional Parking:** (i) Kleever Rd. (Sucker Rd.) roadside parking. (ii) Deep Woods Lake wayside located on Forest Rd. 0.1 mi north of the Trail access.

### THE HIKE

This segment is named for the abundance of lakes located in the area that serve as the headwaters for the Hunting, Eau Claire, and Prairie rivers and features the Parrish Terminal Moraine. The segment passes through a portion of the 130,877-acre Langlade County Forest.

The first section, between CTH-T and the crossing of the West Branch of the Eau Claire River, traverses the Parrish Terminal Moraine’s high-relief hummocky topography. East of the river, the segment follows the base of the highest outer moraine ridge on the entire Ice Age Trail.

You’ll also pass Bogus Swamp, a unique 870-acre State Natural Area. This wetlands complex includes a variety of northern wetland types, none of which are swamp. The swamp, a large kettle filled with peat, is home to several rare plants, birds, and butterflies. A former glacial lake, the wetland formed behind the Parrish Terminal Moraine. Over thousands of years, soil and organic materials from the surrounding landscape filled the shallow basin, creating today’s swamp. All lakes eventually become wetlands given a millennium or more.

From the Trail access on CTH-T, climb a ridge. During leaf-off season, you’ll enjoy a view of the Parrish Moraine and more distant Green Bay Moraine. This section has several intersecting logging roads; watch carefully for blazes and directional arrows.

Cross the West Branch of the Eau Claire River. The crossing can be tricky in high water, but river rocks can be used as stepping stones when water is low. For drier passage, head upstream to find a narrower river channel, a trek that may be difficult and require bushwhacking through thick underbrush.

Just beyond the river crossing, the segment reaches “Five Points,” a former rail line junction where five fire lanes or logging roads meet. Eastbound hikers should follow the distinct logging road headed southeast – the second lane/ road to the left or clockwise. Westbound hikers should follow the logging road headed northwest. Do not follow ATV trail signs, especially those with yellow markings. (For a short side trip: take the logging road headed northeast. It offers a view of the West Branch of the Eau Claire River, which bisects the Parrish Terminal Moraine. Massive volumes of glacial meltwater, now represented by the tame West Branch, cut through the moraine.)

From Five Points, continue southeast through a mix of county forest and private land along an unimproved forest lane. Go around a gate on the east edge of county forest land. Just past a second gate, a logging road enters from the right; bear left to stay on the Trail. Continuing south, you’ll reach the west end of Kleever Road.

To reach the segment’s next portion of blazed Trail, follow a connecting route: turn left on Kleever Road and head east half a mile to Forest Road. Turn left and go north and east 4.2 miles on Forest Road to the Trail access on the right. Along the way, you’ll pass Bogus Swamp, the unique wetlands complex mentioned above.

Depart Forest Road and head east a short distance on a wide logging road before making a sharp right turn at an intersection with another logging road. Shortly, you’ll make a quick left turn onto a wide-track forest road and head generally southeast through the woods. Soon you’ll see Alta Lake to the south.

Make another sharp left turn onto a single-track tread to connect with Camp Susan’s nature trails network with its many informational/educational signs. Then, follow a rolling ridge – dividing the area’s lakes and wetland – created by rocky material sloughed off from the ice masses that formed the area’s kettle lakes. The elevated view highlights Lake Susan glittering through white birch and pine forests.

Leave the footpath and drop down to connect with Camp Susan’s entrance drive. Continue east on the drive through high-relief hummocky topography. Notice erratics and some large conifers as you approach CTH-B. Enjoy a fine view of Alta Lake as the Trail skirts the north edge of a peat bog along the way. At CTH-B, turn right and proceed south along its west side. The segment’s terminus is a short distance away, where a snowmobile trail crosses the road.

![Map of the Parrish Hills Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through Langlade County Forest, past numerous lakes including Townline, Wolf, Partridge, and Sucker Lakes. The map includes mileage markers, road names like Kleever Rd and Bogus Rd, and a scale bar.](page_158_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Summit Lake:** Restaurant, convenience store. From the Camp Susan entrance road and CTH-B, take CTH-B north for 2.5 mi. Summit Lake Park (antigochamber.com) on the west side of USH-45 has swimming beach, picnic and playground area and pit toilets.

**Elcho:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, medical service. From the Camp Susan entrance road and CTH-B, take CTH-B and USH-45/STH-47 north for 6.5 mi.

![Map of the Summit Moraine Segment area showing various lakes including Birch Lake, Greater Bass Lake, Alta Lake, and Susan Lake, as well as the Bogus Swamp State Natural Area and the trail route.](page_159_image_1_v2.jpg)

**Veterans Memorial Park:** Camping. See Summit Moraine Segment, p. 128. From CTH-B Trail access, go south 1.0 mi. At Koepenick Rd. (becomes CTH-J), go east 3.3 mi. At Park Rd. go south 0.2 mi to park entrance.

[Icon: Antigo city logo] **Antigo:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From CTH-B Trail access, go south, east and south ~ 15 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.parent_text:
## Summit Moraine Segment (Atlas Maps 33f, 34f)

**Summary:** The Summit Moraine Segment is a 12.4-mile trail in Langlade County (Atlas Map 33f, 34f), running from CTH-B at snowmobile trail (old railroad grade) to CTH-A. Traveling near the outer edge of the Summit Lake Moraine and through portions of the 130,877-acre Langlade County Forest, this varied segment highlights the Jack Lake Ski Trails, Veterans Memorial Park, and several scenic and remote lakes.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: segment icons] **12.4 miles**: CTH-B at snowmobile trail (old railroad grade) to CTH-A

*   ![water and facilities icons](page_160_image_5_v2.jpg) [Icon: toilet icon] At Veterans Memorial Park. Portable toilet also available near the Jack Lake Ski Trails shelters.
*   [Icon: hunting icon] Portion of segment crossing private land between CTH-B and Langlade County Forest (just east of USH-45/STH-47) is closed during gun deer season.
*   [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
*   [Icon: water icon] From Game Lake, Upper Ventor Lake and several other lakes and ponds in the area.
*   [Icon: road/trail overlap icon] Portions overlap roads, Veterans Park campground road, logging/forest roads, snowmobile and ski trails.
*   [Icon: camping icons] Primitive camping on county forest lakes [Icon: dispersed camping icon], walk-in site [Icon: camping icon] on Game Lake and a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: dispersed camping icon] on Upper Ventor Lake.
*   Segment intersects Jack Lake Ski Trails and the Veterans Memorial Park trail network including Game Lake Nature Trail.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **CTH-B Trail Access (45.34934, -89.17394)**: From Antigo at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64, take USH-45/STH-47 north 12.1 mi. At Koepenick Rd. turn left (CTH-J turns to the right) and go west 0.8 mi. At CTH-B turn right and go north 1.0 mi to a snowmobile crossing. **No Parking**. Instead, park roadside along CTH-B at Camp Susan’s gated entrance drive, 0.1 mi north on CTH-B. Parking permitted on the side of the access road. Do not block the gate!

[Icon: 2] **CTH-A Trail Access (45.30762, -89.05074)**: From Antigo at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64, take USH-45/STH-47 north 3.5 mi. At CTH-A turn right and go northeast 8.6 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at the Peters Marsh Wildlife Area parking area 0.1 mi south on CTH-A. A 200-ft spur trail leads to the IAT.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Jack Lake Cross-Country Ski Area on CTH-J. (ii) Veterans Memorial Park (see Area Services). (iii) Pence Lake Rd.

### THE HIKE

Traveling near the outer edge of the Summit Lake Moraine and through portions of the 130,877-acre Langlade County Forest, this varied segment highlights the Jack Lake Ski Trails, Veterans Memorial Park, and several scenic and remote lakes.

You will pass through yellow birch, tamarack, and balsam in lowland bogs/wetlands and paper birch, maple, and aspen in second-growth hardwood forests, which grew after the great white pines were logged.

The area’s railroads, initially built in the 1850s, hauled logs from the extensive timber harvest areas to the main line at Koepenick Junction during the county’s peak logging period. These lines also carried materials, supplies, and passengers until abandoned in the early 1980s.

From the Trail access on CTH-B, follow a snowmobile trail up onto an old railroad grade and then turn south for 0.2 miles. Leave the railroad grade to the east and travel through forested, relatively flat terrain. This section is prone to flooding during wet periods, and water may extend across the Trail for

several feet. A metal gate and sign mark the segment’s approach to busy USH-45/STH-47. Cross the highway with caution.

Continue east through mixed forest, cross a powerline right-of-way, and traverse an old grove of towering red pines. Enter the Jack Lake Ski Trails area, where the Trail joins wide ski trails and is relatively flat to rolling. Even with the numerous ski trail intersections, the segment is well-marked and easy to follow. You’ll arrive at a cross-country ski trail warming shelter with a table, chairs, and a fireplace. A portable toilet sits outside. After that, the Trail mostly follows ski trails as it makes its way to the Spychalla Lodge (day use only), a wonderfully quaint wood shelter with table, chairs, and a fireplace with a cooking grate. The lodge is at the trail access for the Jack Lake Ski Trails. The parking area serves the Ice Age Trail, the ski trails, and an ATV/snowmobile trail. From the parking area, continue south on CTH-J (Park Road) for 0.5 miles to the entrance of Veterans Memorial Park.

Meander through the park, which highlights Jack Lake. The park offers a campground, non-motorized boat ramp, picnic shelters, walk-in campsites, beach, showers, water, three cabins, and mountain bike, snowshoe, and nature trails. Yellow blazes and signposts will easily guide you along the campground road, onto shared trails, and then north and east out of the campground. Just outside the campground, enter the Langlade County Arboretum, offering interpretive information about 500 native trees and shrubs.

Leaving the arboretum, the Trail runs alongside a small bog lake and hemlock grove as it nears Game Lake and soon meets the Game Lake Nature Trail. A left turn leads to a spur trail to a walk-in campsite (reservable and fee required – see Area Services) with a fire ring, privy, two picnic tables, and room for four tents. To stay on the Trail, turn right at the intersection and follow the Game Lake Nature Trail, skirting Game Lake on the south side. Pass through spruce stands and groves of black maple and birch trees while reading interpretive signs describing the area’s wildlife. The Trail in this section is twisty and rocky, and low-lying areas may be muddy.

There are several lakes in the area, but Game Lake is one of four (along with High, Low, and Jack) reported to have been named by card-playing loggers. Look for signs of beaver feeding, beaver lodges, and a floating bog that migrates over the lake’s surface depending on the prevailing wind direction. Also watch for birds, bog laurel, leatherleaf, Labrador tea, pitcher plants, and other unique wetland flora and fauna.

On the southeast end of Game Lake, turn right and climb a steep hill in a southeasterly direction. The Trail turns, twists, and climbs up and down before reaching a Leopold bench at the south end of a narrow-necked pond. From here, follow a mix of wide-track tread and forest roads through mature stands of aspen and a younger managed forest before reaching Pence Lake Road.

East of Pence Lake Road, wind through hummocky topography across skillfully constructed tread. MSC volunteers completed several projects along this Trail section, including two boardwalks and stone steps. You’ll follow narrow footpaths and wide forest roads as you pass through multi-aged mixed forest. Also, notice exposed multicolored glacial drift stones and erratics. You

![Map of the Highland Lakes Segment showing Jack Lake Ski Trails, Noboken Lake, Spychalla Lodge, Pence Lake Rd., Jack Lake, Arboretum, Veterans Memorial Park, Game Lake, Langlade County Forest, High Lake, and Low Lake.](page_162_image_1_v2.jpg)

may also see grouse, wild turkey coveys, woodcock, deer, bear, and wolves. The Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources maintains several wildlife openings for wildlife management that are noticeable along the Trail corridor. Swallowtail and mourning cloak butterflies frequent these sunny areas filled with milkweed, blue beebalm, butterfly weed, and blue asters.

Shortly beyond Pence Lake Road, you’ll reach an unnamed scenic lake with a bench overlooking it. Continuing mainly on forest roads in rolling terrain, you’ll eventually follow a narrow footpath up a hill on a series of wonderfully crafted stone steps. At Summit Knob, the segment is atop a portion of the outer edge of the Summit Lake Moraine. Enjoy a bench and interpretive marker highlighting this glacial feature. Reaching Upper Ventor Lake, you’ll soon pass a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers in a beautiful hemlock stand. Continue south, passing a large white pine and then skirting the edge of private property along Lower Ventor Lake. Parallel CTH-A southwest, cross a private driveway, duck north toward Ventor Lake, then head back out to the segment’s endpoint at CTH-A.

![Map of the Lumbercamp Segment of the Ice Age National Scenic Trail showing Veterans Memorial Park, Jack Lake, Game Lake, Pence Lake, and Ventor Lake.](page_163_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Summit Lake:** Restaurant, convenience store. From the USH-45/STH-47 Trail access go north 2.5 mi.

**Elcho:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, medical service. From the USH-45/STH-47 Trail access go north ~7 mi.

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Antigo**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the USH-45/STH-47 Trail crossing go south 13.0 mi on USH-45/STH-47. Most services on USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64. Area info available from the Antigo Chamber of Commerce (888-526-4523, antigochamber.com).

**Veterans Memorial Park (Jack Lake Campground)**: Camping. On Trail. (N8375 Park Rd., Deerbrook, 715-623-6214, jacklakecampground.com). The County Park has a campground, walk-in campsites, three rental cabins, showers, beach, hiking, mountain biking and snowshoe trails. All campsites, including the Game Lake campsite, can be reserved and require a fee. Reserve: Memorial Day weekend to mid-October. From the north side of Antigo at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 with STH-64, take USH-45/STH-47 north 12.1 mi to CTH-J. At CTH-J turn right and continue east then south 2.6 mi as CTH-J becomes Park Rd. to the park’s entrance.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.parent_text:
## Lumbercamp Segment (Atlas Maps 34f, 35f)

**Summary:** The Lumbercamp Segment: This segment features the 1,687-acre Peters Marsh State Wildlife Area, containing a network of other hiking and horse trails and a variety of wildlife habitats home to numerous species.

### SNAPSHOT

2 4 12.0 miles: CTH-A to STH-52

[Icon: No reliable sources of water icon]
No reliable sources of water.

[Icon: Primitive camping icon]
Primitive camping on county forest lands.

[Icon: Dogs leashed icon]
Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.

[Icon: Snowmobile trail icon]
A significant portion of the segment overlaps snowmobile trails and logging/forest roads.

[Icon: Wildlife area trail icon]
Trails in the Peters Marsh State Wildlife Area.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① CTH-A Trail Access (45.30762, -89.05074): From Antigo at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64, take USH-45/STH-47 north 3.5 mi. At CTH-A turn right and go northeast 8.6 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Peters Marsh Wildlife Area parking area 0.1 mi south on CTH-A.

② STH-52 Trail Access (45.27672, -88.90267): From Antigo at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-64, take STH-64 east 1.6 mi. At STH-52 turn left and go northeast 15.0 mi. to Kettlebowl Ski Area. **Parking:** Roadside between the gate for Kettlebowl Ski Area and STH-52. Do not block the gate! **Additional Parking:** CTH-S.

![Map of the Lumbercamp Segment showing the trail route through Peters Marsh State Wildlife Area, passing Peters Lake and Fischer Lake, with road access points at CTH-A, Hill Rd, and CTH-S.](page_164_image_1_v2.jpg)

### THE HIKE

This segment features the 1,687-acre Peters Marsh State Wildlife Area, containing a network of other hiking and horse trails and a variety of wildlife habitats home to numerous species. Spring wildflowers include spring beauty, Dutchman’s breeches, yellow trout lily, trillium, hepatica, and bloodroot.

The segment also travels through portions of the 130,877-acre Langlade County Forest, where the remains of the Norem Lumber Camp are a visible reminder of the region’s logging history. From the 1920s until 1938, the camp contained several log structures, including a bunkhouse, hayshed, stable, community kitchen, and mess hall. Unfortunately, all that remain are a root cellar and several log foundations. The once-renovated root cellar, affectionately called the “Hillbilly Hilton,” used to offer hikers shelter. However, it’s fallen into disrepair. (This structure is not sponsored or maintained by the Langlade County Chapter.)

Peters Lake and Fischer Lake are subject to seasonal groundwater fluctuations and are known to go dry; they are not dependable water sources. (The water of Baker Lake, in the dramatic Baker Lake basin at the east end of the segment, contains significant amounts of sediment.)

From the Trail access on CTH-A, head generally southeast through a portion of the Peters Marsh State Wildlife Area. East of CTH-S, the landscape transitions from open marshland to more forested land cover. For most of the remainder of the segment, you’ll travel across the relatively flat surface of a pitted outwash plain deposited by streams flowing away from the Summit Lake

![Map of the Kettlebowl Segment showing Augustyn Springs, Old Lumber Camp, Baker Lake, and the trail route through Langlade County Forest.](page_165_image_1_v2.jpg)

Moraine. Logging can occur along this segment, and there are several intersecting logging roads; pay close attention to signage. The remains of Norem Lumber Camp are in a trailside clearing just west of a primitive road that becomes Otto Mauk Road to the south.

Continue east from the primitive road. A short distance west of Baker Lake, enter the high-relief hummocky topography representing the front of the Summit Lake Moraine. This portion of the segment is well-marked but rocky and challenging. As the Trail reaches Baker Lake’s west end, you will notice a wooden slide for getting small boats and canoes down to the water.

As you head through the Baker Lake basin toward the segment’s endpoint on STH-52, you will pass a sign for “Stoney Hill.” It points to a dramatic exposure of massive boulders deposited by the ice on the north slope of the Summit Lake Moraine.

### AREA SERVICES

**Veterans Memorial Park:** Camping. See Summit Moraine Segment, p. 128. From the CTH-A Trail access go north and west ~7 mi.

[Icon: Antigo logo] **Antigo:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the STH-52 Trail access go south and west ~17 mi.

![Hikers on the Kettlebowl Segment](page_166_image_1_v2.jpg)
KRIS VAN HANDEL

*Kettlebowl Segment: Hikers immersed in bright blue and vivid green, classic summer hues.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::langlade-count.parent_text:
## Kettlebowl Segment (Atlas Maps 35f, 36f)

**Summary:** The Kettlebowl Segment is a 10.2-mile trail in Langlade County (Atlas Map 35f, 36f), running from STH-52 to Sherry Rd.. This rugged and isolated segment winds through hilly terrain dotted with kettles and huge granite erratics and has the most topographical relief of all segments in Langlade County.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3] [Icon: 4] **10.2 miles**: STH-52 to Sherry Rd.

***Note:*** *New Ice Age Trail and a parking area is planned south of STH-64 in IATA’s Moraine-Outwash Preserve, approximately 4 miles east of Antigo. New IAT and a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) is planned west of Old 26 Rd. in IATA’s Ice Override Preserve, approximately 3 miles south of Antigo. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

[Icon: water icon] No reliable sources of water.

[Icon: camping icon] Primitive camping on county forest lands.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

Portions overlap cross-country ski trails. A significant portion overlaps logging/forest roads; some of these are frequently used by ATVs and snowmobiles.

[Icon: spur trail icon] Spur trails to the Big Stone Hole and Kent Hill.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **① STH-52 Trail Access (45.27672, -88.90267):** From Antigo at the intersection of USH45/STH-47 and STH-64, take STH-64 east 1.6 mi. At STH-52 turn left and go northeast 15.0 mi. to Kettlebowl Ski Area. **Parking**: Roadside between the gate for Kettlebowl Ski Area and STH-52. Do not block the gate!

[Icon: 2] **② Sherry Rd. Trail Access (45.18269, -88.91842):** From Antigo at the intersection of USH45/STH-47 and STH-64, take STH-64 east 11.8 mi. At Sherry Rd., turn left and go north and west 1.6 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the ATV parking area 0.3 mi east on Sherry Rd.

### THE HIKE

This rugged and isolated segment winds through hilly terrain dotted with kettles and huge granite erratics and has the most topographical relief of all segments in Langlade County.

The Trail travels through portions of the 130,877-acre Langlade County Forest’s glacially formed topography. It highlights deep “frost pockets,” a local expression for depressions with steeply sloping sides. In some cases, they may represent “kettles,” “kettle holes,” or “kettlebowls” formed by remnants of enormous blocks of ice that broke from the ice sheet. The timber or tree line observed in the frost pockets is inverted – the reverse of the phenomenon seen on many mountaintops. Because heavier, cooler air prevails at the lower elevations in the pockets, the lower levels usually lack woody vegetation. These areas were more pronounced in years past. However, with warmer year-round temperatures, woody species have started to populate the once distinctive grassy bottoms.

The segment follows logging and forest roads used by and signed for multiple users, including Ice Age Trail hikers, snowmobilers, skiers, and ATV users. There are numerous intersections with logging and forest roads. Segment signage can be obscure during growing seasons and is sparse in some areas, so pay careful attention. Trail signage consists of yellow blazes, black block arrows on yellow signs, and yellow and black signs with the Ice Age Trail logo. Carry a map and a compass, or use FarOut, a mobile app featuring GPS-enabled Ice Age Trail maps.

From the Trail access on STH-52, the segment follows the Kettlebowl Ski Area access road southeast to the base of the ski slopes. Not located in a kettle, the Kettlebowl Ski Area’s ski runs use the north slope of the recessional

Summit Lake Moraine. Depart the Ski Area at a yellow marker near the bottom of the southernmost ski run.

Cross primitive private Kent Fire Tower Road. A quarter mile later, you may notice a frost pocket as described above, and large rocks, often Wolf River granite, line both sides of the Trail. As you continue east and south, you’ll meet a short, unmaintained spur trail. (Take it for a side trip to “Big Stone Hole,” a large kettle loaded with granite erratics; use caution if you follow it.) Continuing south, you’ll meet a logging lane marked by a yellow metal post without a sign. (Take it 0.8 miles east for another side trip to experience Kent Hill, the highest point in Langlade County. Kent Hill is just 50 feet below Timm’s Hill, the state’s highest point, and 45 feet lower than the peak at Rib Mountain. The vista is tremendous during leaf-off. Views to the southwest include distant Rib Mountain. Visible to the northeast, across the Wolf River valley, are McCaslin and Thunder mountains in Oconto and Marinette counties.)

Back on Trail, continue south and west through the high-relief hummocky topography of the terminal Parrish Moraine.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail showing the Lumbercamp Segment, Kettlebowl Ski Area, Kent Hill, and various frost pockets within the Langlade County Forest. Baker Lake, 1, 0.4, Lumbercamp Segment, Kettlebowl Ski Area, 52, Kent Fire Tower Rd. (private road), Kent Rd. (primitive road), 0.3, Frost Pocket, 1.1, Big Stone Hole, Kent Hill, 1.0, 1.1, Burma Rd., 0.9, 1.5, Frost Pocket, 0.5, 0, 1/2, 1, miles, Langlade County Forest.](page_168_image_2_v2.jpg)

![Detailed map of a trail segment showing frost pockets, Old Barn Foundation, and connections to Sherry Rd. and Oak Rd., leading towards the Plover River Segment. Frost Pocket, 0.5, 24.7-mi CR to Plover River Segment, 1.7, Old Barn Foundation, 0.8, 2, P, Sherry Rd., Oak Rd.](page_168_image_1_v2.jpg)

The Trail runs roughly parallel to the recessional Summit Lake Moraine in this area. At primitive Burma Road, you’ll enter an active logging and ATV area. Bend west to follow Burma Road before departing from the road and continuing south.

As the segment departs Burma Road, it travels along logging roads skirting a decade-old clear-cut. This area has grown into an early successional forest of young aspen, birch, and cherry trees, grasses, shrubs, and other vegetation. Forest succession – the process in which vegetation establishes and progresses over time – occurs following a disturbance like wind, fire, or timber harvest. Renewal begins as grasses initially populate the site, followed by shrub species, and, over time, trees start to establish. Deer, bear, grouse, turkey, and porcupines inhabit the area.

Farther on, the segment cuts through another frost pocket where a few trees grow, but you’ll mostly move through a sea of grass, which gets long in the summer. About half a mile later, the Trail skirts yet another frost pocket shortly before turning south. As you approach Oak Road/Sherry Road, turn sharply south to follow a former railroad grade on the east side of Oak Road/Sherry Road. You may notice some railroad ties underfoot. Besides the timber and lumber camp supplies carried along this track, stock cars transporting sheep also used it during the 1920s and early 1930s. Continue to the segment’s terminus on Sherry Road.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Antigo**: Summit Moraine Segment, p. 128. From the STH-52 Trail access go south and west ~17 mi or from the Sherry Rd. Trail access go west and south ~13 mi.

Kettlebowl Ski Area: On Trail.

Polar: Restaurant. From the Sherry Rd. Trail access go east, south and west 5.6 mi. Mueller Lake Town Park at Polar Rd. and STH-64 has a boat ramp, swimming beach, and picnic area.

> *I was awed, and sometimes moved to tears, by the beauty, diversity of environments, and opportunity to see my home (Wisconsin) in ways I had never seen it before. Sometimes a single tree, or flower, or creature would stop me in my tracks and humble me with its will to survive. It gave me the strength to keep walking when my body ached with pain. The rewards were glorious: I watched mated swans, beavers with their industrious lives, ferns unrolling in the spring, thunderstorms building and filling the sky with their power and majesty.*

THERESA JANSEN, THOUSAND-MILER

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Marathon County. The map shows the trail route through the county, highlighting the Plover River Segment, Dells of the Eau Claire Segment, Thornapple Creek Segment, and Ringle Segment. It also shows major roads like Highway 29, 39, 51, and 45, and locations such as Wausau, Weston, Rib Mountain State Park, and Dells of the Eau Claire County Park. An inset map shows Marathon County's location within Wisconsin. The map includes labels for the Ice Age Trail Alliance website (iceagetrail.org) and a compass rose.](page_170_image_1_v2.jpg)

![Map of Marathon County showing the Ice Age Trail segments. The map highlights the Ringle Segment and White Cedar Segment. It also shows the Mountain Bay State Trail, connecting routes, and other trails. Major roads such as WI-29, WI-153, WI-49, and I-39/US-51 are marked. A legend identifies the Ice Age Trail, unmarked connecting routes, other trails, county boundaries, and public or IATA land. A scale bar indicates a distance of 0 to 5 miles. The map also shows the boundary between Marathon and Portage counties.](page_171_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.parent_text:
# Marathon County

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Marathon County](page_172_image_2_v2.jpg)

Atlas Maps 39f–44f; Databook pages 37–39

**Total miles:** 43.7 (**Trail** 20.9; **Connecting Route** 22.8)

The glacial deposits in Marathon County vary in age and time periods. The eastern part of the county is where two lobes abutted, the Green Bay Lobe and the Langlade Lobe. Glacial deposits in central and western Marathon County are from an earlier glaciation. The Ice Age Trail follows the Hancock Moraine, an end moraine of the Green Bay Lobe, for several miles north of the town of Ringle, before dipping into the previously glaciated landscape.

The main feature of the Trail west of the moraine is its walk along the Eau Claire River (French for “clear water”) and the Dells of the Eau Claire. The Dells of the Eau Claire are metamorphic rock bluffs carved when ice from the Green Bay and Langlade lobes thawed, and the rush of a tremendous amount of meltwater found a fault in the bedrock. The mylonite bedrock, dated at about 1.8 billion years old, is similar in strength to granite, although it looks more like sandstone. This same bedrock type is also found along the Wisconsin River’s Grandfather Falls in Lincoln County. Today, the Dells of the Eau Claire County Park features a 65-foot-deep gorge with water cascading into a series of pools formed among the rock ledges.

The once vast forests of Marathon County attracted the interest of businessmen in a newly formed and rapidly developing United States. The lumber industry took full advantage of the area’s lush pine forests and the Wisconsin River flowing through them. The river became a major artery, transporting logs downstream, as sawmills sprang up at desirable sites along it. Logging operations began by cutting the more accessible timber along the river and its tributaries. By 1847, 24 mills on the river ran 45 “saws.” Just six years later, this number had increased to more than 100.

The Dells of the Eau Claire County Park contains several structures built by the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) in the 1930s. The park offers several individual camping sites as well as a group camping site.

The Ringle and White Cedar segments offer Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Central Moraines Chapter, formed in the early 1970s, created the county’s first six miles of Ice Age Trail in 1973: the Ringle Segment. Three years later,

volunteers built six more miles of Trail: the Thornapple Creek and Dells of the Eau Claire segments. This momentum led to numerous MSC events dedicated to crafting the Plover River Segment. Following these successes, the Chapter helped lead MSC efforts to reroute the Ringle Segment, away from multiuse trails and onto land dedicated to the Ice Age Trail. In addition to Trailbuilding, the Chapter promotes the use of the Trail for recreation and education. It hosts several trail improvement days and hikes throughout the year, including a snowshoe hike, a spring wildflower hike, and a fall color hike.

The Chapter’s hiking award program, Meandering the Central Moraines, offers hikers a patch and certificate to those who hike all Ice Age Trail miles in the county. Start your Meandering adventure by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/central-moraines-chapter to learn more about the Chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Marathon County Visitor Information:** 715-261-1000, co.marathon.wi.us

**Wausau/Central Wisconsin Convention & Visitors Bureau:** 888-948-4748 or 715-355-8788, visitwausau.com

![Plover River Segment: A peaceful view rewards hikers.](page_173_image_1_v2.jpg)
DAVID PEREZ

Plover River Segment: A peaceful view rewards hikers.

## Plover River Segment (Atlas Map 39f)

**Summary:** The Plover River Segment is a 5.9-mile trail in Marathon County (Atlas Map 39f), running from CTH-HH to Sportsman Dr.. This beautiful segment highlights a dramatic traverse of the terminal moraine, following the front of the Almond Moraine for its entire length.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **5.9 miles**: CTH-HH to Sportsman Dr.

* [Icon: water drop icon] From the Plover River.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: globe icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: trail icon] A spur trail to a parking area on CTH-HH and a blue-blazed spur trail to a parking area on Hatchery Rd.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **CTH-HH Trail Access (45.05090, -89.22395)**: From Wausau on US-51, exit onto STH-52 and go east ~26 mi. At CTH-HH turn left and go north 1.5 mi. From Antigo at the intersection of USH-45/STH-47 and STH-52/STH-64, take USH-45/STH-47/STH-52 south 11.0 mi. Continue on STH-52 west for 1.3 mi. At CTH-HH turn right and go north 1.5 mi. **Parking**: 0.1 mi south on CTH-HH. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the Ice Age Trail.

[Icon: 2] **Sportsman Dr. Trail Access (45.00100, -89.26361)**: From Wausau take STH-29 east to Exit 185 and follow CTH-Y north for 7.5 mi. At CTH-Z turn right and go east 2.0 mi. At North Pole Rd. turn left and go north 1.0 mi. At Sportsman Dr. turn right and go east 1.0 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) STH-52 (ii) Hatchery Rd. A 0.1-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

This beautiful segment highlights a dramatic traverse of the terminal moraine, following the front of the Almond Moraine for its entire length. As a result, most boulders along the route are Wolf River granite. The Plover River State Fishery Area includes hardwood and cedar forests, lowlands, and spring ponds along the Plover River, a Class I, high-quality trout stream with a naturally sustaining trout population. The Plover River, originating near Aniwa in southern Langlade County, flows through Marathon County on pitted outwash. The river continues into Portage County, where it converges with the Wisconsin River.

From the Trail access at CTH-HH, head west across rolling, forested hills lined by mossy boulders and bright green ferns. Watch for wetlands, a variety of wildflowers, deep kettles, a stone hole, and a boulder train as you cross the terminal moraine. Over 1,000 feet of a volunteer-built boardwalk will keep boots high and dry through wetlands just north of STH-52. The north section of the structure hosts an observation deck with comfortable benches.

There is a good line of vision at the STH-52 crossing, but it still requires caution. Just south of STH-52, you will reach a stretch of puncheon and a native timber bridge before continuing southward through a pine forest on the west side of the Plover River. Wood nettle may border this section – its stems and leaves, armed with stinging hairs, are not fun to brush up against.

Near the river, the Trail is rock-strewn and swampy. Footing can be treacherous, with rocks and roots in the path. At the river crossing, you may have your feet cooled by the river’s waters or step across on boulders rising above the surface.

![Map of a trail segment showing the Plover River, Spring Lake, Pleasant Lake, and the village of Aniwa. The trail route is marked with a dashed line and mileage markers. Parking areas are indicated with 'P' icons. Road names like Nolan Rd., STH 52, Hatchery Rd., and Sportsman Dr. are labeled.](page_175_image_1_v2.jpg)

Now on the east side of the river, continue southward through a riparian zone and soon meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading east 0.1 miles to the Hatchery Road parking area.

Continue south along a ridge through thick stands of balsam fir, northern white cedar, and hemlock before returning to hike beside the river. Next, you’ll traverse an interesting stone-lined glacial wash and a ridgetop before entering rolling, open grassland. Cross Aniwa Creek just before you reach the segment’s endpoint at the Sportsman Drive parking area and informational kiosk.

### AREA SERVICES

* [Icon: Trail Community logo] **Antigo**: See Summit Moraine Segment, p. 128. From the STH-52 Trail access go east then north ~13 mi.

* **Wausau**: See Ringle Segment, p. 147. From the STH-52 Trail access go west ~25 mi.

* **Aniwa**: Restaurant east of USH-45 and STH-52 intersection.

* **Birnamwood**: Restaurant, lodging. Take Hatchery Rd. south until it becomes CTH-D. Continue south to CTH-N, then turn left (southeast on CTH-N).

## Dells of the Eau Claire Segment and Thornapple Creek Segment (Atlas Maps 40f, 41f)

**Summary:** The Dells of the Eau Claire Segment and Thornapple Creek Segment: 1. **[Icon: 1] Sportsman Dr. Trail Access (45.00082, -89.33512):** From Wausau take STH-29 east to Exit 185 and follow CTH-Y north for 9.5 mi.

### [Icon: 3] [Icon: 2] Dells of the Eau Claire Segment—2.6 miles: Sportsman Dr. to CTH-Z

![water, camping, picnic, hiking, and toilet icons](page_176_image_18_v2.jpg) At Dells of the Eau Claire County Park [Icon: Ice Age Trail Alliance logo].

[Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: hunting closure icon] Portion of segment between Dells of the Eau Claire County Park and CTH-Z is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: water drop icon] From the Eau Claire River.

[Icon: leashed dogs icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: trail network icon] Dells of the Eau Claire County Park trail network.

### [Icon: 2] [Icon: 4] Thornapple Creek Segment—4.0 miles (3.0 IAT, 0.9 CR): CTH-Z to CTH-N

[Icon: water drop icon] From Thornapple Creek.

[Icon: leashed dogs icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: hunting closure icon] Portion of segment crossing private land immediately east of Thornapple Creek Rd. is closed for hunting Sept. 1 to Dec. 31.

[Icon: roadwalk icon] Segment includes a connecting route roadwalk. Portions overlap gravel Helf Rd. and a snowmobile trail.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. **[Icon: 1] Sportsman Dr. Trail Access (45.00082, -89.33512):** From Wausau take STH-29 east to Exit 185 and follow CTH-Y north for 9.5 mi. At Sportsman Dr. (gravel road) turn right and go east 0.3 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

2. **[Icon: 2] CTH-Z Trail Access (44.98656, -89.35704): Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

3. **[Icon: 3] CTH-N Trail Access (44.94293, -89.34686):** From Wausau take STH-29 east to Exit 185 and follow CTH-Y north for 4.5 mi. At CTH-N turn left and go west 1.0 mi to Helf Rd. **Parking:** Roadside on Helf Rd.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Dells of the Eau Claire County Park. (ii) Gold Dust Rd. roadside parking. (iii) CTH-N. A 0.2-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

## Dells of the Eau Claire Segment

**Summary:** The Dells of the Eau Claire Segment: The cascading Dells of the Eau Claire River winds through a series of ledges and pools in the mylonite bedrock gorge formed thousands of years ago by the glacial meltwater of the Green Bay Lobe.

The cascading Dells of the Eau Claire River winds through a series of ledges and pools in the mylonite bedrock gorge formed thousands of years ago by the glacial meltwater of the Green Bay Lobe. In addition, potholes — up to five feet in diameter — are found along the river, with smaller potholes continuing to form. This segment boasts multiple scenic overlooks with spectacular views, especially in spring and after rainy periods when water levels are up. It also features the Alliance-owned Eau Claire River Preserve, which protects about 2,000 feet of the river’s shoreline. You’ll walk through a northern mesic forest: tree species include northern hemlock, white pine, sugar maple, and yellow birch. Spring brings a wide variety of ephemeral flora.

From the Trail access on Sportsman Drive, head north along a mowed path and bend west near a trail junction where a Dells of the Eau Claire County Park trail continues straight on the south shore of the Eau Claire River to CTH-Y. Turn right and cross the river on a walkway above a dam that creates a small lake for the park’s campground. Above the dam are a safe swimming and beach area, ample parking, restrooms, and a picnic ground.

![Map of the Dells of the Eau Claire County Park and Ringle Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing trail paths, road crossings, parking areas, and landmarks like the Fire Tower and Eau Claire River.](page_177_image_1_v2.jpg)

After crossing the dam, head west, past the Park Manager’s office, on the park’s North River Trail. At several places before reaching CTH-Y, you may step out on ancient volcanic rock palisades for a closer view and feel of the river’s roaring rapids. Shortly after crossing CTH-Y, pass near the park’s main picnic shelter – constructed by the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) – and picnic tables scattered beneath large shade trees. Then continue westward along the north bank on the bluff above the river.

Just before the North River Trail starts to head southwest, turn southeast to cross 30 feet above the river on the Dells High Bridge, built by the CCC in the 1930s and spanning 120 feet. South of the bridge is a trail junction; the Ice Age Trail heads southwest along the river while county park trails lead south into Dells of the Eau Claire River State Natural Area.

Continue southwest along the quiet and peaceful east bank of the river past two benches that were an Eagle Scout service project. Next, you’ll encounter large Sandberg Island, the southern foot of which marks the boundary of the county park. Just south of the park boundary, after a roped area, steps lead down to the river to where the Ice Age Trail crosses the 45th parallel – the halfway point between the North Pole and equator.

Continue through the Alliance’s Eau Claire River Preserve, the pine forest giving way to deciduous woods. Then, as the Trail leaves the river, you’ll cut through a recently harvested forest, cross a boardwalk, and make your way to the segment’s terminus at CTH-Z.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.parent_text:
## Thornapple Creek Segment

**Summary:** The Thornapple Creek Segment: This segment crosses private land and its namesake creek before wandering through a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA).

This segment crosses private land and its namesake creek before wandering through a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA). As the Trail approaches Helf Road, notice Wolf River granite boulders. They’re evidence of how easily ice picked up and deposited debris along the Hancock Moraine.

The area between Thornapple Creek Road and the intersection of Gold Dust and Helf Roads is sometimes wet with primitive tread. You may bypass this area by continuing on Thornapple Creek Road west and south, then turning east on Gold Dust Road to its intersection with the Trail. Note: You must use this bypass from September 1 to December 31 during hunting season. The route along the farm field is on private land and is closed at the owner’s request.w

From the Trail access at CTH-Z, head west along CTH-Z and then south on Thornapple Creek Road. Depart Thornapple Creek Road along a farm field and then cross Thornapple Creek on a ground-level plank footbridge.

Upon leaving a wooded area, you’ll emerge onto a powerline right-of-way. Take it for a short stretch before winding west and south through the woods. Once again intersect the powerline right-of-way, following it for a short distance before the Trail reenters the woods to the east on a snowmobile trail.

Stay on the snowmobile trail until the Trail intersects a wide, grassy two-track logging road. Turn south on the logging road, following it until it goes around a gate and meets Gold Dust Road; it becomes HelfRoad as you continue south.

The Trail gradually transitions from a grassy two-track path at the edge of a cultivated field to gravel. Moderate-size erratics, strewn along the path’s edge, rest among spring-blooming trilliums. When the road reaches a stretch of open farmland, note a fire tower perched atop a hilljust east of the road. Then, you’ll arrive at the segment’s endpoint, the intersection of CTH-N and Helf Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Dells of the Eau Claire County Park**: Camping. On Trail. For information and reservations, contact Marathon County Park and Recreation Department (715-261-1550, co.marathon.co.wi.us).

**Wausau:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-N. Trail access go west ~13 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.parent_text:
## Ringle Segment (Atlas Map 41f)

**Summary:** The Ringle Segment is a 8.8-mile trail in Marathon County (Atlas Map 41f), running from CTH-N to Curtis Ave. (CTH-Y). The Ringle Segment parallels the Hancock Moraine – the terminal moraine of the Green Bay Lobe – highlighting excellent end-moraine topography.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiker icon with number 3] **8.8 miles**: CTH-N to Curtis Ave. (CTH-Y)

* [Icon: library icon] At the Marathon County Library-Hatley Branch.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not allowed on private lands north of Mole Brook Rd.**
* [Icon: water icon] From small streams and ponds and the Plover River.
* [Icon: trail overlap icon] Portions overlap Phoenix St. and multiuse Mountain Bay state Trail (MBST).
* [Icon: tent icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area DCA [Icon: DCA icon] north of Poplar Ln.
* [Icon: trail direction icon] The MBST continues west and east.
* [Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.
* [Icon: hunting season icon] Portion of segment crossing private land northeast of Phoenix St. (formerly Meadowlark Ln.) and Mole Brook Rd. is closed during gun deer season.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

* [Icon: numbered marker 1] **CTH-N Trail Access (44.94293, -89.34686):** From Wausau take STH-29 east to Exit 185 and follow CTH-Y north for 4.5 mi. At CTH-N turn left and go west 1.0 mi to Helf Rd. **Parking:** Roadside on Helf Rd.
* [Icon: numbered marker 2] **Curtis Ave. (CTH-Y) Trail Access (44.88812, -89.33934):** From Wausau take STH-29 east to Exit 185 and follow CTH-Y (Curtis Ave.) north for 0.5 mi to the Mountain-Bay State Trail (MBST). **Parking:** At Hatley Branch Library just north of the Trail.
**Additional Parking:** (i) CTH-N. A 0.2-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT. (ii) Poplar Ln.

### THE HIKE

The Ringle Segment parallels the Hancock Moraine – the terminal moraine of the Green Bay Lobe – highlighting excellent end-moraine topography. Supraglacial (on top of the ice) sediments slid off the melting front of the glacier and formed the end-moraine ridge.

The glacier picked up, carried, and dropped the Wolf River granite boulders scattered along the route. Beginning at the Helf Road Trail access, the Trail travels high-relief, hummocky topography to the Mountain-Bay State Trail (MBST), which crosses a tunnel channel as it approaches Hatley. The Hatley Trail access parking lot rests on a pitted outwash plain crossed by the Plover River.

The segment’s namesake, Ringle, is an unincorporated community. They are named for John Ringle, who founded the Ringle Brick Company nearly by accident – his sawmill was on land containing clay, making the building and slope unstable. When he closed the mill, John used the land’s clay mixed with hard shale to make bricks, which, after firing, gave Ringle bricks a distinct, singed orange color.

The Town of Ringle was established in 1901, and an influx of Dutch immigrants proved capable farmers despite the challenging landscape.

From the intersection of CTH-N and Helf Road, head south for approximately 30 yards along Helf Road. Then depart the road and head southwest into the woods. You’ll pass through beautiful, heavily wooded land with

![A detailed map showing a portion of the Ice Age Trail, specifically the Thornapple Creek Segment and its connection to the White Cedar Segment and the Mountain-Bay State Trail. The map includes road names such as Mole Brook Rd, Phoenix St, and Poplar Ln, as well as landmarks like the village of Hatley and the Plover River. Trail mileages and parking locations are also indicated.](page_180_image_1_v2.jpg)

maples, oaks, and pines. Ephemerals decorate the ground in spring, and a colorful array of mushrooms dot the forest floor in the fall. After nearly half a mile, the Trail meets a blue-blazed spur trail leading north 0.2 miles to the CTH-N parking area. Continue south and west, and in about a mile, cross onto private land marked by a “No Dogs” sign. Note: *Please respect the landowner’s request.* About half a mile later, you’ll take a short series of switchbacks into a stand of mature pines, then down to the intersection of Mole Brook Road and Phoenix Street.

Upon reaching this intersection, continue south on Phoenix Street briefly, then onto a private driveway for 450 feet. The Trail leaves the driveway to the

left at a sign, snakes through the woods, and crosses a series of boardwalks, bridges, and stepping stones placed over wet areas and small streams. Crossing forest roads, you’ll be treated to stone staircases leading down to the roads and back up into the woods. After several miles, cross another series of four boardwalks. After the third boardwalk, the Trail meets a short blue-blazed spur trail leading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Enjoy hiking up and down high-relief, hummocky terrain as you pass through the woods and past numerous various-sized erratics on the way to Poplar Lane.

Cross Poplar Lane, jog slightly to the left and continue southward through a more open forest. Parallel an old forest road (to the right) as you wind south and east. Eventually, you’ll arrive at an impressive glacial erratic called the Stone Lion. Soon after, you’ll reach a bench overlooking a small, ephemeral pond.

After leaving the pond, emerge from the woods into a clearing and skirt the edge of the Marathon County landfill. You can see and may hear landfill operations, a stark contrast to the previous woodland tranquility. Turn southeast, away from the landfill, reenter the woods, and make your way to where the Trail meets the 83.4-mile-long Mountain-Bay State Trail (MBST), which occupies the former Chicago and Northwestern Railroad right-of-way between Wausau and Green Bay. Turn left and head east toward Hatley on the MBST’s wide crushed limestone surface. You’ll pass a lovely pond with an adjacent bench before crossing the Plover River. The segment ends in Hatley at Curtis Avenue (CTH-Y) at an Ice Age Trail/MBST kiosk.

### AREA SERVICES

**Mountain-Bay State Trail (MBST):** On Trail (715-261-1550, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/mountainbay).

**Ringle:** Restaurant. From the Poplar Ln. parking area, go west on Poplar Ln. 1.3 mi, then southwest on CTH-Q 1.2 mi. From the MBST trail intersection go west 3.0 mi.

**Hatley:** Restaurant, convenience store, library. On Curtis Ave. (CTH-Y) and on State Rd. (STH-29 frontage road).

**Pike Lake:** Restaurant, lodging. From the intersection of the Mountain-Bay State Trail and Curtis Ave. (CTH-Y), go south on CTH-Y for 5.0 mi, at the intersection of CTH-Y and N. South Shore Dr.

**Wausau Area:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the Curtis Ave. (CTH-Y) Trail access, go south 0.5 mi, then take STH-29 west 14.5 mi For area info, contact Wausau/Central Wisconsin Convention & Visitors Bureau (888-948-4748, visitwausau.com).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marathon-count.parent_text:
## White Cedar Segment (Atlas Map 42f)

**Summary:** The White Cedar Segment is a 0.6-mile trail in Marathon County (Atlas Map 42f), running from Rice Lake Dr. to Wodora Acres Rd.. This segment highlights the Alliance-owned 64-acre Rice Lake Preserve.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiker icon] [Icon: 1 icon] **0.6 miles**: Rice Lake Dr. to Wodora Acres Rd.

[Icon: water drop icon] From Rice Lake and Rice Lake Creek.

[Icon: hunting icon] Hunting is allowed

[Icon: tent icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA logo].

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 in circle] **Rice Lake Dr. Trail Access (44.83537, -89.34656):** From Hatley at the intersection of STH-29 and CTH-Y, take CTH-Y south 3.2 mi. At CTH-II, turn left and go east 0.3 mi. At Rice Lake Dr. turn right and go south 0.4 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2 in circle] **Wodora Acres Rd. Trail Access (44.83042, -89.34979):** From Hatley at the intersection of STH-29 and CTH-Y, take CTH-Y south 3.9 mi. At Wodora Acres Rd. turn left and go east 0.1 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Rice Lake Dr. parking area.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights the Alliance-owned 64-acre Rice Lake Preserve. The Preserve’s prized feature is the 1,000 feet of shoreline (out of 8,400 feet) of the 25-acre, undeveloped, spring-fed marl lake containing bluegill, northern pike, and largemouth bass. Marl, carbonate-rich sediment containing clays and silt, is most often found in areas of recent glaciation. Notably, three miles west of the Preserve rests the Hancock Moraine, the terminal moraine of the Green Bay Lobe of the late Wisconsin Glaciation.

Trail access begins along Rice Lake Drive. Head west through a second-growth, upland forest populated with red maple, white pine, basswood, white ash, and the occasional quaking aspen. Small-car-sized erratics strewn about in the undergrowth highlight the glacier’s impact. The Preserve’s land formed as glacial meltwater stream sediment flowed west toward the Plover River. The forces further shaping the topography included melting underground blocks of glacial ice and the peat and muck forming since the end of the Pleistocene epoch.

In short order, you’ll arrive at an 873-foot-long boardwalk winding through an excellent example of a healthy, mature, white-cedar swamp. The soil throughout – Seelyville Muck – is prone to flooding, the muck reaching depths up to five feet. You may appreciate the cool, dark understory blanketed in sphagnum and True Mosses from the viewing platforms.

After leaving the boardwalk, you’ll skirt a wooded wetland populated by common water-tolerant species, such as cedar, hemlock, and birch. In this area, the Preserve protects a quarter-mile-long riparian corridor of Rice Lake Creek, providing habitat for great blue herons, belted kingfishers, and other water-loving species.

A blue-blazed trail meets the path; it runs along the top of an esker and leads to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

Continue straight ahead as the Trail descends, drops off the esker, and crosses a low-lying area on stepping stones. Ascend a second small esker, then intersect

an old forest road which you’ll follow to the segment’s terminus at Wodora Acres Road.

![Map of the White Cedar Segment area showing trail route, local roads like Wodora Acres Rd. and CTH-Y, and nearby lakes including Rice Lake and Pike Lake.](page_183_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Ringle:** Restaurant. From CTH-Y and Wodora Acres Rd. go north on CTH-Y 3.6 mi., then west on STH-29 3.9 mi.

**Hatley:** Restaurant, convenience store, library. On Curtis Ave. (CTH-Y) and on State Rd. (STH-29 frontage road). From CTH-Y and Wodora Acres Rd. go north on CTH-Y 4.1 mi.

**Pike Lake:** Restaurant, lodging. From Wodora Acres Rd. Trail access, go west on Wodora Acres Rd. for 0.1 mi. Turn left and go south 0.5 mi on CTH-Y. Continue on to N. Shore Dr. for 0.2 mi.

**Bevent:** Grocery store. From Wodora Acres Rd. Trail access, go west on Wodora Acres Rd. for 0.1 mi. Turn left and go south 4.9 mi on CTH-Y.

**Wausau:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From CTH-Y and Wodora Acres Rd. go north on CTH-Y 3.6 mi, then west on STH-29 14.5 mi.

![A wooden boardwalk and viewing platform winding through a lush, green forest.](page_183_image_2_v2.jpg)
DAVE CALIEBE

White Cedar Segment: A viewing platform offers a chance to slow down and look around.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail and surrounding areas in Portage and Marathon counties, showing Stevens Point, Rosholt, Amherst, and the Hartman Creek Segment. The map includes a legend for the Ice Age Trail, Connecting Route (unmarked), Other Trail, County Boundary, and Public or IATA Land, along with a scale in miles.](page_184_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.parent_text:
# Portage & Waupaca Counties

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Portage and Waupaca Counties. The trail route is marked in red, passing through the New Hope Segment, Iola Ski Hill Segment, Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment, Waupaca River Segment, and Emmons Creek Segment. Key locations include Iola, Scandinavia, Waupaca, and Hartman Creek State Park. Road markers for highways 49, 161, 22, 110, 54, and 10 are shown. An inset map of Wisconsin highlights the location of Portage and Waupaca Counties.](page_185_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Portage & Waupaca Counties

*Atlas Maps 44f–49f; Databook pages 41–44*
*Total miles: 52.1 (Trail 20.0; Connecting Route 32.1)*

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting the location of Portage and Waupaca counties](page_186_image_2_v2.jpg)

Several glacial advances formed the landscape of eastern Portage County and Waupaca County. The Green Bay Lobe moved from the east, its farthest advance extending a few miles east of Stevens Point. Successive glacial advances often stopped short of previous ones, leaving their own moraines.

Along the county border, the Ice Age Trail passes over several Elderon Moraines, deposited approximately 13,000 years ago. Like several others to the west, these moraines are narrow and non-contiguous, broken by channels cut by westward torrents of glacial meltwater and pitted outwash. The meltwater drainageways flushed glacial debris of sand and gravel onto stagnant glacier ice.

After the buried ice melted, the deposited glacial debris collapsed onto the ground. There it formed the undulating and irregular landscape of ridged hills and knobs interspersed with steep-sided depressions that later filled and became kettle lakes. The geological area known as the Farmington Drumlins is the largest group of drumlins (known as swarms) along the entire Ice Age Trail. Miles from the terminal moraine, the drumlins were formed beneath a continental glacier through the sculpting of sand, pebbles, and other glacial debris. Drumlins have a distinctive teardrop, oval, or cigar shape.

Portage County is named for the once vital passage between the Fox and Wisconsin rivers. However, due to boundary changes, it no longer hosts its namesake. “Waupaca,” a Menominee word for both the county and its river, is translated as either “pale water” or “Place of Tomorrow Seen Clearly.”

Both counties, created from the Wisconsin Territory, were organized in the mid-1800s. The area’s rivers and location on the southern boundary of a great pine forest attracted white settlers of Polish, German, and Scandinavian descent. The first sawmill started in Mukwa in 1848. It was soon followed by one in Weyauwega. Cleared ground and fertile soil led to potato farming. The arrival of the railroad in 1871 only helped the industry, giving farmers a market for their plentiful potato crops. By the turn of the 20th century, Waupaca led the nation in the shipment of potatoes.

. . .

The Ice Age Trail passes through several state fishery and natural areas and Hartman Creek State Park. In addition to camping at Hartman Creek State Park, the Portage County Parks Department offers camping near the Ice Age Trail at Collins and Lake Emily county parks.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Ice Age Trail crosses back and forth between Portage and Waupaca counties. The Portage/Waupaca County Chapter has cared for the Trail since the late 1980s. Chapter volunteers promote the Trail segments winding through their region by staffing numerous outreach events, planning seasonal hikes, and hosting an annual fundraising event, the Fall Hike-a-Thon, in October. In addition, the Chapter works closely with Hartman Creek State Park and local landowners, ensuring Trail stewardship and maintaining Trail privileges. The Chapter also offers the Drumlin Drifter hiking award.

Participants earn a patch for hiking all Ice Age Trail segments in Portage and Waupaca counties. Visit IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards to begin your adventure. Visit IceAgeTrail.org/portage-waupaca-county-chapter to learn more about Chapter activities.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

| Portage County Information                        | co.portage.wi.us                                |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Portage County Parks Department                   | 715-346-1433, co.portage.wi.us/department/parks |
| Stevens Point Area Convention and Visitors Bureau | 715-344-2556, stevenspointarea.com              |
| Waupaca County Information                        | waupacacounty-wi.gov                            |
| Waupaca Area Chamber                              | 715-258-7343, waupacaareachamber.com            |
| Iola/Scandinavia Area Chamber                     | ischamber.com                                   |

![A sweet little bridge bathed in late afternoon light.](page_187_image_1_v2.jpg)

HOLLY THOMPSON

Emmons Creek Segment: A sweet little bridge bathed in late afternoon light.

## New Hope Segment and Iola Ski Hill Segment (Atlas Map 45f)

**Summary:** The New Hope Segment and Iola Ski Hill Segment: 1.  **Sunset Lake Rd. Trail Access (44.57252, -89.26379):** From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take USH-10 east 8.0 mi.

### [Icon: 4] [Icon: 3] New Hope Segment—3.6 miles: Sunset Lake Rd. to CTH-T

| [Icon: water drop] From Severson Lake.                                        | [Icon: ColdCache] One ColdCache site on segment.                                     |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| [Icon: camping/park] At Collins County Park \~9 mi west (see Area Services).  | [Icon: hunting] Segment is closed during gun deer season.                            |
| [Icon: picnic] [Icon: swimming] At Sunset Lake County Park. | [Icon: dog on leash] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. |

### [Icon: 3] [Icon: 2kt] Iola Ski Hill Segment—2.5 miles: CTH-T to CTH-MM

**Note:** A reroute is planned east of Stoltenberg Rd. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.

| [Icon: faucet] [Icon: water] At Iola Winter Sports Club lodge (winter only). | [Icon: hunting] Segment is closed during gun deer season.                                               |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Icon: water drop] From a marsh pond along the Trail.                                          | [Icon: dog on leash] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.                    |
| [Icon: shelter] Walk-in shelter \[icon] on Iola Winter Sports Club property.                   | [Icon: road overlap] Portions overlap a farm road and Stoltenberg Rd.                                   |
| [Icon: camping/park] At Collins County Park (see Area Services).                               | [Icon: trail network] A farm road, forest roads, and the network of Iola Winter Sports Club ski trails. |

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1.  **Sunset Lake Rd. Trail Access (44.57252, -89.26379):** From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take USH-10 east 8.0 mi. At STH-161 turn left and go east 3.8 mi. At CTH-A turn left and go north 4.0 mi. At CTH-Z turn right and go east 1.5 mi. At Sunset Lake Rd. turn left and go north 0.9 mi. From Waupaca at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take STH-49 north 20.5 mi. At CTH-Z turn left and go west 2.8 mi. At Sunset Lake Rd. turn right and go north 0.9 mi.
    **Parking:** Roadside 200 ft north on the east side of Sunset Lake Rd.

2.  **CTH-T Trail Access (44.54372, -89.23497):** No Parking.

3.  **CTH-MM Trail Access (44.53656, -89.20913):** From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take USH-10 east 8.0 mi. At STH-161 turn left and go east 3.8 mi. At CTH-A turn left and go north 2.3 mi. At CTH-MM turn right and go east 4.0 mi. From Waupaca at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take STH-49 north 17.5 mi. At CTH-MM turn left and go west 1.6 mi.

**Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) New Hope Pines State Natural Area. (ii) CTH-Z.
