iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.parent_text:
## New Hope Segment

**Summary:** The New Hope Segment: Before beginning this segment, visit New Hope Pines State Natural Area (SNA) if you are looking for extra adventure.

Before beginning this segment, visit New Hope Pines State Natural Area (SNA) if you are looking for extra adventure. It’s a 0.1-mile walk north from the segment’s starting point on Sunset Lake Road. The SNA features one of the largest and least disturbed northern

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment in Portage and Waupaca Counties. The map shows the trail route from New Hope Pines State Natural Area (point 1) southeast towards Iola Winter Sports Club (point 3). Key features include Sunset Lake, Minister Lake, Skunk Lake, Severson Lake, and various roads like CTH-Z, CTH-T, and CTH-MM. Distances between points are marked (e.g., 0.4, 0.2, 1.3, 0.9, 0.8, 0.5, 1.1 miles). The boundary between Portage and Waupaca Counties is indicated.](page_189_image_1_v2.jpg)

dry-mesic forests remaining in central Wisconsin. It’s reminiscent of the region’s vast “pineries” before European settlement. A forest of red oak, white oak, and red maple exists beneath the canopy of white and red pines. Though the stand is relatively “young,” with only scattered old growth, the dominant white pines are more than 100 feet high and up to 2 feet in diameter. The SNA provides an off-road exploring opportunity, but no established trails, signage, or facilities exist.

The segment traverses areas of high-relief hummocky topography and has a strong northwoods character. It highlights Severson Lake, a scenic hard-water seepage lake situated in a kettle and ringed by a mix of pine and hardwood forest.

From the Trail access on Sunset Lake Road, head southeast through dense forest, dropping down into a low area and climbing out as you approach CTH-Z.

Cross CTH-Z and follow the mowed path on the south side of the road around to the east for 0.2 miles to the CTH-Z parking area.

Reenter the woods and continue southeast. Between CTH-Z and the segment’s end on CTH-T, the Trail moves through dramatic high-relief hummocky topography. The area’s high water table and clay-laden soil produce marshes in the small depressions, harboring nesting ducks. A few larger kettles contain lakes, including Severson (Budsberg) Lake. This section also traverses open

meadows and climbs in and out of three steep wooded ravines. Short bridges cross wet, marshy areas, but rainy periods may partially submerge them. Red and white pine and birch typical of the Northwoods mix with Hill’s oak. Several of the oaks and pines may be more than 100 years old.

After turning east and heading toward the south end of Severson Lake, you’ll reach the segment’s terminus at CTH-T.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.parent_text:
## Iola Ski Hill Segment

**Summary:** The Iola Ski Hill Segment: The segment crosses the property of Iola Winter Sports Club.

The segment crosses the property of Iola Winter Sports Club. It offers 20K of cross-country ski trails along with ski jumping and snowshoeing. The ski jumps you’ll see atop Norseman Hill use the relief and steep slopes of a deep kettle for their landing zones. The total relief — approximately 100 feet — suggests the ice block buried here was at least 100 feet thick.

From the Trail access at CTH-T, head east and briefly follow a farm road before entering woods to the south. Climb uphill and continue meandering up and down through the forest, passing several deep kettles.

At a gravel driveway, jog left onto Stoltenberg Road, following it a short distance before departing it to the left. Head east into an area where a recent clear-cut removed invasive black locust trees.

Soon you’ll enter the grounds of the Iola Winter Sports Club. Look carefully for yellow blazes and directional arrows. The Sports Club also offers an overnight shelter for Ice Age Trail hikers’ use; watch for signs and the path leading to it.

The Trail passes through areas of high-relief hummocky topography in pine and hardwood forests with ample evidence of glacial activity, including kettles and eskers. After descending along the top of an esker, you’ll skirt the southern edge of a large marsh where a boardwalk provides a spot for viewing migratory birds. Just beyond it, a sizeable trailside log can be a comfortable seat.

Arrive at two stone staircases where the Trail nears the ski jump structures of the Sports Club. The tallest of these structures is more than 130 feet high; please stay off the ski jumps.

The Trail then descends to a “grand staircase” at the Iola Winter Sports Club chalet and a large parking area and soon reaches its end point at CTH-MM.

*As my son Paul and I journeyed the Ice Age Trail, the most frequent advice we got from experienced Thousand-Milers was to hike our own hike. We figure we averaged 1.5 miles per hour, hence our trail names... And we can truly say that we did it our way.*

MAGGIE & “THE TURTLE” CARRAO, THOUSAND-MILER

### AREA SERVICES

**Alban:** Restaurant, grocery store. From the Sunset Lake Rd. access, go north to CTH-T. Continue north on CTH-T then CTH-A to STH-66. Small grocery store at CTH-A and STH-66. Restaurant (seasonal) is 0.7 mi east on STH-66.

**Rosholt:** Restaurant, grocery store, camping, library. From the Sunset Lake Rd. access go north to CTH-T. Continue north on CTH-T then CTH-A to STH-66. Go west 1.0 mi on STH-66. Camping at Collins County Park (715-346-1433, co.portage.wi.us/department/parks/collins-park), 3.5 mi south and west of town.

**Iola Winter Sports Club:** Seasonal facilities (winter) (715-445-3411, iolawintersportsclub.org/). Primitive shelter available for camping for long-distance, multiday hikers.

**Iola:** Restaurant, grocery store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-MM access go east on CTH-MM to STH-49. Turn right and follow STH-49 for 3.5 mi.

**Stevens Point:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, medical service. From the CTH-MM Trail access go west then south ~18 mi.

**Waupaca:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-MM Trail access go east then south ~21 mi.

![MSC volunteers craft a new path along a Trail reroute on the Iola Ski Hill Segment.](page_191_image_1_v2.jpg)

Iola Ski Hill Segment: MSC volunteers craft a new path along a Trail reroute.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.parent_text:
## Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment and Waupaca River Segment (Atlas Map 47f)

**Summary:** The Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment and Waupaca River Segment: **Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment**

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3 3L icon] **Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment**—4.4 miles (3.6 miles IAT, 0.8 miles CR):
N. Foley Rd. Northern Trail Access to USH-10

[Icon: water drop icon] From trailside lakes and streams.

[Icon: dashed line icon] Segment includes two connecting route roadwalks.

[Icon: crossed lines icon] A white-blazed loop on northwest end, a blue-blazed spur to a parking area and several DNR access trails.

[Icon: hunting icon] Portion of the segment between Indian Valley Rd. and Grenlie Rd. is closed for hunting Sept. 1 to Jan. 5. Portion of the segment between Grenlie Rd. and Rieben Rd. is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: 2 2L icon] **Waupaca River Segment**—4.9 miles (2.3 miles IAT, 2.6 miles CR):
USH-10 to STH-54

[Icon: water drop icon] From the Waupaca River.

[Icon: hiker and cabin icon] The WCC trailside cabin [Icon: bed icon] (first come, first served) just west of Foley Dr.

[Icon: hunting icon] Portions of the segment crossing private lands north of the Waupaca River State Fishery area and east of Townline Rd. are closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: dashed line icon] Segment includes two connecting route roadwalks and a field road.

[Icon: crossed lines icon] A white-blazed loop trail west of the segment near the Waupaca River and a blue-blazed spur trail to the Foley Dr. parking area.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] **1 N. Foley Rd. Northern Trail Access (44.43034, -89.20912):** From Waupaca at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take USH-10 west 6.0 mi. At Foley Dr. turn right and go north 2.1 mi. From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take USH-10 east 18.0 mi. At Foley Dr. turn left and go north 2.1 mi. *Note: Foley Dr. becomes N. Foley Rd. at Floistad Rd.* **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Skunk and Foster Lakes SNA 0.1 mi south on N. Foley Rd. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

[Icon: 2 icon] **2 USH-10 Trail Access (44.40098, -89.21749):** **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Rieben Rd. parking area. See Additional Parking.

![3 icon](page_192_image_10_v2.jpg) **3 STH-54 Trail Access (44.35244, -89.21364):** From Waupaca at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take STH-54 west 5.0 mi. From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take I-39 south 6.5 mi. Exit STH-54 and go east 17.0 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Indian Valley Rd. roadside parking. (ii) Grenlie Rd. roadside parking. (iii) Rieben Rd. Parking area is on private land. Do not block farm field access. (iv) Waupaca River State Fishery Area on Foley Dr. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

**Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment**

This segment travels a horseshoe-shaped route in the Skunk and Foster Lakes State Natural Area (SNA), featuring a cluster of five undeveloped glacial pothole lakes. A second-growth forest of paper birch, white pine, red maple, elm, and white and red oak surround the lakes nestled in pitted outwash moraine topography.

The segment crosses the Farmington Drumlins swarm, the most significant swarm of drumlins along the entire Ice Age Trail. Drumlins, elevated tear-shaped land formations, indicate the flow of the glaciers. Their steep end faces the flow of the ice, and the tail end tapers to the ground like a ramp. They are always found in groups called swarms. The Alliance-owned Farmington Drumlins Preserve contains portions of two drumlins and several large erratics.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment through Skunk and Foster Lakes State Natural Area and Farmington Drumlins Preserve, showing trail route, mileage, and local landmarks.](page_193_image_1_v2.jpg)

From the segment’s starting point at the North Foley Road northern Trail access, head west and parallel Sannes Creek, which flows through the ancient meltwater channel surrounding the SNA. As the segment bends south through white and red pines, you’ll approach the north shore of 11-acre spring-fed Skunk Lake. Arrive at an intersection with a white-blazed loop trail and a DNR-access forest road. (For additional adventure, head west on the 0.8-mile loop trail, which explores a scenic mature maple forest set in rolling topography.)

From the junction with the loop trail, continue southeast, crossing another access path leading to the shore of Skunk Lake, which is surrounded by a wetland dominated by cattails and bulrush. Continue into hummocky terrain east of Skunk Lake. Numerous impressive-sized Wolf River granite boulders are scattered throughout the area. Descend through second growth forest for a glimpse of seven-acre shallow, hard water Foster Lake, then circle east toward Grenlie Lake, a clear lake fed by seepage and springs.

At the southwest corner of Grenlie Lake, you’ll pass a hillside filled with large ferns and then follow the north shore of the lake. Near the east end of the lake is a blue-blazed spur trail leading to the parking area on North Foley Road. Head south across the lake’s outlet on a bridge and then follow the shoreline west, where you’ll enjoy views of waterfowl habitat. Ascend an erratic-filled field, fern garden, and pine forest on the way to the North Foley Road southern Trail access.

Follow a connecting route roadwalk northeast on North Foley Road and then southeast and south on Indian Valley Road to where Indian Valley Road turns sharply east.

Leave Indian Valley Road and head generally south through woods on an uneven forest road. Erratics of various sizes populate the Trail between here and the end of the segment. Climb steadily up the side of a drumlin on the western edge of the Farmington Drumlins swarm described above.

The canopy opens up to a fenced pasture with a large cell/transmission tower in the middle. Hug the tree line along the field, enter the Alliance’s Farmington Drumlins Preserve, and descend through woods and open prairie. As the Trail nears Grenlie Road, huge erratics line up alongside it.

Continue across Grenlie Road and follow the edge of a farm field before diving east into the woods. The Trail weaves under a beautiful canopy of hardwoods and intersects several forest roads. Carefully follow Trail blazes through this area; ATV trails and timber harvest have contributed to several unmarked trails. The Trail gradually ascends, zigzagging east then south to skirt the western edge of a large drumlin before descending about 100 feet on a steep dirt path. Footing can be treacherous, especially when the tread is wet. Jog right then left before emerging along a farm field and heading southeast to Rieben Road.

Follow a 0.3-mile connecting route roadwalk west on Rieben Road, then south on Foley Drive, and across the Canadian National Railroad tracks to the segment’s terminus on the north side of USH-10.

**Waupaca River Segment**

The segment highlights a steep ridge and the scenic Waupaca River. Its pitted outwash topography features glacial debris left behind when the glaciers retreated. The steep slope just east of the segment’s start resulted from buried ice melting and the outwash surface collapsing.

From the Trail access on the north side of USH-10, begin a connecting route roadwalk south on Foley Drive. Cross four-lane USH-10 with extreme caution. USH-10 is on the route of the historic Yellowstone Trail, the first transcontinental highway in the northern tier of the United States. Follow Foley Drive south to where the off-road portion of the segment heads west, ascending steeply from the road.

You may hear equipment operating from a sand and gravel pit west of the Trail. A small, simple cabin sits beside the Trail. It was built on private land by the Wisconsin Conservation Corps in 1986 and is open to the public for overnight stays. South of the cabin, you’ll cut along the edge of a restored prairie hosting summertime butterflies before meeting the northern junction of a scenic blue-blazed loop trail splitting off to the west.

Continue through rolling terrain with steep slopes on the sides of the Trail in mixed oak woodland and pine forest. South of the southern junction with the loop trail, the segment flattens to follow the placid Waupaca River. Portions of the Trail along the river can become wet or flooded. Leave the riverbank briefly and meet a white-blazed spur trail leading to the parking area on Foley Drive. Then return to the river before emerging at the Cobbtown Road and Foley Drive intersection.

Cross the river on the bridge and head west on a connecting route on Cobbtown Road. Turn left and follow Townline Road south until the segment heads offroad east across a pitted outwash landscape along a field edge, then heads south to its terminus at STH-54. A prominent swarm of drumlins sits about half a mile east of the parking area.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Lake Emily County Park:** 8 mi west of the Ice Age Trail. From the Skunk and Foster Lakes SNA parking area on N. Foley Rd., take N. Foley Rd. north to CTH-V, turn left and go 7.6 mi, turning left on CTH-A and turning right on CTH-KK. Take Lake Dr. to Park Dr. (3968 Park Dr., Amherst Junction).

Lake Emily County Park has 15 conical, oval and linear Indian mounds in various groups out of a previous 22 or more. Look for an information board citing locations in the park.

### AREA SERVICES

**Waupaca:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the USH-10 Trail access, go ~6 mi east on USH-10. Most services in the area of USH-10 and STH-54/49. Area info available from the Waupaca Area Chamber of Commerce (715-258-7343, waupacaareachamber.com).

**Amherst:** Restaurant, grocery store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the USH-10 Trail access go ~5 mi northwest on USH-10.

**Amherst Junction:** Convenience store, lodging, camping. From the USH-10 Trail access go ~7 mi northwest on USH-10. Camping at Lake Emily County Park (3968 Park Dr., 715-346-1433, co.portage.wi.us/department/parks/lake-emily-park).

**Stevens Point:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, medical service. From the USH-10 Trail access go ~18 mi northwest on USH-10. Area info available from the Stevens Point Area Convention and Visitor Bureau (715-344-2556, stevenspointarea.com).

**Hartman Creek State Park:** See Hartman Creek Segment, p. 164. From the STH-54 Trail access go east and then south on Hartman Creek Rd. 2.5 mi.

> *I love my daughter Emily. I love my home state of Wisconsin. To hike the Ice Age Trail with her was an idea that came about naturally. I had just retired, and Emily had just moved back to Wisconsin from Oregon. The timing was right!*

MICHAEL HOFFMAN, THOUSAND-MILER

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.parent_text:
## Hartman Creek Segment (Atlas Map 48f)

**Summary:** The Hartman Creek Segment is a 5.5-mile trail in Portage & Waupaca Counties (Atlas Map 48f), running from STH-54 to Emmons Creek Rd.. This segment features the forested, hilly terrain of Hartman Creek State Park (HCSP), one of the largest erratics on the entire Ice Age Trail, and meadows of stately lupine.

### SNAPSHOT

![Snapshot icons](page_196_image_1_v2.jpg)

**5.5 miles**: STH-54 to Emmons Creek Rd.

* Portion of segment crossing private land between STH-54 and Edminster Rd. is closed during gun deer season.

At various locations throughout HCSP ▲. Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

Portions overlap HCSP trails. Hike well off to the side when ski trails are groomed.

Water: From Allen Creek and Allen Lake. Side Trails: Several unsigned side trails and the extensive HCSP trail network.

One ColdCache site on segment.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **STH-54 Trail Access (44.35244, -89.21364):** From Waupaca at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take STH-54 west 5.0 mi. From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take I-39 south 6.5 mi. Exit STH-54 and go east 17.0 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **Emmons Creek Rd. Trail Access (44.30489, -89.23434):** From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take I-39 south 6.5 mi. Exit STH-54 and go east 15.0 mi. At CTH-D turn right and go south 2.5 mi. Continue south on Stratton Lake Rd. 0.8 mi. At Emmons Creek Rd. turn left and go east 0.5 mi. From Waupaca at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take STH-54 west 6.6 mi. At CTH-D turn left and go south 2.5 mi. Continue south on Stratton Lake Rd. 0.8 mi. At Emmons Creek Rd. turn left and go east 0.5 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.2 mi east on Emmons Creek Rd.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Edminster Ln. (ii) Hartman Creek State Park (HCSP) on Windfeldt Ln.

### THE HIKE

This segment features the forested, hilly terrain of Hartman Creek State Park (HCSP), one of the largest erratics on the entire Ice Age Trail, and meadows of stately lupine.

HCSP’s long history begins with the purchase by the Wisconsin Conservation Department, the precursor of the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (DNR), of 309 acres from the Allen and Hartman family estates in 1939 for $8,500. The Conservation Department dammed a creek to create Allen Lake in honor of George W. Allen and named the property “Hartman Creek State Fish Hatchery” in honor of the Hartman family. Today, the park consists of approximately 1,400 acres.

The park is located on terrain formed as the Wisconsin glacier retreated down a regional slope. The eastern part of the park lies on gently rolling ground moraine and pitted outwash where flowing rivers from the melting glacier deposited layers of gravel and sand. The resulting topography is pitted outwash to the west; as the glacier melted back from this position, it left a substantially lower land surface to the east. A kiosk explaining the geological landscape of the Elderon Moraine and the outwash plain is a short distance east of the segment’s route at a parking area on Windfeldt Lane.

The segment also enjoys passage across the Emmons Creek Barrens State Natural Area. Located on sandy glacial outwash, the area supports a semi-open oak savanna with scattered open-grown bur and white oaks. Dense patches of wild lupine provide favorable

habitat – and the only known larval food – for the federally endangered Karner blue butterfly.

From the Trail access on STH-54, head south between farm fields, enter a hilly wooded area, and cross Allen Creek on a wooden plank bridge. Then, traverse a pine grove before emerging onto Edminster Road. Watch for yellow blazes as the Trail intersects several unsigned side trails.

South of Edminster Road, you’ll enter HCSP, intersecting and sharing portions of the park’s extensive trail network. Take note of an enormous erratic next to a Leopold bench. From here, you may depart from the segment to access HCSP’s campground and facilities. (Head east 0.2 miles, then south 0.1 miles on park hiking and biking trails.)

South of Windfeldt Lane, you’ll travel through the forest and cut across an open prairie near the edge of an old orchard where bluebirds and other songbirds thrive. You’ll intersect several park hiking trails and intersect and parallel mountain bike paths. You will see a large clearing as you enter the Emmons Creek Barrens State Natural Area’s hilly terrain. The Trail narrows and descends into the clearing as it approaches its endpoint on Emmons Creek Road. During late spring and early summer, enjoy walking through a sea of pink and purple lupine and the butterflies it attracts.

![Map of the trail segment showing the route from STH-54 south through Hartman Creek State Park to Emmons Creek Road. Key landmarks include Allen Creek, Edminster Rd, Windfeldt Ln, and the Emmons Creek Barrens State Natural Area.](page_197_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

*   **Waupaca**: See Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment and Waupaca River Segment, p. 160. From the STH-54 Trail access go east ~5 mi.
*   **Stevens Point**: See Skunk and Foster Lakes Segment and Waupaca River Segment, p. 160. From the STH-54 Trail access go west ~23.5 mi.
*   **Turner’s Fresh Market**: From STH-54 parking area go 0.2 mi west on STH-54. Seasonal farmer’s market with fresh fruits, vegetables, and drinks (715-258-3355, turnersfreshmarket.com).
*   **Hartman Creek State Park (HCSP)**: Camping, summer concession stand and recreational rentals. On Trail. (715-258-2372, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/hartmancreek; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::portage-waupac.parent_text:
## Emmons Creek Segment (Atlas Map 48f)

**Summary:** The Emmons Creek Segment is a 2.6-mile trail in Portage & Waupaca Counties (Atlas Map 48f), running from Emmons Creek Rd. to 2nd Ave.. This segment and its accompanying Faraway Valley Loop highlight charming Emmons Creek and oak woodland and savanna areas.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiker icon with number 3]
[Icon: car icon with number 2]
**2.6 miles**: Emmons Creek Rd. to 2nd Ave.

[Icon: water icon]
From Emmons Creek.

[Icon: dog icon]
Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: loop icon]
White-blazed Faraway Valley loop and white-blazed Murry Creek loop south of 2nd Ave.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: circled number 1]
**① Emmons Creek Rd. Trail Access (44.30489, -89.23434):** From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take I-39 south. Exit STH-54 and go east 15.0 mi. At CTH-D turn right and go south 2.5 mi. Continue south on Stratton Lake Rd. 0.8 mi. At Emmons Creek Rd. turn left and go east 0.5 mi. *From Waupaca* at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take STH-54 west 6.6 mi. At CTH-D turn left and go south 2.5 mi. Continue south on Stratton Lake Rd. 0.8 mi. At Emmons Creek Rd. turn left and go east 0.5 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.2 mi east on Emmons Creek Rd.

[Icon: circled number 2]
**② 2nd Ave. Trail Access (44.27997, -89.24977):** From Stevens Point at the intersection of I-39 and USH-10, take I-39 south 6.5 mi. Exit STH-54 and go east 13.2 mi. At CTH-TT turn right and go south 2.5 mi. Join CTH-D south for another 0.5 mi. Where CTH-D turns west, continue south on 16th Rd. for another 2.2 mi. At 2nd Ave. turn left and go east 1.5 mi. *From Waupaca* at the intersection of STH-49, USH-10, and STH-54, take STH-54 west 8.4 mi. At CTH-TT turn left and go south 2.5 mi. Join CTH-D south for another 0.5 mi. Where CTH-D turns west, continue south on 16th Rd. for another 2.2 mi. At 2nd Ave. turn left and go east 1.5 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Murry Creek loop trail parking area 150 ft east on 2nd Ave.

**Additional Parking:** Stratton Lake Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment and its accompanying Faraway Valley Loop highlight charming Emmons Creek and oak woodland and savanna areas. It travels through the Emmons Creek State Fishery Area, which protects Emmons Creek, a cold-water trout stream.

From the Trail access on Emmons Creek Road, head south and west through the Emmons Creek State Fishery Area, climbing a dramatic ridge through oak woodlands. The river valley below is revealed when leaves are off trees. Descend from the ridge and head west and southwest through a pine grove to Stratton Lake Road and the segment’s main parking area.

Just past the parking area, you’ll meet the white-blazed Faraway Valley Loop. (For a side trip, take it and head west through a meadow, cross Emmons Creek on the 3rd Avenue road bridge, return to the woods, and head south and then east to rejoin the segment south of Emmons Creek.)

Continuing on the Trail, south of Stratton Lake Road, pass through a large meadow before crossing Emmons Creek on a scenic footbridge. Boardwalks offer passage through a seasonally wet area noted for skunk cabbage and marsh marigolds. The Trail overlooks the Emmons Creek valley, reaches the southern junction with the Faraway Valley Loop, and heads south on a straight line through mostly forested hills toward the segment’s endpoint on 2nd Avenue.

Upon arriving at 2nd Avenue, you may explore a white-blazed lollipop loop (with a total distance of 2.7 miles) heading south from 2nd Avenue. (Cross narrow Murry Creek, and then wind through pine groves. Small meadows here sometimes yield sightings of the rare and endangered Karner blue butterfly.)

![Map of Emmons Creek Segment showing trail path, roads (Stratton Lake Rd., Emmons Creek Rd., 2nd Ave.), water bodies (Fountain Lake, Deans Lake, Emmons Creek, Murry Creek), and points of interest.](page_199_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Almond**: Restaurant, library. From the 2nd Ave. Trail access go west ~9 mi.

**Hartman Creek State Park**: See Hartman Creek Segment, p. 164. From the 2nd Ave. Trail access go east then north ~7 mi.

**Waupaca:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the 2nd Ave. Trail access go east then north ~12 mi.

**Stevens Point:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, medical service. From the 2nd Ave. Trail access go west then north ~28 mi.

PHOTOGRAPHER UNKNOWN

![A close-up photograph of a blue Karner blue butterfly resting on a green lupine leaf.](page_199_image_2_v2.jpg)

Emmons Creek Segment: A Karner blue butterfly rests on a bed of lupine.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Waushara, Marquette, Juneau, and Adams Counties. The map shows the trail route in red and connecting routes as red dotted lines. Labeled segments include Deerfield Segment, Bohn Lake Segment, Greenwood Segment, Mecan River Segment, Chaffee Creek Segment, and Wedde Creek Segment. Key locations shown are Wautoma, Coloma, Friendship, Adams, Roche-A-Cri State Park, and Greenwood Wildlife Area. Major roads like I-39, US-51, and State Highway 21 are marked. A legend indicates the Ice Age Trail, connecting routes, other trails, county boundaries, and public or IATA land. An inset map shows the location of these counties within Wisconsin. The Ice Age Trail Alliance website iceagetrail.org is also listed.](page_200_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.parent_text:
# Waushara County

Atlas Maps 49f—53f-E and 53f-W;
Databook pages 46–49
Total miles: 42.6 (Trail 21.5; Connecting Route 21.0)

Within the Central Sand Hills region, the Ice Age Trail corridor in Waushara County courses through a complex terminal moraine zone of the westward-flowing Green Bay Lobe. The moraines along the Trail were deposited during the late Wisconsin Glaciation. Most of these moraines were deposited after the Green Bay Lobe reached its maximum extent about 18,000 years ago. As the ice sheet retreated and advanced, it deposited several moraines. When the westward torrents of glacial meltwater flowed through the region, the moraines became pitted, and meltwater channels sliced through them. The moraines were also partially buried by the glacial outwash debris.

A mixture of farmland, woodlots, wetlands, small kettle lakes, cold-water streams, and rural communities characterizes the county. The Ice Age Trail passes through land managed by the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (DNR), including Bohn Lake State Ice Age Trail Area/State Natural Area, Greenwood State Wildlife Area, and Mecan River System State Fishery Areas, which include Wedde Creek and Chaffee Creek. These public lands protect many plant communities, including wetlands, restored prairie, and rare oak and pine barrens. Private landowners also host Trail segments through handshake agreements or easements providing permanent Trail protection.

In southern Waushara County, the eastern and western branches of the Ice Age National Scenic Trail (the bifurcation) split or rejoin, depending on hike direction. The eastern branch of the Trail hosts several segments and passes near the Whistler Mounds Group historical site. The western branch of the Trail has only the Baraboo Segment, but passes near Roche-a-Cri State Park (in Adams County) and Indian Mounds Park (in Juneau County). Contact the Ice Age Trail Alliance for more information about the bifurcation.

. . .

Camping is available at a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) in the Mecan River Segment and at private campgrounds near Hancock and Coloma.

Note: Hikers should be aware that many of the roads in Waushara County share the same name and differ only in the road “suffix” paired with the road name (e.g., Beechnut Dr., Beechnut Rd.; 7th Ave., 7th Ln., 7th Dr.).

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Waushara County Chapter works to build new, and maintain current, Ice Age Trail segments. The Chapter hosts several popular gatherings each year including a fall colors hike and a winter candlelight snowshoe hike.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/waushara-county-chapter to learn more about the Chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Waushara County:** co.waushara.wi.us
**Waushara County Tourism:** wausharatourism.com

![Chaffee Creek Segment: A sun-dappled bridge encourages hikers forward.](page_203_image_1_v2.jpg)
MARK AND PAT EALY

*Chaffee Creek Segment: A sun-dappled bridge encourages hikers forward.*

## Deerfield Segment (Atlas Map 50f)

**Summary:** The Deerfield Segment is a 3.7-mile trail in Waushara County (Atlas Map 50f), running from CTH-O to Beechnut Dr.. This segment features well-managed oak woodlands, a trek across the crest of the Almond Moraine, and a descent into a dramatic tunnel channel.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **3.7 miles**: CTH-O to Beechnut Dr.

[Icon: water icon] From a small kettle lake reachable via a blue-blazed spur trail.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: hunting icon] Entire segment closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: trail icon] Short blue-blazed spur trail to small kettle pond, white-blazed loop trail to additional parking area and other types of trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] **1 CTH-O Trail Access (44.15580, -89.37052)**: From I-39 take Exit 131 (Hancock) and follow CTH-V east for 4.3 mi. Join CTH-B and go north on CTH-B 1.5 mi. At CTH-O turn right and go east 3.6 mi. From Wautoma take STH-73 north 6.7 mi. At CTH-O turn left and go west 0.4 mi. **Parking**: Roadside on the south side of the road.

[Icon: parking icon 2] **2 Beechnut Dr Trail Access (44.13707, -89.41547)**: From I-39 take Exit 131 (Hancock) and follow CTH-V east for 4.3 mi. Join CTH-B and go north 0.2 mi. At Beechnut Dr. (CTH-V) turn right and go east 1.3 mi. Where CTH-V turns north continue straight (east) on Beechnut Dr. for 0.2 mi. **Parking**: Roadside on the northeast corner of Beechnut Dr.

**Additional Parking**: Beechnut Dr. A white-blazed loop trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

This segment features well-managed oak woodlands, a trek across the crest of the Almond Moraine, and a descent into a dramatic tunnel channel. Shortly after leaving CTH-O, the hummocks the Trail traverses contain till released from the glacier as underlying ice melted.

The private landowners hosting this segment are vital partners in its development and maintenance. They practice sustainable forestry, on display in several red pine groves thinned to enable white pines to regenerate naturally in the forest understory. The landowners are also restoring the prairies along the route and have erected numerous bluebird houses. In the summer, you will also find many beautiful wildflowers and ripe blackberries. However, be aware of poison ivy along the segment.

From the Trail access on CTH-O, head south, then west, through hummocky, pitted outwash to 12th Avenue. After crossing the road, ascend to the crest of the Almond Moraine. The segment intersects various trails, including the Stagecoach Trail, a track laid down by 19th-century stagecoaches traveling regularly between Wautoma and Plainfield.

As the segment approaches its endpoint on Beechnut Drive, you’ll descend dramatically into a tunnel channel with a deep kettle at the bottom. A 0.1-mile blue-blazed spur trail leads north to the kettle. Also, a half-mile white-blazed loop trail departs south to an additional parking area on Beechnut Drive, then returns to the main route a bit farther west.

From the departure point of the blue-blazed spur trail, continue northwest, ascending out of the tunnel channel to the segment’s end on Beechnut Drive.

![Map of the Ice Age National Scenic Trail in Waushara County, showing the route between Bohn Lake Segment and Emmons Creek Segment, with road names like Beechnut Dr., 11th Ave., 12th Ave., and CTH-V.](page_205_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Wautoma:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-O Trail access go east then south 7.1 mi.

**Wild Rose:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-O Trail access go east 5.7 mi to STH-22, then head north on STH-22 for 1.7 mi.

**Hancock:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the CTH-O Trail access go west ~9 mi.

> *What a ride it was! Never have I done anything so awesome, so powerful, so unexpected, so crazy, so difficult, and so fun. And the icing on the cake was being able to spend two years, eight months, one week, and a day with my best friend, my partner in crime, and... my sister: Tweedle Dum!*

> NANCY “TWEEDLE DEE” LEHMAN, THOUSAND-MILER

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.h2: Bohn Lake Segment and Greenwood Segment (Atlas Map 51f)
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.segment_name: Bohn Lake Segment and Greenwood Segment
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.parent_text:
## Bohn Lake Segment and Greenwood Segment (Atlas Map 51f)

**Summary:** The Bohn Lake Segment and Greenwood Segment: **Bohn Lake Segment**

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: icon] [Icon: icon] **Bohn Lake Segment—1.8 miles: 9th Ln. to CTH-C at CTH-B**

* [Icon: water icon] From Bohn Lake.
* [Icon: loop trail icon] A 0.6-mi white-blazed loop trail (Lake Loop).
* [Icon: camping icon] At Hancock-area campgrounds ~4 mi west (see Area Services).
* [Icon: accessible trail icon] Nearby at the 0.2-mi Bohn Lake Accessible Hiking Trail.
* [Icon: dogs icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: icon] **Greenwood Segment—5.0 miles (4.5 IAT, 0.5 CR): 9th Ave. to Bow String Dr.**

* [Icon: no water icon] No reliable sources of water.
* [Icon: dogs icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.
* [Icon: camping icon] At Hancock-area campgrounds ~4 mi west (see Area Services).
* [Icon: road walk icon] Road walk along Brown Deer Ct.
* [Icon: loop trail icon] A white-blazed kettle loop.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **9th Ln. Trail Access** (44.12268, -89.43163): From I-39 take Exit 131 (Hancock) and follow CTH-V east for 4.4 mi. At CTH-B turn left and continue north on CTH-V/CTH-B 0.2 mi. Turn right and continue on CTH-V east 0.8 mi. At 9th Ln. turn right and go south 1.0 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Bohn Lake Accessible Trail parking area on 9th Dr.

② **CTH-C at CTH-B Trail Access** (44.11218, -89.44589): **No Parking.** Instead, park at the CTH-B parking area.

③ **9th Ave. Trail Access** (44.09840, -89.44579): **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

④ **Bow String Dr. Trail Access** (44.08117, -89.49036): From I-39 take Exit 124 (Coloma) and follow STH-21 east for 1.4 mi. At 6th Ave. turn left and go north 2.6 mi. At Buttercup Dr. turn right and go east 0.6 mi. At 6th Ln. turn left and go north 0.4 mi. At Bow String Dr. turn left and go west 90 ft. **Parking:** Roadside.

**Additional Parking:** (i) CTH-GG roadside parking. (ii) 7th Dr./Brown Deer Ct. roadside parking. (iii) Brown Deer Ct.

### THE HIKE

**Bohn Lake Segment**

This segment traverses the Bohn Lake State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA), including the Bohn Lake State Natural Area (SNA). It features Bohn Lake, an undeveloped 13-acre hard-water seepage lake that is part of a 14-mile-long glacial tunnel channel. The tunnel channel was created by a meltwater river flowing beneath glacial ice whose outlet was where the village of Hancock stands today. The lake basins along and within the tunnel, such as Bohn Lake and Fish Lake, were created from buried blocks of ice left behind when the tunnel collapsed. The northwest part of the SIATA contains a portion of an end moraine.

![Map of the Bohn Lake and Greenwood State Wildlife Area segments of the Ice Age National Scenic Trail in Waushara County, showing trail paths, access points, parking areas, and connecting routes.](page_207_image_1_v2.jpg)

From the Trail access on 9th Lane, travel through an oak forest mixed with pine and then downhill to Bohn Lake. Meet the eastern end of the white-blazed Lake Loop trail, which goes around the south side of the lake. A blue-blazed spur trail leads east from it to the 9th Drive parking area.

(For a side trip, take the white-blazed Lake Loop past a handsomely crafted log bench and interpretive panel explaining the creation of kettle lakes. This overlook section of the Lake Loop, coupled with the blue-blazed spur trail, make up the Bohn Lake Accessible Hiking Trail, an "all access" 0.2-mile route built by local volunteers offering a compacted surface with minimal grade. The DNR seeded the fields abutting the white- and blue-blazed trails with native species, converting them to prairie in support of the Karner blue butterfly.)

Back on the Trail, from the eastern junction of the Lake Loop, cross a boardwalk between Bohn Lake and a small pond. Fluctuating lake levels have joined the two water bodies, necessitating the structure. Follow the north and west shores of the lake, where tipped-over trees and mature pines and oaks surrounded by water are evidence of the changing lake levels.

Depart the lake and climb through a mixed forest with towering pines. Cut through a stand of pines and reach a parking area and the western end of the Lake Loop at CTH-B. Cross the road and meander through Scotch and white pines before the canopy opens and the Trail hooks around a dry kettle and fallow farm fields. Pass through a small stand of oaks, revealing a rock garden, an excellent place to take a break before reaching the segment’s terminus at the intersection of CTH-C and CTH-B.

From the CTH-C at CTH-B Trail access, cross CTH-C and hike 1.0 miles south on 9th Avenue to reach the Greenwood Segment.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.parent_text:
## Greenwood Segment

**Summary:** The Greenwood Segment: This segment features deep kettles, restored prairies, oak savannas, and a loop trail.

This segment features deep kettles, restored prairies, oak savannas, and a loop trail. It travels between two major meltwater tunnel channels. To the south is the Mecan River tunnel channel. To the north is the Hancock Lakes tunnel channel. Many grassy openings throughout the segment are carpeted in blue lupine in late spring and early summer, creating a welcoming environment for the endangered Karner blue butterfly. In addition, the Greenwood State Wildlife Area features a restored native prairie, providing critical habitat for grassland-dependent species.

From the Trail access at the 9th Avenue parking area, head south, then west, through the pitted outwash of the Almond Moraine. A mixed forest of stately oaks and various other woodland species shades this section. Meander along rolling hills and ridges with views of deep kettles. George and Lois Siler and family generously gifted 80 acres of land, which the Trail traverses.

Upon reaching CTH-GG, cross the road, jog left, turn right, and head west on a connecting route, following Brown Deer Court. At 7th Drive, turn right and head north for a short distance until the Trail leaves the road to the west. Continue northwest into the rolling terrain of the Greenwood State Wildlife Area.

Soon you’ll arrive at Pine Tree Point. Sit a spell on a beautifully crafted (and very comfortable) log couch and ottoman tucked under the shady pines. Enjoy a panoramic view of an open prairie edged by an oak savanna. The restored prairie, one square mile (the largest in Waushara County), features bottle gentian, blazing star, leadplant, and prairie willow.

Continue southwest from Pine Tree Point along the edge of the Almond Moraine. Cross Brown Deer Court and head southeast, where the Trail is a short distance from the moraine’s outer edge. Continue southwest and then west before dropping toward a 200-foot-deep glacial kettle that was likely part of a tunnel channel running out to the Hancock Moraine several miles west. The narrow, banana-shaped kettle is almost a mile long and has four separate ponds at the bottom. A white-blazed loop trail weaves along the north and east ridge overlooking the ponds and rejoins the segment on the south side of the kettle. From the junction with the white-blazed loop trail, head south to the segment’s endpoint on Bow String Drive.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Whistler Mounds Group & Enclosure:** Located in Whistler Mounds Park on Fish Lake just east of Hancock. From Hancock, take CTH-FF east 1.0 mi, then continue straight on Beechnut Dr. 0.3 mi to 6th Ave. (N4140 6th Ave., Hancock).

The Whistler Mounds Group & Enclosure, listed on the National Register of Historic Places, consists of two rows of conical mounds (13 mounds) and a low earthen double enclosure, one of only a few enclosures surviving development in Wisconsin. Archaeologists think the double enclosure was a sacred space where special ceremonies were held. The mounds and enclosure are believed to have been constructed by Woodland Indians during the period 650 to 1200 AD. A short trail travels past the mounds and enclosure area.

### AREA SERVICES

**Hancock:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the CTH-B Trail crossing, go north on CTH-B for 1.1 mi. At CTH-V turn left and go west 3.9 mi.

**Coloma:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the Bow String Dr. Trail access go south then west 5.6 mi.

**Wautoma:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the Bow String Dr. Trail access go south then east ~14 mi.

![A field of wild lupine flowers in a wooded area.](page_209_image_1_v2.jpg)
WILLIAM PARK

*Greenwood Segment: The Karner blue caterpillar feeds exclusively on wild lupine leaves.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.parent_text:
## Mecan River Segment (Atlas Map 52f)

**Summary:** The Mecan River Segment is a 7.5-mile trail in Waushara County (Atlas Map 52f), running from Buttercup Dr. to Cumberland Ave.. The segment travels through the Mecan River State Fishery Area, containing some of the finest trout streams in central Wisconsin.

### SNAPSHOT

* [Icon: distance icon] **7.5 miles (7.1 IAT, 0.4 CR)**: Buttercup Dr. to Cumberland Ave.
* [Icon: water icon] From the Mecan River.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: camping icon] A Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA logo] south of Buttercup Dr.
* [Icon: road walk icon] Road walk along 9th Ave.
* [Icon: campground icon] At a nearby private campground.
* [Icon: spur trail icon] A blue-blazed spur trail to the DCA, a blue-blazed spur trail to the CTH-GG parking area, and a blue-blazed spur trail to the Mecan River vista.
* [Icon: coldcache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: hunting icon] Portion of the segment crossing private land between CTH-GG and 9th Ave. is closed during gun deer season.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] ① **Buttercup Dr. Trail Access (44.07571, -89.48727)**: From I-39 take Exit 124 (Coloma) and follow STH-21 east for 1.4 mi. At 6th Ave. turn left and go north 2.6 mi. At Buttercup Dr. turn right and go east 0.8 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2 icon] ② **Cumberland Ave. Trail Access (44.02584, -89.44235)**: From I-39 take Exit 124 (Coloma) and follow STH-21 east for 4.2 mi. At 9th Ave. turn right and go south 0.3 mi, merging with Cumberland Ave. Curve east and continue on Cumberland Ave. 0.1 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) 6th Ln. (ii) CTH-GG. A 0.1-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT. (iii) DNR parking areas on 9th Ave. located 200 ft, 0.6 mi, and 0.8 mi north of STH-21.

### THE HIKE

The segment travels through the Mecan River State Fishery Area, containing some of the finest trout streams in central Wisconsin. The crystal-clear, fast-flowing Mecan River supports a naturally reproducing rainbow trout population. It is also a major tributary of the Fox River watershed in the Lake Michigan drainage.

The segment also features an excellent example of the tunnel channels that formed all along the western edge of the Green Bay Lobe during its maximum extent at the Hancock Moraine. The channel extends west several miles through the Almond Moraine to the edge of the Hancock Moraine. As the Trail bends around the western and then southern edge of the channel, you’ll have outstanding views of the Mecan Springs more than a hundred feet below. Many migratory and wetland birds visit the area, including sandhill cranes, northern bob-white quail, red-shouldered hawks, and bald eagles.

From the Trail access at the Buttercup Drive parking area, note the sign announcing the Trail’s approximate halfway point, and then head south on a grass-and-dirt access road. Head west through a pine grove, where one of the Waushara County Chapter’s signature log benches offers a shady respite. A signed spur trail leads north to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

Continue through a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA) property containing rolling hills, steep slopes, and scenic panoramas of the rural farm countryside. The property is a mix of pine groves, upland woods, and grasslands. As you go

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment in Waushara County, showing the trail route from Buttercup Dr. south through the Mecan River State Fishery Area to Richford. The map includes labels for local roads (Chicago Rd., Cottonville Ave., Cumberland Ave., CTH-GG, CTH-B, Hwy 21), water bodies (Lake Virginia, Lake Jeanette, Bing Lake, Mecan River), and parking areas. Two yellow boxes provide connecting route (CR) information: one at the top for a 0.5 mi CR to the Greenwood Lake Segment and one in the middle for a 2.3-mi CR to the Wedde Creek Segment.](page_211_image_1_v2.jpg)

south toward Chicago Road, you’ll pass through a large open field where white-tail deer are often spotted at dawn and dusk.

South of Chicago Road, head into the Mecan River headwaters area and intersect an outstanding example of a tunnel channel mentioned above. Bend south away from the springs, cross CTH-GG, and meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading west to the CTH-GG parking area.

Continue south and then east for a short distance before arriving above the banks of the Mecan River, a trout fishing stream renowned in central Wisconsin. A little farther on, a rewarding side trip on the blue-blazed Mecan Vista Trail to the north offers views of the Mecan River.

Depart the Mecan River State Fishery Area briefly and pass near the Wisconsin Operating Engineers training grounds. For safety’s sake, stay on the Trail to avoid heavy equipment.

Approaching the segment’s first crossing of 9th Avenue, you’ll reenter the fishery area property and cross through a timber harvest area, part of an oak

regeneration project. Cross 9th Avenue, continue east, and hit another pine timber harvest area. Pay close attention to Trail signage in these logged areas.

Pass rock piles cleared from pioneer farmers’ fields and traverse deciduous forests and openings of prairie and wetlands along the river. Bend back to the west and pass through a pine grove before emerging again onto 9th Avenue. Turn left and walk south along the road until the Trail leaves it again, heading southeast along the river for a short distance before reaching STH-21.

Carefully cross STH-21 and continue south, following a beautiful section of the Mecan River. Along the way to the segment’s terminus at the Cumberland Avenue parking area, you’ll find a log bench and cross a 70-foot-long boardwalk and a 32-foot-long bridge built by volunteers.

### AREA SERVICES

**Hancock:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the Buttercup Dr. Trail access go west then north ~6 mi.

**Coloma:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the STH-21 Trail access go west 4.0 mi on STH-21. Area info available at visitcoloma.com.

**Wautoma:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the STH-21 Trail access go east ~10 mi on STH-21. Area info available from Waushara Area Chamber of Commerce (920-787-3488, wausharachamber.com).

**Richford:** Restaurant, lodging. From the Cumberland Ave. Trail access go east 0.5 mi.

![Photograph of a trail winding through a dense pine forest with a carpet of needles on the ground. Photo credit: CHRIS TEMME](page_212_image_1_v2.jpg)

Mecan River Segment: A carpet of pine needles softens hikers’ footfalls.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.parent_text:
## Wedde Creek Segment and Chaffee Creek Segment (Atlas Maps 52f, 53f-E)

**Summary:** The Wedde Creek Segment and Chaffee Creek Segment: 1. **[Icon: 1] Cypress Rd. Trail Access (44.00448, -89.45647):** From I-39 take Exit 124 (Coloma) and follow STH-21 east for 4.9 mi.

### [Icon: segment icon] Wedde Creek Segment—1.2 miles: Cypress Rd. to Czech Ave.

[Icon: water drop icon] From Wedde Creek.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: snowmobile icon] Portion of the segment overlaps a snowmobile trail.

[Icon: spur trail icon] Short spur trail to parking area.

### [Icon: segment icon] Chaffee Creek Segment—7.3 miles (3.2 IAT, 4.1 miles CR): Czech Ave. to 3rd Ave.

*Note: New Ice Age Trail is planned in the Mecan River SFA west of I-39/USH-51. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, *IceAgeTrail.org*) for details.*

[Icon: rest area icon] At the Interstate 39 rest area.

[Icon: water drop icon] From Chaffee Creek.

[Icon: campground icon] At a nearby private campground (see Coloma Area Services, p. 178).

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: spur trail icon] A blue-blazed spur trail to the I-39 southbound rest area.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. **[Icon: 1] Cypress Rd. Trail Access (44.00448, -89.45647):** From I-39 take Exit 124 (Coloma) and follow STH-21 east for 4.9 mi. At CTH-B turn right and go south 0.7 mi. At CTH-JJ turn right and go south-west 1.5 mi. At Cypress Rd. turn right and go west 0.1 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

2. **[Icon: 2] Czech Ave. Trail Access (43.99683, -89.47042):** No Parking. Instead, park at the parking area 0.3 mi east on Czech Ave. A short spur trail leads to the IAT.

3. **[Icon: 3] Czech Ave. Trail Access (43.99663, -89.48415):** No Parking. Instead, park at the parking area 0.1 mi east on Czech Ave.

4. **[Icon: 4] 3rd Ave. Trail Access (43.99040, -89.56512):** From I-39 take Exit 124 (Coloma) and follow STH-21 west for 1.2 mi. At Old Hwy 21, turn left and go southeast 450 ft. At 4th Ave, turn right and go south 1.8 mi. At CTH-CC turn right and go west 1.1 mi. At 3rd Ave. turn left and go south 0.4 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) 6th Ave. cul-de-sac. (ii) I-39 Southbound Rest Area. A 0.7-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.parent_text:
## Wedde Creek Segment

**Summary:** The Wedde Creek Segment: This segment’s namesake, Wedde Creek, is a tributary to Chaffee Creek and supports a naturally reproducing rainbow trout population.

This segment’s namesake, Wedde Creek, is a tributary to Chaffee Creek and supports a naturally reproducing rainbow trout population. The valley hosting the Wedde Creek floodplain was once the bed of a small lake that ice dammed when it sat at, then retreated from, the Elderon Moraine.

From the Trail Access on Cypress Road, head south through hardwood forest and a prairie restoration area to a wooden bridge crossing of the South Branch of Wedde Creek. Before the creek crossing, you’ll meet and share a snowmobile trail.

![Map showing trail segments, roads, and natural areas in Waushara and Marquette counties, including Pleasant Lake, Wood Lake, and Chaffee Creek.](page_214_image_1_v2.jpg)

Once across the bridge, follow a ridge above the south side of the creek for a short distance before heading south and then west along the crest of a small end moraine. Look for a large erratic in an oak opening south of the Trail before meeting a spur trail leading south to a parking area on Czech Avenue.

Continue west through conifer forests and oak barrens to the segment’s endpoint on Czech Avenue, 0.3 miles west of the parking area.

Head west on a connecting route along Czech Avenue to reach the next segment.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waushara-count.parent_text:
## Chaffee Creek Segment

**Summary:** The Chaffee Creek Segment: This segment travels through three types of public land – a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA), a State Fishery Area, and a State Natural Area – protecting Chaffee Creek.

This segment travels through three types of public land – a State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA), a State Fishery Area, and a State Natural Area – protecting Chaffee Creek. The Upper Chaffee Creek Meadow recognizes the unique flora growing in the Chaffee Creek valley, including wet-mesic prairie, wetland fen complex, and sedge meadow.

The area boasts more than 100 native plant species, and prairie grasses include big and little bluestem, blue-joint grass, and slender wheat grass. The Trail also crosses privately owned Pleasant Lake Management District land. The District aims to protect and improve the 120-acre mesotrophic seepage lake for generations to come.

From the Trail access on Czech Avenue, head south on a narrow strip between two former agricultural fields, now seeded with native plants in a prairie restoration effort. As the Trail heads southwest toward the Chaffee Creek valley, you’ll pass several trailside kettles while traversing grassy meadows and savanna woodlands. Cross into the Upper Chaffee Creek Meadow, one of three units of the Upper Fox Headwaters State Natural Area, and continue toward Chaffee Creek.

![Map of the trail segment showing Chaffee Creek, I-39, and various roads including Czech Avenue, 6th Avenue, and 3rd Avenue.](page_215_image_1_v2.jpg)

At Chaffee Creek, continue west under I-39. A short distance west of the underpass is a T-intersection. To leave the Trail and reach the I-39 rest area, turn south and follow a blue-blazed spur trail. To stay on route, turn north and head toward 6th Avenue to begin a connecting route road walk. Follow 6th Avenue until it meets 5th Road. Turn northwest, following 5th Road until its junction with Czech Avenue. Follow Czech Avenue west to the intersection of CTH-CC and CTH-CH (Czech Avenue becomes CTH-CC). Continue west on CTH-CC to the intersection of 4th Avenue. Head south on 4th Avenue until it meets Czech Drive. Head west 0.4 miles, take a short jog north on 3rd Drive, then continue west on Czech Drive to where the Trail departs Czech Drive at Trail signage and enters Pleasant Lake Management District property to the north. Soon you’ll meet a white-blazed trail heading straight west and ending on 3rd Avenue. Continue on the the Trail until the segment’s endpoint on 3rd Avenue.

This is the point at which a bifurcation occurs in the Ice Age Trail, splitting the Trail into eastern and western branches that rejoin at Devil’s Lake State Park in Sauk County. Turning south on 3rd Avenue takes you along the eastern branch; going north takes you along the western branch.

### AREA SERVICES

**Richford:** Restaurant, lodging. From the Cypress Rd. Trail access go east and north ~2 mi.

**Westfield:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. From the I-39 southbound rest area go south 6.6 mi on I-39. Area info available from the Westfield Chamber of Commerce (608-296-4146, Westfield-wi.com).

**Coloma:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the Cypress Rd. Trail access go northwest ~7 mi.
**Wautoma:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the Cypress Rd. Trail access go east and north ~12 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marquette-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marquette-coun.parent_text:
# Marquette County

Atlas Map 53f-E–56f-E; Databook pages 50–51
Total miles: 43.0 (Trail 1.8; Connecting Route 41.1)

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting the location of Marquette County](page_216_image_2_v2.jpg)

The Green Bay Lobe entirely covered Marquette County. The region is part of the central sands of Wisconsin. The county is mostly undeveloped, with pine and oak forests and extensive grass marshes. The mucky-peat soils of these marshes owe their origin to two major geologic events. First, the glacier ice, advancing across east central Wisconsin, gouged out the original basins. Then water from the melting glaciers transformed the basins into lakes, sandy along the beaches, and the deep water containing fine water-sorted deposits. As the climate warmed, vegetation encroached upon the water. Over 13,000 years, sedges and marsh grasses slowly replaced the water areas. The wet environment of the marsh inhibits the decomposition of these plant remains, so it accumulates. Today’s organic soils are decayed plant residue accumulated over thousands of years. Each winter adds another layer of dead plants. Research on bogs in Wisconsin shows these organic soils form at a rate of about one inch every 40 years. Some area marshes have an organic layer more than 10 feet thick.

John Muir, known as the father of America’s National Parks, grew up on a farm near Fountain Lake, now called Ennis Lake, in an area that had been home to the Ho-Chunk. Muir’s boyhood home is the site of a national historic landmark, state natural area, and county park. The Trail segment mostly surrounds the lake and highlights the landscape where John Muir developed a profound love for nature.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Marquette County Chapter advocates for the Ice Age Trail and engages in ongoing Trail maintenance. The Chapter promotes the Trail by offering guided hikes and developing relationships with community organizations with interests in health, conservation, and preservation.

Learn more about the Chapter and download a John Muir Park Ice Age Trail guide created by chapter volunteers at IceAgeTrail.org/marquette-county-chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Marquette County Convention and Visitors Bureau**: 608-297-1000, travelmarquettecounty.com

**Marquette County Information**: co.marquette.wi.us

![A bumblebee on a purple swamp thistle flower in a field of wildflowers.](page_217_image_1_v2.jpg)

KRISTIN ALBIRHGT

John Muir Park Segment: A swamp thistle provides nourishment for a bumble bee.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marquette-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::marquette-coun.parent_text:
## John Muir Park Segment (Atlas Map 56f-E)

**Summary:** The John Muir Park Segment is a 1.8-mile trail in Marquette County (Atlas Map 56f-E), running from CTH-F to John Muir Memorial County Park entrance drive. This segment passes through prairies and meadows and is filled with wildflowers in the summer.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 1] **1.8 miles**: CTH-F to John Muir Memorial County Park entrance drive

[Icon: water] From Ennis Lake. [Icon: geology] One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: dogs] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: hiking] In John Muir Memorial County Park. A blue-blazed trail leading to the John Muir Memorial County Park parking area and a few side trails leading to Ennis Lake.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **① CTH-F Trail Access (43.69706, -89.39296):** From Portage take STH-33 east. At CTH-F turn left and go north 10.4 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **② John Muir Memorial County Park Entrance Drive Trail Access (43.69045, -89.39555):** From Portage take STH-33 east. At CTH-F turn left and go north 10.0 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

### THE HIKE

This segment passes through prairies and meadows and is filled with wildflowers in the summer. This land, the boyhood home of John Muir, made a positive and lifelong impression on the eminent naturalist and founder of the Sierra Club. Featuring views of picturesque Ennis Lake, it passes through and near open oak forests, sedge meadows, and fens bordered by tamarack and bog birch.

As the vast continental glacier melted and disintegrated, it left hummocky till and pitted outwash in the area hosting this segment. As a result, Ennis Lake sits in a kettle, and the teardrop-shaped hills of John Muir Memorial County Park and the Alliance-owned Muir Preserve are drumlins. These elongated hills — occurring in groups called swarms — formed at the bed of a glacier and parallel the direction of ice flow. While Wisconsin has thousands of drumlins, the Preserve and adjacent County Park offer one of the few places along the Trail to hike on them.

The segment crosses land homesteaded by the Ennis and Muir families; the Muirs lived on the northeast side of Ennis Lake — at the time known as Fountain Lake — from 1849 to 1856. Although John Muir traveled worldwide, he never forgot his family homestead and tried several times to buy and preserve it.

Several memorials to John Muir exist in the County Park, including a granite monument and an extensive informational display near the parking area. There is also an exhibit on his life at the Montello Museum (55 West Montello Street). The Preserve and the parks are likely the best memorials to the man; you can walk in Muir’s footsteps and see a landscape little changed since he lived here.

The segment begins on the west side of CTH-F north of the parking area, which is a site on the Marquette County John Muir Nature and History Route. It contains interpretive signage regarding the Eggleston Family, farming, and John Muir’s boyhood home. Cross CTH-F into the Alliance-owned Muir Preserve and travel east through a field undergoing conversion to a prairie.

![Map of the trail segment showing Ennis Lake, John Muir Memorial County Park, and the IATA's Muir Preserve.](page_219_image_1_v2.jpg)

Turn south and follow a tree line between another field and a sedge meadow before reaching Gillette Drive.

Cross Gillette Drive, entering John Muir Memorial County Park and State Natural Area (SNA). Climb to the top of a rise with outstanding panoramic views of Ennis Lake, a spring-fed 30-acre kettle lake occupying a marshy pocket in a ground moraine. Follow the edge of a mesic prairie, and soon you’ll meet a blue-blazed trail. It heads west around the north side of Ennis Lake to the John Muir Memorial County Park parking area.

Continue around the eastern and southern shores of Ennis Lake. Trailside benches offer a chance to enjoy the natural surroundings and contemplate the area’s rich history. Cross the rich fen surrounding the lake’s eastern inlet on a boardwalk bridge. The calcareous fen and surrounding prairies contain unusual and rare species.

As you curve around the lake’s southern end, you’ll find a bog near the lake’s southeast corner. It offers the right environment for a northern wet forest dominated by tamarack, poison sumac, and bog birch, with numerous pitcher plants beneath. Cross the fen surrounding the western outlet of the lake on another boardwalk bridge. Enter John Muir Memorial County Park and the segment’s endpoint at the park entrance on CTH-F.

### AREA SERVICES

**Montello:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, laundry, library, medical service. From John Muir Memorial County Park, go north on CTH-F 7.8 mi. At STH-22 (Main St.) turn left and continue north for 0.8 mi. Area info available from the Montello Area Chamber of Commerce (608-297-7420, montellowi.org).

[Icon: Portage icon] **Portage:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library and medical service. On Trail. Most services on USH-51/STH-16/Wisconsin St. and on STH-33. For area info, contact the Portage Area Chamber of Commerce (608-742-6242 or 800-474-2525, portagewi.com). Train service at the Portage Amtrak Station (800-872-7245, amtrak.com), an unstaffed station consisting of a small waiting room with no station services. From John Muir Memorial County Park go south ~10 mi.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Sauk and Columbia Counties, showing trail segments like Baraboo and Devil's Lake, along with connecting routes, public lands, and nearby towns like Reedsburg, Wisconsin Dells, and Baraboo.](page_220_image_1_v2.jpg)
