iat-trail-guidebook-2023::sauk-columbia-.parent_text:
# Sauk & Columbia Counties

![Map of Ice Age Trail segments in Sauk and Columbia Counties, including French Creek Wildlife Area, Portage Canal Segment, Sauk Point Segment, Merrimac Segment, Gibraltar Rock Segment, Fern Glen Segment, and Lodi Segment.](page_221_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::northern-colum.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::northern-colum.parent_text:
# Northern Columbia County

*Atlas Maps 56f-E–58f-E; Databook page 53*

*Total miles: 18.2 (Trail 2.5; Connecting Route 15.7)*

![Map of Wisconsin with Columbia County highlighted](page_222_image_2_v2.jpg)

The Green Bay Lobe covered nearly all of Columbia County during the Wisconsin Glaciation. When the ice sheet receded, the Green Bay Lobe left behind the Fox River, one of the few major rivers in Wisconsin flowing northward.

The Fox River empties into Lake Winnebago and flows northward to Green Bay. The waters eventually flow through the Great Lakes and the St. Lawrence River to the north Atlantic. North of the Wisconsin River, the Ice Age Trail traverses low, rolling topography with extensive wetlands, grasslands, and forested woodlots.

Portage, an Ice Age Trail Community, lies on a large outwash plain deposited by the Wisconsin River when it carried meltwater from the retreating glacier.

The Trail winds its way through the city dotted with historical sites, including the Portage Canal and locks, the Historic Indian Agency House, and the Fort Winnebago Surgeon’s Quarters. (Juliette M. Kinzie’s book, *Wau-Bun: The “Early Day” in the Northwest*, recorded transactions with Ho-Chunk (Winnebago) populations and featured these sites.)

The Surgeon’s Quarters is the only remaining building of Fort Winnebago, a National Historic Landmark. Fort Winnebago, built in 1828, underscored the strategic importance of the portage between the Fox and Wisconsin rivers, a heavily traveled connection between the Great Lakes and the Mississippi River. Its location was also near the site where Red Bird, a Ho-Chunk leader in the skirmish known as the Winnebago War, surrendered.

In 1822, the United States’ westward expansion significantly encroached on Ho-Chunk land: the government began leasing lands in southwestern Wisconsin to lead miners. Conflicts between lead miners and the Ho-Chunk people led to an 1827 skirmish known as the Winnebago War – a dramatic name given to the few attacks on American civilians by a small number of Ho-Chunk. A show of U.S. military force ended the conflict, and Ho-Chunk chiefs surrendered eight men who had participated in the violence. One of them, Red Bird, the suspected ringleader, died in prison in 1828 while awaiting trial. However, the conflict encouraged the construction of Fort Winnebago and brought about the Treaty of 1829, the first of several land losses for the Ho-Chunk.

Today, Portage, a bustling Trail Community, eagerly shares its history, through self-guided walking tours and numerous museums. It also offers plenty

of small-town charm along with pre- or post-hike meals and various retail therapy options.

The Ice Age Trail route in the northern part of Columbia County is yet to be determined. The connecting route (see map on p. 189) currently highlights the Fox River and French Creek State Wildlife Area. The western connecting route near Wisconsin Dells passes not far from the Kingsley Bend Mound Group, both a historical and a religious Ho-Chunk location.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Baraboo Hills/Heritage Chapter cares for the Ice Age Trail as it winds through Northern Columbia County. The Chapter works with the City of Portage and local conservation agencies to build and maintain the Trail.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/baraboo-hills-heritage-chapter to learn more about the Chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Columbia County Visitor Bureau:** 608-742-6161, travelcolumbiacounty.net

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Portage:** 608-742-6242, portagewi.com

![A canoe sits in the Portage Canal with reflections of trees and sky on the water.](page_223_image_1_v2.jpg)
BRAD CONRAD

Portage Canal Segment: A summer afternoon’s serene reflections.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::northern-colum.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::northern-colum.parent_text:
## Portage Canal Segment (Atlas Map 57f-E)

**Summary:** The Portage Canal Segment is a 3.6-mile trail in Northern Columbia County (Atlas Map 57f-E), running from Agency House Rd. to STH-33 Wisconsin River Bridge (south end).

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **3.6 miles (2.5 IAT, 1.1 CR):** Agency House Rd. to STH-33 Wisconsin River Bridge (south end)

[Icon: water icon] At the Historic Indian Agency House (when open) and Pauquette Park. Restrooms also available at Riverside Park.

[Icon: no hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.

[Icon: restroom icon] [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: water icon] [Icon: bike icon] From the Fox River. Segment overlaps Portage Canal Bike Path.

[Icon: parking icon] [Icon: levee icon] At the Historic Indian Agency House, STH-33 Portage Canal parking area, Riverside Park and Pauquette Park. Wisconsin River Levee Trail.

[Icon: swimming icon] [Icon: wheelchair icon] At Pauquette Park. Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::northern-colum.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::northern-colum.parent_text:
## TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKWING

[Icon: access point 1 icon] **① Agency House Rd. Trail Access (43.56044, -89.43544):** From I-39 take STH-33 east for 3.1 mi. At E. Albert St. turn left and then take a quick right onto Agency House Rd. (Rustic Road R69) and go northeast for 0.8 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: access point 2 icon] **② STH-33 Wisconsin River Bridge (south end) Trail Access (43.53522, -89.47403):** From I-39 take STH-33 east 1.1 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) STH-33 Portage Canal Parking area. (ii) Riverside Park. (iii) Pauquette Park. (iv) City and County public parking areas along the canal.

### THE HIKE

This segment takes hikers through one of Wisconsin’s most historically significant communities. The Trail’s route through Portage, an Ice Age Trail Community, highlights the city’s unique geographic location on a narrow neck of land between the Fox and Wisconsin Rivers.

You’ll briefly travel on Wauona Trail, a street following the canoe portage path used by Indigenous peoples, French explorers, and fur trappers as they moved between the Great Lakes and the Mississippi River basin. The settlement, which developed because of the resulting traffic, was known as “Wau-wau-ohah,” Ho-Chunk for “carry on the shoulder.” With the arrival of the French, the area became known as “le portage.”

The economic importance of these waterways, and conflicts between the area’s Indigenous tribes and American settlers, led to the construction of Fort Winnebago in 1828. The route passes near the Surgeons Quarters, (fortwinnebagosurgeonsquarters. org) the fort’s only remaining building, a National Historic Landmark (1828–1845).

The segment also features the Historic Indian Agency House (agencyhouse.org. ) Built in 1832, it is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The U.S. government constructed it as a residence for John Kinzie, the first Indian agent to the Ho-Chunk tribe.

In the 1830s, Indian agents primarily supervised commercial trading between traders and Indians. Over time, their role evolved to negotiating treaties, overseeing treaty terms, and eventually, the removal of tribes from areas procured for white settlement to reservations. Then, Indian agents supervised reservation life in an effort to assimilate Indigenous people into American culture.

The Trail also travels along the Portage Canal, the segment’s namesake. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places, its construction began in 1838. Use of the canal began in the early 1850s, and boats used the canal until 1951, when the locks were permanently closed.

The Alliance-owned Canal Preserve permanently protects land along the canal for the Ice Age Trail. The City of Portage offers a wide range of amenities befitting an Ice Age Trail Community. Its small town charm and plentiful restaurant options will satisfy any hiker.

From the Trail access and parking area on Agency House Road, cross a footbridge and walk on a flat, wide, tree-lined, packed-gravel path following the east bank of the Portage Canal. Along the way, boundary markers indicate when you enter and exit the Alliance-owned Canal Preserve. Arrive at another parking area, just north of STH-33. (From here, the Fort Winnebago Surgeons Quarters is a 0.5-mile side trip [one way] northeast on STH-33.)

Hike 0.2 miles through the STH-33 parking lot and then east on STH-33, walking along the shoulder. At Wauona Trail (a street), turn right and follow it south for 0.7 miles. At Morgan Street, turn right, following it west to East Mullett Street. Go straight across East Mullett Street to a paved path along the canal. Follow it, heading southwest, paralleling the canal.

Upon reaching busy Wisconsin Street (USH-51), cross at the crosswalks and join the bike path on the north side of the canal. You’ll pass a pedestrian bridge leading to the Portage Levee, a shared-use path along the Wisconsin River, from which you can view a historic restored watchtower. When you reach

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Portage, Wisconsin, showing the route along the Portage Canal, through city streets, and connecting to the John Muir Park and Sauk Point segments via the Eastern Bifurcation route. Key landmarks include the Historic Indian Agency House, Fort Winnebago Surgeon's Quarters, IATA's Canal Preserve, and various city parks and streets.](page_225_image_1_v2.jpg)

the Wisconsin River lock at the south end of the canal, jog to the right on Lock Street and then follow Edgewater Street to the left.

Continue on Edgewater Street, passing 506 West Edgewater Street, a large white Greek Revival-style house built in 1906 by Pulitzer Prize-winning author Zona Gale, for her parents. It is now home to the Portage Women’s Civic League. Gale’s study and other exhibits on local history can be seen nearby at the Museum at the Portage (portagemuseum.org) about half a mile off the Trail at 804 MacFarlane Road in a home she shared with her husband, William Breese. Historian Frederick Jackson Turner also called Portage home.

Near the segment’s end, you’ll arrive at the STH-33 bridge across the Wisconsin River. At the northwest corner of the bridge crossing is Pauquette Park, named after Pierre Pauquette, the son of a Frenchman and a Ho-Chunk woman. An agent for the American Fur Company, Pauquette also operated a ferry and trading post at the site between the mid-1820s and 1834. Follow STH-33 across the river and finish your hike at the bridge’s south end.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community logo]

**Portage**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library and medical service. On Trail. Most services on USH-51/STH-16/Wisconsin St. and on STH-33. For area info, contact the Portage Area Chamber of Commerce (608-742-6242 or 800-474-2525, portagewi.com). Train service at the Portage Amtrak Station (800-872-7245, amtrak.com), an unstaffed station consisting of a small waiting room with no station services.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

*   **Man Mound Park**: See Baraboo Segment, p. 198
*   **Aldo Leopold Legacy Center**: See Devil’s Lake Segment, p. 203
*   **International Crane Foundation (ICF)**: See Devil’s Lake Segment, p. 203

> *Every day was an experience. I have lived most of my life in Wisconsin, yet I did not know the state. I learned as I went. Every corner, town, and view had something to teach me.*
>
> — MARK W. ASPLUND, THOUSAND-MILER

![A photograph of a brown metal pedestrian bridge spanning a calm river, with its reflection visible in the water. Bare trees and some green vegetation are on the riverbanks.](page_227_image_1_v2.jpg)

AMY BAYER

*Portage Canal Segment: A pedestrian bridge spans the Wisconsin River.*

# Sauk County

Atlas Maps 58f-E—59f-E, 58f-W—60f-W and 61f—62f
Databook pages 54–56
Total miles: 35.1 (Trail 18.5; Connecting Route 16.5)

![Map of Sauk County showing the Ice Age Trail route](page_228_image_2_v2.jpg)

**T**he Baraboo Hills are the dominant feature of the Ice Age Trail in Sauk County. The hills, formed 1.6 billion years ago, rise 500 feet above the surrounding landscape. The Green Bay Lobe covered the eastern half of these hills and deposited the end moraine that dams Devil’s Lake at both ends. The Ice Age Trail crosses glacial outwash plains and small moraines to the north and south of the Baraboo Hills.

Devil’s Lake State Park is an Ice Age National Scientific Reserve unit, and the Ice Age Trail winds through the park and around the lake. A combination of fascinating geology, diverse fauna, prehistoric effigy mounds, historic Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) buildings, and spectacular scenery makes Devil’s Lake a popular destination. East of Devil’s Lake State Park, Parfrey’s Glen State Natural Area anchors the current Ice Age Trail route. In addition, the state recognizes several other designated State Natural Areas within the park, including East Bluff State Natural Area, Devil’s Lake Oak Forest State Natural Area, and South Bluff/Devil’s Nose State Natural Area.

The name “Devil’s Lake” is a mistranslation of several names Indigenous people gave the lake, which they believed to be the abode of good and evil spirits. In addition to the Bird Effigy Mound and other mounds in Devil’s Lake State Park, Man Mound Park, a National Historic Landmark near Baraboo, contains the only human-shaped effigy in North America.

In Devil’s Lake State Park, the two branches of Ice Age Trail’s bifurcation, which split in Waushara County, reunite (or split depending on hike direction). The western branch arrives after passing through the lakebed of Glacial Lake Wisconsin, the Wisconsin Dells, Mirror Lake, and Baraboo. The eastern branch arrives after passing through Marquette County, the city of Portage, and Pine Island State Wildlife Area. Contact the Ice Age Trail Alliance for more information on the bifurcation.

The City of Baraboo, an Ice Age Trail Community, boasts a vibrant, picturesque downtown, offering an array of shops, restaurants, and services. Steeped in circus tradition, it is the former winter home of the Ringling Brothers’ Circus.

. . .

Camping is available at Devil’s Lake State Park and private campgrounds near Baraboo, along with options listed in connection to the Merrimac Segment.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Baraboo Hills/Heritage Chapter members conserve, create, and maintain the Ice Age Trail in Sauk County. They also promote the Trail by hosting family-friendly hikes and offering the "Hikin' the Hills" hiking award program. Participants earn a patch for hiking all Ice Age Trail segments in Sauk County. Start your Hikin' the Hills adventure by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

At IceAgeTrail.org/baraboo-hills-heritage-chapter you can learn more about the Chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Sauk County Tourism Information**: co.sauk.wi.us

[Icon: Ice Age Community logo] **Ice Age Community, Baraboo and West Baraboo**: 608-448-3490, downtownbaraboo.com; and the Baraboo Area Chamber of Commerce, 608-356-8333, baraboo.com.

![Devil’s Lake Segment: Orange leaves offer a perfect complement to the lake’s deep blue.](page_229_image_1_v2.jpg)

CATHY WILLIAMS

Devil’s Lake Segment: Orange leaves offer a perfect complement to the lake’s deep blue.

## Baraboo Segment (Atlas Map 60f-W)

**Summary:** The Baraboo Segment is a 4.0-mile trail in Sauk County (Atlas Map 60f-W), running from UW-Platteville Baraboo Sauk County to Effinger Rd. at Manchester St. This segment begins along the edge of the Johnstown Moraine before dropping into the delta of what was once glacial Lake Baraboo.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: access point 2 icon] [Icon: access point 1 icon] **4.0 miles**: UW-Platteville Baraboo Sauk County to Effinger Rd. at Manchester St

*Note: A reroute is planned south of Water St. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

* [Icon: water tap icon] On the UW-Platteville Baraboo Sauk County campus, Ochsner Park and Zoo, Attridge Park, Mary Rountree Park, Statz Park and Maxwell-Potter Conservancy Area.
* [Icon: compass icon] Segment is entirely in the city of Baraboo.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: water drop icon] From the Baraboo River.
* [Icon: bike and pedestrian icon] Portions follow sidewalks or roads and the Baraboo Riverwalk. The Riverwalk is also open to bikes.
* [Icon: trail intersection icon] Other trails in some city parks.
* [Icon: restroom icon] At several of the city parks. Restroom also available at UW-Platteville Baraboo Sauk County campus.
* [Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: access point 1 icon] **① UW-Platteville Baraboo Sauk County Trail Access (43.48397, -89.76321):** From I-90/94 take Exit 92 and follow USH-12E south for 7.0 mi. Take Exit 218 and follow STH-33 (Linn St.) east for 0.6 mi. At Connie Rd. turn left and go north 0.5 mi. Trail access is at the north end of the parking area to the west of the disc golf kiosk. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: access point 2 icon] **② Effinger Rd. at Manchester St. Trail Access (43.46004, -89.72798):** From I-90/94 take Exit 106 and follow STH-33 west for 11.9 mi. At Washington St. turn left and go south 0.6 mi. At Water St. (STH-113) turn left and go east less than 0.1 mi. At Effinger Rd. turn right and go south 0.4 mi to its intersection with Manchester St. **No Parking.** Instead, park roadside on Water St.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Ochsner Park and Zoo. (ii) Attridge Park. (iii) Lower Ochsner Park. (iv) Mary Rountree Park. (v) Statz Park. (vi) Maxwell-Potter Conservancy on Hill St.

### THE HIKE

This segment begins along the edge of the Johnstown Moraine before dropping into the delta of what was once glacial Lake Baraboo. It also shows off a picturesque Ice Age Trail Community, with the Baraboo River flowing beside a network of city parks. Baraboo’s vibrant downtown offers an outstanding array of shops, restaurants, and services.

From the Trail access at the UW-Platteville Baraboo Sauk County parking area, follow a ridge with fine views toward the Baraboo Range. Pass through campus property featuring mixed hardwoods, remnant prairie, and oak savanna. You will also pass a small, scenic glacial kettle with cattails and a rock ledge. Cross the campus’ disc golf course and bend south, skirting area schools’ athletic fields. Pass through Baraboo High School’s parking area and meet Berkeley Boulevard.

Head east, then south, a short distance on Berkeley Boulevard, then continue east on 9th Avenue. At Draper Street, turn south and cross 8th Avenue (Ringling Boulevard/STH-33). Continue through a wooded area in Ochsner Park and Zoo, then down steep stone stairs to meet the Baraboo Riverwalk. The

![Map of the Baraboo Riverwalk segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the route through West Baraboo and Baraboo, with connections to Chaffee Creek and Devil's Lake segments.](page_231_image_1_v2.jpg)

Riverwalk parallels the Baraboo River for most of its route as it goes through or connects to several city parks.

Once on the Riverwalk route, head south through Lower Ochsner Park, passing an old bridge crossing the Baraboo River to Attridge Park. Bend east on 2nd Avenue a short distance to skirt private property along the river. Then head south into Mary Rountree Park and back toward the river before resuming an eastward course into Statz Park. Cross underneath Broadway Street and continue to follow the Riverwalk to Water Street (STH-113).

On Water Street (STH-113), you’ll pass Circus World. This National and State Historic Landmark was the site of Ringlingville, the Ringling Bros. Circus winter quarters from 1884 to 1918. It is home to 200 preserved circus wagons and displays extensive historical circus exhibits.

Depart from Water Street at Effinger Road and head south to the segment’s terminus at Manchester Street.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Man Mound Park:** 4.7 mi from the Ice Age Trail. From Water St. (STH-113), head east and turn left on Washington Ave., then right on 8th St. (STH-33), then left on CTH-T, then right onto Man Mound Rd. to the park (E13085 Man Mound Rd., Baraboo).

Man Mound is the only surviving anthropomorphic (human-shaped) effigy in North America, a National Historic Landmark, measuring 214 ft by 48 ft, its legs damaged by road construction.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community Logo] **Baraboo and West Baraboo:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Baraboo area info available from the Baraboo Chamber of Commerce (800-227-2266, baraboo.com and Downtown Baraboo (608-448-3490, downtownbaraboo.com).

**Devil’s Lake State Park:** Seasonal concession stand, camping. On Trail (608-356-8301, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/devilslake; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). From the UW-Platteville Baraboo Sauk County campus go south ~5 mi.

## Sauk Point Segment (Atlas Map 61f)

**Summary:** The Sauk Point Segment is a 3.9-mile trail in Sauk County (Atlas Map 61f), running from CTH-DL to STH-113. This segment highlights a very quiet corner of Devil’s Lake State Park and offers access to an extra adventure, spectacular Parfrey’s Glen State Natural Area (SNA), Wisconsin’s first SNA.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage icon]
**3.9 miles (3.8 IAT, 0.1 CR):** CTH-DL to STH-113

[Icon: water drop icon]
Parfrey’s Glen Creek is ~ 0.1 mi from the Trail on the Parfrey’s Glen Trail.

[Icon: dog icon]
Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not allowed in Parfrey’s Glen State Natural Area (SNA) except on the IAT.**

[Icon: picnic and restroom icon]
At Parfey’s Glen parking area.

[Icon: globe icon]
Two ColdCache sites on segment.

[Icon: spur trail icon]
Trail into Parfrey’s Glen State Natural Area and blue-blazed spur trail to Solum Ln. parking area.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon]
① **CTH-DL Trail Access (43.40946, -89.63694):** From Baraboo at the intersection of USH-12 and STH-33, take STH-33 east (Linn St. becomes 8th Ave.) for 2.5 mi. At Washington Ave. turn right and go south 0.6 mi. At STH-113 turn left and continue south 5.4 mi. At CTH-DL turn left and go east 2.0 mi to Parfrey’s Glen State Natural Area. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access. Overflow roadside parking available.

[Icon: 2 icon]
② **STH-113 Trail Access** (43.42547, -89.68648): From Baraboo at the intersection of USH-12 and STH-33, take STH-33 east (Linn St. becomes 8th Ave.) for 2.5 mi. At Washington Ave. turn right and go south 0.6 mi. At STH-113 turn left and continue south 3.8 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** Solum Ln. parking area. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights a very quiet corner of Devil’s Lake State Park and offers access to an extra adventure, spectacular Parfrey’s Glen State Natural Area (SNA), Wisconsin’s first SNA. Native wildflowers are abundant along the segment during the spring and summer. You may notice occasional large “wolf trees” surrounded by younger trees. This configuration indicates the area was probably farmland or open pasture before being allowed to transition back to forest a few decades ago. The segment passes into the state park and a more mature forest, including red oak, white oak, sugar maple, red elm, and shagbark hickory, as well as jack-in-the-pulpit and trillium. You may note oaks with double trunks. These trees, harvested 60 to 80 years ago, regenerated, sending up multiple sprouts. The segment climbs up and follows the edge of the Johnstown Moraine.

From the Trail access at the Parfrey’s Glen parking area, climb gently but consistently uphill on a narrow single-track trail through a mixed hardwood forest, including pockets of red cedar and groves of aspen. As you traverse several openings in the woods, you may glimpse a nearby cell tower marking Sauk Point, the highest point in Sauk County. Head west and soon reach a junction with a blue-blazed spur trail leading 0.3 miles south to a parking area on Solum Lane.

Once past the spur trail, you’ll climb to the segment’s highest elevation – the moraine crest – at the Blue Mounds Vista. A bench in a grassy area (likely overgrown in the summer) offers spectacular views of the lower Wisconsin River

![Map of Devil's Lake State Park and Parfrey's Glen State Natural Area showing the Ice Age Trail segments, including the Devil's Lake Segment via the Eastern Bifurcation route and the route to the Canal Segment.](page_233_image_1_v2.jpg)

valley and (about 30 miles away) the Blue Mounds. On a clear day when leaves are off, you can look southeast and see the state capitol’s dome in Madison.

From the Blue Mounds Vista, you’ll head downhill. Traverse hummocky ridges, rocky downhill stretches, and a few dry stream crossings. You’ll briefly cross a farm field before following a rolling ridge to the segment’s endpoint on STH-113; the Trail access is at the Johnstown Moraine.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Parfrey’s Glen State Natural Area:** On Trail. (1377 CTH-DL, Merrimac, devilslakewisconsin.com/parfreys-glen).

Designated in 1952 as Wisconsin’s first State Natural Area (SNA), Parfrey’s Glen offers a 0.7-mi nature trail passing through a spectacular deep gorge carved into the sandstone conglomerate of embedded quartzite pebbles and boulders on the south flank of the Baraboo Hills. The uppermost part of the Glen, with its jumble of huge fallen rocks, is nearly 100 feet deep. Hikers will pass moss-covered walls moist from seepage before the trail ends at a small waterfall. The Glen’s unique microclimate grows northern flora, including white pine, yellow birch, mountain maple and rare cliff plants. An unusual aquatic ecosystem flourishes in the fast, cold, hardwater stream that flows through the gorge and the Glen harbors a diverse insect fauna with a few rare and threatened species. Pets are not allowed in Parfrey’s Glen SNA except along the Ice Age Trail. The area is open from 6 a.m. to 8 p.m.

### AREA SERVICES

**Devil’s Lake State Park:** Seasonal concession stand, camping. On Trail (608-356-8301, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/devilslake; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). From the STH-113 Trail access go west ~3 mi.

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Baraboo and West Baraboo:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Baraboo area info available from the Baraboo Chamber of Commerce (800-227-2266, baraboo.com and Downtown Baraboo (608-448-3490, downtownbaraboo.com). From the STH-113 Trail access go north ~5 mi.

![A photograph of a forest trail featuring a path of large, flat stones laid through a wet, wooded area.](page_234_image_1_v2.jpg)

JILL ELLINWOOD

*Sauk Point Segment: Elegant stonework mitigates a wet section of Trail.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::sauk-county::d.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::sauk-county::d.parent_text:
## Devil’s Lake Segment (Atlas Map 61f)

**Summary:** The Devil’s Lake Segment is a 10.9-mile trail in Sauk County (Atlas Map 61f), running from STH-113 Northern Trail Access to STH-113 Southern Trail Access. This segment offers the most dramatic Ice Age Trail route as it explores Wisconsin’s largest and most-visited state park.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 4] [Icon: 3] **10.9 miles:** STH-113 Northern Trail Access to STH-113 Southern Trail Access

* [Icon: water faucet] [Icon: hiker] [Icon: picnic table] At various locations throughout Devil’s Lake State Park (DLSP).
* [Icon: globe] Six ColdCache sites on segment.
* [Icon: dog] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: swimmer] Water from Devil’s Lake.
* [Icon: dashed line] Portions overlap DLSP roads, bike and ski trails. Hike well off to the side when ski trails are groomed.
* [Icon: tent] At DLSP campgrounds and several nearby private campgrounds [Icon: triangle].
* [Icon: dotted line] Extensive DLSP trail network.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. [Icon: 1] **STH-113 Northern Trail Access (43.42547, -89.68648):** From Baraboo at the intersection of USH-12 and STH-33, take STH-33 east (Linn St. becomes 8th Ave.) for 2.5 mi. At Washington Ave. turn right and go south 0.6 mi. At STH-113 turn left and continue south 3.8 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.
2. ![2](page_235_image_9_v2.jpg) **STH-113 Southern Trail Access (43.40561, -89.67520):** From Baraboo at the intersection of USH-12 and STH-33, take STH-33 east (Linn St. becomes 8th Ave.) for 2.5 mi. At Washington Ave. turn right and go south 0.6 mi. At STH-113 turn left and continue south 5.3 mi. From Merrimac at the intersection of Baraboo St. and STH-113/78, take STH-113/78 west for 2.5 mi. Continue on STH-113 north for 2.3 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** Parking areas throughout DLSP.

### THE HIKE

This segment offers the most dramatic Ice Age Trail route as it explores Wisconsin’s largest and most-visited state park. It offers magnificent views from 500-foot quartzite bluffs overlooking a 360-acre lake. Devil’s Lake State Park is a place of striking contrasts, beginning with its name. “Devil’s Lake” misinterprets the Ho-Chunk’s names for it. Te Wakacakra (Ta-wah-cun-chunk-dah) or De Wakacakra (Da-wa-kah-char-graa) are better translated to “Holy Lake” or “Spirit Lake.”

The purple rock in the walls of Devil’s Lake gorge, called Baraboo quartzite, is more than 1.6 billion years old. The glacially deposited ridges – moraines – blocking both ends of the gorge are only about 16,000 years old. A three-dimensional topographical map of the park displayed at the Nature Center shows that the moraines wrapped around high points in the landscape. Ice filled the lowlands in the park’s eastern part and flowed into both ends of the gorge but did not advance onto the higher western parts of the landscape. The ice left behind two prominent moraines, and they plugged both ends of the ancient gorge, now occupied by Devil’s Lake.

You will enjoy breathtaking western views of the park, showcasing the unglaciated Driftless Area from the many rock ledges the Trail passes along East Bluff’s rim. Cold airflow from the bluffs provides a habitat for unusual northern plant species. Areas of dry prairie, red oak, and maple forest are found atop the bluffs.

From the northern Trail access parking area on STH-113, head northeast on a short blue-blazed spur trail to the Trail. Head southwest and quickly meet DLSP’s Upland Trail Loop. Turn right and hike northwest (following the loop counterclockwise) to a junction with the park’s Johnson Moraine Loop. At the junction, turn right and head north across CTH-DL.

Make your way west through a mix of meadows and woodlands, then drop down to the park’s Ice Age campground and meet the campground road between sites 419 and 420. Follow the campground road south and then west. Follow a park road out of the campground and under CTH-DL. Continue southwest along the park road, skirting the Northern Lights campground. Turn sharply left past the park’s amphitheater parking lot, cut through woods, and join the Ice Age and Northern Lights campgrounds’ exit road. Soon you’ll reach the park’s main exit road. This intersection is where the two branches of the Ice Age Trail bifurcation reunite. The connecting route from the western branch (which originated at the south end of the Baraboo Segment) enters from the northwest on the park’s exit road.

From the bifurcation intersection, continue on Park Road, cross a set of railroad tracks, and turn left just before the park headquarters. Pass through the North Shore Picnic Area, with a seasonal concession stand, water, and restrooms. Continue west, paralleling the north shore of Devil’s Lake on an asphalt path. Head north from the lakeshore to connect with the park’s West Bluff Trail.

The portion of the segment that follows the West Bluff Trail climbs steeply to the top of the West Bluff, 500 feet above lake level. Along the top of the bluff are lookout points with excellent views of Devil’s Lake and the surrounding hills and moraines. Because it is in the Driftless Area, there are no erratics on the West Bluff. It is a popular rock-climbing area that is said to have more than 2,000 possible climbing routes. The segment passes through mixed woodlands with hardwoods and pine. It also features many woodland wildflowers, such as jack-in-the-pulpit, Solomon’s seal, wild geranium, tinkers weed, tick trefoil, and milkweed.

Descend gradually from the West Bluff to South Lake Road (South Lake Drive). Head east along the road’s left side and Devil’s Lake’s south shore. Where the road departs the lake, turn left along a boardwalk and traverse the South Shore Picnic Area, with a seasonal concession stand, water, and restrooms. Pass Bird Effigy Mound, the largest and most impressive of many Native American mounds in the park.

Cross railroad tracks and turn left. Follow the park’s Balanced Rock Trail up steep stone steps and past Balanced Rock (slightly off-trail) to an intersection with the park’s East Bluff Trail. This short ascent can be strenuous and challenging. Use caution when climbing on the rocks (especially when wet), and pay close attention to Trail signage.

When you meet the East Bluff Trail, turn right and head east along it, where you’ll enjoy spectacular views of the South Bluff, Baraboo Hills, and the terminal moraine below. This section is shaded in places by small oaks and gnarled red cedars. There are also remnants of dry prairie here with flora, including blazing star, leadplant, yellow false foxglove, shooting star, sunflower, goldenrod,

![Map of Devil's Lake State Park showing trail segments, including the Ice Age Trail, West Bluff Trail, East Bluff Trail, Upland Trail Loop, and connections to Baraboo and Sauk Point segments.](page_237_image_1_v2.jpg)

and aster, as well as big bluestem grass. From the many rock ledges along the south face of the East Bluff, watch for turkey vultures and raptors soaring, sometimes at eye level. Signs point to a short loop trail to Devil’s Doorway, the iconic rock formation of the park.

Soon after, you’ll reach a junction with the Potholes Trail. From here, you can choose an exciting but strenuous side trip. (Create a loop trail by hiking down the bluff on the Potholes Trail, turning east at the bottom of the bluff on the Grottos Trail, and climbing back up the bluff on the CCC Trail to reconnect with the Ice Age Trail near the Moldy Buttress cliffside rock formation. The Potholes Trail passes ancient potholes or depressions, not far below the top of the bluff, that were carved in the rock by fast-moving water. The CCC Trail commemorates the CCC workers who built many of the park’s trails during the Great Depression. Not allowed to use dynamite, they placed the rocks by hand.)

Continuing east, depart from the park’s East Bluff Trail at the Moldy Buttress cliffside rock formation via a connector trail heading north. Turn east to connect with the park’s Upland Trail Loop. Follow the Upland Trail Loop east through woodlands and depart from it at another trail intersection. Continue east, dropping down the south face of the East Bluff. You may catch views of an immense unbroken sandstone escarpment in the park, a rare hanging sedge

meadow, and many rock outcrops. Pass a babbling brook in a diverse woodland with several species of ferns and wildflowers such as mayapple, milkweed, goldenrod, aster, and tick trefoil.

Cross South Lake Road (South Lake Drive) and enter Roznos Meadow. Here the segment traverses open prairie and grassland, giving 360-degree views of the surrounding Baraboo Hills towering above. A National Park Service interpretive sign explains the moraine dam, an important glacial feature influencing the surrounding landscape. End your hike at the STH-113 southern Trail access parking area.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Aldo Leopold Legacy Center:** From the Baraboo area on USH-12, take exit 218 for STH-33 East and STH-136. Take STH-33 (Linn St. becomes 8th Ave.) east for 2.9 mi. At CTH-T turn left and go north 7.0 mi. At Levee Rd. (Rustic Road 49) turn left and go east 2.4 mi to the entrance of the Legacy Center on the right (E13701 Levee Rd., Baraboo, 608-355-0279, aldoleopold.org).

The Aldo Leopold Foundation operates the Aldo Leopold Legacy Center, which is an educational and interpretive facility near the Leopold “Shack.” Here Aldo Leopold converted a chicken coop on his farm and wrote part of his conservation classic masterpiece on land ethics, A Sand County Almanac. This is also the very same land where Aldo Leopold died in 1948 fighting a brush fire. The Legacy Center is an excellent place to learn more about this famous American naturalist and see how the Leopold Foundation is carrying out his message of land ethics today. The Center is open from mid-April to the end of October with more limited hours during winter. Various guided and self-guided tours are available. Visit the website or contact the Aldo Leopold Foundation and Legacy Center for more information.

**International Crane Foundation (ICF):** From the Baraboo area on USH-12, exit 214 for North Reedsburg Rd. Take N. Reedsburg Rd. east 0.4 mi. At CTH-BD turn left and go north 1.0 mi. At Shady Lane Rd. turn right and go 1.0 mi. (E11376 Shady Lane Rd., Baraboo, 608-356-9462, savingcranes.org).

The International Crane Foundation (ICF) works worldwide to conserve cranes and the eco-systems, watersheds and flyways on which they depend. ICF is dedicated to providing experience, knowledge and inspiration to involve people in resolving threats to these ecosystems.

At its Baraboo headquarters, visitors can see all 15 species of cranes. The site offers guided and self-guided tours, an interactive educational center, live crane exhibits and over 4.0 mi of hiking trails over 100 acres of restored prairie, oak savanna and wetlands. *See website for visiting hours.*

### AREA SERVICES

**Devil’s Lake State Park:** Seasonal concession stand, camping. On Trail (608-356-8301, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/devilslake; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

[Icon: Trail access icon] ****Baraboo and West Baraboo:**** *See Baraboo Segment, p. 198.* From the STH-113 northern Trail access go north ~5 mi.

> *My favorite time of day to hike was always in the late afternoon. I loved the glowing red-orange sun, long shadows on the Trail and CRs, and the significance of another hike ending.*
>
> *MARTA WELDON, THOUSAND-MILER*

![A late-afternoon sky over a field of wildflowers and trees at the Devil's Lake Segment. Photo by Ross Mori.](page_239_image_1_v2.jpg)

Devil’s Lake Segment: A late-afternoon sky sets the scene.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::sauk-county::m.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::sauk-county::m.parent_text:
## Merrimac Segment (Atlas Map 61f)

**Summary:** The Merrimac Segment is a 3.7-mile trail in Sauk County (Atlas Map 61f), running from STH-113 to Marsh Rd. Southern Trail Access. This segment offers a quiet break from the crowds at Devil’s Lake.

### SNAPSHOT

* [Icon: distance icon] **3.7 miles**: STH-113 to Marsh Rd. Southern Trail Access
* **Note**: Riverland Conservancy recommends all visitors to the Merrimac Preserve (this entire segment) wear blaze orange during gun deer and muzzleloader hunting seasons.
* [Icon: camping icon] At nearby private campground and Devil’s Lake State Park.
* [Icon: snowmobile icon] Briefly overlaps snowmobile trails.
* [Icon: trail network icon] Riverland Conservancy trail network.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking 1] **STH-113 Southern Trail Access (43.40561, -89.67520)**: From Baraboo at the intersection of USH-12 and STH-33, take STH-33 east (Linn St. becomes 8th Ave.) for 2.5 mi. At Washington Ave. turn right and go south 0.6 mi. At STH-113 turn left and continue south 5.3 mi. From Merrimac at the intersection of Baraboo St. and STH-113/78, take STH-113/78 west for 2.5 mi. Continue on STH-113 north for 2.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: parking 2] **Marsh Rd. Southern Trail Access (43.38577, -89.64333)**: From Merrimac at the intersection of STH-113/78 and Baraboo St., take Baraboo St. north 0.3 mi. At Cemetery St., which becomes Marsh Rd., turn left and go west then north 0.9 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

### THE HIKE

This segment offers a quiet break from the crowds at Devil’s Lake. It passes through the Merrimac Preserve, owned and managed by the Riverland Conservancy, and encompasses more than 1,800 acres. The Preserve provides a wildlife corridor between the Baraboo Bluffs and the Wisconsin River. Riverland Conservancy began in 1997 as Wisconsin Power and Light Stewardship Trust through Wisconsin Power and Light Company’s generous donation of lands and equipment. During the warmer months, the prairies along the segment come alive with the sounds of insects and stunning flowers and grasses.

From the STH-113 Trail access, enter a wooded area, cross a bridge, and enter a prairie opening leading to CTH-DL. Cross the road and continue south through a diverse prairie before descending toward a wetland and a boardwalk crossing. After a gentle climb, you’ll enter a savanna area with western views featuring the bluffs of Devil’s Lake State Park and the terminal moraine between them. Also, enjoy informational signs about groundwater and invasive species. Return to the woods and cross a long boardwalk protecting a wetland’s fragile ecosystem. Continue alongside a meadow and briefly join a snowmobile trail before reaching the northern Trail access on Marsh Road.

East of Marsh Road, head generally east through a mix of wooded areas, recent timber harvest areas, and restored oak savannas and prairies. After a mile or so, turn south. The Trail turns northwest shortly before the segment ends on Marsh Road.

![Map of the Merrimac Preserve area showing trail segments and local roads.](page_241_image_1_v2.jpg)

2.0-mi CR to Gibraltar Rock Segment. South and east on Marsh Rd. (becomes Cemetery St.), south on Baraboo St., east and south on STH-113.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Merrimac Ferry:** On Trail. STH-113 across Lake Wisconsin (wisconsindot.gov/Pages/travel/water/merrimac/default.aspx).

Listed in the National Register of Historic Places, the *Colsac III* ferry (a.k.a. the Merrimac ferry) is the lone survivor of about 500 ferries that used to operate in the 19<sup>th</sup> century across Wisconsin. The name "Colsac" is the phonetic rendering of the two counties the ferry connects, Columbia and Sauk. The ferry crosses the Wisconsin River at a wider part of the river known as Lake Wisconsin, which is a reservoir produced by a dam downstream. At that spot, it is both the Wisconsin River and Lake Wisconsin. The ferry takes seven minutes to cross approximately 0.45 mi. State operated, the service is free to all, including cars, bikes and pedestrians, and runs 24 hours, 7 days a week April 15 through November 30. The ferry is closed December 1 through April 14. There is a separate lane for pedes-trians and bicycles, boarding from the sidewalk on the east (upriver) side of the ferry. *Note: Merrimac ferry closes for periodic maintenance during regular operating season. STH-113 has flashing signage when closed.*

### AREA SERVICES

**Devil's Lake State Park:** See Devil's Lake Segment, p. 203. From STH-113 Trail access go north 2.1 mi. and west on CTH-DL 2.3 mi to the park entrance.

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Baraboo and West Baraboo:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Baraboo area info available from the Baraboo Chamber of Commerce (800-227-2266, baraboo.com and Downtown Baraboo (608-448-3490, downtownbaraboo.com). From the STH-113 Trail access go north ~6 mi.

**Merrimac:** Restaurant, lodging and convenience store. From the Marsh Rd. southern Trail access go south and east 0.9 mi on Marsh Rd., which becomes Cemetery St. At Baraboo St. turn right and go south 0.3 mi. For area info, contact Merrimac Visitor Information (townofmerrimac.net).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.parent_text:
# Southern Columbia County

*Atlas Maps 62f–63f; Databook pages 52, 57–58*

*Total miles: 13.0 (Trail 7.6; Connecting Route 5.4)*

![Map of Wisconsin](page_242_image_2_v2.jpg)

[Icon: Map of Wisconsin with Southern Columbia County highlighted]

**Southern Columbia County** is home to iconic Gibraltar Rock and the Lodi Marsh State Wildlife Area. The Ice Age Trail in this region passes through some unique geological features and diverse ecological habitats. Hikers can expect an excellent experience of glacially shaped valleys, bluffs, and drumlins while passing through prairies, savannas, and woodlands.

This region drew white settlers for the rivers, streams, and fertile soil. Before that, it had attracted Indigenous people for the same reasons. By 1835, what is now Columbia County had been surveyed. Public land sales began at $1.25 per acre. The first white settler, Wallace Rowan, built a log house with a dual purpose, trading with the Ho-Chunk and accommodating travelers. He cultivated a plot next to his tavern, growing oats, corn, potatoes, and other vegetables. Poverty and political upheaval in Europe encouraged immigrants from Wales, Scotland, Ireland, Norway, and Germany to make Columbia County their home.

The Ice Age Trail also winds through the City of Lodi, a charming Ice Age Trail Community featuring turn-of-the-century homes and store fronts. Spring Creek, a spring-fed brown trout stream, runs from the Lodi Marsh through downtown Lodi. Since portions of the creek do not freeze during winter, it hosts waterfowl, particularly mallards, leading Lodi to adopt “Susie the Duck” as a mascot.

Conveniently, a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers exists on the Gibraltar Rock Segment and one along the Lodi Marsh Segment.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

Lodi Valley Chapter volunteers construct and maintain the Ice Age Trail, lead hikes and field trips, educate and assist in land stewardship. They are visible in their community, participating in and hosting local events. Notable activities include the Mammoth Fun Run and Hike, a Fall Colors Run, Tyke Hikes (for children), and monthly Full Moon hikes. They also promote the Trail by offering the “Glacial Drifters” hiking award program. Participants earn a patch for hiking all Ice Age Trail segments in Southern Columbia County. Start your Glacial Drifters adventure at IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Learn more about the chapter by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/lodi-valley-chapter

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Columbia County Visitor’s Bureau:** 608-742-6161, travelcolumbiacounty.net
[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Lodi:** 608-592-4412, lodilakewisconsin.org

![Gibraltar Rock Segment: A resilient cedar tree surveys the valley. Photo by KELLY ANKLAM](page_243_image_1_v2.jpg)

KELLY ANKLAM
*Gibraltar Rock Segment: A resilient cedar tree surveys the valley.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.parent_text:
## Gibraltar Rock Segment (Atlas Map 62f)

**Summary:** The Gibraltar Rock Segment is a 4.8-mile trail in Southern Columbia County (Atlas Map 62f), running from Merrimac ferry south landing wayside to CTH-V. The Trail winds through breathtakingly beautiful public land, beginning with the Alliance-owned 60-acre Steenbock Preserve, containing secondary woods and prairie remnants undergoing restoration.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **4.8 miles (4.0 IAT, 0.8 CR)**: Merrimac ferry south landing wayside to CTH-V

*Note: New white-blazed loop trail is planned between the parking areas on Gibraltar Rock Rd. and CTH-V. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

![water and picnic icon](page_244_image_13_v2.jpg) At Merrimac ferry north landing and south landing wayside.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: camping icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA icon].

[Icon: roadwalk icon] Segment includes a connecting route roadwalk.

[Icon: ColdCache icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.

[Icon: spur trail icon] A spur trail to the DCA, three white-blazed loop trails (two north of Slack Rd. and one near the top of Gibraltar Rock) and Gibraltar Rock State Natural Area trails and road.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking 1 icon] **1 Merrimac ferry south landing wayside Trail Access (43.36547, -89.62055)**: From Lodi at the intersection of STH-113 and STH-60, take STH-113 north 6.0 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: parking 2 icon] **2 CTH-V Trail Access (43.34456, -89.61048)**: From Lodi at the intersection of STH-113 and STH-60, take STH-113 north 4.0 mi. At CTH-V turn left and go west 1.7 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Slack Rd. (ii) Gibraltar Rock Rd.

### THE HIKE

The Trail winds through breathtakingly beautiful public land, beginning with the Alliance-owned 60-acre Steenbock Preserve, containing secondary woods and prairie remnants undergoing restoration. The Preserve, centered on a bedrock bluff of fractured Prairie du Chien dolomite overlaying Cambrian sandstone, offers a scenic view of Lake Wisconsin and the surrounding region. Preserved in a State Natural Area, Gibraltar Rock is a flat-topped butte, an outlier of the Magnesian escarpment, with a thin dolomite cap over St. Peter sandstone. It rises 1,234 feet above sea level. Its 200-foot sheer cliffs offer spectacular panoramic views of the Wisconsin River valley and Lake Wisconsin. On the south side of the butte is a rock face bluff overlooking a sizeable leather-leaf bog and a bucolic valley. Migrating raptors catch thermals formed by the warm cliff face.

The segment starts at the Merrimac ferry’s south landing wayside at the ferry ramp near a large, six-panel Ice Age Trail kiosk. Follow yellow-blazed posts through the grassy wayside area. Traverse woods filled with white oak, shag-bark hickory, and maple before crossing busy STH-113 (cross with caution) near Northern Cross Arm Road.

Travel through the Alliance’s Steenbock Preserve as you ascend the north face of a 200-foot hill, taking in lovely views of Lake Wisconsin through a screen of substantial oaks. Continue southeast through red cedar and remnant prairie areas with big bluestem, Indian grass, little bluestem, and other native grass species. Enjoy more scenic views of Lake Wisconsin and the surrounding landscape.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment near Merrimac and Gibraltar Rock, showing Lake Wisconsin, Steenbock Preserve, and various connecting routes.](page_245_image_1_v2.jpg)

from various points along the way. This section also includes a short white-blazed loop trail west of the main route.

Continue straight ahead until the white-blazed Trail reconnects with the main route. Shortly past this point, you’ll meet a spur trail with signage pointing to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Then, continuing a steady climb in the open prairie, you’ll reach a stunning panoramic vista of the Wisconsin River and Baraboo Hills.

Meet another white-blazed trail, making a loop west of the main route and heading toward the Slack Road parking area. On the main Trail, an overlook offers excellent views during the leaf-off season of the surrounding hills and Wisconsin River Valley. Drop almost 200 feet on a series of switchbacks through woods of white pine and oak, eventually reaching a boulder-studded dry ravine. Cross the ravine and ascend to the Slack Road parking area, the other end of the white-blazed loop.

Follow a connecting route along Slack Road east and south to reach the next off-road section. At CTH-V, turn right and continue southwest to Gibraltar Rock Road. Turn left and walk through the parking area for the Gibraltar Rock State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA), and look for a kiosk and Trail entrance up two stone steps.

Enter the forest and soon cross a mixed gravel and crumbling blacktop DNR access road leading to the top of Gibraltar Rock. Continue to wind through a second-growth forest of white pine, hickory, and oak. Then climb 50 steps constructed by volunteers, leading to the top of Gibraltar Rock. Just after the last step, a marked loop trail stretches across the backside of Gibraltar Rock. It allows you to bypass a popular route traveling near the sheer cliffs.

At the top of Gibraltar Rock, there are no safety guardrails. Therefore, use extreme caution when hiking near or along the bluff. As you meander along the top, enjoy views of the countryside below from several rock outcrops framed by weathered red cedars.

Leave the magnificent view and begin a gradual descent, first through a maple forest and then through a white pine–dominated woods as the Trail winds around the bluff’s base. Black locust and stone retaining walls line much of the route.

Emerge from the woods to the full panoramic “Horton Vista,” offering outstanding long views of the field opening, the valley below, and the distant Baraboo Hills. Continue through a former farm field, noting a prominent mulberry tree and more views before returning to a wooded canopy. Continue to descend past the field opening, jutting into it briefly before arriving at the CTH-V parking area and the segment’s end, the site of the former Horton homestead.

### AREA SERVICES

**Merrimac:** Restaurant, lodging and convenience store. From the ferry wayside Trail access take the ferry across to the north side of Lake Wisconsin. On STH-113 go north then east 1.0 mi.

**Devil’s Lake State Park:** Seasonal concession stand, camping. On Trail (608-356-8301, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/devilslake; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). From the ferry wayside Trail access take the ferry across to the north side of Lake Wisconsin. Take STH-113 north and west ~10 mi.

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Baraboo and West Baraboo:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Baraboo area info available from the Baraboo Chamber of Commerce (800-227-2266, baraboo.com and Downtown Baraboo (608-448-3490, downtownbaraboo.com). From the ferry wayside Trail access take the ferry across to the north side of Lake Wisconsin. Take STH-113 north ~12 mi.

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Lodi:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Services along Main St. or on STH-60 and STH-113. For area info contact the Lodi and Lake Wisconsin Chamber of Commerce (608-592-4412, lodilakewisconsin.org). From the ferry wayside Trail access take STH-113 south 6.0 mi.

**Okee**: Restaurant. From the CTH-V Trail access, go east on CTH-V for 2.0 mi.

> *The Trail became a thread woven into my life just as it was at each stage. I aspired to enjoy the Trail, not just check it off [my list]. I concentrated on each hike for its own sake and savored what it had to offer. I am still savoring!*
>
> DIANA MATTHEWS, THOUSAND-MILER

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.segment_name: Fern Glen Segment and Lodi Segment
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.parent_text:
## Fern Glen Segment and Lodi Segment (Atlas Maps 62f, 63f)

**Summary:** The Fern Glen Segment and Lodi Segment: **Fern Glen Segment**

### SNAPSHOT

**[Icon: 5] [Icon: 2] Fern Glen Segment—1.3 miles: CTH-J to Bilkey Rd.**

* [Icon: water source] No reliable sources of water.
* [Icon: dogs leashed] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: hunting season] Segment is closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: spur trail] A short spur trail to "Susie's Rock"

**[Icon: 3] [Icon: 2] Lodi Segment—2.2 miles: Lodi School Complex to Corner St. (STH-113)**

* ![restrooms](page_247_image_11_v2.jpg) ![restrooms](page_247_image_11_v2.jpg) At town public buildings. Restrooms also available at Goeres Park.
* [Icon: no hunting] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* ![water source](page_247_image_11_v2.jpg) From Spring Creek.
* [Icon: dogs leashed] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* ![camping](page_247_image_11_v2.jpg) At private campground.
* [Icon: road walk] Most of the segment follows city roads and sidewalks.
* ![park and pool](page_247_image_11_v2.jpg) ![park and pool](page_247_image_11_v2.jpg) At Strangeway Playlot and Veterans Memorial Park and at nearby Goeres Park and Pool (175 Fair St.).
* [Icon: wheelchair accessible] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. **[Icon: 1] CTH-J Trail Access (43.32934, -89.57975)**: From Lodi at the intersection of STH-113 and STH-60, take STH-113 north. At CTH-J turn left and go west 2.0 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.
2. **Bilkey Rd. Trail Access (43.32221, -89.56377)**: **Parking**: Roadside.
3. **Lodi School Complex Trail Access (43.31474, -89.54904)**: **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.
4. **Corner St. (STH-113) Trail Access (43.30456, -89.52551)**: From Lodi at the intersection of STH-113 and STH-60, take STH-113 south 0.7 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: City of Lodi public parking area on STH-113.

### THE HIKE

**Fern Glen Segment**

This segment is in an area of rounded hills covered with a thin layer of glacial till supporting fields and woods. As it travels, the Trail skirts around a drumlin's north side, passes through a dense hardwood forest, and climbs up and down a steep ravine. Several species of ferns and spring ephemerals highlight the segment. Be wary of poison ivy and wild parsnip.

From the Trail access at the CTH-J parking area, you'll begin a steady climb up a steep hill. At the top is a bench near a beautiful oak tree. The bench's plaque recognizes the Groves family's Fern Glen Farm and the conservation easement protecting 436 acres and several miles of Ice Age Trail. Then, begin a steep descent until you reach a blue-blazed spur trail leading to "Susie's Rock," a favorite spot of Susan Groves (1860–1906) and a place of remembrance for the Groves family. Retrace your steps back to the Trail route. Continue until the terminus at Bilkey Road, crossing a low spot prone to flooding during rainy periods.

![Map of the Lodi Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through the city of Lodi, including landmarks like Susie's Rock, Lodi High School, Lodi Middle School, and Goeres Park, as well as connecting routes.](page_248_image_1_v2.jpg)

*From the Bilkey Road Trail access, reach the next segment via the following 2.4-mile connecting route: Hike east and then north on Bilkey Road for 0.5 miles. Turn right and go east on CTH-J for 0.7 miles. Turn right and hike south on Development Drive (which turns into Strangeway Avenue) for 1.1 miles to the intersection of Strangeway Avenue and Prospect Avenue.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::southern-colum.parent_text:
## Lodi Segment

**Summary:** The Lodi Segment: This segment features Lodi, a charming Ice Age Trail Community founded in 1846 by Isaac Palmer in the Pleasant Valley Precinct of the Wisconsin Territory.

This segment features Lodi, a charming Ice Age Trail Community founded in 1846 by Isaac Palmer in the Pleasant Valley Precinct of the Wisconsin Territory. Spring Creek’s water power played a part in the town’s location, first powering a sawmill and then a grist mill. Spring Creek, a spring-fed brown trout stream, runs from the Lodi Marsh through downtown Lodi and empties into Lake Wisconsin. Since portions of the creek do not freeze during winter, it hosts waterfowl, particularly mallards, leading Lodi to adopt “Susie the Duck” as a mascot.

Note: Hikers interested in hiking just the Lodi Segment may wish to begin their hike at the Lodi School complex.

From the Trail access at the intersection of Strangeway Avenue and Prospect Avenue, hike an out-and-back stretch of Ice Age Trail to the west of Strangeway Avenue. Head west on Prospect Avenue to Strangeway Playlot, where you’ll leave the pavement. Follow blazed posts through the playlot, across Prospect Avenue, and into the woods paralleling Lodi Golf Club.

Pass prairie restoration efforts behind Lodi Middle School before crossing a 150-foot-long footbridge spanning a ravine between Lodi’s middle and high schools. Ron Wolf, a U.S. Forest Products Lab forester and Lodi Valley Chapter member, designed the structure, which is based on the 16th-century Chinese Rainbow Bridge. The twin arches, decking, and railing are constructed of black locust and demonstrate the economical use of small-diameter invasive species.

From the bridge’s western end, continue a short distance to the end-of-segment sign at the Lodi School complex. Then backtrack to Strangeway Avenue and resume hiking the remainder of the Lodi Segment.

From the intersection of Prospect Avenue and Strangeway Avenue, hike south on Strangeway Avenue. You’ll find a well-marked yellow-blazed route through town. Turn left and go east on Sauk Street. Turn left and go northeast on Church Street. Turn right and go southeast on Prairie Street. Turn left and go northeast on Lodi Street (STH-60). Turn right and go southeast on Main Street (STH-113).

At Doctor’s Park, depart Main Street and turn southwest, passing through the park and by a six-sided Ice Age Trail kiosk, then onto a footbridge over Spring Creek. Follow a grassy path southward along the creek through Veterans Memorial Park. Turn southeast and recross Spring Creek on the Pleasant Street road bridge.

Just after the bridge, Pleasant Street meets Corner Street (STH-113). Head south on a sidewalk on the east side of Corner Street. As you leave town, carefully cross to the west side of Corner Street (STH-113) and continue beside the street on a path. Reach the end of the segment at a parking area on the west side of the highway.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Lodi:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Services along Main St. or on STH-60 and STH-113. For area info contact the Lodi and Lake Wisconsin Chamber of Commerce (608-592-4412, lodilakewisconsin.org).

![A hiker enjoys the view from the Rainbow Bridge.](page_249_image_1_v2.jpg)
JANET WOOD

Lodi Segment: A hiker enjoys the view from the Rainbow Bridge.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::c.parent_text:
# Dane County

*Atlas Maps 63f–68f; Databook pages 60–66*

*Total miles: 68.4 (Trail 44.2; Connecting Route 24.2)*

![Map of Dane County showing the Ice Age Trail route](page_252_image_1_v2.jpg)

Dane County straddles two distinct physical landscapes, which the Ice Age Trail weaves back and forth between. Western Dane County sits firmly in the Driftless Area, an unglaciated landscape with narrow, stream-cut valleys and angular ridges of exposed sandstone and dolomite. Whereas layers of sand, clay, and gravel left after a series of glacial advances cover the eastern two-thirds of the county.

The Green Bay Lobe covered most of this eastern portion 15,000 years ago. Prairie Moraine County Park, near Verona, is a great place to see the Johnstown End Moraine, marking the farthest advance of the Green Bay Lobe.

A string of public lands protects portions of the Ice Age Trail in Dane County. From north to south, these include Lodi Marsh State Wildlife Area; Indian Lake County Park; the Cross Plains National Scientific Reserve; Ice Age Junction Area; Badger Prairie and Prairie Moraine County Parks; and Brooklyn State Wildlife Area.

The Trail also passes through two Ice Age Trail Communities, Cross Plains and Verona. Cross Plains has small-town charm with its historic buildings and small but bustling downtown. While Verona is more urban with a range of shopping and dining choices.

. . .

Five Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance, multiday hikers are spread across Dane County, with campsites on the Lodi Marsh, Springfield Hill, Cross Plains, Verona, and Montrose segments.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Dane County Chapter excels in organizing and leading prairie and woodland restoration projects. Along Alliance-owned preserves, Chapter volunteers have transformed properties described as "invasive species nightmares" into healthy, thriving prairies. Chapter activities include leading hikes, interpretive walks, and hosting awareness-raising displays at community functions and annual expos.

The Chapter also offers a patch and certificate to those who hike all the segments within their "Dane Drifters" hiking award program. To start your Dane Drifters adventure, visit IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Learn more about the Chapter at IceAgeTrail.org/dane-county-chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Dane County Parks**: 608-224-3730, danecountyparks.com

**Camping in Dane County Parks**: 608-224-3730, reservedane.com

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Cross Plains**: crossplainschamber.net

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Verona**: visitveronawi.com

![Table Bluff Segment: Pale purple coneflowers shine in a restored prairie. Photo by John Anderson.](page_253_image_1_v2.jpg)

JOHN ANDERSON

*Table Bluff Segment: Pale purple coneflowers shine in a restored prairie.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::l.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::l.parent_text:
## Lodi Marsh Segment (Atlas Map 63f)

**Summary:** The Lodi Marsh Segment is a 5.0-mile trail in Dane County (Atlas Map 63f), running from Corner St. (STH-113) to field road at Lodi-Springfield Rd.. This segment features stunning views and remarkable prairies.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Lodi Marsh Segment icon] **Lodi Marsh Segment—5.0 miles:** Corner St. (STH-113) to field road at Lodi-Springfield Rd.

* [Icon: Water icon] From Spring Creek and numerous springs.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.
* [Icon: Camping icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA icon].
* [Icon: Private camping icon] At private campgrounds. [Icon: Road icon] A short portion parallels STH-113.
* [Icon: ColdCache icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment. [Icon: Trail icon] Spur trails leading to Riddle Rd., a DCA, and an additional parking area as well as a short connector trail.
* [Icon: Hunting icon] Portion of segment crossing private land between STH-113 parking area and Lodi Marsh State Wildlife Area is closed during gun deer season.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

**① Corner St. (STH-113) Trail Access (43.30456, -89.52551):** From Lodi at the intersection of STH-113 and STH-60, take STH-113 south 0.7 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**② Field road at Lodi-Springfield Rd. Trail Access (43.27955, -89.54782):** From Lodi at the intersection of STH-113 and STH-60, take STH-60 west 0.6 mi. At Riddle Rd. (which becomes Lodi-Springfield Rd. in Dane County), turn left and go south 2.2 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Robertson Trail Access parking area 0.2 mi north on Lodi-Springfield Rd.

**Additional Parking:** Twin Pines Trail Access on Lodi-Springfield Rd. A short spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

This segment features stunning views and remarkable prairies. At first it follows several dolomite ridges, outliers of the Magnesian Cuesta, a sister escarpment of the more famous Niagara escarpment to the east. Glacial ice sculpted, not obliterated, these ridges. The segment incorporates outstanding views of a glacial tunnel channel, Central Bluff, Gibraltar Rock, the Baraboo Hills, and the marsh below. You will pass through areas of stately oak, shagbark hickory, walnut, and other hardwoods.

To the west of the Lodi-Springfield Road, the southern portion of the segment winds through glacially sculpted landscapes of dolomite hills and deep valleys. It travels along the edge of Lodi Marsh, through a lowland prairie, and over limestone bedrock ridges and drumlins. The Green Bay Lobe, a portion of the most recent ice sheet that flowed from Green Bay toward Madison 18,000 years ago, shaped these features.

Lodi Marsh is a large, open wetland complex in a valley partially filled with glacial till. It contains diverse community types and more than 160 native plant species. It borders the headwaters and upper two miles of Spring Creek, which has numerous springs and spring runs, a southern sedge meadow, and a cattail marsh.

From the Trail access at the parking area on the west side of STH-113, hike west across lands owned by Lodi Canning Company. Cross under a railroad trestle and traverse a field edge before entering a narrow, wooded easement between agricultural fields. Note large erratics and cleared fieldstones, and be wary of poison ivy and wild parsnip along this section.

Begin a climb through classic southern Wisconsin woods intermixed with a few dolomite outcrops, then enter Lodi Marsh State Wildlife Area. Ongoing efforts of local volunteers have restored and maintained several hundred acres of prairie and oak savanna here. Bring a wildflower guide to identify the immense variety of prairie flowers in the marsh.

Before the Trail crests the hill, there’s a spectacular view of the marsh. It’s affectionately named “Dave’s View” for two Dane County Chapter leaders – both named Dave – who scouted the route still in use today.

Straight west of Dave’s View is the striking U-shaped cut between Center Bluff and the bluff to the north. It is believed this cut was formed by a large under-glacier river draining meltwater toward the ice margin near the Sauk City area. Farther to the north-west are Gibraltar Rock and the Baraboo Hills.

Start a downhill trek on a long switchback route. Enter the woods and meet

![Map of the Lodi Marsh segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route, Lodi Marsh State Wildlife Area, and nearby roads like Lodi-Springfield Rd.](page_255_image_1_v2.jpg)

a short spur trail southwest leading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Next, enter a large prairie opening with magnificent views of the Lodi Marsh and bluffs. Soon, a blue-blazed spur trail to the right leads to the Twin Pines Trail access. Traverse alternating woods and prairie before reaching Lodi-Springfield Road and the Robertson Trail access.

Cross Lodi-Springfield Road and continue west, dropping into a wetland. A boardwalk snakes through this area to keep the trail passable and hikers’ feet dry. Next is a beautifully restored oak savanna with large open-growth bur oaks and hickory. Volunteers have worked to keep this area brush-free and “oak friendly.”

Ascend through a restored prairie with big and little bluestem, Indian grass, showy goldenrod, sky-blue aster, and the state-threatened prairie thistle. After a blue-blazed connector trail heads south, continue west, then south, east, and northeast, circling a ridge and eventually intersecting a field road. Head east on the field road, quickly meet the southern end of the connector trail, and then cross fields to the segment’s terminus at a gate at Lodi-Springfield Road.

### AREA SERVICES

*   [Icon: Trail City icon] **Lodi**: See Fern Glen Segment and Lodi Segment, p. 215. From the field road at Lodi-Springfield Rd. go north ~3 mi.

## Springfield Hill Segment and Indian Lake Segment (Atlas Map 64f)

**Summary:** The Springfield Hill Segment and Indian Lake Segment: **③ Matz Rd. Trail Access (43.20206, -89.61895): No Parking.** Instead, park at Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area on STH-19.

### [Icon: 3 2] Springfield Hill Segment—1.6 miles: Loop trail east of Ballweg Rd.

* [Icon: no water] No reliable sources of water.
* [Icon: dog] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: DCA] At a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA logo].
* [Icon: spur trail] A blue-blazed spur trail to the DCA.

### [Icon: 3 2] Indian Lake Segment—3.5 miles: Matz Rd. to STH-19 Western Trail Access at Indian Lake County Park

*Note: Reroutes are planned in Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area and on the southwest side of Indian Lake County Park. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, *IceAgeTrail.org*) for details.*

* [Icon: DCA] A camping area near the warming house is available for youth groups (see Area Services for more information).
* [Icon: water tap] [Icon: picnic table] [Icon: shelter] By open-sided shelter.
* [Icon: dog] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: shelter] [Icon: water drop] From Indian Lake.
* [Icon: ski trail] Portions overlap ski trails. Hike off to the side when groomed.
* [Icon: spur trail] [Icon: spur trail] Indian Lake County Park ski trails and Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

**① ② Ballweg Rd. Trail Access (43.22115, -89.62043):** From Sauk City at the intersection of USH-12 and BUS USH-12, take USH-12 east 6.8 mi. At Ballweg Rd. turn left and go north 0.2 mi. From Middleton at the intersection of USH-12 and USH-14, take USH-12 west 12.0 mi. At Ballweg Rd. turn right and go north 0.2 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**③ Matz Rd. Trail Access (43.20206, -89.61895): No Parking.** Instead, park at Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area on STH-19.

**④ STH-19 Western Trail Access at Indian Lake County Park (43.18978, -89.63496):** From Sauk City at the intersection of USH-12 and BUS USH-12, take USH-12 east 4.8 mi. At CTH-KP turn right and go south 2.2 mi. At STH-19 turn right and go east 1.3 mi. From Middleton at the intersection of USH-12 and USH-14, take USH-12 west 10.0 mi. At STH-19 turn left and go west 2.8 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Indian Lake County Park main parking area 1.0 mi east on STH-19. *Note: The parking area at the STH-19 Western Trail Access is intended for boat launch and dog park users (NOT hikers).*
