## Springfield Hill Segment

**Summary:** The Springfield Hill Segment: This segment, a short loop that can be hiked in either direction, highlights the 198-acre Springfield Hill Natural Resource Area.

This segment, a short loop that can be hiked in either direction, highlights the 198-acre Springfield Hill Natural Resource Area. This property consists of steep wooded valleys, glacial kettle ponds, and open meadow. The stunning prairie changes with the seasons and demonstrates what’s possible through extensive and ongoing restoration efforts. The Trail’s route provides outstanding views from atop Springfield Hill, one of the highest points in the area.

![Map showing the trail route through Springfield Hill Natural Resource Area, Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area, and Indian Lake County Park. It includes road names like Ballweg Rd., Collins Ridge Rd., and Matz Rd., as well as points of interest like the Matz Farmstead ruins and a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA).](page_257_image_1_v2.jpg)

From the Trail access at the parking area on Ballweg Road, to hike the loop clockwise, take the left branch of the loop. Gradually ascend a berry-lined path to the crest of a south-facing slope offering dramatic views to the west. A nearby bench offers the chance to rest and enjoy the view and summer wildflowers, including monarda, spiderwort, and coreopsis. Descend the ridge and meet a short spur trail to the east that climbs a staircase leading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multi-day hikers. Descend through beautiful oak savanna and woodlands, then cross a boardwalk before passing near the remains of a log outbuilding, dating to the late 19th century. Both the DCA staircase and the boardwalk were Eagle Scout projects. Continue along a grassy goat-prairie hillside and pass an old cattle road underpass, the small central lake, and an agricultural field before returning to the parking area.

To reach the Indian Lake Segment, start a 2.1-mile connecting route by heading south on Ballweg Road 0.2 miles. At USH-12 (use caution – a very busy highway) turn left and walk east on the gravel shoulder for 0.4 miles. Cross USH-12 at Collins Ridge Road and go south 0.1 miles. At Matz Road, turn right and go west and south 0.6 miles to the first left. Turn left and continue on Matz Road east and south for 0.8 miles to the Trail access on the west side of the road.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::i.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::i.parent_text:
## Indian Lake Segment

**Summary:** The Indian Lake Segment: This segment traverses Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area and Indian Lake County Park.

This segment traverses Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area and Indian Lake County Park. The 148-acre Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area comprises a mix of crop fields and steep wooded hillsides. Besides hiking, the area is open for deer and turkey hunting, foraging, and snowshoeing. It offers photographers an evocative setting amongst the stone ruins of the Matz farmstead, built in 1907 and burned down in 1949.

The 791-acre Indian Lake County Park has 8 miles of ski and hiking trails. The Trail route overlaps some of the park’s ski trails, which have their own color-coded markings and directional designations. It may also sometimes follow a ski trail in the “wrong”

direction. The Trail is generally well-blazed, but because of the other nearby trails, pay attention to the yellow blazes. Please respect the ski trails in winter; do not hike on groomed trails.

From the Trail access at the Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area on Matz Road, you’ll initially drop sharply into a wooded area. The Trail quickly flattens out as it enters a large field. Follow a wide, grassy path between two agricultural fields, which narrows as it reaches the Matz Farmstead site. Continue through to the Halfway Prairie Wildlife Area parking area and head about 300 feet to the east along STH-19.

Cross STH-19 (use caution – a busy road) at the Indian Lake County Park eastern entrance. Follow the park road to the main parking area, with water, restrooms, and a nearby picnic shelter. A side trail from the parking area leads to the historic St. Mary of the Oaks Chapel (look for signage behind and to the right of the pit toilets). Wildflowers near the parking area include monarda and coreopsis.

From the parking area, head south past the Indian Lake Trailhead sign. Quickly turn left, then right onto a gravel path at a brown gate. The old stairs, straight ahead, provide another way to access the historic chapel. When the segment leaves the gravel path for a large grassy area, you will find a warming house off to the right and up the sledding hill. It is open during ski season, and a restroom is nearby. Past the warming house, the Trail alternates between grass and woods, then enters a wooded area where you’ll climb up and down steep hills.

Exit the woods near a large erratic and follow the south side of Indian Lake, crossing a meadow and passing a marsh. Skirt around a dog exercise area, cross a bridge over Halfway Prairie Creek, and reach the segment’s terminus at Indian Lake County Park’s dog exercise/lake access parking area on STH-19.

### AREA SERVICES

**Sauk City**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. From the Ballweg Rd. Trail access go south 0.2 mi. At USH-12 turn right and go northwest for 7.0 mi. For area info contact the Sauk Prairie Area Chamber of Commerce (608-643-4168, saukprairie.com).

**Middleton**: See Valley View Segment and Madison Segment, p. 235. From the Ballweg Rd. Trail access go south 0.2 mi. At USH-12 turn left and go east then south ~12 mi.

**Indian Lake County Park**: Camping. For official youth groups and long-distance, multiday hikers with advanced permission only from the Dane County Parks Department (608-224-3730, danecountyparks.com/park/IndianLake). The group camp area is located near the park’s warming house.

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Cross Plains:** Restaurants, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on Main St. (USH-14). Area info available from the Cross Plains Chamber of Commerce (608-843-3166, crossplainschamber.net). From the STH-19 western Trail access go west then south ~10 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::t.parent_text:
## Table Bluff Segment (Atlas Map 64f, 65f)

**Summary:** The Table Bluff Segment: This segment offers a trek through stunning prairies with long ridgetop views and showcases the results of remarkable stewardship efforts.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3] [Icon: 2] **Table Bluff Segment—4.8 miles (3.6 IAT, 1.2 CR): Pine Rd. to Scheele Rd.**

| [Icon: No water icon] No reliable sources of water.                                                                                                          | [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.                                                             |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Icon: Picnic/View icon] At the top of the bluff 0.4 mi north of Scheele Rd.                                                                                 | [Icon: Trail icon] Two short blue-blazed spur trails leading to parking areas, two white-blazed loop trails, and additional informal trails. |
| [Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.                                                                                                        |                                                                                                                                                                |
| [Icon: Hunting icon] Between Table Bluff Rd. and Scheele Rd., the southern two-thirds of the segment crossing private land is closed during gun deer season. |                                                                                                                                                                |

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

* ① **Pine Rd. Trail Access (43.15597, -89.66024)**: From Cross Plains take USH-14 west 1.2 mi. At CTH-KP turn right and go north 2.9 mi. At Pine Rd. turn right and go northeast 0.4 mi. Parking: 0.1 mi north on Pine Rd. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.
* ② **Scheele Rd. Trail Access (43.11888, -89.67355)**: From Cross Plains take USH-14 west 1.2 mi. At CTH-KP turn right and go north 0.3 mi. At Scheele Rd. turn left and go west 0.3 mi. to a gravel road. No Parking. Instead, park at the parking area 0.2 mi north on the gravel road. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.
* **Additional Parking**: Table Bluff Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment offers a trek through stunning prairies with long ridgetop views and showcases the results of remarkable stewardship efforts. It begins at the Liebetrau Prairie, a 78.5-acre Dane County property located on the edge of the Driftless Area, just up ice from the terminal moraine. The surrounding steep valleys, mildly sculpted by the glacier, characterize the area’s topography.

The Prairie is the culmination of nearly two decades of prairie restoration efforts. It displays an ever-changing explosion of plants. In spring, shooting stars and other early prairie flowers; in summer and fall, compass plants, monarda, rudbeckia, coneflowers, coreopsis, milkweed, liatris, and many more.

South of Table Bluff Road, the segment travels farther into the Driftless Area, traversing the Alliance-owned Holmes Preserve, a 73-acre property also featuring restored prairie. Finally, it winds through the Alliance-owned SwampLovers Preserve, a 433-acre property featuring two impeccably managed, prominent goat prairies.

Table Bluff’s 200 feet of total vertical relief offers a magnificent view of the Driftless Area and the valley below. Meltwater from the Laurentide ice sheet poured through a pre-glacial valley, depositing sand and gravel. It partially filled the valley floor, which now holds wetlands and a tributary stream of Black Earth Creek, a Class I trout stream.

From the Trail access on Pine Road, head east and quickly meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading north to the Pine Road parking area. A little farther on is the beginning of a lollipop loop. Note: Watch for poison ivy. Following the loop to the right (counterclockwise), begin a gradual descent and enter an area of mixed

![Map of the trail segment showing the route through Holmes Preserve, Swamplovers Preserve, and Andersen Preserve, with connecting routes and local roads like Pine Rd., CTH-KP, and USH-14.](page_260_image_1_v2.jpg)

hardwoods. Cross a small partially stone-lined ravine and gradually climb back up to the prairie toward the loop’s highest point, with sweeping views in multiple directions. Return to the beginning of the lollipop and make your way back to Pine Road.

At Pine Road, start hiking a connecting route to the next part of the segment: turn left and head south 0.4 miles. At CTH-KP, turn left and continue south 0.5 miles. At Table Bluff Road, turn right and go west 0.3 miles to the Trail access and parking area.

Head south across the Alliance’s Holmes Preserve. After traversing a prairie, meet a white-blazed loop trail offering a 0.5-mile “bubble” to the west and south. (It goes up a ridge through a mixed hardwood forest containing a grove of several large oaks.)

Back on the Trail, from your initial encounter with the white-blazed loop trail continue south across a large restored prairie with a thriving population of rare pale purple coneflower as well as shooting star, rattlesnake master, stiff gentian, hoary vervain, prairie smoke, cream gentian, and more. Enter a wooded area and dip onto a valley floor before meeting the other end of the white-blazed loop trail.

The remainder of the segment crosses the Alliance’s SwampLovers Preserve. Climb a ridge and traverse oak and hickory woodlands before entering a prairie/oak savanna being restored to its native state. This area, including a second white-blazed loop, features dramatic rock outcroppings of Ordovician dolomite and views southwest to the Blue Mounds. The top of the bluff, known as “Picnic Hill,” hosts a shelter with picnic tables available to hikers when not in use for authorized Alliance activities. There’s a portable toilet near the shelter, too.

From the top of the bluff, the segment drops precipitously on a series of switchbacks to its endpoint, where the Preserve’s access road meets Scheele Road. (The road to the top of the bluff is not open to the public.)

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: T/C logo] **Cross Plains:** Restaurants, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on Main St. (USH-14). Area info available from the Cross Plains Chamber of Commerce (608-843-3166, crossplainschamber.net). From Scheele Rd. and CTH-KP, go south and then east on USH-14 ~1 mi.

**Middleton**: See Valley View Segment and Madison Segment, p. 235. From Scheele Rd. and CTH-KP, go south and then east on USH-14 ~9 mi.

**Madison**: See Valley View Segment and Madison Segment, p. 235. From Scheele Rd. and CTH-KP, go south then east on USH-14 ~14 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::c.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::c.parent_text:
## Cross Plains Segment (Atlas Map 65f)

**Summary:** The Cross Plains Segment is a 9.9-mile trail in Dane County (Atlas Map 65f), running from Hickory Hill St. to W. Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S). This segment highlights the Cross Plains Unit of the Ice Age National Scientific Reserve, one of nine units in the country, representing a world-renowned example of glaciated and unglaciated terrain interface.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiking icon] **9.9 miles (6.9 IAT, 3.0 CR):** Hickory Hill St. to W. Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S)

*Note: New Ice Age Trail is planned southeast of North Birch Trail. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

[Icon: water icon] At the public library, public pool (seasonal) and IATA headquarters.

[Icon: road icon] Portions follow town streets and sidewalks.

[Icon: water icon] From Black Earth Creek.

[Icon: camping icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area DCA on the IATA’s Andersen Preserve.

[Icon: spur trail icon] A blue-blazed spur trail to the DCA and trails in Hickory Hill Conservation Area and John I. and Ida Hillebrand Glacial Valley Conservancy. A white-blazed loop trail at Mammoth’s Back Preserve and from the parking area at the Ice Age Reserve Wildlife Area.

[Icon: park icon] [Icon: pool icon] At Legion Park, 1 block west of the Trail on American Legion Dr. and at municipal pool on Caesar St.

[Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

[Icon: coldcache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] **① Hickory Hill St. Trail Access (43.11840, -89.65212):** From the Ice Age Trail Alliance Headquarters in Cross Plains take USH-14 west for 0.3 mi. At Hickory St. turn right and go north 0.2 mi until the road curves east. At Hickory Hill St. turn left and continue north to the Hickory Hill Conservation Park gate. **Parking:** Roadside.

[Icon: parking icon 2] **② W. Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S) Trail Access (43.06032, -89.60109):** From west of Madison on the Beltline Hwy. (USH-12/14) take Exit 254 and follow Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S) west for 4.0 mi to Timber Ln. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Shoveler Sink Waterfowl Production Area 0.3 mi north on Timber Ln.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Lewis St. roadside parking (ii) Village of Cross Plains Municipal Park pool at 2106 Lewis St. (behind the library). (iii) Mill Creek Parkway. Take footpath south across Black Earth Creek to the IAT. (iv) Bourbon Rd. roadside parking. (v) Stagecoach Rd. (vi) Old Sauk Pass Rd. A white-blazed loop trail leads to the IAT. (vii) Timber Ln.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights the Cross Plains Unit of the Ice Age National Scientific Reserve, one of nine units in the country, representing a world-renowned example of glaciated and unglaciated terrain interface. The route also showcases the village of Cross Plains, an Ice Age Trail Community situated along Black Earth Creek in a valley between tall bluffs. It sits at the boundary of the most recent glaciation to the east and north and the Driftless Area to the west and south. Meltwater from the receding glacier eroded and carried the terminal moraine downstream from the village’s current location. Cross Plains is named for the intersection of two early roads: the military road from Fort Crawford (Prairie du Chien) to Fort Howard (Green Bay) and the lowland road from Arena to Madison.

The segment travels through the Alliance-owned Andersen Preserve, a 52-acre property highlighting a successful transformation of a former agricultural field into a restored native prairie. It also contains a mature oak-dominated woodland featuring native flora

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Cross Plains, showing the Table Bluff Segment and Valley View Segment, including landmarks like Mammoth's Back Preserve, NPS Cross Plains Interpretive Site, and various conservancy areas.](page_262_image_1_v2.jpg)

such as jack-in-the-pulpit, shooting stars, wild ginger, and the glacial drainage network known as the Three Gorges.

The route also winds through the Alliance-owned Mammoth’s Back Preserve. This 47-acre property features high-quality, glacially influenced wetlands and spring-fed ponds. Dedicated restoration efforts by Dane County Chapter volunteers transformed the Preserve from a tangle of invasive species to native prairie and oak savanna in record time. The high point along the Trail as it crosses the NPS Cross Plains Interpretive Site offers some of the longest views of the region.

Start at the Trail access at the north end of Hickory Hill Street. Go around a gate across an access road leading uphill into the Hickory Hill Conservancy. Leave the access road and enter a restored native prairie where you'll see a sign for a "cut-off" path heading east. To continue on route, veer northwest at this junction and enter the Alliance's Andersen Preserve. Shortly after entering the woodland, you'll meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

Exit the forest and once more cross the prairie. After passing the other end of the cut-off path, continue southeast through an oak savanna undergoing restoration and climb the side of a dolomite ridge. Enjoy the views to the south and east of what conservationist Increase Lapham described as the "Great Dividing Ridge." On a distant rise to the south, you may see a lone majestic white pine, marking the approximate location of the Cross Plains National Scientific Reserve Unit. The Blue Mounds are also clearly visible to the west.

A series of steep switchbacks take you down into a residential neighborhood. A grassy path between two houses leads to Lewis Street. From here, the segment continues on sidewalks and roads through residential Cross Plains.

Head southeast on Lewis Street to its intersection with Caesar Street. Turn right and head southwest on the east side of Caesar Street, passing the Village of Cross Plains Municipal Park and Pool and Rosemary Garfoot Public Library. At busy Main Street (USH-14), cross cautiously and turn left, heading east down Main Street.

Arrive at 2110 Main Street, the Ice Age Trail Alliance's headquarters (open Monday-Friday, 8 a.m. - 5 p.m.), leave Main Street and turn southwest to cross the Alliance's property along a garden path. Though it's not a true "Visitor Center," hikers are encouraged to stop in. Alliance staff love to chat with Trail users! Restrooms and drinking water are available for all, as are shower and laundry facilities for thru-hikers. You may purchase Ice Age Trail merchandise as well.

Exit the Alliance's property, cross Mill Creek Parkway/Jovina Street, and head for a bridge spanning Black Earth Creek. After crossing the bridge, turn right and follow the paved path parallel to the railroad tracks. Turn left on a boardwalk, which crosses the creek and railroad tracks. At the next intersection, turn left and make a quick right to stay on a sidewalk paralleling the Cross Plains fire station. Cross Bourbon Road and head south on a grassy path between buildings. Climb steeply up into the John I. and Ida Hillebrand Glacial Valley Conservancy and meander southward along the top of a wooded ridge, overlapping and crossing portions of the Conservancy trails. After passing behind a dairy farm, exit the forest and descend a steep hill to CTH-P.

After crossing CTH-P (caution!), continue east between agricultural fields, cross a boardwalk over a low-lying area, and intersect a farm road, following it until it curves to the right. Leave the road, heading east past an old barn to the north, and enter the Alliance's Mammoth's Back Preserve. Make a sharp right turn and head south between fields toward "Mammoth's Back," a ridge of unglaciated bedrock hills. At the base of the hills you'll meet a white-blazed loop trail leading around the southwest side of the ridge. Continuing ahead, make a sharp left turn, curving around the northeast side of Mammoth's Back. Eventually

you’ll meet the other end ofthe white-blazed loop trail. Continue skirting along the edge ofan agricultural field until you reach Stagecoach Road.

To reach the next portion ofoff-road Trail, follow this connecting route: Turn left on Stagecoach Road and go east 0.8 miles. Turn right at North Birch Trail (a road) and go south 0.8 miles. At Old Sauk Pass Road, turn left and head east, following it 0.8 miles to the Trail access at the Ice Age National Scenic Trail Interpretive Site kiosk.

Follow an asphalt driveway into the former Wilkie family homestead and quickly meet a 0.4-mile white-blazed lollipop loop trail offering access to the Old Sauk Pass Road parking area. (A side trip on the loop trail features a view of a proglacial lakebed formed either by the damming action ofa moraine during the retreat ofa melting glacier, a glacial ice dam, or by meltwater trapped in a depression.) The Trail passes the house, garage, and large red barn ofthe homestead, which is the National Park Service administrative office for the Ice Age National Scenic Trail (building not open to the public), and then proceeds south to a prairie with wide mowed paths. Enjoy long, scenic views to the east, north, and west at a high point. Soon, you’ll enter woods and meander gradually downhill. From here, skirt an old farm field, pass a sandstone rock outcropping, and head uphill. After a few switchbacks, enter an open field with a cell tower to the south and a parking area at Timber Lane.

To reach the next section ofthe Trail, follow this connecting route: Turn right from the parking area and head south on Timber Lane for 0.6 miles.

Leave Timber Lane and follow a mowed grassy path through prairie along the edge ofthe Shoveler Sink Waterfowl Production Area (see Points ofInterest below) to the segment’s end point on West Mineral Point Road (CTH-S).

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Cross Plains National Scientific Reserve Unit and National Park Service Ice Age National Scenic Trail Interpretive Site:** On Trail. (8075 Old Sauk Pass Rd., Cross Plains, www.nps.gov/iatr).

This area features lands owned by Dane County, the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (DNR) and the National Park Service (NPS) and includes the Cross Plains Unit of the Ice Age National Scientific Reserve, one of nine such units in the country. The area represents a world-renowned example ofthe interface ofglaciated and unglaciated terrain. The relationship between moraine and glaciated landscapes on one side ofthe moraine and unglaciated bedrock landscapes on the other side is strikingly exhibited. Rugged ridges of the moraine formed during the Wisconsin Glaciation meet the eroded Driftless Area to the south and west.

Unsigned hiking trails weave across land owned by the DNR north of Old Sauk Pass Rd. South of Old Sauk Pass Rd, land owned by NPS and Dane County hosts a portion of the Cross Plains Segment. Dogs are welcome on leash. There are no public restrooms or other visitor facilities.

**Shoveler Sink Waterfowl Production Area:** On Trail. (Mineral Point Rd./Timber Lane) Leopold Wetland Management District/US Fish and Wildlife Service (608-742-7100, fws.gov/wetland/leopold).

Shoveler Sink is a small, permanent, remnant glacial depression providing excellent habitat for migratory birds such as waterfowl, shorebirds, songbirds, and waterbirds seeking a freshwater pond, marsh, and grassland. At least 17 species ofwaterfowl have been observed using the 173.5-acre pond, designated a Waterfowl Production Area (WPA). The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service manages these units of the National Wildlife Refuge System. WPAs are considered prime duck-producing sites and have collectively been called the duck factory of America. Funds from selling duck stamps help protect these threatened and productive migratory bird habitats.

Shoveler Sink is open to recreational activities. Dogs must be on a leash except when hunting.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Cross Plains:** Restaurants, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on Main St. (USH-14). Area info available from the Cross Plains Chamber of Commerce (608-843-3166, crossplainschamber.net).

**Ice Age Trail Alliance Headquarters:** On Trail. (2110 Main St., 800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org). Headquarters hours: 8 am to 5 pm weekdays except during special events. Hikers welcome, though not a true "Visitor Center." Shower and laundry facilities for thru-hikers. Trail information and updates.

**Mendota County Park:** Camping. From the IATA headquarters go east ~10 mi. (5133 CTH-M, Middleton, 608-224-3730, parks-lwrd.countyofdane.com/park/mendota).

**Middleton:** See Valley View Segment and Madison Segment, p. 235. From the IATA headquarters on USH-14 go east ~8 mi.

**Madison:** See Valley View Segment and Madison Segment, p. 235. From the IATA headquarters on USH-14 go east ~13 mi.

![A wooden boardwalk path leading through a green field with tall grass and corn.](page_265_image_1_v2.jpg)
JOHN ANDERSON

*Cross Plains Segment: A boardwalk leads hikers to Mammoth's Back Preserve.*

![Valley View Segment: A raspberry-colored sun sets over a sea of prairie grasses. In the foreground, a wooden trail post with a yellow blaze stands amidst tall, dry prairie grass.](page_266_image_1_v2.jpg)

YOLANDA DELOACH

Valley View Segment: A raspberry-colored sun sets over a sea of prairie grasses.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::v.segment_name: Valley View Segment and Madison Segment
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::v.parent_text:
## Valley View Segment and Madison Segment (Atlas Maps 65f, 66f)

**Summary:** The Valley View Segment and Madison Segment covers approximately 6.5 miles in Dane County (Atlas Map 65f, 66f). It comprises the Valley View Segment (3.4 mi, W. Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S) to Mid Town Rd. at Shady Oak Ln.), the Madison Segment (3.1 mi, Woods Rd. to McKee Rd. (CTH-PD)). **Valley View Segment**

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Segment icon] **Valley View Segment—3.4 miles (3.1 IAT, 0.3 CR):** W. Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S) to Mid Town Rd. at Shady Oak Ln.

* [Icon: No water icon] No reliable sources of water.
* [Icon: Dogs leashed icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: No hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: Trail network icon] An extensive, color-coded trail network through the prairie area.

***

[Icon: Segment icon] **Madison Segment—3.1 miles:** Woods Rd. to McKee Rd. (CTH-PD)

* ![Clubhouse icon](page_267_image_13_v2.jpg) At the University Ridge Golf Course clubhouse (seasonal).
* [Icon: Dogs leashed icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not allowed between Woods Rd. and CTH-M.**
* [Icon: No hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: Path icon] Portions overlap golf course paths, city roads, a driveway and sidewalks and a multiuse recreation path.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Access point 1 icon] **① W. Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S) Trail Access (43.06023, -89.60109):** From west of Madison on the Beltline Hwy. (USH-12/14) take Exit 254 and follow Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S) west for 4.0 mi to Timber Ln. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Shoveler Sink Waterfowl Production Area 0.3 mi north on Timber Ln.

**② Mid Town Rd. at Shady Oak Ln. (43.03110, -89.57805): No Parking.** Instead park at Town of Middleton Ice Age Trail Access park on Moraine Ridge Rd.

**③ Woods Rd. Trail Access (43.02294, -89.55303): Parking:** Roadside.

[Icon: Access point 4 icon] **④ McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) Trail Access (43.01598, -89.52193):** From Verona at the intersection of Verona Ave. (Bus. Rt. USH-18/151) and Main St. (CTH-M), take CTH-M north 1.7 mi. At McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) turn right and go east 0.6 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Ice Age Trail Junction Area 0.2 mi east on CTH-PD. A 0.1-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

**Additional Parking:** (i) University Ridge Golf Course lower parking area accessed from McKee Rd. (CTH-PD), open seasonally during golf course hours. (ii) O.J. Noer Turfgrass Research and Education Center on CTH-M (Pleasant View Rd.). (iii) Flagstone Dr. roadside parking.

### THE HIKE

**Valley View Segment**

This segment features the Alliance-owned Valley View Preserve. The 109.5-acre property is a fine example of prairie and oak savanna restoration driven by area landowners collaborating with the Alliance and the Town of Middleton. So far, their efforts restored 150 contiguous acres into native prairie and savanna plant communities.

Enjoy walking along the Johnstown Terminal Moraine between Ice Age Lane and Moraine Ridge Road. You can see the Blue Mounds from the prairie on a clear day. Increase Lapham wrote that the Blue Mounds “were very important landmarks to guide the traveler in his course through the boundless prairies.”

From the Trail access at the intersection of Timber Lane and West Mineral Point Road, head south, paralleling Timber Lane for a mile. To the west lies agricultural land and a pond attracting migrating waterfowl, and to the east, the terminal moraine and houses. Along the way, the Trail crosses an ancient trail that once ran between Lake Mendota and the Blue Mounds and beyond. At the summit of the first knoll, a high point on the Valley View segment, the Blue Mounds can be seen on the western horizon, 13 miles distant. The Trail eventually descends 200 feet from this knoll to a low point near Ice Age Lane.

![Map of the Valley View segment of the Ice Age Trail. The map shows the trail route starting from West Mineral Point Road, heading south past Shoveler Sink and through IATA's Valley View Preserve, crossing Ice Age Lane and Moraine Ridge Road, and ending near Mid Town Road. It includes road names, mileage markers, and parking locations.](page_268_image_1_v2.jpg)

Leave Timber Lane at an Ice Age Trail sign and head east through thick woods, following switchbacks down a hill. At the bottom, cross a streambed on a short footbridge and head south. You’ll soon cross a bog hosting a boardwalk, followed shortly by a second one. Finally, head east, cross a brook, and reach Ice Age Lane.

After crossing Ice Age Lane, follow a board rail fence to the east. After passing through a patch of woods, cross a residential driveway at a white lantern post. From here, enter a short section of woods and emerge in a large open prairie – the Alliance’s Valley View Preserve – with a series of looped side trails and two trailside benches. After exiting the prairie, enter more woods and cross a dry-run gully before passing through a residential neighborhood featuring prairie-style homes. Please respect private property by staying on the Trail.

After regaining 200 feet of elevation, arrive at the Ice Age Trail Access parking area at Moraine Ridge Road, a Town of Middleton conservancy.

*Continue on the following 0.3-mile connecting route to reach the next off-road section: Head south out of the parking area, turn left on Moraine Ridge Road, then right on Mound View Road. At Saracen Way, turn left and go 0.1 miles to the Trail access.*

Head south through a wooded area. Once you emerge from the woods, continue south on a grassy path between a residential area and farm fields. Then, bend east and parallel Mid Town Road. You will pass a residential sign for Glacier’s End and cross Fernglade Road before reaching the segment’s terminus.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail Madison and Verona segments, showing University Ridge Golf Course, Hawks Landing Golf Course, and surrounding roads like Mid Town Rd, Woods Rd, and Valley View Rd.](page_269_image_1_v2.jpg)

at a yellow-blazed Trail post on the northeast side of the intersection of Shady Oak Lane and Mid Town Road.

*To reach the Madison Segment, follow a 1.8-mile connecting route: head east on Mid Town Road for 1.3 miles, then south on Woods Road for 0.6 miles.*

**Madison Segment**

A large portion of this segment crosses the University Ridge Golf Course, designed by Robert Trent Jones Jr. This public course is the official home course of the University of Wisconsin men’s and women’s golf teams. University Ridge derives its name from the landscape features left by the last retreating glacier. The course sits on the terminal moraine, forming the backbone of the ridge that dictated course construction as designers followed the land’s contours. The seventh hole’s green sits at the base of a large glacial drumlin.

From the Trail access on Woods Road, head east through a trail gate into the woods between two holes of the golf course. Respect the Trail host by walking quietly when passing golfers and avoiding groomed cross-country ski trails during the winter.

Emerge briefly from the woods, turning sharply right at a Trail sign, and cross a cart path before continuing southwest through another wooded area. Emerge again from the woods and meet another paved path – the golf course’s entrance road. You may follow it to access the golf course’s clubhouse, open to Ice Age Trail hikers. The clubhouse has water, restrooms, and a restaurant. Shortly after crossing the road to the clubhouse, turn right and briefly follow a paved cart path. Where the cart path turns right, continue straight on the blazed Trail.

Continue southeast through a grassland/prairie area west of a fairway, bend east, cross another paved cart path, and pass between two tee boxes. Then meet and briefly follow a cart path. Leave the cart path to the right, travel through a small wooded area, and again cross a cart path. The Trail makes its way up a moraine with expansive views to the east and south. Continue north through grassland/prairie before cutting into the woods and bending east.

Exit the woods, hike past an agricultural field, go between two small patches of woods, and follow the southern edge of a grassy area until you meet a paved path leading to the University’s Noer Turfgrass Research Center. Turn left and follow the paved path northeast, briefly follow the Noer Center’s driveway about 50 feet, then turn left onto a gravel path. From here, bear to the right on the gravel path, cross a covered bridge, and make your way through a trail gate to a multiuse path. Join it and follow it underneath Pleasant View Road (CTH-M).

After leaving the underpass, follow the multiuse path for about 300 feet and then leave it, turning sharply right through yet another trail gate. Head through a prairie and a small wooded area and turn right to join a paved multiuse path. After crossing a bridge and then Raymond Road, briefly follow the multiuse path before heading left along the west side of a large drainage basin. Head due south into a small aspen and oak grove giving way to prairie. Note: Along the north/south stretch between Raymond Road and CTH-PD, a multiuse paved path separate from the Ice Age Trail parallels the segment a short distance to the west.

Pass through an area of native grasses and wildflowers that is part of a restoration project begun by Veridian Homes. Climb to the top of a grassy moraine, cross a paved multiuse path. From here, quickly join another multiuse path and then turn sharply right onto another multiuse path to cross McKee Road (CTH-PD) on a pedestrian bridge. The south end of the bridge is the end of the segment.

### AREA SERVICES

| Location                                              | Services and Information                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| University Ridge Golf Course                          | Restaurant. On Trail. Open seasonally (9002 CTH-PD, Madison, 608-845-7700 or 800-897-4343, universityridge.com).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Middleton                                             | Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the Pleasant View Rd. (CTH-M) Trail access, go north on CTH-M 2.5 mi. At Mineral Point Rd. (CTH-S) turn right and go east 0.3 mi. At USH-12 go north 2.6 mi to the USH-14 Exit. Area info available from the Middleton Chamber of Commerce (608-827-5797, middletonchamber.com). |
| Madison                                               | Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) Trail access go east and north \~5 mi to West Towne Mall area. Area info available from the Greater Madison Chamber of Commerce (608-256-8348, madisonbiz.com).                                                                                               |
| Mendota County Park                                   | Camping. From From the McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) Trail access go west then north \~10 mi.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| [Icon: Trail Community logo] Verona | See Verona Segment, p. 239. For access to Verona’s downtown, from the McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) Trail access go west then south \~3 mi.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::v.parent_text:
## Verona Segment (Atlas Map 66f)

**Summary:** The Verona Segment is a 6.3-mile trail in Dane County (Atlas Map 66f), running from McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) to Prairie Moraine County Park at Wesner Rd.. This suburban segment makes good use of three Dane County parks: The Ice Age Trail Junction Area, Badger Prairie County Park, and Prairie Moraine County Park.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage icon] **6.3 miles**: McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) to Prairie Moraine County Park at Wesner Rd.

*   [Icon: water icon] [Icon: restroom icon] At Reddan Soccer Park parking area, Verona Public Library, Badger Prairie County Park main shelter and Ceniti Park Baseball Complex.
*   [Icon: ColdCache icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.
*   [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
*   [Icon: camping icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) in Badger Prairie County Park.
*   [Icon: path icon] Portions overlap multiuse paths, town sidewalks and roads and the Military Ridge State Trail (MRST).
*   [Icon: picnic icon] [Icon: playground icon] At Reddan Soccer Park and Badger Prairie County Park. Playground also available at Ceniti Park Baseball Complex.
*   [Icon: trail junction icon] MRST continues east and west from the Trail; also numerous bike trails and spur trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking 1] **McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) Trail Access (43.01598, -89.52193):** From Verona, at the intersection of Verona Ave. (Bus. Rt. USH-18/151) and Main St. (CTH-M), take CTH-M north 1.7 mi. At McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) turn right and go east 0.6 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Ice Age Trail Junction Area 0.2 mi east on CTH-PD. A 0.1-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

[Icon: parking 2] **Prairie Moraine County Park at Wesner Rd. Trail Access (42.96159, -89.51360):** From USH-18/151 south of Verona, take Exit 79 and follow CTH-PB south for 1.0 mi. At Wesner Rd. turn left to enter Prairie Moraine County Park. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Reddan Soccer Park on Cross Country Rd. (No parking during tournaments.) (ii) Verona Public Library on Silent St. (iii) Badger Prairie County Park (iv) Military Ridge State Trail (MRST)/Ice Age Trail access and Verona Park & Ride parking area on Old PB Rd. (v) Ceniti Park Sports Complex on E. Verona Ave. (vi) Lincoln St. roadside parking. (vii) IATA’s Moraine Kettles Preserve on CTH-M.

### THE HIKE

This suburban segment makes good use of three Dane County parks: The Ice Age Trail Junction Area, Badger Prairie County Park, and Prairie Moraine County Park. It offers access to the services of Verona, an Ice Age Trail Community with the moniker “Hometown U.S.A.” The segment also highlights the Alliance-owned Moraine Kettles Preserve. This 24-acre property includes a portion of the Johnstown Terminal Moraine. The property also boasts the transformation from agricultural fields into a restored prairie.

From the Trail access on McKee Road (CTH-PD) at the south end of the pedestrian bridge, turn right off the multiuse trail and head across a prairie. Shortly you’ll meet a blue-blazed spur trail on the left leading to the Ice Age Trail Junction Area parking area.

From the junction with the blue-blazed trail, continue south through the prairie and begin a slight descent toward a wooded area. Look south for a fine overview of features you will encounter on the segment – the hills of Badger Prairie County Park, Badger Mill Creek, and the moraine on the horizon at the end of the segment.

Enter the wooded area and head left at a fork. Travel south through another prairie before reaching a short spur trail (near a maintenance shed) leading

![Map of the Ice Age Trail Madison Segment / Junction Area, including Reddan Soccer Park, Badger Prairie County Park, and Prairie Moraine County Park.](page_272_image_1_v2.jpg)

2.9-mi CR to Montrose Segment. West on Wesner Rd., south on CTH-PB, east on Sunset Dr., south on Borchert Rd., east on Purcell Rd.

west. Continue south, passing Reddan Soccer Park. Several mowed paths intersect the Trail near the soccer park; continue to follow the Trail south toward Cross Country Road.

At Cross Country Road turn right and follow the Trail, which runs west along a fence on the north side of the road to the entrance of the soccer park. Cross the road south at a marked pedestrian crossing. Several restaurants are 0.3 miles west on Cross Country Road. Enter Badger Prairie County Park and continue southward. You’ll quickly meet a mowed park path and briefly follow it west and south before heading east. To the southwest is the Verona Public Library, which is accessible by following a mowed park path.

After turning east, you’ll ascend a ridge, cross the top of it, and descend it, first on a gravel road, then on a mowed path. Finally, just before reaching the park’s main shelter, you’ll pass a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multi-day hikers, in the restored prairie dominating the park.

After passing the DCA, the main park shelter, and parking area, continue south and east, hooking up with a paved bike

trail as you head toward Verona Avenue (CTH-MV). You’ll pass a large shopping area to the west with restaurants and a large grocery store. Continue heading west on Verona Avenue (CTH-MV) for additional services, including lodging.

Go under Verona Avenue (CTH-MV) via a tunnel, turn left and continue east, then southeast on the bike trail to the Military Ridge State Trail (MRST) Park & Ride parking area off Old County Road PB. The multiuse MRST, connecting Madison to Dodgeville, is located on a railroad grade, abandoned in the 1970s, that once extended to Galena, Ill.

Head west on the MRST. Just past mile marker 3, depart from the MRST on a gravel path and go south, then southwest along the west side of Badger Mill Creek, which flows through a meltwater gap in the moraine. At the point where the gravel path turns left, leading to a bridge over the creek, you’ll continue straight ahead along the northwest bank of the creek.

Pass through a wetland area, hiking on top of a berm for part of the route. After leaving the berm, you’ll reach a small pine grove with a trailside bench. Turn left to cross a bridge to the east side of the creek and turn right. Briefly follow a paved path before turning left onto a dirt path. You will cross a swampy area with a boardwalk/puncheon span and then head into the woods.

Briefly skirt an open area and then reach a junction with a paved path connecting residential neighborhoods. (If you head west on it, the paved path leads across the creek on the Lincoln Street footbridge and connects with the street’s dead end. Lincoln Street provides access to Verona’s city center less than a mile away. To reach several restaurants, a supermarket, and a hardware store, walk northwest on Lincoln Street, left on Valley View Street, and right on South Main Street.)

Continue south, following the paved path along the creek. In short order, leave the paved path and turn left, cutting into the woods. Make a steep ascent from the creek before gradually descending to CTH-M. Continue east near or along the north side of CTH-M, cross USH-18/151 on an overpass, continue on the north side of CTH-M, and then cross it and start hiking south through a restored prairie. Soon you’ll meet a spur trail leading to the CTH-M parking area. From here, enter the woods and pass several kettle ponds in the Alliance’s Moraine Kettles Preserve. Leave the woods, cut through another prairie, and reenter the woods, where the Trail heads east toward CTH-PB. At the intersection with CTH-PB, briefly walk north and cautiously cross the road at the end of a median. *Note: Traffic along CTH-PB is heavy; exercise caution at this Trail crossing.*

Enter a wooded area of the 160-acre Prairie Moraine County Park, which features a 0.8-mile-long stretch of the terminal moraine on St. Peter sandstone bedrock. Head south and then southeast, gently ascending the north-northeast side of a terminal moraine through restored prairie and savanna. At the top of the moraine, a spur trail heads east to a viewing platform. It offers views of a ravine cut in the moraine by meltwater and of the upper Sugar River Valley in the Driftless Area to the south.

From a saddle on the moraine, descend westward using several short switchbacks. Curve southward to reach the segment’s end at a gate in the northwest corner of the park’s parking area near CTH-PB. The moraine and the Ice Age Trail are fenced off from a heavily used dog park south and east of the Trail.

**AREA SERVICES**

**Madison**: See Valley View Segment and Madison Segment, p. 235. From the McKee Rd. (CTH-PD) Trail access go east and north ~5 mi to the West Towne Mall area.

[Icon: Verona Trail Community logo] **Verona**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the E. Verona Ave. (CTH-MV) Trail crossing go west 0.9 mi on E. Verona Ave. Area information available from the Verona Chamber of Commerce (608-845-5777, visitveronawi.com).

![Verona Segment: A quiet forest glows in the afternoon sunlight.](page_274_image_1_v2.jpg)

LAUREN RAPINCHUK

Verona Segment: A quiet forest glows in the afternoon sunlight.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::m.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::m.parent_text:
## Montrose Segment (Atlas Map 67f)

**Summary:** The Montrose Segment is a 7.5-mile trail in Dane County (Atlas Map 67f), running from Purcell Rd. to CTH-D. This segment starts by heading southwest on the Badger State Trail (BST), which extends north to Madison’s Capital City Trail and south to the Illinois border.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiking icon] **7.5 miles**: Purcell Rd. to CTH-D

[Icon: no water icon] No reliable sources of water.

[Icon: camping icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA logo] north of CTH-D.

[Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: multiuse trail icon] Portions overlap the multiuse Badger State Trail (BST) and Piller Rd.

[Icon: spur trail icon] BST continues north and south. Also, prairie spur trails and blue-blazed spur trail to the DCA.

[Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] **1 Purcell Rd. Trail Access (42.94340, -89.48199)**: From USH-18/151 south of Verona, take Exit 79 and follow CTH-PB south for 2.2 mi. At Purcell Rd. turn left and go east 1.7 mi. **Parking**: Immediately east and south on Sayles Tr.

[Icon: parking icon 2] **2 CTH-D Trail Access (42.87582, -89.49207)**: From Madison take Fish Hatchery Rd. (CTH-D) ~14 mi south. **No Parking**. Instead, park at the Brooklyn State Wildlife Area on CTH-DD. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

**Additional Parking**: (i) CTH-A roadside parking near the intersection where CTH-A heads west from STH-69. (ii) Frenchtown Rd./Piller Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment starts by heading southwest on the Badger State Trail (BST), which extends north to Madison’s Capital City Trail and south to the Illinois border. Just south of the Purcell Road Trail access, the segment crosses the Johnstown Moraine through a cut in the moraine made by railroad construction crews before traversing several refurbished trestle bridges spanning intermittent creeks.

From the Trail access at Purcell Road, head southwest, following the BST on a crushed limestone surface. Depart the BST at the Ice Age Trail sign and bench (near the intersection of CTH-A and STH-69, where CTH-A heads west). Hike between two agricultural fields, cross a 40-foot-long bridge, and meander through a small wooded area before reaching Piller Road. Turn south and follow a field edge and then Piller Road itself.

From the intersection of Piller Road and Frenchtown Road, the site of a large parking area, continue due south between two agricultural fields before entering a wooded area. Immediately wind uphill and bend west, south, then east, climbing up to a farm field. You’ll pass an impressive bedrock outcropping dotted with ferns along the way. Head southeast through open fields to a high plateau from which you can enjoy long views, especially in leaf-off seasons.

Turn east, enter another wooded area, drop down a ridge, then continue south across a farm field and reenter the woods. Climb gradually up the west side of a sandstone ridge, eventually making your way to a fine goat prairie. At a trailside bench enjoy outstanding views across a glacial outwash plain to the Sugar River watershed and the city of Belleville.

2.9-mi CR to Verona Segment. West on Purcell Rd., north on Borchert Rd., west on Sunset Dr., north on CTH-PB, east on Wesner Rd.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment showing the route from Purcell Rd. to CTH-D, including landmarks like Basco, Paoli, and the Badger State Trail intersection.](page_276_image_1_v2.jpg)

Turn northeast and cross a high prairie plateau where volunteers are engaged in prairie restoration. Meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading south 0.1 miles to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. From the junction with the spur trail, enter the woods and turn southeast to reach the segment’s terminus on CTH-D.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: TJ logo] **Verona:** See Verona Segment, p. 239. From the Purcell Rd. Trail access go west then north ~4 mi.

**Paoli:** Restaurant. From the Purcell Rd. Trail access go west 1.6 mi, then south 1.1 mi on CTH-PB, then west on Sun Valley Pkwy. *Note: There is no access to Sun Valley Parkway from the Ice Age Trail/Badger State Trail.*

**Basco:** Restaurant (tavern).

**Belleville:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. From the CTH-D Trail access go west then south ~3 mi.

![A narrow dirt path winds through a lush forest understory filled with bright yellow Rudbeckia flowers. Tall trees with green leaves form a canopy above. A yellow trail blaze is visible on a tree trunk to the right. Photographer credit: DORIS DIX](page_277_image_1_v2.jpg)

Montrose Segment: Rudbeckia line the path, lighting up the forest.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::dane-county::b.parent_text:
## Brooklyn Wildlife Segment (Atlas Map 68f)

**Summary:** The Brooklyn Wildlife Segment is a 3.4-mile trail in Dane County (Atlas Map 68f), running from CTH-D to Hughes Rd.. This segment traverses an area featuring highly eroded glacial deposits from a glacial advance more than 60,000 years ago.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **3.4 miles:** CTH-D to Hughes Rd.

[Icon: water icon] From a hand pump south of the southern CTH-DD parking area and at Exeter Park in nearby Dayton (1.8 mi south).

[Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.

[Icon: camping icon] [Icon: restroom icon] At Exeter Park in nearby Dayton (1.8 mi south).

[Icon: spur trail icon] Two blue-blazed spur trails to parking areas.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **CTH-D Trail Access (42.87582, -89.49207):** From Madison, take Fish Hatchery Rd. (CTH-D) ~14 mi south. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Brooklyn State Wildlife Area on CTH-DD. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

② **Hughes Rd. Trail Access (42.84255, -89.49922):** From Belleville at the intersection of STH-92 and STH-69, take STH-92 east then south 3.0 mi to Dayton. At Dayton take Green County’s CTH-D north 1.0 mi. At Hughes Rd. turn right and go east 0.3 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** Brooklyn State Wildlife Area parking area on CTH-DD. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

*Note: Don’t get confused! In Dane County, the county’s CTH-D crosses the Ice Age Trail as the road is making its way southwest toward Belleville. In Green County, the county’s CTH-D runs north/south, passing through the unincorporated town of Dayton on its way to the Green/Dane county line. Upon reaching the county line, Green County’s CTH-D becomes CTH-DD, which in turn connects up a mile north with Dane County’s CTH-D.*

### THE HIKE

This segment traverses an area featuring highly eroded glacial deposits from a glacial advance more than 60,000 years ago. Highlights are meadows, woodlands, oak savannas, and colorful prairies. Story Creek, a Class II trout stream, runs north to south through the property. Also, the area’s habitat creates excellent bird-watching opportunities – herons and cranes fish in the Story Creek wetlands. You might stir up pheasants, which are stocked by the DNR in the State Wildlife Area.

Volunteers from the Friends of Brooklyn State Wildlife Area and the Ice Age Trail Alliance’s Dane County Chapter have worked extensively on prairie restoration and invasive species removal throughout the 3,096-acre Brooklyn Wildlife Area. During periods of rain or snow melt, portions of the Trail can become wet and muddy, especially in the woods near the south end of the segment. This section can also become very buggy.

From the Trail access on CTH-D head south. Several side trails make loops with the Ice Age Trail. Two blue-blazed spur trails lead to the parking areas on CTH-DD. Pay attention to signage, as intersections can be confusing. In addition to designated spur trails, there are numerous fire breaks and side trails to or on private property; please stay off them. About halfway through the segment is a particularly impressive overlook of the Story Creek wetlands and the

Johnstown Moraine. Farther on is a trailside hand water pump. From here, continue south to the segment’s end at Hughes Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Belleville:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. From the Dane County CTH-D Trail access go west 2.6 mi on CTH-D and then south 0.3 mi on STH-69. John Frederick Memorial Park (Library Park), downtown on Main St., has a drinking fountain. Additional village parks with restrooms, picnic areas and playgrounds. Public swimming pool with showers at the Belleville Aquatic Center (seasonal).

**New Glarus:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the CTH-D Trail access go west then south ~11 mi.

**Dayton:** Restaurant (tavern). From the Hughes Rd. Trail access, go west 0.3 mi and south on Green County CTH-D for 1.0 mi.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment showing the route from Bellbrook Rd. south to Hughes Rd., passing through the Brooklyn State Wildlife Area and crossing the Dane County/Green County line. The map includes waypoints 1 and 2, parking areas, a water pump icon, and a scale bar.](page_279_image_1_v2.jpg)

> *I didn’t like being [housebound during Covid]. I started hiking by myself or with my family. It started with just three miles, then five, then seven, and then nine miles. The Ice Age Trail gave us back our freedom. Before the summer arrived, I finished hiking all of the Dane County miles and earned my first patch!*

GRACE ZHANG, THOUSAND-MILER

# Green County

Atlas Maps 68f–71f; Databook pages 59, 67–68
Total miles: 22.7 (Trail 15.9; Connecting Route 6.8)

![Map of Wisconsin with Green County highlighted](page_280_image_1_v2.jpg)

Green County was largely untouched by the Green Bay Lobe of the late Wisconsin Glaciation. At its maximum extent, roughly 15,000 years ago, this lobe crept as far south as the village of Brooklyn. Much earlier glacial advances covered most of the county, except a portion to the northwest that is part of the Driftless Area.

Describing Green County in 1846, Increase Lapham wrote, “The mineral country extends nearly to the eastern part of this county, where the lead-bearing rock crops out... There are already several very valuable discoveries of lead, and many flattering prospects of more. These mines are nearer Lake Michigan than any other in the mining country.”

In the northern part of the county, the Ice Age Trail follows the Badger State Trail (BST). A 2007 addition to the state’s rail-trail system, the BST runs on the former Illinois Central Railroad corridor. It travels from Madison’s Capital City Trail across the state line to Orangeville, Ill., joining the Grand Illinois Trail system.

The Sugar River State Trail (SRST) was created in 1974 after the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources purchased the abandoned railbed of the former Milwaukee Railroad. The crushed limestone rail-trail stretches 23.1 miles from New Glarus to Brodhead, and with its 1% grade, it is perhaps one of the few flat surfaces in the county. It follows the Sugar River and crosses it 14 times on various trestle bridges.

The Ice Age Trail shares the SRST as it passes over rushing streams and through picturesque rolling hills, dairy farms, verdant meadows, and state wildlife refuges. Hikers may find wild edible plants along this wildlife corridor during warmer months. The SRST is a popular weekend destination for bicyclists. Hiking on these two rail-trails is free; bike use requires a pass and fee payable at trail access points. Snowmobiles use the SRST in winter.

No camping is allowed along either rail-trail. However, camping is available at New Glarus Woods Park and private campgrounds near Albany.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

Green County segments of the Ice Age Trail are maintained by Dane and Rock County Chapter volunteers.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Green County Visitor Information:** 608-328-1838, greencounty.org
**Sugar River State Trail:** 608-527-2335, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/sugarriver
**Badger State Trail:** 608-523-4427, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/badger

![Albany Segment: A ribbon of Trail stretches out beneath a summer sky. Photo by EMMA TEMME](page_281_image_1_v2.jpg)

Albany Segment: A ribbon of Trail stretches out beneath a summer sky.

## Monticello Segment (Atlas Maps 68f, 69f)

**Summary:** The Monticello Segment is a 6.6-mile trail in Green County (Atlas Map 68f, 69f), running from CTH-W to CTH-EE. Although the 1,260-foot-long Stewart Tunnel is currently closed, you can walk up to the gated entrance to peer inside.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiking and map icons] **6.6 miles**: CTH-W to CTH-EE

*Note: The Stewart Tunnel is closed indefinitely, pending reconstruction. Check with the DNR (dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/badger/tunnel) for details. Until then, enjoy a lovely walk along rolling backcountry roads on the posted detour route: from the first intersection of the Trail and Tunnel Rd., take Tunnel Rd. west and south 2.2 mi to where the Trail crosses Tunnel Rd. a second time.*

[Icon: water icon] At Monticello’s Old Train Depot on the Sugar River State Trail (SRST).

[Icon: fishing icon] From the Little Sugar River.

[Icon: camping icon] At nearby New Glarus Woods State Park ~3 mi west of the Trail (see Area Services).

[Icon: picnic icon] At a small pavilion south of Stewart Tunnel and at the Monticello Old Train Depot area on the SRST.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: bicycle icon] [Icon: snowmobile icon] The BST is open to bicycles and snowmobiles.

[Icon: trail continues icon] The BST continues north and south.

[Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] ① **CTH-W Trail Access (42.82856, -89.55252)**: From Belleville at the intersection of STH-92 and STH-69, take STH-92 east then south 2.6 mi. At CTH-W turn right and go west 1.3 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park roadside at the CTH-CC Trail crossing located 0.2 mi west on CTH-W and then 0.4 mi south on CTH-CC.

[Icon: 2 icon] ② **CTH-EE Trail Access (42.74148, -89.57769)**: From Monticello at the intersection of STH-69 and Lake Ave., take Lake Ave. east 1.0 mi. At Pratt Rd. turn right and go south 0.3 mi. At CTH-EE turn left and go east 0.1 mi. **No Parking**. Instead park at the Sugar River State Trail/Old Train Depot parking area on Pratt Rd. just north of CTH-EE.

**Additional Parking**: Tunnel Rd. northern Trail access roadside parking.

### THE HIKE

Although the 1,260-foot-long Stewart Tunnel is currently closed, you can walk up to the gated entrance to peer inside. See how far you can gaze into the tunnel; you may even hear a bat! After getting your fill of the tunnel, head out on the posted detour route, which takes you along the rolling hills and roads of Green County. You’ll enjoy peaceful countryside and a nice view from Tunnel Road before joining up with the Sugar River State Trail.

[Icon: trail alert] From the Trail access at CTH-W, head southwest on the wide, crushed-gravel-surfaced Badger State Trail (BST). Follow the posted detour route onto Tunnel Rd., following it until you intersect with the Trail. Then, cross Exeter Crossing Road.

(At Exeter Crossing Road, you can take an extended side trip to the Swiss-style village of New Glarus, known as America’s Little Switzerland. Head west 0.4 miles on Exeter Crossing Road to the Sugar River State Trail (SRST), then north and west 3.4 mi on the SRST. You can also access the New Glarus Woods State Park by taking the Sugar River Spur Trail 1.6 mi south from the SRST in New Glarus.)

![Map showing the Badger State Trail near Stewart Tunnel with a temporary bypass and a connecting route to the Brooklyn Wildlife Segment.](page_283_image_2_v2.jpg)

On Trail, continue heading south, cross Coates Avenue (CTH-C), and pass Monticello. Continue to CTH-EE, the segment’s end, near the historic Monticello train depot.

![Map showing the Badger State Trail passing through Exeter and Monticello, with connections to the Sugar River State Trail and the start of the Albany Segment.](page_283_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**New Glarus Woods State Park**: Camping. From Exeter Crossing Rd. Trail access go west 2.5 mi on Exeter Crossing Rd. then north on STH-69 2 mi. Or walk from the Exeter Crossing Rd. Trail access: Go west 0.4 mi. At the Sugar River Trail walk north and west 3.4 mi. (608-523-4427, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/ngwoods; reservations: 888-947-2757,wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

**New Glarus**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From E. Lake Ave. in Monticello take the Sugar River State Trail north ~6 mi or drive north from Monticello on STH-69. Area info available from the New Glarus Chamber of Commerce and Tourist Information (608-527-2095,swisstown.com).

**Monticello**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library. On Trail. At Coates Ave. (CTH-C) go west 0.8 mi to Main St. Most services on Main St. or STH-69.

![Map of Albany Segment showing the trail route from Monticello to Albany, including the Sugar River State Trail and Badger State Trail.](page_284_image_1_v2.jpg)

## Albany Segment (Atlas Maps 69f, 70f)

**Summary:** The Albany Segment is a 9.3-mile trail in Green County (Atlas Map 69f, 70f), running from CTH-EE to Bump Rd.. Most of this segment shares the wide, crushed-gravel-surfaced Sugar River State Trail (SRST).

### SNAPSHOT

![Snapshot overview](page_284_image_2_v2.jpg)
![Snapshot details](page_284_image_6_v2.jpg)
**9.3 miles**: CTH-EE to Bump Rd.

*   [Icon: Parking and water icon] At Monticello’s Old Train Depot and Sugar River State Trail (SRST) parking area and in Albany.
*   From the Little Sugar and Sugar rivers.
*   [Icon: Campground icon] Private campground south of Bump Rd.
*   One ColdCache site on segment.
*   [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
*   [Icon: Bicycle and snowmobile icon] The BST and SRST are open to bicycles and snowmobiles.
*   The SRST continues north and south. The Badger State Trail continues north and south.
*   [Icon: Wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **CTH-EE Trail Access (42.74148, -89.57769)**: From Monticello at the intersection of STH-69 and Lake Ave., take Lake Ave. east 1.0 mi. At Pratt Rd. turn right and go south 0.3 mi. At CTH-EE turn left and go east 0.1 mi. **No Parking**. Instead park at the Sugar River State Trail/Old Train Depot parking area on Pratt Rd. just north of CTH-EE.

② **Bump Rd. Trail Access (42.70484, -89.42763)**: From Albany at the intersection of STH-59 and Cincinnati St., take Cincinnati St. south 0.8 mi. At Bump Rd. turn left and go east 0.1 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at the Sugar River State Trail parking area on 4th St.

### THE HIKE

Most of this segment shares the wide, crushed-gravel-surfaced Sugar River State Trail (SRST). It begins and ends in small towns, and long stretches offer pastoral views. It

![Map of the Sugar River State Trail segment through the Albany State Wildlife Area, showing the trail route, road crossings, and the Little Sugar River.](page_285_image_2_v2.jpg)

meanders along the Little Sugar River and through the Albany Wildlife Area, featuring rustic wooden bridges, wildlife sightings, and sounds of bullfrogs and dairy cows.

At the segment’s end in Albany, the town’s historical museum is a few blocks off Trail. It offers a nice slice of area history, explaining that the Ho-Chunk people called the river “Tonasookarah,” meaning sugar, referring to the maple trees along the riverbank. The Sugar River’s abundance of oysters and clams, now protected, led it to be called the “River of Pearls.”

From the Trail access at CTH-EE, head south on the Badger State Trail (BST) for 0.2 miles until it intersects the SRST, paralleling the Little Sugar River. Turn left and follow the SRST in a southeasterly direction; the BST continues south toward the Illinois border. Watch for wild turkeys, pheasant, deer, sandhill cranes, snakes, and turtles that cross the path to lay their eggs.

As you near the village of Albany, you’ll cross the Little Sugar River and then, on a long curving bridge with a picturesque view, the Sugar River. The latter crossing is just upstream from where the two rivers converge. Continue on the SRST southwest into Albany, where the segment ends at Bump Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Monticello:** *See Monticello Segment, p. 250*. At CTH-EE go west 0.1 mi. At the SRST or Pratt Rd. go north 0.3 mi. past the Monticello Old Train Depot. At Lake Ave. go west 0.7 mi. Most services on Main St. or STH-69.

**Albany:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. On Trail. From the Trail access, the business district is a few blocks west.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Dane and Rock Counties, showing segments including Evansville, Gibbs Lake, Arbor Ridge, Devil's Staircase, and Janesville to Milton. The map includes a legend for Ice Age Trail Community, Ice Age Trail, Connecting Route (unmarked), Other Trail, County Boundary, and Public or IATA Land.](page_286_image_1_v2.jpg)

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through Rock, Walworth, and Jefferson Counties, showing segments including Blue Spring Lake, Blackhawk, Whitewater Lake, Clover Valley, Storrs Lake, Milton, and Janesville. iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::6.parent_text:
# Rock County Atlas Maps 71f–77f; Databook pages 70–75 Total miles: 64.5 (Trail 25.1; Connecting Route 39.4) ![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Rock County](page_288_image_1_v2.jpg) In Rock County, home to the southernmost segments of the Ice Age Trail, the Green Bay Lobe lost power and began to retreat about 20,000 years ago. As a result, the northern third of the county has hills, kettles, and other landforms left behind when the ice sheet came to a stop. The Green Bay Lobe worked like a conveyor to drop glacial material near the ice edge, forming the Johnstown Moraine. Hikers can see this terminal moraine along the Ice Age Trail route between Janesville and Milton. The Johnstown Moraine, named after a town in eastern Rock County, extends north to Waushara County. The remainder of Rock County was glaciated more than 100,000 years ago and is part of an area that stretches south beyond the Illinois border as prairie flatlands. The Ice Age Trail crosses the Rock River, a massive glacial meltwater channel during the last glacial advance and retreat. Today it carries a smaller volume of water. The river traces its origin north through Horicon Marsh and to the west edge of the Kettle Moraine near Allenton in Washington County. The Sauk, Fox, Potawatomi, and Ho-Chunk nations were all in this area at one time. They named the Rock River after the rocks that caused the rapids at the mouth of the river where it empties into the Mississippi. Historical sites significant to Black Hawk War events are located throughout the county, including a few located along the Ice Age Trail route. The Trail winds through two Ice Age Trail Communities, Janesville and Milton, highlighting their Ice Age and cultural history. The first permanent structure in soon-to-be Janesville was built in 1835. By the 1840s, dams and bridges, as well as lumber, grist, and woolen mills, had been built along the river, water power contributing to Janesville’s early prosperity. In the following decades, flour milling, woolen and cotton production, and eventually automobile manufacturing, fueled a growing economy and population. Today, hikers will enjoy the amenities of Janesville, a thriving city with a vibrant, revitalized downtown. Joseph Goodrich founded Milton, building the first frame house. But his legacy is his 1844 hexagonal stagecoach inn, the Milton House, the first poured grout building in the United States. Today, it is a National Historic Landmark with a verifiable connection to the Underground Railroad. Goodrich, a staunch abolitionist, had a tunnel built beneath the inn, providing a hiding place for freedom seekers. The Milton House, now a museum, offers tours conducted by the Milton Historical Society. *Ice Age Trail Guidebook* 2023 Today, the City of Milton celebrates its well-preserved history, and its charming downtown offers a wide variety of shopping and dining experiences. ### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION The Rock County Chapter hosts trail stewardship days and special events throughout the year. Chapter members work in collaboration with the Rock Trail Coalition in developing trails in the county for hiking and biking. The Chapter’s “Walk Across Rock County” program rewards those who walk all of Rock County’s Ice Age Trail segments with a patch and certificate. Start your Walk Across Rock County at IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards. Learn more about the Chapter or locate volunteers willing to shuttle hikers by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/rock-county-chapter. ### COUNTY INFORMATION | Rock County Tourism Council | rockcounty.org | | ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | | Rock County Parks Department | 608-757-5450,
co.rock.wi.us/departments/public-works-parks | | Ice Age Trail Community, Janesville | 800-487-2757, janesvillecvb.com | | Ice Age Trail Community, Milton | visitmilton.com | ![Janesville Segment: A curvy path winds beneath a shady canopy.](page_289_image_1_v2.jpg) LORI DALRYMPLE *Janesville Segment: A curvy path winds beneath a shady canopy.*
