## Evansville Segment (Atlas Maps 72f)

**Summary:** The Evansville Segment is a 1.7-mile trail in Rock County (Atlas Map 72f), running from S. 2nd St. to N. Madison St. (STH-59/213). This segment features a charming turn-of-the-century small town.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: segment icon] **1.7 miles**: S. 2nd St. to N. Madison St. (STH-59/213)

*Note: New Ice Age Trail is planned on the southwest side of Evansville. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

![amenities icons](page_290_image_6_v2.jpg) Leonard-Leota Park.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: trail path icon] Leonard-Leota Park paths.

[Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

[Icon: no hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] **1 S. 2nd St. (42.77210, -89.30326):** From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171B and follow USH-14 west for 16.7 mi. Continue west on E. Main St. 0.5 mi. At S. 2nd St. turn left and go south 0.6 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

[Icon: parking icon 2] **2 N. Madison St. (STH-59/213) Trail Access (42.78394, -89.29930):** From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171B and follow USH-14 west for 16.7 mi. At N. Union Rd (USH-14) turn right and go north and northwest 0.4 mi. At N. Madison St. turn left and go southwest 500 ft. to Antes Dr. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Leonard-Leota Park parking area 0.2 mi west and northwest on Antes Dr.

### THE HIKE

This segment features a charming turn-of-the-century small town. According to the Wisconsin Historical Society, Evansville is home to “the finest collection of the 1840s to 1915 architecture of any small town in Wisconsin.” A walkable five-block area near and along Main Street showcases the more than 300 properties listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Enjoy walking along the reclaimed brick pavers, which line Main Street.

The segment ends at the town’s Leonard-Leota Park, established in 1883 and later expanded. It is listed on the National Register of Historic Places for its 1930s-era landscape design. The park’s crown jewel is the 40-acre stream-fed Lake Leota. The property attracted motorists traveling Highway 14, once the primary route from Chicago to La Crosse to Minneapolis. Suffragist and visionary Susan B. Anthony spoke along the shores of Lake Leota twice as part of Chautauqua gatherings. The historic Baker Office Building, located near the park’s main entrance, is now a museum with rotating local history exhibits.

From the Trail access on South 2nd Street, enter Wind Prairie Park and cut across it, following yellow blazes to South 1st Street, where you will turn left and head north. At West Liberty Street, turn right and head east. Cross South Madison Street (STH-59/STH-213) and continue another block to Maple Street. Turn left and head north on Maple Street, crossing East Church Street. At Main Street, turn left and head west two blocks before intersecting South 2nd Street, where you will turn right and head north to Leonard Park Drive.

![Map of the Evansville Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the route through the city of Evansville, Lake Leota, and connecting routes to the Albany Segment and Gibbs Lake Segment.](page_291_image_1_v2.jpg)

Cross Leonard Park Drive and enter the park utilizing a horseshoe-shaped sidewalk connecting you to the central path leading north toward the lake. At Leonard Park Drive, turn right and follow it until it becomes Antes Drive. Continue on Antes Drive, following it around and south until it turns left toward North Madison Street, the segment’s terminus.

### AREA SERVICES

Evansville: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, and medical service. On Trail. Area info available from the City of Evansville (608-882-2266, ci.evansville.wi.gov/explore_evansville) and the Evansville Chamber of Commerce & Tourism (608-882-5131, evansvillechamber.org).

CAMERON GILLIE

![Photograph of historic turn-of-the-century storefronts on Main Street in Evansville.](page_291_image_2_v2.jpg)

Evansville Segment: Turn-of-the-century Main Street storefronts welcome hikers.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::g.parent_text:
## Gibbs Lake Segment (Atlas Maps 72f)

**Summary:** The Gibbs Lake Segment is a 1.3-mile trail in Rock County (Atlas Map 72f), running from W. Gibbs Lake Rd. to N. Eagle Rd.. This segment highlights a tranquil 278-acre park hosting 73-acre Gibbs Lake.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiking icon] **1.3 miles**: W. Gibbs Lake Rd. to N. Eagle Rd.

[Icon: water and picnic icons] Water pump and pit toilet at parking lot.

Picnic shelter and picnic area.

[Icon: hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: horse trail icon] Segment shares a horse trail around the lake’s east side and private driveway.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] **1 W. Gibbs Lake Rd. Trail Access (42.78589, -89.18121)**: From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171A and follow STH-26 (Milton Ave.) south for 0.5 mi. At Humes Rd. (USH-14) turn right and go west 9.3 mi. At N. Eagle Rd. turn right and go north 2.8 mi. At W. Gibbs Lake Rd. turn left and go west 0.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2 icon] **2 N. Eagle Rd. Trail Access (42.78101, -89.17130)**: From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171A and follow STH-26 (Milton Ave.) south for 0.5 mi. At Humes Rd. (USH-14) turn right and go west 9.3 mi. At N. Eagle Rd. turn right and go north 2.3 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at Gibbs Lake County Park main entrance on W. Gibbs Lake Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights a tranquil 278-acre park hosting 73-acre Gibbs Lake. Ringed by woods, the lake is home to panfish, largemouth bass, and northern pike. The park includes a picnic area, shelter, pit toilets, and lake access from a public boat landing. Enjoy a quiet respite between road walks along the connecting route.

![Map of Gibbs Lake Segment showing the trail route around Gibbs Lake, parking areas, and connecting routes to Evansville and Arbor Ridge segments.](page_292_image_1_v2.jpg)

From the Trail access at West Gibbs Lake Road, head east across the parking lot to connect with the Trail. Immediately join a horse trail and follow it south as it meanders through the woods. Enjoy occasional glimpses of Gibbs Lake

through the trees. Following the yellow blazes, you’ll make your way to the segment’s most southern point before turning east and following the Trail as it weaves along a tree line edging a field. A sharp left turn takes you north to a private drive. Turn right at the driveway and follow it to the segment’s terminus at North Eagle Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Evansville:** See Evansville Segment, p. 258. Turn left onto W. Gibbs Lake Rd and head southwest 0.5 mi. Turn left onto N. Casey Rd. for 0.2 mi. Turn right onto W. Gibbs Lake Rd. and go 1.0 mi. Turn left at the first cross street onto N. Riley Rd. and go 1.2 mi. Turn right onto US-14 W for 4.4 mi.

**Janesville:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, medical service. On Trail. Area info available from the Janesville Area Convention and Visitors Bureau (seasonal visitor center located on the Trail in Palmer Park; 800-487-2757, janesvillecvb.com), City of Janesville Parks Department (608-755-3025) and City of Janesville Recreation Department (608-755-3030, janesvillewi.gov). Access to services: (i). Turn right onto W. Gibbs Lake Rd. and head northwest 0.5 mi. Turn right onto N. Eagle Rd. and go 2.8 mi. Turn left onto US-14 E and go 9.2 mi.

![A calm morning creates a mirror image on the water at Gibbs Lake.](page_293_image_1_v2.jpg)

CAMERON GILLIE

Gibbs Lake Segment: A calm morning creates a mirror image on the water.

## Arbor Ridge Segment and Devil’s Staircase Segment (Atlas Maps 73f, 74f)

**Summary:** The Arbor Ridge Segment and Devil’s Staircase Segment covers approximately 3.9 miles in Rock County (Atlas Map 73f, 74f). It comprises the Arbor Ridge Segment (2.1 mi, Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum Upper Parking Area to N. Washington St. (CTH-E)), the Devil’s Staircase Segment (1.8 mi, N. Washington St. (CTH-E) to Riverside Park South Pavilion). ① [Icon: Access point 1] **Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum Upper Parking Area Trail Access (42.70672, -89.07151):** From Janesville at the intersection of N. Washington St. and W. Memorial Dr., take W. Memorial Dr., which becomes CTH-A, west 1.9 mi.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Arbor Ridge Segment icon] **Arbor Ridge Segment—2.1 miles:** Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum Upper Parking Area to N. Washington St. (CTH-E)

* [Icon: water tap] At the Arboretum Outdoor Educational Lab area.
* [Icon: water drop] From Marsh Creek.
* [Icon: picnic table] In the Arboretum near the Marsh Creek area.
* [Icon: no hunting] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: dog on leash] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not permitted in Janesville Parks May 15 to Sept. 15.**
* [Icon: asphalt walkway] Small portion overlaps asphalt walkway in the arboretum.
* [Icon: trail network] Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum trail network.
* [Icon: wheelchair accessibility] Portions of this segment are suitable for those using wheelchair or similar device. In addition, there is a network of paved trails accessible from the Arboretum’s upper parking area.

[Icon: Devil's Staircase Segment icon] **Devil’s Staircase Segment—1.8 miles:** N. Washington St. (CTH-E) to Riverside Park South Pavilion

* [Icon: water tap] [Icon: picnic table] [Icon: restroom] [Icon: people icon] At Riverside Park North and South pavilions.
* [Icon: water drop] [Icon: water drop] From the Rock River.
* [Icon: ColdCache] Two ColdCache sites on segment.
* [Icon: no hunting] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: dog on leash] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not permitted in Janesville Parks May 15 to Sept. 15.**
* [Icon: golf course path] Small portions overlap golf course paths.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① [Icon: Access point 1] **Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum Upper Parking Area Trail Access (42.70672, -89.07151):** From Janesville at the intersection of N. Washington St. and W. Memorial Dr., take W. Memorial Dr., which becomes CTH-A, west 1.9 mi. At the paved entrance drive for the Arboretum turn right and go north 0.4 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

② [Icon: Access point 2] **N. Washington St. (CTH-E) Trail Access (42.71533, -89.05413): Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

③ [Icon: Access point 3] **Riverside Park South Pavilion Trail Access (42.70637, -89.03616):** From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171B and follow USH-14 west for 4.3 mi. At N. Washington St. (CTH-E) turn left and go south 2.5 mi. At Parkside Dr. turn left and go northeast 0.4 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum lower parking area. (ii) Arbor Ridge subdivision roadside parking. (iii) Riverside Park on Parkside Dr.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::a.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::a.parent_text:
## Arbor Ridge Segment

**Summary:** The Arbor Ridge Segment: This segment traverses an area of bedrock hills covered with till from glacial advances much older than those explored by most of the Ice Age Trail.

This segment traverses an area of bedrock hills covered with till from glacial advances much older than those explored by most of the Ice Age Trail. It also crosses the Robert

Cook Memorial Arboretum-Janesville Schools Outdoor Laboratory, where trailside signage provides educational information on trees and local flora. The arboretum is a 160-acre City of Janesville park (managed by the Janesville school district) open to the public from sunrise to sunset.

Trail access begins at the upper parking area for the Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum—Janesville Schools Outdoor Laboratory (JSOL). Head under the JoLyn Beeman Memorial Trail Archway and into the heart of the arboretum property. Turn off the paved path at the log cabin and enter the woods. The

![Map of the Janesville Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the Robert Cook Memorial Arboretum, Riverside Park, and the Rock River.](page_295_image_1_v2.jpg)

Arboretum’s Outdoor Educational Lab area and buildings are visible through the trees. Several short spur trails offer access to them. Next, descend a long bedrock ridge to the valley floor lined with intermittent tributaries of Marsh Creek. Upon reaching a junction with the Arboretum/JSOL main trail, turn north and follow it for about 300 feet before heading east and climbing a ridge with beautiful views of Marsh Creek below. After a gradual descent, make your way to the edge of the Arboretum/JSOL property. Enjoy a large viewing platform overlooking a prairie full of flowers during the summer, pass under a power line, pass another information kiosk, and exit the Arboretum/JSOL property.

Bend away from Marsh Creek, hiking within a 30-foot-wide wooded easement granted to the Alliance for foot traffic only. Volunteers have worked hard to remove invasive species and open the mixed hardwood forest to views of magnificent oaks. Parallel an active railway to the northeast, then meet Northridge Drive, an Arbor Ridge subdivision road. Turn left and follow Trail signage east along Northridge Drive to the segment’s endpoint at the Ice Age Trail parking area across North Washington Street (CTH-E).

**Devil’s Staircase Segment**

When the glacier was at the Johnstown Moraine, the present channel of the Rock River did not exist. Instead, a braided stream system covered much of the area, filling the deep valley with sand and gravel. As the ice began to retreat, drainage concentrated into a few channels that began to downcut the outwash surface. However, the erosion forming the Rock River channel slowed, and the channel widened as braided streams formed, carrying more sediment. Then, as the ice retreated from the Milton Moraine, the river

Rock County | 263

again became a single channel, flowing with greater erosive power, downcutting to its present level. These changes to the river channel created two levels within the riverbed called terraces. The City of Janesville’s Riverside Golf Course occupies the lower terrace.

This segment offers remarkable views of the Rock River along a path built in the late 1920s by the Public Works Department as the City of Janesville was developing Riverside Park. During the intervening years, however, it deteriorated to the point where it became impassible. So, with cooperation from the city, golf course, environmental groups, local businesses, and dozens of local and statewide volunteers, the Ice Age Trail Alliance rebuilt the Trail with an emphasis on keeping the area as natural as possible. As a result, you’ll enjoy outstanding views of the Rock River. Skillfully placed rock steps, blending into the landscape, take you up and down steep hills. A portion of the route incorporates and preserves stonework attributed to the original 1920s-era trailbuilders.

From the Trail access at the parking area on the east side of North Washington Street (CTH-E), head southeast a short distance parallel to the road, then emerge onto the west end of the Riverside Golf Course property. Walk past a couple of the golf course’s tee and green areas and cross railroad tracks. Be respectful of golfers when passing through this area: keep voices low and yield to those teeing off, hitting approach shots, or putting. Stay on the mulched pathway and off the grass.

As you leave the golf course, you’ll parallel a high-tension power line and enter a wooded area. Soon you’ll descend a steep switchback into the natural feature known as the Devil’s Staircase. This area features a huge gully (usually dry) descending from the golf course across the Trail to the river. Large rocks line the gully, appearing to have been purposely placed as steps, explaining the segment’s name.

Depart the gully and continue to the shore of the Rock River, where you’ll ascend into the wooded area between the golf course and the river. On the uphill (south) side of the Trail, you will soon encounter carbonate calcareous rock faces, some rising to 50 feet above the Trail. Numerous cliff-dwelling plants and woodland wildflowers highlight this portion of the segment.

At a hand-crafted stone bench, you’ll leave the wooded area by descending a steep set of hand-made steps and arrive at Riverside Park’s North Pavilion area. From here to the segment’s endpoint at the park’s South Pavilion, you’ll hug the Rock River’s shoreline, generally sticking to the thin strip of land between the river and Parkside Drive.

### AREA SERVICES

* [Icon: Trail access icon] **Janesville:** See Janesville Segment, p. 266. From the N. Washington St. (CTH-E) Trail access go south into the city.
* Evansville: See Evansville Segment, p. 258. From the N. Washington St. (CTH-E) Trail access go north 1.3 mi. At USH-14 go west 12.5 mi.

![A serene view of Marsh Creek in the Arbor Ridge Segment, showing a rocky stream bed and surrounding forest in autumn.](page_297_image_1_v2.jpg)

MEG TURVILLE-HEITZ

*Arbor Ridge Segment: A serene view of Marsh Creek.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::j.parent_text:
## Janesville Segment (Atlas Map 74)

**Summary:** The Janesville Segment is a 10.3-mile trail in Rock County (Atlas Map 74), running from Riverside Park South Pavilion to West Rotamer Ct.. This segment is entirely on outwash, one of the larger ones in Wisconsin, with sand and gravel over 300 feet thick in places.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **10.3 miles**: Riverside Park South Pavilion to West Rotamer Ct.

* [Icon: water icon] At the Riverside Park South Pavilion, JTS Downtown Transfer Center and the many other parks along the segment’s route.
* [Icon: restroom and picnic icon] [Icon: restroom and picnic icon] At the Riverside Park South Pavilion, downtown’s Town Square and many other parks along the segment’s route.
* [Icon: water drop icon] From the Rock River.
* [Icon: no hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not permitted in Janesville Parks May 15 to Sept. 15s.**
* [Icon: multiuse path icon] Portions overlap multiuse recreational paths, sidewalks and roads.
* [Icon: side trail icon] Side trails in city parks.
* [Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

![Map of the Janesville Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the route through the city of Janesville along the Rock River, with parking areas, parks, and street names labeled.](page_298_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. **[Icon: parking 1 icon] Riverside Park South Pavilion Trail Access (42.70637, -89.03616)**: From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171B and follow USH-14 west for 4.3 mi. At N. Washington St. (CTH-E) turn left and go south 2.5 mi. At Parkside Dr. turn left and go northeast 0.4 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.
2. **[Icon: parking 2 icon] W. Rotamer Ct. Trail Access (42.73297, -88.98951)**: From I-39/90 take Exit 171A and follow STH-26 (Milton Ave.) north for 0.3 mi. At Kettering St turn left and go west 0.1 mi. At Whitney St. turn right and go northeast 0.2 mi. At W. Rotamer Ct. turn right and go southeast 400 ft. **Parking**: Roadside.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Ashland Ave. at N. Washington St. A 0.2-mi spur trail leads to the IAT. (ii) N. Franklin St. (2-hour parking) just north of Mineral Point Ave. (iii) W. Milwaukee St. at the Rock River. (iv) S. River St. (v) Dawson Ball Field. (vi) Rotary Botanical Gardens. (vii) Palmer Park. (viii) Blackhawk Meadows Park. (ix) N. Wright Rd. roadside parking. (x) Amhurst Rd. roadside parking. A short spur trail from the cul-de-sac leads to the IAT. (xi) Deerfield Dr. between Home Depot and theaters. A short spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

This segment is entirely on outwash, one of the larger ones in Wisconsin, with sand and gravel over 300 feet thick in places. Much of the outwash probably came from the tunnel channel to the north.

The segment highlights the City of Janesville, an Ice Age Trail Community. The route caters to various interests, from history to nature to recreation to entertainment. It takes advantage of the City of Janesville’s extensive paved trail system linking parks to create an urban greenbelt. For portions of the Spring Brook Bike Trail, you may forget you are in a city as you travel through woods and restored prairies.

The revitalized Town Square area, along the river between East Milwaukee Street and West Court Street, draws visitors and locals alike to the waterfront. Notice large murals, like the “The Adventurer,” which is painted on the side of the Harris Ace Hardware building. The Janesville Public Art Trail includes more than 20 downtown murals. For more information, contact Janesville Area Convention and Visitors Bureau (see Area Services). Extend your hike with a stroll through the Rotary Botanical Gardens.

From the Trail access at Riverside Park’s South Pavilion, exit the park and follow Ice Age Way for two blocks. Then continue south along the Rock River on the Kiwanis Bike Trail.

![Map of the Janesville to Milton Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the route through the city of Janesville, including parks like Riverside, Palmer, and Woodcrest, and landmarks like Rotary Gardens and Kiwanis Pond.](page_299_image_1_v2.jpg)

Continue along a dirt trail built in the early 1990s by the Wisconsin Conservation Corps and pass the ruins of the Croak Brewery (1904–1920), one of Janesville’s many pre-prohibition breweries. Cross Centerway Street and enter the City of Janesville’s downtown area. Continue on South River Street or along the river on a mix of sidewalk and bike trails. The Town Square area along the river can become very icy in winter.

At the end of South River Street, head west for a short distance on Rockport Road sidewalks and then turn south on the City of Janesville’s Spring Brook Bike Trail. Cross the Rock River on a pedestrian bridge – the site of an old railroad bridge – where you can enjoy a view of the river away from traffic. During spring, pelicans and eagles frequent the area. Continue along Spring Brook Trail, which you’ll follow almost to the segment’s terminus.

Continue on the bike trail, passing south of Jeffris Park’s Dawson Ball Park. Note a historical marker commemorating the site of the winter – and eventually year-round – grounds of the Burr Robins Circus, once the world’s third largest circus. Follow Blackhawk Creek, an intermittent stream once a large glacial meltwater river.

Continuing on the bike trail, bend north to Palmer Drive, which you’ll parallel on the south side heading east. (For a side trip: walk west down Palmer Drive and explore the Rotary Botanical Gardens and Lions Beach. Details in the Points of Interest section).

Still on the bike trail, continue northeast along Palmer Drive, passing the Blackhawk Golf Course. You’ll also pass the historical marker of the Black Hawk War/Black Hawk’s Grove. It notes where men, women, and children of the Sauk, Fox, and Kickapoo Nations camped during the Black Hawk War.

Pass under I-39/90 and follow the bike trail to the left. Cross North Wright Road. (For a side trip to a restored prairie: Before crossing North Wright Road, you’ll meet a paved spur trail heading south near a kiosk. Take the spur trail east 0.6 miles.)

Continue on the bike trail. Bend north, eventually crossing under USH-14. From here, turn left and northwest, paralleling USH-14. Again cross North Wright Road and continue north. Skirt Briar Crest Park and briefly head west before continuing north again.

Cross East Rotamer Road at Tanglewood Drive. Continue north and west on Tanglewood Drive a short distance, then pick up a multiuse path and cross the STH-26 pedestrian bridge to the end of the segment at West Rotamer Court.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

Lincoln-Tallman House: From the intersection of the Trail and N. River St., go north (right), N. River St. becomes Ravine St., turn right onto N. Jackson St. (440 N. Jackson St., Janesville, 608-756-4509, rchs.us/sites/lincoln-tallman-house).

The Lincoln-Tallman House is Rock County’s most iconic historical structure. This six-floor mansion, constructed between 1855 and 1857, is an excellent example of Italianate-style architecture. From the basement to the cupola, each floor offers visitors a unique glimpse of daily life in 19th-century Rock County. Over 70 percent of the furniture is original, making it one of the most complete historic houses in the Midwest. In October of 1859, Abraham Lincoln stayed in the home after a series of speeches in Beloit, forever cementing the reputation of the Lincoln-Tallman House as “where Lincoln slept.”

**Rotary Botanical Gardens and Lions Beach:** Just west of the Trail’s western intersection with Palmer Dr. (1455 Palmer Dr., Janesville, 608-752-3885, rotarybotanicalgardens.org).

The 20-acre Rotary Botanical Gardens, dedicated to international peace and friendship, showcases 18 different thematic gardens, many with an international theme, along with award winning roses, unusual plant combinations, special collections of annuals and more. There is an admission fee. Just west of the Gardens is Lions Pond and Beach, with its large swimming area and changing rooms.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail City logo]
**Janesville:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, medical service. On Trail. Area info available from the Janesville Area Convention and Visitors Bureau (seasonal visitor center located on the Trail in Palmer Park; 800-487-2757, janesvillecvb.com), City of Janesville Parks Department (608-755-3025) and City of Janesville Recreation Department (608-755-3030, janesvillewi.gov). Access to services: (i) From the Trail at N. Washington St. (CTH-E) go east to Golf Course Rd. to access restaurants. (ii) From the Trail through the downtown area along the river find nearby restaurants, convenience store, lodging, library and medical service. (iii) From the Trail at Mt. Zion Ave. go west 0.3 mi for restaurants and convenience store. (iv) Near Briar Crest Park find access to restaurants, groceries, convenience store, general shopping, lodging and medical services in the vicinity of Deerfield Dr. and Humes Rd. (USH-14) and Milton Ave. (STH-26).

![A colorful mural along the route through town. Photo by Eric Haney.](page_301_image_1_v2.jpg)

Janesville Segment: A colorful mural along the route through town.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::j.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::j.parent_text:
## Janesville to Milton Segment (Atlas Map 75f)

**Summary:** The Janesville to Milton Segment is a 3.2-mile trail in Rock County (Atlas Map 75f), running from W. Rotamer Ct. to E. Manogue Rd. (W. High St.) at Vincent St.. This segment links two suburban areas utilizing a lightly traveled road and a pleasant, tree-lined converted railway path.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 1L icon] **3.2 miles (1.7 IAT, 1.5 CR):** W. Rotamer Ct. to E. Manogue Rd. (W. High St.) at Vincent St.

[Icon: No water icon] No reliable sources of water.

[Icon: Leashed dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Multiuse path icon] Segment shares a multiuse recreational path and includes a portion of road walk.

[Icon: Railroad path icon] The multiuse path on the abandoned railroad bed extends southwest from the segment into Janesville.

[Icon: Wheelchair accessible icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] **① W. Rotamer Ct. Trail Access (42.73297, -88.98951):** From I-39/90 take Exit 171A and follow STH-26 (Milton Ave.) north for 0.3 mi. At Kettering St. turn left and go west 0.1 mi. At Whitney St. turn right and go northeast 0.2 mi. At W. Rotamer Ct. turn right and go southeast 400 ft. **Parking:** Roadside.

[Icon: 2 icon] **② E. Manogue Rd. (W. High St.) at Vincent St. Trail Access (42.77366, -88.96957):** From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171A and follow STH-26 (Milton Ave.) north for 1.1 mi. At E. McCormick Dr. turn left and go west 0.3 mi. At CTH-Y turn right and go north 2.2 mi. At E. Vincent St. turn left and go west and north 0.4 mi to E. Manogue Rd. (W. High St.). **Parking:** Roadside on Vincent St.

**Additional Parking:** NW Rotamer Rd. just south of Townline Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment links two suburban areas utilizing a lightly traveled road and a pleasant, tree-lined converted railway path. Between E M H Townline Road and East Manogue Road, the route follows the edge of the tunnel channel that once carried

![Map of Janesville to Milton Segment showing trail route, access points 1 and 2, and surrounding roads like E. MH Townline Rd. and STH-26.](page_302_image_1_v2.jpg)

a large flow of glacial meltwater from beneath the ice of the Milton Moraine. This tunnel channel was the source of much of the sand and gravel beneath the enormous outwash plain surrounding Janesville today.

From the Trail access on West Rotamer Court, head northwest for a short distance. At West Rotamer Road (Whitney Street becomes West Rotamer), turn right and continue north on West Rotamer Road. After crossing railroad tracks, you’ll follow Northwest Rotamer Road, a narrow, paved country road with no shoulders.

Depart the road and merge onto an abandoned railroad bed converted for public use as a multi-use recreational path. Continue northeast on the railroad bed, which parallels Northwest Rotamer Road until EMH Townline Road.

Cross EMH Townline Road, and then continue on the railroad bed through a tree-lined corridor in an agrarian landscape to the segment’s endpoint at the intersection of Vincent Street and East Manogue Road (West High Street).

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: T/C icon] **Janesville:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, medical service. On Trail. Area info available from the Janesville Area Convention and Visitors Bureau (seasonal visitor center located on the Trail in Palmer Park; 800-487-2757, janesvillecvb.com), City of Janesville Parks Department (608-755-3025) and City of Janesville Recreation Department (608-755-3030, janesvillewi.gov). Access to services: (i). From the West Rotamer Court Trail access go west then south 1.0 mi to a large commercial area.

[Icon: T/C icon] **Milton:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on or near Janesville St. in Milton and south of STH-59 and at the old railroad depot area at Gayman St. and Vincent St. Area info available from the Milton Chamber of Commerce (608-868-6222, visitmilton.com). From E. Manogue Rd. Trail access go north and east into town.

> *Most towns along the Trail were a sure bet to find hot breakfast and coffee. We decided the oldest national chain must be “The Main Street Café.” We soon learned to ask the locals a question, which was an invitation to offer us a condensed history of their town, its claims to fame, and the places we simply must visit before leaving.*

> *TERRIE SANDERS, THOUSAND-MILER*

![A yellow blaze on a tree trunk marks a dirt path winding through a lush, green forest.](page_304_image_1_v2.jpg)

CAMERON GILLIE

*Storrs Lake Segment: A yellow blaze beckons hikers along a shaded path.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::m.parent_text:
## Milton Segment and Storrs Lake Segment (Atlas Map 75f, 76f)

**Summary:** The Milton Segment and Storrs Lake Segment: **Note**: *New Ice Age Trail is planned in Storrs Lake State Wildlife Area north of N. Bowers Lake Rd. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

### [Icon: Milton Segment marker] Milton Segment—4.3 miles: E. Manogue Rd. (W. High St.) at Vincent St. to E. Storrs Lake Rd.

* [Icon: Water icon] At Central Park, Veterans Park, South Goodrich Park and North Goodrich Park.
* [Icon: No hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: Restroom icon] At North Goodrich Park and South Goodrich Park.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: Camping icon] At private campgrounds [Icon: Private campground map symbol] north of Milton.
* [Icon: Road/sidewalk icon] Almost entirely on sidewalks and roads.
* [Icon: Wheelchair accessibility icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### [Icon: Storrs Lake Segment marker] Storrs Lake Segment—1.8 miles: E. Storrs Lake Rd. to N. Bowers Lake Rd.

**Note**: *New Ice Age Trail is planned in Storrs Lake State Wildlife Area north of N. Bowers Lake Rd. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

* [Icon: Water icon] From Storrs Lake and Bowers Lake.
* [Icon: Hunting closure icon] Portion of the segment crossing private land south of Bowers Lake Rd. is closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.
* [Icon: Social trails icon] Hunting/social trails in the State Wildlife Area.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. [Icon: Access point 1 icon] **E. Manogue Rd. (W. High St.) at Vincent St. Trail Access (42.77366, -88.96957)**: From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171A and follow STH-26 (Milton Ave.) north for 1.1 mi. At E. McCormick Dr. turn left and go west 0.3 mi. At CTH-Y turn right and go north 2.2 mi. At E. Vincent St. turn left and go west and north 0.4 mi to E. Manogue Rd. (W. High St.). **Parking**: Roadside on Vincent St.

2. [Icon: Access point 2 icon] **E. Storrs Lake Rd. Trail Access (42.77940, -88.91762)**: **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

3. [Icon: Access point 3 icon] **N. Bowers Lake Rd. Trail Access (42.79604, -88.91416)**: From I-39/90 at Janesville, take Exit 171A and follow STH-26 (Milton Ave.) north for 4.7 mi. Take Exit 8 and follow STH-59 west for 0.6 mi. At S. Janesville St. turn right and go north 1.3 mi. At N. Bowers Lake Rd. turn right and go east 1.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: Bowers Lake access on N. Bowers Lake Rd. A 0.4-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::m.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::m.parent_text:
## Milton Segment

**Summary:** The Milton Segment: This segment takes you through a charming Ice Age Trail Community packed with fascinating history, unique shops, and multiple wineries.

This segment takes you through a charming Ice Age Trail Community packed with fascinating history, unique shops, and multiple wineries. Local lore suggests the town is named for poet John Milton, author of *Paradise Lost*. A settler remarked the community was his “*Paradise Regained*” after leaving his previous home, which he thought of as a paradise lost.

![Map of the Milton Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the route through the city of Milton and connecting to the Janesville to Milton Segment and the Clover Valley Segment. Key landmarks include Milton House Museum, Milton College Historic District, and Storrs Lake State Wildlife Area.](page_306_image_1_v2.jpg)

Originally named Prairie du Lac, Milton was settled in 1838 by Joseph Goodrich. By November 1840, Goodrich had organized a Seventh Day Baptist Church and, in 1844, a school that would become Milton College. He also built the Milton House, an inn that during the Civil War became part of the Underground Railroad network.

From the Trail access at the corner of Vincent Street and East Manogue Road (West High Street), head north on a grassy path along the east side of East Vincent Street and enter a short section of tree-lined Trail. Turn right on Capman Street and then left on Elm Street.

This area, with an old railroad depot, used to be a separate community named Milton Junction until it merged with Milton in 1967. It is considered Milton’s second downtown area and has restaurants, bars, specialty shops, and parks. Follow yellow blazes on utility poles and street signs along Elm Street, Vernal Avenue, John Paul Road (CTH-Y), and Municipal Drive. Finally, pass Milton High School and the school’s outdoor laboratory, which exhibits a restored prairie project.

Turn south along Hilltop Drive and walk to East High Street, where the Trail passes the Milton Area Veterans Memorial. It’s noted for a soaring bronze eagle atop an obelisk, flanked by the “Cost of Freedom” and “POW/MIA” memorials with benches for rest and reflection.

Next, follow the yellow blazes on East High Street, Rogers Street, and College Street. Along the way, you’ll travel past Milton College Historic District – buildings built in the mid-1800s – which sits atop the terminal moraine of the Green Bay Lobe. Milton College closed in 1982. Its former campus is now home to a museum, office buildings, a public library, and a park. The Milton

College Preservation Society owns and operates the “Main Hall” as a museum of Milton College history.

Once on College Street, go east three blocks through the Milton historic area. Cross Parkview Drive and continue southeast through South Goodrich Park to East High Street and north onto Janesville Street. Pass the remarkable Milton House Museum (see Points of Interest) and turn right on East Storrs Lake Road.

The segment’s endpoint is at the northeast corner of the large parking area for the Storrs Lake State Wildlife Area.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::s.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::rock-county::s.parent_text:
## Storrs Lake Segment

**Summary:** The Storrs Lake Segment: The segment features the 950-acre Storrs Lake State Wildlife Area.

The segment features the 950-acre Storrs Lake State Wildlife Area. This property is a rich brocade of old oak trees, tall prairie grasses, dry kettles, wildflowers, and lakes teeming with northern pike, walleye, and sunfish. It is home to sandhill cranes, deer, wood ducks, mallards, turkeys, egrets, pheasants, and barred and great horned owls.

On July 1, 1832, young Abraham Lincoln camped beside Storrs Lake as one of 4,500 soldiers commanded by Brigadier General Henry Atkinson. They were in pursuit of Sauk Chief Black Hawk, who was fleeing north and west along the Rock River with 400 warriors and 1,200 women and children.

From the Trail access at the northeast corner of the Storrs Lake State Wildlife Area parking area, head north. Wind through a tranquil forest of old bur and white oaks, glacial kettles, and mixed hardwoods. Enjoy delightful views of Bowers Lake along an upland terrace before emerging from the woods at a signed intersection with a gravel service road.

Continue north, east, and then north again, meandering between wetland and forest as you traverse a portion of the Milton Moraine. Exit the woods, continue north past an old apple orchard and along the edge of a cornfield before reaching North Bowers Lake Road. Cross the road and head right, arriving at the segment’s endpoint at the parking area on North Bowers Lake Road.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Milton House Museum: On Trail.** (18 S. Janesville St., Milton, 608-868-7772, miltonhouse.org).
The Milton House is certified as an Underground Railroad site with the National Park Service, Network to Freedom program, and in 1998 was granted the designation of a National Historic Landmark due to its documented role on the Underground Railroad. Currently, the Milton House Museum is the only certified Underground Railroad site in Wisconsin that is open to the general public.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail City logo] **Milton:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on or near Janesville St. in Milton and south of STH-59 and at the old railroad depot area at Gayman St. and Vincent St. Area info available from the Milton Chamber of Commerce (608-868-6222, visitmilton.com).

[Icon: Trail City logo] **Janesville:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, library, medical service. On Trail. Area info available from the Janesville Area Convention and Visitors Bureau (seasonal visitor center located on the Trail in Palmer Park; 800-487-2757, janesvillecvb.com), City of Janesville Parks Department (608-755-3025) and City of Janesville Recreation Department (608-755-3030, janesvillewi.gov). Access to services: (i). From the E. Storrs Lake Rd. Trail access go south ~5 mi.

# Walworth & Jefferson Counties

Atlas Maps 77f–80f; Databook pages 76–77

Total miles: 25.2 (Trail 20.3; Connecting Route 4.9)

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Walworth and Jefferson counties](page_308_image_2_v2.jpg)

In 1846, famed early scientist and conservationist Increase Lapham wrote of Walworth County: “It is one of the richest and most important agricultural counties in the Territory; possessing a rich soil, with about the proper proportion of timber and prairie land to suit the convenience and fancy of the first settlers of a new country.”

The Kettle Moraine reaches its southern end near Whitewater Lake in Walworth County. Here, the Green Bay and Lake Michigan lobes parted to extend west and south. The Ice Age Trail in this section is mostly in the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU). It features many forests of white, black and bur oak, oak savannas, prairies, lakes, eskers and kettles. One highlight is the trip to the top of Bald Bluff, which showcases a thriving prairie and offers panoramic views to the west. Controlled burns by the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources have rejuvenated the original prairie landscape and have encouraged the return of native prairie flowers.

Whitewater, an Ice Age Trail Community, offers plenty of retail therapy in its historic downtown. Browse art, antiques, vintage clothing, and local crafts and gifts. Stay overnight in cozy B&Bs, hotels, or pitch a tent in a nearby campground. A range of restaurants and eateries, along with three breweries and a winery, will satisfy any craving. The city’s west side offers a window into cultural history, the Whitewater Effigy Mounds Preserve located within the city limits. The University of Wisconsin-Whitewater is the state’s first Ice Age Trail Campus.

A Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers is located on the Clover Valley Segment.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

Since 1993, the Walworth/Jefferson County Chapter members have led popular weekly walks and special-interest outings like Full Moon, Prairie Flower, and Fall Color hikes. Additional activities include an Adopt-a-Segment program, monthly Trail Improvement days, family events, and potlucks. The Chapter promotes the Trail to residents in Whitewater, Elkhorn, Lake Geneva, East Troy and Palmyra, raising awareness by participating in local festivals and parades.

The Chapter also offers a patch and certificate to those who hike all the segments within their “Kettle Trekkers” hiking award program. For more information, visit IceAgeTrail.org/walworth-jefferson-county-chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU)**: 262-594-6200, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/kms

**Jefferson County Tourism Council**: enjoyjeffersoncounty.com

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Whitewater**: 262-473-4005, discoverwhitewater.org

**Walworth County Visitors Bureau**: 800-395-8687, visitwalworthcounty.com

![A trail through a field of tall grass and wildflowers under a blue sky with wispy clouds. A brown wooden post with a yellow blaze and an Ice Age Trail marker stands in the foreground.](page_309_image_1_v2.jpg)
CHRIS TEMME

*Clover Valley Segment: A quintessential summer hike – blue sky and freshly mowed Trail!*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::walworth-jeffe.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::walworth-jeffe.parent_text:
## Clover Valley Segment (Atlas Map 77f)

**Summary:** The Clover Valley Segment is a 1.6-mile trail in Walworth & Jefferson Counties (Atlas Map 77f), running from N. County Line Rd. to Island Rd.. This segment features a short, quiet walk through the Clover Valley State Wildlife Area, a 1,034-acre property.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **1.6 miles**: N. County Line Rd. to Island Rd.

[Icon: water icon] From Spring Brook.

[Icon: camping icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA icon].

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the State Wildlife Area.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] ① **N. County Line Rd. Trail Access (42.78376, -88.78316)**: From Whitewater at the intersection of USH-12, STH-59, and STH-89, take STH-59 west for 2.6 mi. At N. County Line Rd. turn left and go south 1.5 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at Island Rd.

[Icon: 2] ② **Island Rd. Trail Access (42.79108, -88.76600)**: From Whitewater at the intersection of USH-12, STH-59, and STH-89, take STH-89 south 2.0 mi. At Island Rd. turn right and go west 0.9 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

### THE HIKE

This segment features a short, quiet walk through the Clover Valley State Wildlife Area, a 1,034-acre property. It is part of a vast wetland previously drained for agricultural use. It has been managed for hunting and trapping waterfowl, deer, pheasant, woodcock, and small game. Other recreational activities available are hiking, berry picking, fishing, and bird and wildlife viewing. Sandhill cranes frequent the area. Portions of this segment can be seasonally flooded or extremely wet after heavy rainfall.

From the Trail access on North County Line Road just west of the Rock/ Walworth county line, follow a tree line of silver maple, box elder, willow, and hackberry. Cross a boardwalk and then a bridge over a small stream as you enter Walworth County. Continue through mixed grasses, shrubs, and

![Map of Clover Valley Segment showing trail path, parking, and dispersed camping area within the Clover Valley State Wildlife Area.](page_310_image_1_v2.jpg)

small trees including nannyberry viburnum and hawthorn. After crossing another boardwalk you’ll pass through an area of native grasses, small oaks and box elder.

Enter a slightly elevated mature hardwood woodland and soon meet a short spur trail that leads to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Exit the woods, cross a short grassland, and enter a lower wet woodland consisting of large aspen and smaller invasive species that tolerate seasonal high-water levels. A large white oak on the southeastern edge of the woods marks the beginning of a 350-foot elevated curving boardwalk over exposed tree roots and slippery wet areas. Exit the woods and continue east through a field with summer-blooming bottle gentians. You may glimpse, on the right, one of the ditches dug to drain the wetland and former glacial lakebed for agriculture use.

Turn north, cross another drainage ditch, and pass through an area of shrub carr (a wetland community of red-osier dogwood, willow, silky dogwood, nannyberry, and other large shrub species adapted to saturated soils). When you reach Spring Brook, turn east and follow the stream for a short distance before crossing it on a wooden bridge.

From the bridge head north through shrub carr, meadow, and prairie to the segment’s terminus at the Island Road parking area.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Whitewater Effigy Mounds Preserve:** From the south edge of Whitewater at the intersection of USH-12, STH-59 and STH-89, take USH-12 west 1.6 mi. At Walworth Ave. turn right and go east 0.4 mi. At Indian Mound Parkway, turn left and go north 0.3 mi to the Preserve on the left side of the road. Roadside parking (288 S. Indian Mound Pkwy, Whitewater).

The Whitewater Effigy Mounds Preserve protects and preserves 13 effigy mounds. They are geometric in shape (conical or linear) or mimic animal shapes like a turtle, thunderbird, or mink. Most mounds in Wisconsin were built by Indigenous people during the Woodland Period, stretching from 500 BC (Early Woodland Period) up to 1200 AD (Late Woodland Period). The mounds are culturally significant sites and contain special or sacred objects or human remains. A level walking path of less than 1 mile — passing through an oak savanna, a stand of silver maples and bur oak, and a meadow wetland — provides access to the 13 mounds. The Preserve is listed on the National Register of Historical Places. Please respect the area; these mounds are burial sites.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail City icon]
**Whitewater:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the Island Rd. Trail access go east then north ~3 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::walworth-jeffe.parent_text:
## Whitewater Lake Segment (Atlas Map 78f)

**Summary:** The Whitewater Lake Segment: This segment features wooded moraines, kettles, and outstanding views of Rice and Whitewater lakes, with Whitewater Lake, the largest at 625 acres, boasting a beach.

### SNAPSHOT

4.6 miles: Clover Valley Rd. to USH-12

* [Icon: potable water] At the Kettle Moraine State Forest- Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Rice Lake Nature Trail, DNR Contact Station and Whitewater Lake Campground.
* [Icon: restrooms] At the KMSF-SU Rice Lake Nature Trail area, Whitewater Lake Campground and USH-12 Trail access parking area.
* [Icon: non-potable water] From Whitewater Creek.
* [Icon: dogs leashed] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: camping] At KMSF-SU Whitewater Lake Campground (on Trail) and at nearby private campgrounds<sup>[Icon: private campgrounds]</sup>.
* [Icon: bridle/snowmobile trail] A small portion of the Trail overlaps a bridle/snowmobile trail.
* [Icon: picnic area] [Icon: nature trail] At KMSF-SU Rice Lake Nature Trail area and at Whitewater Lake Campground.
* [Icon: spur trails] Connects with KMSF-SU Rice Lake Nature Trail and Whitewater Lake Recreation Area and DNR Contact Station spur trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **Clover Valley Rd. Trail Access (42.78034, -88.70351):** From Whitewater at the intersection of USH-12, STH-59, and STH-89, take STH-89 south 1.5 mi. At Anderson Rd. turn left and go east 0.8 mi. At Clover Valley Rd. continue east, south, then east, following Clover Valley Rd. for 2.6 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **USH-12 Trail Access (42.80401, -88.65244):** From LaGrange at the intersection of STH-12 and CTH-H, take USH-12 west 2.7 mi. From Whitewater at the intersection of USH-12 and STH-89, take USH-12 east 5.0 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

![Map of the Whitewater Lake Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail path from Clover Valley Road to USH-12, with points of interest such as Rice Lake, Whitewater Lake, campgrounds, and nature trails.](page_312_image_1_v2.jpg)

**Additional Parking:** (i) Rice Lake Nature Trail parking area on State Park Rd. To access the IAT, take the Rice Lake Nature Trail loop northwest for 0.4 mi. Continue on the 0.1-mi blue-blazed spur trail across Kettle Moraine Dr. to the IAT. (ii) KMSF-SU Whitewater Lake DNR Contact Station on Kettle Moraine Dr. A 0.2-mi spur trail leads to the IAT. (iii) Esterly Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment features wooded moraines, kettles, and outstanding views of Rice and Whitewater lakes, with Whitewater Lake, the largest at 625 acres, boasting a beach. Both offer lake access at public boat landings; fish include panfish, largemouth bass, and northern pike. The segment also winds through Bluff Creek State Natural Area. This property hosts a series of springs and seepage slopes (wetlands located on the sides of rolling hills) originating at the base of a morainal ridge. In addition, the property protects high-quality mound fens (raised areas where peat has accumulated), sedge meadows, a dry-mesic forest, and stretches of cold, fast-moving, Bluff Creek.

From the Trail access on Clover Valley Road, pass through a shady pine grove before crossing Whitewater Creek, the outlet stream from Rice Lake, on a series of puncheons and a 30-foot bridge. These structures protect the sensitive wetland bordering Whitewater Creek and offer tranquil views of the surrounding wetland and valley. Many spring wildflowers, such as blue-flag iris, marsh marigold, and skunk cabbage, line the creek valley.

After crossing the creek, skirt the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Whitewater Lake Campground’s walk-in campsites. Reach a junction on the right with a blue-blazed spur trail. It heads south across Kettle Moraine Drive to a self-guided 0.6-mile nature trail loop along the shore of Rice Lake. A large parking area is located off the loop trail.

From the junction with the spur trail, continue east, crossing Hi-Lo Road before reaching another spur trail on the right labeled “Office.” It heads south-east 0.2 miles to the KMSF-SU Whitewater Lake DNR Contact Station parking area. After the path to the office, you’ll encounter another side trail; this one, on the left, leads to the entrance of the KMSF-SU Whitewater Lake Campground.

Continue east through rugged, rolling terrain past many trailside kettles. After passing behind several KMSF-SU Whitewater Lake Campground drive-in campsites, climb a steep rise to a bench and lookout offering views of Rice and Whitewater lakes. Cross CTH-P and climb steeply to a secluded pine grove before crossing Esterly Road. Briefly join a bridle/snowmobile trail before branching off and crossing a cleared area beneath a power line. The headwaters of Bluff Creek lie below to the west. Just beyond the power line, pass through Bluff Creek State Natural Area, where you’ll travel across cavernous kettles with ancient oaks providing the canopy. This area is beautiful after a wet-heavy snowfall. Shortly before the segment’s end at USH-12, a clearing offers views to the west of the glacial outwash plain and the city of Whitewater.

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Whitewater Lake Campground (seasonal):** Camping May through October. On Trail. (262-473-7501, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/kms/recreation/camping; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

**LaGrange**: Restaurant. From the USH-12 Trail access parking area go 3.0 mi east on STH-12 to CTH-H (Kettle Moraine Scenic Dr.) intersection.

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Whitewater**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the USH-12 Trail access parking area, go 5.0 mi west on USH-12 and follow Business USH-12. Area info available from the Whitewater Chamber of Commerce (262-473-4005, whitewaterchamber.com).

![A group of hikers with large backpacks walking along a narrow, wooded trail under a large tree branch](page_314_image_1_v2.jpg)

MARISA LENGERICH

Blackhawk Segment: Hikers with Outward Bound gain backpacking experience on the Trail.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::walworth-jeffe.parent_text:
## Blackhawk Segment (Atlas Map 79f)

**Summary:** The Blackhawk Segment is a 7.0-mile trail in Walworth & Jefferson Counties (Atlas Map 79f), running from USH-12 to Young Rd.. This segment’s name honors Black Hawk, the Sauk warrior who led 400 warriors and 1,200 women and children across the Mississippi River.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Distance icon] [Icon: Segment icon] **7.0 miles**: USH-12 to Young Rd.

* [Icon: Water icon] At nearby Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Nordic Trails parking area south of CTH-H Trail access.
* [Icon: Water icon] From Lake La Grange and a pond along Duffin Rd. near KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 3.
* [Icon: Camping icon] At KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 3 (reservations required).
* [Icon: Restroom icon] At the USH-12 Trail access parking area and KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 3 (please respect those who have reserved the shelter) and parking areas for nearby KMSF-SU John Muir and Nordic trails.
* [Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: Leashed dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: Snowmobile icon] A small portion overlaps a snowmobile trail.
* [Icon: Hiking icon] White-blazed loop trail and several spur trails, including one to KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 3.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Parking icon 1] **USH-12 Trail Access (42.80401, -88.65244)**: From LaGrange at the intersection of USH-12 and CTH-H, take USH-12 west 2.7 mi. From Whitewater at the intersection of USH-12 and STH-89, take USH-12 east 5.0 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: Parking icon 2] **Young Rd. Trail Access (42.84261, -88.60656)**: From Palmyra area at the intersection of STH-59 and CTH-H, take CTH-H southwest 3.0 mi. At Young Rd. turn left and go east 0.1 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Duffin Rd. roadside parking. (ii) Oleson Cabin Historic site on Duffin Rd., roadside parking.

### THE HIKE

This segment’s name honors Black Hawk, the Sauk warrior who led 400 warriors and 1,200 women and children across the Mississippi River. They intended to reclaim at least some of their ancestral lands after a hard winter and the government’s failure to provide adequate corn, as per agreed-upon terms.

However, Black Hawk’s hopes of living along the Rock River soon vanished. First, the British aid he’d expected did not materialize. His other allies, the Ho-Chunk and Potawatomi, fearing U.S. military reprisal, also refused support.

Quickly outmatched by the U.S. military forces, several attempts to surrender were rebuffed or misinterpreted by the American troops as they tried to capture Black Hawk’s band of people. For 16 weeks, Black Hawk and his warriors created tactical diversions while the noncombatants tried to make their way back across the Mississippi. As a result, the band ran out of food and water several times; many died of hunger, thirst, or exhaustion.

When militiamen discovered the band’s trail, they also encountered dozens of starving Sauk and Fox, mostly older people and children. The troops quickly killed them. Then, in the final battle, or massacre, at Wisconsin’s Bad Axe River, the remaining members of the band, who were trying to make their way back across the Mississippi, were slaughtered.

By the time Black Hawk surrendered to authorities, only about 150 of his initial travelers had survived. On the other side, some 70 soldiers and settlers died in the conflict.

As a result of the Black Hawk War, the Sauk and Fox were stripped of valuable and extensive landholdings. In addition, the government forced an equally severe treaty on the neighboring Ho-Chunk.

From the USH-12 Trail access information kiosk and parking area (a popular meeting point for events sponsored by the Alliance’s Walworth/Jefferson County Chapter), climb a hill with long views of deciduous forests and a nice view toward Whitewater. Descend on a serpentine course and then meander north and east around Lake La Grange past marshes bordering the lake and through woods and open tall-grass prairies. Several benches offer spots to enjoy scenic views of Lake La Grange.

Past Lake La Grange, you’ll meet a white-blazed path heading south and southwest along the eastern side of Lake La Grange toward the USH-12 Trail access.

![Map of the Blue Spring Lake and Whitewater Lake segments of the Ice Age Trail, showing landmarks like Young Prairie State Natural Area, Bald Bluff, Ole Oleson Historic Log Cabin, and Lake La Grange.](page_316_image_1_v2.jpg)

access. From this junction, continue south, east, then north, passing through a hardwood forest before turning south and descending 200 feet to Duffin Road.

Cross Duffin Road, and you’ll meet a spur trail leading 0.1 miles east to Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Backpack Shelter 3, atop a steep hill surrounded by dense forest.

A bit farther north from the shelter spur is another spur trail heading east 0.6 miles to a pioneer lime kiln with a descriptive plaque. Then, in another 150 feet, you’ll meet a third spur trail. It runs west 0.2 miles to the historic Ole Oleson Homestead, where an early Norwegian pioneer built a two-story tamarack log cabin. Nearby, an old sleigh similar to the one used by Ole Oleson to transport the logs for the house is on display.

From the Ole Oleson spur trail, head north through dense forest, following the edges of large kettle depressions, among a diverse and undisturbed quantity of flora and fauna. Pass a small lake and then enter a dense pine grove.

After crossing Bluff Road, traverse a section of red, black, and jack pine before crossing CTH-H. Climb steeply through a savanna-like open forest of hardwoods pockmarked by kettle depressions and scattered boulders. At the top is a beautiful view overlooking woods and the surrounding terrain. Head downhill to the segment’s terminus on Young Road.

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Backpack Shelter 3**: Camping. On Trail. Reservations required: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com. Only one group (up to 10) per shelter per night is allowed.

**LaGrange:** Restaurant. From the USH-12 Trail access go east 2.7 mi.

[Icon: Trail City logo] **Whitewater:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the USH-12 Trail access go west ~5 mi.

**Palmyra:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library. From the Young Rd. Trail access go west then north ~3 mi.

![Blackhawk Segment: Puffy clouds float above Lake La Grange.](page_317_image_1_v2.jpg)

Blackhawk Segment: Puffy clouds float above Lake La Grange.

![A large, split boulder resembling an elephant's head in a wooded area.](page_318_image_1_v2.jpg)

PHOTOHTPHER UNKNOWN

*Blue Spring Lake Segment: The stone elephant.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::walworth-jeffe.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::walworth-jeffe.parent_text:
## Blue Spring Lake Segment (Atlas Map 79f, 80)

**Summary:** The Blue Spring Lake Segment is a 7.1-mile trail in Walworth & Jefferson Counties (Atlas Map 79f, 80), running from Young Rd. to CTH-Z. This hilly segment features panoramic views from Bald Bluff and highlights the Stone Elephant, a massive granite erratic.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **7.1 miles**: Young Rd. to CTH-Z

[Icon: amenities icons] At Kettle Moraine State Forest- Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Horseriders’ Camp on Little Prairie Rd. and KMSF-SU Emma Carlin Trails parking area.

[Icon: water icon] From small ponds and springs near the segment.

[Icon: ColdCache icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: trail icon] Bald Bluff trails, Stone Elephant spur trail and KMSF-SU Emma Carlin bike/ hike trail system.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking 1 icon] ① **Young Rd. Trail Access (42.84261, -88.60656)**: From Palmyra area at the intersection of STH-59 and CTH-H, take CTH-H southwest 3.0 mi. At Young Rd. turn left and go east 0.1 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

[Icon: parking 2 icon] ② **CTH-Z Trail Access (42.87197, -88.54341)**: From Eagle at the intersection of STH-59 and STH-67, take STH-59 west 3.7 mi. At CTH-Z turn left and go south 0.6 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at Emma Carlin Trails parking area 0.1 mi south on CTH-Z. A short blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Bald Bluff Scenic Overlook on CTH-H. A spur trail to Bald Bluff leads to the IAT. (ii) Tamarack Rd. roadside parking. (iii) Horseriders’ Camp on Little Prairie Rd.

![Map of the Blue Spring Lake Segment showing the trail route from Young Rd. to CTH-Z, including landmarks like Bald Bluff, Blue Spring Lake, Stone Elephant, and Emma Carlin Trails.](page_319_image_1_v2.jpg)

### THE HIKE

This hilly segment features panoramic views from Bald Bluff and highlights the Stone Elephant, a massive granite erratic. Bald Bluff, one of the highest points in Jefferson County, rises 1,050 feet above sea level and 200 feet above the surrounding area. Indigenous peoples used the bluff as a lookout and for ceremonial dancing.

Bald Bluff’s gravel knob and steep south- and west-facing ridges support the area’s largest and most diverse dry native prairie opening. Look for prairie flowers and grasses such as little bluestem, sideoats grama, prairie dropseed, silky aster, pasqueflower, grooved yellow flax, and rough blazing star. A brochure for a self-guided nature trail describing the history and prairie plants of the area is available at the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) headquarters or at the Bald Bluff trailhead on CTH-H.

The section of Trail from Bald Bluff to the KMSF-SU Horseriders’ Camp has outstanding views of glacial moraines, eskers, kettles, and erratics, including spectacular long views of the surrounding topography during leaf-off seasons. In addition, a short spur trail leads to the Stone Elephant, so named by early settlers because of its color, size, and shape. In the 1920s, it was a popular day outing for tourists, who traveled to the site by horse-drawn surrey.

You will traverse the Kettle Moraine Oak Opening State Natural Area between Young Road and Tamarack Road. This area is a mixture of oak openings and oak woodland dominated by open-grown bur and black oaks. The Trail also passes a few native dry prairie remnants. The first is located 0.2 miles past the Stone Elephant spur intersection in a field on the north side of the Trail. The showy rough blazing star, a dry prairie species, is found here; the best time to see it is late August. About 0.4 miles east, on a south-facing slope, is the second remnant (or about 0.5 miles southwest of Tamarack Road). Found here are flora such as silky aster, leadplant, goldenrod, purple prairie clover, flowering spurge, sunflowers, arrow-leaf aster, and several kinds of prairie grasses.

From the Trail access on Young Road, head north and soon meet a spur trail leading west to the CTH-H Bald Bluff parking area. From this junction, climb Bald Bluff (described above) on a series of switchbacks. After descending Bald Bluff, you’ll come to a small clearing on the left with an impressive stand of white pines. Then meet a short spur trail leading north downhill to the erratic known as the Stone Elephant.

Three trails (horse, hiking, and biking) converge/diverge near the Tamarack Road crossing, so pay close attention to signage. Continue through the woods, climb, and follow a steep ridge offering an impressive panorama. Next, cross Little Prairie Road and again ascend to another high point. Here, a short side trail leads to a clearing with a bench and views to the northwest. From the junction with the side trail, continue toward the KMSF-SU Horseriders’ Camp, which is for equestrian camping only. However, showers are available for hikers who have reservations at one of the three KMSF-SU backpack shelters.

After crossing the Horseriders’ Camp, you’ll pass a small trailside pond and continue northeast on level terrain past a few more small ponds and springs. Then, the terrain changes, and you’ll travel up and down hills again. In early spring, look for fields of mayapples. You may also notice a rock seat embedded in a tree and an old foundation nearly obscured by daylilies. Just south of the segment’s endpoint on CTH-Z, a short spur trail leads south to the KMSF-SU

Emma Carlin Trails parking area. *Note: As you approach the end of the segment, you may hear gunfire. There’s a public shooting range nearby.*

### AREA SERVICES

**Palmyra:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library. From the Little Prairie Rd. Trail access go 1.0 mi northwest on Little Prairie Rd.

**Eagle:** *See Eagle Segment, p. 296.* From the CTH-Z Trail access go north then east ~4 mi.

![A close-up photograph of a purple shooting star wildflower in a wooded area.](page_321_image_1_v2.jpg)
JANET WOOD

*Blue Spring Lake Segment: A lucky sighting of a shooting star, an ephemeral spring wildflower.*

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Waukesha County, Wisconsin, showing segments including Monches, Merton, Hartland, Delafield, and Lapham Peak. The map includes local roads, parks, and a locator map of Wisconsin.](page_322_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
# Waukesha County

![Map of Waukesha County showing Ice Age Trail segments: Waterville Segment, Scuppernong Segment, Eagle Segment, and Stony Ridge Segment. The map also shows the Kettle Moraine State Forest - Southern Unit, Glacial Drumlin State Trail, and various roads and towns including Wales, Waukesha, Eagle, and Palmyra. A legend indicates trail types, county boundaries, and public land.](page_323_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Waukesha County

*Atlas Maps 80f–83f; Databook pages 79–83*

*Total miles: 45.5 (Trail 40.1; Connecting Route 5.4)*

![Map of Waukesha County showing the Ice Age Trail route](page_324_image_1_v2.jpg)

The Ice Age Trail is within the Kettle Moraine for most of Waukesha County. Still, it occasionally veers to the west onto an outwash plain where small areas of the Niagara Escarpment protrude from the soil. The Ice Age Trail crosses major glacial meltwater spillways near the villages of Wales and Hartland. Many glacial landforms are visible along the Trail, such as kettles, eskers, drained lake plains, drumlins, and kames, and erratics are abundant. Vegetation includes oak forests, oak openings, and wet and dry prairie. Seasonal wildflowers punctuate all these areas. The Trail highlights area city and county parks, multiuse recreation trails, and the Kettle Moraine State Forest’s Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) and Lapham Peak Unit (KMSF-LPU).

The KMSF-SU contains 20,000 undeveloped acres of forest. It has numerous hiking, nature, mountain biking, equestrian, snowmobile, and cross-country ski trails. Also, within the unit are recreational sites for camping, picnicking, boating, and swimming. The KMSF-SU headquarters in the Stony Ridge Segment features natural and historical exhibits about the surrounding area.

The Ice Age Trail winds through two Trail Communities, Delafield and Hartland. The city of Delafield is nestled on the southwest edge of Lake Nagawicka. Named for Charles Delafield, owner of the area’s first gristmill, the settlement served as a resting place for travelers on the Blue Mound plank road between Milwaukee and Madison. Delafield’s historic and bustling downtown continues to cater to visitors with its small-town charm. It offers inspired drink and dining options, specialty shops, and local boutiques.

The village of Hartland’s local lore holds that in 1838, Stephen Warren, the first white settler, walked from the city of Ann Arbor in Michigan to the Hartland area in search of desirable farmland. Soon, native-born migrants and Swedish, Danish, and German immigrants settled in the area, establishing farms and growing wheat. Summer homes and resorts on nearby lakes followed in the 1890s. Hartland continues to be a great place to live and vacation. Enjoy high-quality post-hike meals and browse one-of-a-kind shops.

The Waterville Segment offers two Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Waukesha/Milwaukee County Chapter, established in 1984, enjoys a robust and active chapter membership. The Chapter regularly hosts Trail improvement days and guided hikes. The Monday Mudders meet weekly to tackle projects of all sizes along local segments. And, the Blazing Babes keep the yellow blazes looking fresh and bright, locally and trailwide.

In addition, the Chapter’s “Walk the Wauk” program rewards hikers who have walked all Waukesha County Ice Age Trail segments. Start your Walk the Wauk adventure by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Learn more about the Chapter by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/waukesha-milwaukee-county-chapter

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Waukesha Area Convention and Visitor Bureau**: 262-542-0330, visitwaukesha.org

**Waukesha County Parks and Land Use Department**: 262-548-7801, waukeshacounty.gov

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU)**: 262-594-6200, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/kms

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Lapham Peak Unit (KMSF-LPU)**: 262-646-3025, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/laphampeak

**Ice Age Trail Community, Hartland**: downtownhartland.com, or hartland.wi-org

**Ice Age Trail Community, Delafield**: visitdelafield.org

![Stony Ridge Segment: A lush summer day out on the Trail.](page_325_image_1_v2.jpg)

RACHEL VONDERHEIDE

Stony Ridge Segment: A lush summer day out on the Trail.

## Stony Ridge Segment (Atlas Map 80f)

**Summary:** The Stony Ridge Segment is a 3.1-mile trail in Waukesha County (Atlas Map 80f), running from CTH-Z to STH-59. The segment traverses the bed of glacial Lake Scuppernong and several southwest-northwest trending eskers before heading out across pitted outwash.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **3.1 miles**: CTH-Z to STH-59

[Icon: water and restroom icon] At KMSF-SU Emma Carlin Trails parking area and at KMSF-SU HQ and Visitor Center. Restrooms also available at KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 2 (please respect those who have reserved the shelter).

[Icon: hunting icon] Portion of segment crossing private land between CTH-S and STH-59 is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: camping icon] At KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 2 (reservations required).

[Icon: road icon] A short portion overlaps roads.

[Icon: trail intersection icon] KMSF-SU Stony Ridge Nature Trail, spur trail to KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 2 and other spur and horse trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking 1 icon] **CTH-Z Trail Access (42.87197, -88.54341):** From Eagle at the intersection of STH-59 and STH-67, take STH-59 west 3.7 mi. At CTH-Z turn left and go south 0.6 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Emma Carlin Trails parking area 0.1 mi south on CTH-Z. A short blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

[Icon: parking 2 icon] **STH-59 Trail Access (42.87912, -88.50286):** From Eagle at the intersection of STH-59 and STH-67, take STH-59 west 1.6 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.3 mi north on the gravel road coincident with the IAT.

**Additional Parking:** KMSF-SU Headquarters on STH-59. A spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

The segment traverses the bed of glacial Lake Scuppernong and several southwest-northwest trending eskers before heading out across pitted outwash. It winds through a pine grove, an oak forest, and sections of dry prairie, highlighting the Kettle Moraine State Forest – Southern Unit’s (KMSF-SU) varied terrain.

It also offers two side trips. The first begins at the segment’s start by following CTH-Z south 0.2 miles to Stute Springs and Homestead Trail. This self-guided 1.3-mile loop features early 20th-century farm buildings, natural springs, and ancient woods. Then, add a mile to climb up Big Hill – 1,050 feet.

The second occurs halfway through the segment at a spur trail leading to the KMSF-SU Headquarters. If you take this path, note the steep-sided ridge south and southeast of the visitor center is an esker. Just east of the water-filled kettle is another esker. The building’s natural history museum has displays and videos on glacial geology, Indigenous people and pioneer history, and animal and plant life throughout the Southern Unit and surrounding areas. In addition, self-guided brochures are available in the gift shop for the many nature trails near the Ice Age Trail. One of these, the Stony Ridge Nature Trail, starts at the headquarters; a part of it runs along the crest of an esker.

From the Trail access on CTH-Z, head east through a relatively flat stretch of woods and meadow and emerge into an open prairie on the bed of glacial Lake Scuppernong. Cross a bridge over a small stream filling in with plant growth, climb gradually across a grassy savanna, and ascend a wooded esker on rocky outwash. A spur trail leads north to the KMSF-SU Headquarters.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail through the Kettle Moraine State Forest - Southern Unit, showing the trail route from the Blue Spring Lake Segment towards the Eagle Segment, including points like KMSF-SU Shelter 2, Visitor Center & Forest Headquarters, and the Stony Ridge Nature Trail.](page_327_image_1_v2.jpg)

Near the spur trail to the headquarters is another spur – it heads 0.2 miles south to KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 2. Continuing east from this junction, climb between a long pond and a deep water-filled kettle, descend into an open field, and follow a sandy track through a fire-scarred forest. Then climb atop a long, steep-sided esker with kettles and marshes at the bottoms of the slopes. After descending the esker, cross railroad tracks and travel through a pine grove en route to CTH-S. The segment ends by crossing two parcels of private land. Please stay on the Trail as you head north along CTH-S and east, paralleling Antique Lane before you reach STH-59.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Old World Wisconsin**: From the STH-59 Trail access, go east on STH-59 for 1.6 mi. At STH-67 turn right and go south 1.4 mi (W372 S9727 WI-67, Eagle, 262-594-6301, oldworldwisconsin.wisconsinhistory.org).

Old World Wisconsin re-creates daily historical scenes of farm and village life from a century ago. Farmsteads and settlements representing German, Finnish, Polish, Norwegian, African American, Danish and Yankee pioneers dot nearly 600 acres. There is an admission fee.

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Backpack Shelter 2**: Camping. On Trail. Reservations required: (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Only one group (up to 10) per shelter per night is allowed. No potable water is available at the shelter. Hikers should obtain water from the Forest HQ, 0.2 mi north of the shelter.

**KMSF-SU Headquarters**: Information. From the STH-59 Trail access, go west on STH-59 1.1 mi (262-594-6200, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/kms).

**Eagle:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. From the KMSF-SU Headquarters on STH-59 go east ~3 mi.

**Palmyra:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library. From the KMSF-SU Headquarters on STH-59 go west ~4 mi.
