iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
## Eagle Segment (Atlas Map 80f)

**Summary:** The Eagle Segment: The first half of this segment traverses the Scuppernong River Habitat Area, one of the most extensive native wet prairies east of the Mississippi.

### SNAPSHOT

5.6 miles: STH-59 to STH-67 Wayside

* [Icon: water icon] From small ponds and springs.
* [Icon: globe icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.
* [Icon: dog on leash icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: road overlap icon] A short portion overlaps a road.
* [Icon: trail intersection icon] Brady’s Rocks white-blazed loop trail, a blue-blazed spur trail to STH-67 parking area and two other short spur trails. Paradise Springs Nature Trail is nearby.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **STH-59 Trail Access (42.87912, -88.50286)**: From Eagle at the intersection of STH-59 and STH-67, take STH-59 west 1.6 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.3 mi north on the gravel road coincident with the IAT.

② **STH-67 Wayside Trail Access (42.91894, -88.47337)**: From I-94 at Oconomowoc, take Exit 282 and follow STH-67 south for 11.2 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) CTH-N. (ii) Wilton Rd. roadside parking. (iii) STH-67 northeast of Brady’s Rocks. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

The first half of this segment traverses the Scuppernong River Habitat Area, one of the most extensive native wet prairies east of the Mississippi. The 173-acre Scuppernong Prairie State Natural Area is part of the habitat area. One of the oldest of Wisconsin’s State Natural Areas, the prairie grows in the bed of the former glacial Lake Scuppernong.

The second half of the segment skirts the edges of the 250-acre Kettle Moraine Low Prairie State Natural Area. The low prairie has been the site of controlled burns and tree removal since 1999. As a result, the entire area has returned to its original condition, and long-dormant plant species specific to the low prairie have dramatically reappeared. In spring, it’s often a wet area, and you may observe ground-nesting birds among the many blooming flowers.

The segment offers a side trip to Brady’s Rocks when taking a white-blazed loop trail weaving between 8-foot-high outcroppings. Named for Irish immigrants Michael and Kathleen Brady, who settled here in 1855, these outcroppings are part of the Niagara Escarpment. A thick layer of dolomite that extends through Door County runs along the Upper Peninsula of Michigan and the Bruce Peninsula of Ontario and undergirds Niagara Falls. This cool, shaded area has a unique fern population, including the walking fern, the fragile fern, and the cliff brake fern, which grows out of cracks in the dolomite bedrock.

From the Trail Access on STH-59, head a short distance up a gravel drive and then travel generally northeast across a prairie toward CTH-N. Cross a small stream fed by Paradise Springs before reaching a bench and a nearby Scuppernong River Habitat Area sign.

North of the CTH-N crossing, you’ll skirt the Scuppernong Prairie State Natural Area. At Wilton Road, turn right and head east for a short way until the segment leaves the road and continues due north.

Head generally northeast until the Trail bends south and meets a white-blazed loop trail on the right leading to Brady’s Rocks (described above).

Past the Brady’s Rocks spur, pass remnants of a rubblestone fence from the Brady farm, and climb a grassy knoll. Just before the top of the knoll is a blue-blazed spur trail on the right leading to the STH-67 parking area. At the top, a Leopold bench offers lovely views to the north of the prairie and drained glacial Lake Scuppernong. A spur trail shortly beyond the bench leads to a scenic overlook.

![Map of the Eagle Segment area showing Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit, Brady's Rocks, and connections to Scuppernong and Stony Ridge segments.](page_329_image_1_v2.jpg)

After crossing a gravel road, you’ll meet another short spur trail leading east to some springs. Cross an intermittent spring-fed creek in the shadows of a cluster of inspiring old oaks. You’ll also traverse three puncheons and a small bridge built by MSC volunteers, totaling close to 400 feet in length. Continue to the segment’s end at a wayside on STH-67.

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Headquarters**: See Stony Ridge Segment, p. 294. From the STH-59 Trail access go west less than 1 mi.

**Eagle**: Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. From the STH-59 Trail access go 1.6 mi east on STH-59.

**Palmyra:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library. From the STH-59 Trail access go west 4.5 mi.

**North Prairie**: See Scuppernong Segment, p. 298. From the STH-59 Trail access go northeast ~7 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
## Scuppernong Segment (Atlas Maps 80f, 81f)

**Summary:** The Scuppernong Segment is a 5.6-mile trail in Waukesha County (Atlas Map 80f, 81f), running from STH-67 Wayside to CTH-C. The segment travels through the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU), featuring hilly hardwood forests, piney woods, and many trailside kettles.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 4 2 icon] **5.6 miles:** STH-67 Wayside to CTH-C

*Note: New Ice Age Trail is planned southeast of CTH-C. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

[Icon: water tap icon] At CTH-ZZ Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Scuppernong Trails trailhead, Pinewoods Campground and Ottawa Lake Campground.

[Icon: water drop icon] From McKeawn Springs.

[Icon: backpacker icon] At KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 1 (reservations required).

[Icon: tent icon] [Icon: shower icon] At KMSF-SU Pinewoods Campground and Ottawa Lake Campground [Icon: red triangle icon].

[Icon: picnic table icon] At KMSF-SU Scuppernong Trails trailhead, Pinewoods Campground and Ottawa Lake Campground.

[Icon: shelter icon] At KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 1 (please respect those who have reserved the shelter), CTH-ZZ Scuppernong Trails trailhead, Pinewoods Campground and Ottawa Lake Campground.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: road icon] A small portion of the segment overlaps a campground road.

[Icon: crossing arrows icon] Spur trail to McKeawn Springs, spur trail to KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 1, two blue-blazed spur trails to parking areas, a horse trail and the Scuppernong ski/hike trail network.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] **STH-67 Wayside Trail Access (42.91894, -88.47337):** From I-94 at Oconomowoc, take Exit 282 and follow STH-67 south for 11.2 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2 icon] **CTH-C Trail Access (42.96667, -88.46120):** From I-94 at Oconomowoc, take Exit 282 and follow STH-67 south for 8.5 mi. At CTH-C turn left and go east 1.3 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Piper Rd. roadside parking. (ii) Scuppernong Hiking and Ski Trail parking areas on CTH-ZZ. Blue-blazed spur trails lead to the IAT. (iii) Pinewoods Campground on Waterville Rd. Park near the restrooms in the group camp loop.

### THE HIKE

The segment travels through the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU), featuring hilly hardwood forests, piney woods, and many trailside kettles. Several benches provide an opportunity to sit, relax and enjoy the forest. As the segment begins, take note: Glacial Lake Scuppernong once inundated the low area west of STH-67. Soon, the segment ascends high-relief hummocky topography, travels along the Kettle Moraine, and climbs onto a high pitted outwash surface. The steep slopes, called ice-contact slopes, formed as braided streams deposited gravel on thin ice. When the ice melted, these deposits were higher than the surrounding landscape.

From the Trail access at the STH-67 wayside, head northeast. Parallel the west side of STH-67 before crossing to the east side of the road. Soon after the crossing, meet a spur trail on the right leading east up to KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 1, perched atop Spy Glass Hill. The vista from the shelter area is of a flat plain left behind after water drained from Glacial Lake Scuppernong. Just past the spur to the shelter, a second spur trail leads left to McKeawn Springs, which flows into a trout pond and Scuppernong Creek.

Cross Piper Road and travel through hilly terrain. Just south of CTH-ZZ, a blue-blazed spur trail heads left to the KMSF-SU Scuppernong Trail network southside parking area. Shortly after the CTH-ZZ crossing, another blue-blazed spur trail to the left leads to the Scuppernong Trail network’s northside parking area and trailhead, with restrooms and water. From this second spur, head east, parallel to CTH-ZZ, and then north, skirting the Mackie Group Picnic Area.

North of CTH-ZZ, reach the segment’s highest point (1,075 feet) at a location marked with a bench. Then cross the Scuppernong skiing/hiking trail several times as you continue north and west. Pass through the KMSF-SU Pinewoods Campground, bend to the west alongside a farmer’s field, and head to the segment’s endpoint on CTH-C.

![Map of the Scuppernong Segment of the Ice Age Trail in Waukesha County, showing the trail route, local roads (CTH-C, CTH-ZZ, STH-67, Piper Rd., Road X), campgrounds (Ottawa Lake, Pinewoods, Group Camp), and natural features like Ottawa Lake and McKeawn Springs.](page_331_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

| Location                                                                   | Services/Information                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Ottawa Lake Campground | Camping. From the STH-67 wayside go north on STH-67 1.7 mi. Where CTH-ZZ heads west from STH-67, turn left on to CTH-ZZ and go 0.2 mi (262-594-6220, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/name/kms; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).                                                                                                    |
| KMSF-SU Backpack Shelter 1                                                 | Camping. On Trail. Reservations required: (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Only one group (up to 10) per shelter per night is allowed. No potable water is available at the shelter. Water available from McKeawn Springs (needs filtration/treatment), spur trail to springs just past the shelter’s spur trail intersection with the Trail. |
| KMSF-SU Pinewoods Campground                                               | Camping (seasonal). On Trail (262-594-6220, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/name/kms; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| Eagle                                                                      | Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. From From the STH-67 wayside go south \~3 mi.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| Dousman                                                                    | Restaurant, convenience store. From From the STH-67 wayside go north \~8 mi.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| North Prairie                                                              | Restaurant, convenience store. From the Piper Rd. Trail access go 3.5 mi east on Piper Rd. and STH-59 (State Rd.).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
## Waterville Segment (Atlas Map 81f)

**Summary:** The Waterville Segment is a 5.8-mile trail in Waukesha County (Atlas Map 81f), running from CTH-D to Jones Rd.. This segment is an exception to most of the Ice Age Trail route in Waukesha County because it lies slightly west of the Kettle Moraine.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: hiker icon] **5.8 miles (5.1 IAT, 0.7 CR):** CTH-D to Jones Rd.

*Note: New Ice Age Trail and a parking area is planned east of Waterville Rd., and a parking area is planned at Jones Rd. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for updates.*

[Icon: water drop icon] From small intermittent streams.

[Icon: dashed line icon] Segment includes a connecting route roadwalk.

[Icon: hiker icon] At two Dispersed Camping Areas [Icon: DCA icon].

[Icon: spur trail icon] Blue-blazed spur trails to the two DCAs and to the Field Station’s gravel drive.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: number 1 icon] **CTH-D Trail Access (42.97715, -88.45161):** From I-94 at Oconomowoc, take Exit 282 and follow STH-67 south for 5.8 mi. At CTH-D turn left and go east 0.8 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

[Icon: number 2 icon] **Jones Rd. Trail Access (43.01436, -88.40377):** From I-94 in Delafield, take Exit 285 and follow CTH-C south for 2.7 mi. *Note: CTH-C becomes Jones Rd. just south of USH-18.* **No Parking.**

**Additional Parking:** (i) Manor House Rd. roadside parking. No parking on *Tallgrass Ct.* (ii) UWM at Waukesha Field Station on Waterville Rd. Limited parking along gravel drive. Do not park in the Wildlife in Need lot or use their building. No overnight parking. A 0.1-mi blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT from the east end of the gravel drive.

### THE HIKE

This segment is an exception to most of the Ice Age Trail route in Waukesha County because it lies slightly west of the Kettle Moraine. The land rolls gently in contrast to the steep hills and kettles of other area segments and showcases remnants of pre-settlement vegetation: oak forest, oak openings, prairie, and wetland.

From the Trail access on CTH-D, head north crossing a series of four board- walks/bridges spanning a low-lying area that is wet, especially after a rainy spell. You may see a small exposed section of Niagara Escarpment dolomite bedrock half a mile from the starting point. Gradually move uphill through mostly forested terrain with occasional breaks into prairies. You will meet two spurs, one on the right and one on the left, to Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

After crossing Tallgrass Court, turn east and cross a 425-foot boardwalk before meeting Waterville Road. Turn left and head north on this road – a narrow, and at times busy, connecting route – for 0.7 miles.

Depart Waterville Road and walk parallel to it, alongside fields, through several small groves of trees and an interesting rock garden.

As you approach the UWM at Waukesha Field Station, head east and then north through the Field Station property to a junction with the Glacial Drumlin State Trail (GDST). Turn right on the GDST and continue to the segment’s end at the intersection of STH-18 and Jones Road. (Jones Road, on the right, is across from Kettle Moraine Drive (CTH-C) on the left.)

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Waukesha County showing the Waterville Segment and connections to Scuppernong and Lapham Peak Segments.](page_333_image_1_v2.jpg)

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**UWM at Waukesha Field Station:** On Trail. (W349 S1480 Waterville Rd., Oconomowoc, 262-955-9384, uwm.edu/waukesha/field-station/).

This property is home to a beautiful restored prairie/oak savanna, a variety of forestland, an ephemeral pond, Henrietta Lake and Scuppernong Creek. Open to the public, there are 3.5 mi of mowed hiking trails, as well as boardwalks over wetland areas. Call for information about educational classes and volunteer opportunities. Also located here is the Wildlife in Need Center (262-965-3090, helpingwildlife.org).

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Southern Unit (KMSF-SU) Pinewoods Campground:** See Scuppernong Segment, p. 298. From the CTH-D Trail access go south ~2 mi.

**KMSF-SU Ottawa Lake Campground:** See Scuppernong Segment, p. 298. From the CTH-D Trail access go south ~2 mi.

**Dousman:** Restaurant, convenience store. From the UWM at Waukesha Field Station, take Waterville Rd. north 0.5 mi to USH-18, turn left and go west 1.7 mi to Main St., turn left and go south 0.4 mi.

**Wales:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. From the CTH-D Trail access go east then north ~5 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.segment_name: Lapham Peak Segment and Delafield Segment
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
## Lapham Peak Segment and Delafield Segment (Atlas Maps 81f, 82f)

**Summary:** The Lapham Peak Segment and Delafield Segment: 1. [Icon: access point 1 icon] **Jones Rd. Trail Access (43.01436, -88.40377):** From I-94 in Delafield, take Exit 285 and follow CTH-C south for 2.7 mi. **Note:** CTH-C becomes Jones Rd. just south of USH-18. **No Parking.** 2. [Icon: access point 2 icon] **Cushing Park Rd. parking area Trail Access (43.05154.

### [Icon: segment icon] Lapham Peak Segment—6.1 miles: Jones Rd. to Cushing Park Rd. parking area

**Note:** A parking area is planned at Jones Rd. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.

* [Icon: water, picnic, restrooms icons] At various locations in the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Lapham Peak Unit (KMSF-LPU).
* [Icon: water source icon] From Scuppernong Creek, a couple of small ponds and Nemahbin Springs.
* [Icon: backpack site icon] At KMSF-LPU backpack site [Icon: site M icon] (reservations required).
* [Icon: ColdCache icon] Four ColdCache sites on segment.
* [Icon: dogs leashed icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: multiuse trail icon] A portion of the segment overlaps the multiuse Glacial Drumlin State Trail.
* [Icon: trail network icon] The Glacial Drumlin State Trail extends west and east from the segment. The KMSF-LPU has an extensive network of trails.

### [Icon: segment icon] Delafield Segment—3.2 miles: Cushing Park Rd. parking area to STH-83

* [Icon: water, picnic, restrooms icons] At Cushing Memorial Park and Naga-Waukee County Park.
* [Icon: campground icon] At Naga-Waukee County Park Campground [Icon: campground A icon].
* [Icon: no hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: dogs leashed icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: multiuse trail icon] The entire segment overlaps the Lake Country Recreational Trail, city sidewalks and roads.
* [Icon: trail network icon] The Lake Country Recreational Trail extends west and east from the segment. Also, a spur trail to Naga-Waukee County Park.
* [Icon: wheelchair accessible icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. [Icon: access point 1 icon] **Jones Rd. Trail Access (43.01436, -88.40377):** From I-94 in Delafield, take Exit 285 and follow CTH-C south for 2.7 mi. **Note:** CTH-C becomes Jones Rd. just south of USH-18. **No Parking.**
2. [Icon: access point 2 icon] **Cushing Park Rd. parking area Trail Access (43.05154, -88.41396): Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.
3. [Icon: access point 3 icon] **STH-83 Trail Access (43.05449, -88.37149):** From I-94 in Delafield, take Exit 287 and follow STH-83 north for 0.3 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access at Nagawaukee Park-and-Ride.

**Additional Parking:** (i) KMSF-Lapham Peak Unit. (ii) Cushing Memorial Park on Cushing Park Rd. (iii) Main St. and Dopkins St. (iv) Naga-Waukee County Park.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
## Lapham Peak Segment

**Summary:** The Lapham Peak Segment: This segment highlights the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Lapham Peak Unit (KMSF-LPU), winding through woods and prairie restoration sites and offering outstanding views from the Lapham Peak observation tower.

This segment highlights the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Lapham Peak Unit (KMSF-LPU), winding through woods and prairie restoration sites and offering outstanding views from the Lapham Peak observation tower. The unit is named after conservationist and geographer Increase Lapham. He spent his lifetime researching and documenting regional

geography, botany, biology, conservation, forestry, geology, and archaeology. In addition, Lapham’s work in meteorology led to the creation of the United States National Weather Service and modern weather forecasts.

The KMSF-LPU covers 1,000 acres and has numerous hiking, mountain bike, and groomed cross-country ski trails. In the spring, the white and bur oak savannas teem with native woodland wildflowers like Dutchman’s breeches, columbine, and Jacob’s ladder. Summer prairie flowers include prairie dock, rattlesnake master, lupine, and pale purple coneflower.

Within KMSF-LPU is Lapham Peak. At 1,223 feet above sea level, the glacier-formed kame is the county’s highest point and the site of the original National Weather Service signal station. Atop it is a 45-foot observation tower offering 360-degree views. Hartland, Holy Hill, and Kettle Moraine ridges are to the north. To the east is downtown Milwaukee. To the south and southwest are Dousman and the southern Kettle Moraine. To the west,

![Map of the Hartland and Waterville segments of the Ice Age Trail, showing Kettle Moraine State Forest - Lapham Peak Unit, Delafield, and surrounding areas including Nagawicka Lake and Naga-Waukee County Park.](page_335_image_1_v2.jpg)

near Oconomowoc, is a large flat area once covered by an ancient glacial lake. The tower area has water (seasonal), restrooms, picnic areas, and parking.

From the Trail access where the Glacial Drumlin State Trail (GDST) meets Jones Road, head east on the GDST. This multiuse paved rail-trail travels 52.0 miles between Cottage Grove to the west and Waukesha to the east. For a short distance, you will parallel busy STH-18. Then, leave the GDST, carefully crossing the highway and heading northeast near Scuppernong Creek. Follow the west bank of an ancient glacial meltwater spillway (river) that once carried water from the east side of the Kettle Moraine to the west.

Soon you’ll enter the KMSF-LPU, and at a confluence of trails, paths head in various directions, creating popular loops for walkers and cross-country skiers. Pay close attention to the signage to continue on the Ice Age Trail route.

From the trail confluence, head up Lapham Peak. Enjoy trailside benches on the way up and down. After passing the tower area, meander down the side of the kame through open woods. Soon a short spur trail leads south to the KMSF-LPU backpack campsite (reservations required) with a table and a privy. Next, follow the low boardwalk to the SE corner of Evergreen Grove parking lot. Continue through the picnic area to cross CTH-C at the Paul Sandgren Trail, then immediately go south on the Trail.

West of Kettle Moraine Drive (CTH-C), pass another pond and weave through the KMSF-LPU’s Prairie Path, which offers long views of the prairies and surrounding areas. A spur trail leads north to Nemahbin Springs, home to a flourishing frog population and many aquatic plants. The segment ends at the Cushing Park Road parking area.

**Delafield Segment**

This segment highlights the city of Delafield, an Ice Age Trail Community, which it winds through. Enjoy a trek through its downtown and along part of the Lake Country Recreation Trail.

From the Trail access at the Cushing Park Road parking area, head north alongside Cushing Park Road on a paved path. Cross Main Street and meet the Wisconsin Veterans Memorial Riverwalk (see Points of Interest). Head east on the walkway to the Delafield municipal parking lot, then cross to the north side of the Bark River and continue east to Genesee Street (CTH-C).

Turn south on Genesee Street and walk through downtown Delafield to Wells Street. Turn left and go east. After you cross Bleeker Street, the Trail transitions to the Lake Country Recreational Trail (LCRT). Pass through a subdivision and view 981-acre Nagawicka Lake, which lies in a preglacial valley, blocked in on the east and west by glacial drift.

Continue eastward along the LCRT, skirting the southern edge of Naga-Waukee County Park. The segment ends on the west side of STH-83 next to the Nagawaukee Park and Ride.

POINTS OF INTEREST

**Cushing Memorial Park and Wisconsin Veterans Memorial Riverwalk:** On Trail. From the intersection of Cushing Park Rd. and Main St., go north on Cushing Park Rd. 0.1 mi to Cushing Memorial Park

and the west end of the Wisconsin Veterans Memorial Riverwalk (600 N. Cushing Park Rd., Delafield, 262-646-6220, cityofdelafield.com/144/Parks-Recreation).

Cushing Memorial Park honors the Civil War hero brothers Navy Commander William Cushing, Army 1<sup>st</sup> Lieutenant Howard Cushing and Medal of Honor recipient Army 1<sup>st</sup> Lieutenant Alonzo Cushing. A 50-foot Barre granite monument stands on part of the original Cushing homestead. The Wisconsin Veterans Memorial Riverwalk (on Trail) links nine separate U.S. war memorials with educational displays about each war. The 0.75-mi walk winds along the Bark River on a boardwalk and gravel path from Cushing Memorial Park’s Peace Garden and Spring (west end), a historic Potawatomi meeting site, to St. John’s Pond (east end) on Genesee St. The Riverwalk’s main entry plaza and bridge are just north of the Ice Age Trail route at Main St. and Dopkins St.

### AREA SERVICES

**Dousman:** Restaurant, convenience store. From the UWM at Waukesha Field Station go north then west ~3 mi.

**Wales:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, library, medical service. From the STH-18 Trail access go 1.4 mi east using the GDST or 0.8 mi east on STH-18, then south 0.5 mi on STH-83.

**Waukesha:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the STH-18 Trail access go ~8 mi east on STH-18.

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Lapham Peak Unit (KMSF-LPU):** On Trail (262-646-3025, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/laphampeak). One walk-in backpack campsite available. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Only one group (up to 10) per night is allowed. No water; pit toilets at campsite. Information about the KMSF-LPU is also available from the KMSF headquarters office (262-594-6200).

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo]
**Delafield:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Services available in the city of Delafield and the area at STH-83 at Golf Rd. Camping at Naga-Waukee County Park (262-646-3555). Area info available from the Delafield Chamber of Commerce and Tourism Council (262-646-8100, visitdelafield.org).

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo]
**Hartland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Cottonwood Ave and Cardinal Ave. Area info available from the Hartland Area Chamber of Commerce (262-367-7059, explorehartland.com). From the STH-83 Trail access go north ~5 mi.

> *[My sister and I] researched it together, joined some Facebook groups and finally decided, “Hey, how hard can this be? Let’s do it!”…We started with the Lapham Peak Segment…And the learning curve became evident immediately as we got lost on our first hike! Our most valuable lesson was learned that day – how to follow those lovely yellow blazes!*
>
> JENNY “TWEEDLE DUM” FUERSTENAU, THOUSAND-MILER

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
## Hartland Segment (Atlas Map 82f)

**Summary:** The Hartland Segment is a 6.8-mile trail in Waukesha County (Atlas Map 82f), running from STH-83 to CTH-K at Centennial Park. This segment highlights the village of Hartland, an Ice Age Trail Community.

### SNAPSHOT

* [Icon: distance icon] **6.8 miles (5.7 IAT, 1.2 CR)**: STH-83 to CTH-K at Centennial Park

* [Icon: water icon] [Icon: restroom icon] At Naga-Waukee County Park, Nixon Park, Bark River Park, Hartbrook Park, Centennial Park, Penbrook Park, and Hartland Public Library.

* [Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.

* [Icon: water drop icon] From the Bark River, which the segment route crosses six times.

* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. Dogs are **NOT allowed on the Trail between Hartbrook Park and Centennial Park.**

* [Icon: camping icon] At Naga-Waukee County Park Campground [Icon: delta symbol].

* Segment includes a connecting route roadwalk. Portions overlap multiuse rec trails and town sidewalks.

* [Icon: overlook icon] At Naga-Waukee County Park, Hartland Ice Age Marsh-John Muir Overlook, Penbrook Park, Nixon Park, Hartbrook Park and Centennial Park.

* [Icon: loop trail icon] Hartland Ice Age Marsh-John Muir Overlook loop trail, Aldo Leopold Overlook trails, and Penbrook Park white-blazed loop trail.

* [Icon: parking icon] At Naga-Waukee County Park, Nixon Park, Hartbrook Park, and Centennial Park.

* [Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

* [Icon: circled 1] **STH-83 Trail Access (43.05449, -88.37149)**: From I-94 in Delafield, take Exit 287 and follow STH-83 north for 0.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access at Nagawaukee Park-and-Ride.

* [Icon: circled 2] **CTH-K at Centennial Park Trail Access (43.11977, -88.33404)**: Near Hartland heading west on STH-16, take Exit 183 and follow Merton Ave. (CTH-KC) north for 0.7 mi. At Lisbon Rd. (CTH-K) turn left and go west 0.2 mi. If heading east on STH-16, take Exit 182 and follow North Ave. (CTH-E) north for 1.0 mi. At Lisbon Rd. (CTH-K) turn right and go east 0.5 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Naga-Waukee County Park. (ii) Hartland Ice Age Marsh-John Muir Overlook on Cottonwood Ave. (iii) Aldo Leopold Overlook on Maple Ave. (CTH-E). (iv) Nixon Park. (v) Bark River Park. (vi) Hartbrook Park.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights the village of Hartland, an Ice Age Trail Community. As you hike the paved trail through the Village of Hartland, you’ll discover shops, restaurants, amenities, and many parks. The area also commemorates (with signs) three individuals in the Conservationists’ Hall of Fame: John Muir, father of the National Park System, John Wesley Powell, explorer and geologist, and Aldo Leopold, ecologist and conservationist. (Only the sign for Leopold is on Trail.)

From the Trail access on the west side of STH-83, head east on the Lake Country Recreational Trail (LCRT), crossing busy STH-83 at the Golf Road stoplight. Shortly after crossing STH-83, depart from the LCRT and head north, skirting the west side of Naga-Waukee Golf Course. Bend east briefly before resuming a northward course, passing behind Elmbrook Church of Lake Country before reaching Fairway Court.

At Fairway Court, begin a 1.2-mile unsigned connecting route: east on Fairway Court, north on Golf Hills Drive, north on Fieldwood Drive, and northwest on Foxwood Drive.

From Foxwood Drive, just before reaching the Foxwood Estate entrance, go off-road northeast over wooded, hilly topography to a crossing of North Shore Drive (CTH-KE). From here, most of the remainder of the segment is paved. Continue north, following the east side of Cottonwood Avenue. Just south of the intersection of Cottonwood Avenue and Lindenwood Drive is the Hartland Ice Age Marsh–John Muir Overlook. This loop trail to the west of the segment route (across Cottonwood Avenue, starting from the Cottonwood Wayside) includes two islands, oak savanna openings, boardwalks, and a picnic shelter.

From the Hartland Ice Age Marsh loop trail access at the Cottonwood Wayside, continue briefly on Cottonwood Avenue. Then head northeast

![Map of the Hartland Segment of the Ice Age Trail in Waukesha County, showing the trail route from the Delafield Segment to the Merton Segment, passing through Naga-Waukee County Park, Hartland Ice Age Marsh, and various city parks in Hartland. Map labels include: Merton Segment, Beaver Lake, Bark River, Centennial Park, Hartbrook Park, Nixon Park, Hartland, IATA's Hartland Marsh, Maple Wayside, Pewaukee Lake, Naga-Waukee County Park & Golf Course, Lake Country Trail, Delafield Segment, Hill St., Capitol Dr., 83, 16, KE, 94, and a scale bar showing 0, 1/2, 1 miles.](page_339_image_1_v2.jpg)

through the Aldo Leopold Overlook in an area bounded by Cottonwood Avenue, Cardinal Lane, Maple Avenue (CTH-E), and Lindenwood Drive. The Leopold site offers trails with boardwalks, two ponds, and a view from a 45-foot glacial hill.

At Maple Avenue (CTH-E), turn north and meet a white-blazed loop trail on the right winding through Penbrook Park. Continue to follow Maple Avenue north. After crossing railroad tracks, turn west into Nixon Park, cross the parking lot, and then go briefly south and west, and then north on a park trail along the Bark River. From here, most of the remainder of the segment travels close to the Bark River. Continue north across East Park Avenue and past the Hartland Public Library. Take a quick route through downtown Hartland along Haight Drive, Goodwin Avenue, across East Capitol Drive, and then into an alley that leads into Bark River Park. North of STH-16, walk east briefly on Hartbrook Drive before leaving the road and entering Hartbrook Park. Between Hartbrook Park and the segment’s endpoint on CTH-K at Centennial Park, you’ll follow a particularly scenic section of the Bark River adjacent to a housing development.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Delafield:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Services available in the city of Delafield and the area at STH-83 at Golf Rd. Camping at Naga-Waukee County Park (262-646-3555). Area info available from the Delafield Chamber of Commerce and Tourism Council (262-646-8100, visitdelafield.org). Many services in the vicinity of the STH-83 Trail access at Golf Rd.

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Hartland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Cottonwood Ave and Cardinal Ave. Area info available from the Hartland Area Chamber of Commerce (262-367-7059, explorehartland.com).

**Brookfield:** General shopping. ~15 mi east of Hartland. Outfitter/camping supplies at REI (262-783-6150, rei.com/stores/brookfield).

**Merton:** Tavern with food. From the CTH-K at Centennial Park Trail access go east then north ~3 mi.

> *It did not take us long to discover the joys of hiking during Mother Nature’s coldest months. There was something indescribably beautiful about hiking in winter, especially during, or soon after, a snowfall. I loved the solitude and stillness of nature…and being able to see and appreciate the simple beauty and elegance of the landscape’s geological features.*

SHARON ANTON, THOUSAND-MILER

## Merton Segment and Monches Segment (Atlas Map 83f)

**Summary:** The Merton Segment and Monches Segment: 1. **[Icon: access point 1 icon] CTH-K at Centennial Park Trail Access (43.11977, -88.33404):** *Near Hartland heading west on STH-16, take Exit 183 and follow Merton Ave. (CTH-KC) north for 0.7 mi.

### [Icon: Merton Segment icon] Merton Segment—5.2 miles (2.7 IAT, 2.5 CR): CTH-K at Centennial Park to E. Kilbourne Rd.

* [Icon: water drop icon] From the Bark River.
* [Icon: hiker icon] At Centennial Park.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: dotted line icon] Segment includes connecting route roadwalks. Portions closely parallel the Bugline Trail.
* [Icon: dashed line icon] A paved spur trail along the west bank of the Bark River.
* [Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### [Icon: Monches Segment icon] Monches Segment—3.1 miles: E. Kilbourne Rd. to CTH-Q

* [Icon: water drop icon] From the Oconomowoc River.
* [Icon: globe icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: dashed line icon] A blue-blazed spur trail leading to a parking area and a short spur trail leading to a bench.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. **[Icon: access point 1 icon] CTH-K at Centennial Park Trail Access (43.11977, -88.33404):** *Near Hartland heading west on STH-16, take Exit 183 and follow Merton Ave. (CTH-KC) north for 0.7 mi. At Lisbon Rd. (CTH-K) turn left and go west 0.2 mi. If heading east on STH-16, take Exit 182 and follow North Ave. (CTH-E) north for 1.0 mi. At Lisbon Rd. (CTH-K) turn right and go east 0.5 mi.* **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.
2. **[Icon: access point 2 icon] E. Kilbourne Rd. Trail Access (43.16113, -88.35559): Parking:** 0.1 mi west on E. Kilbourne Rd. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.
3. **[Icon: access point 3 icon] CTH-Q Trail Access (43.19311, -88.34631):** From Menomonee Falls take USH-41/45 north. Exit onto County Line Rd. (CTH-Q) and go west 11.5 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.1 mi north on CTH-K. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Merton Community Park on CTH-VV. (ii) DNR parking area on County Line Rd. (CTH-Q).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::waukesha-count.parent_text:
## Merton Segment

**Summary:** The Merton Segment: This segment courses along the Bark River and closely parallels the paved Bugline Trail for 1.9 miles.

This segment courses along the Bark River and closely parallels the paved Bugline Trail for 1.9 miles. Built on an old railbed, the Bugline travels 16 miles from Menomonee Falls to North Lake. If you prefer to walk on the Bugline along this section, you may do so instead of hiking on the Ice Age Trail.

From the Trail access on CTH-K, start a connecting route that heads east on CTH-K for 0.3 miles, north on Dorn Road for 1.4 miles, and then west on Richter Road for 0.4 miles. Use caution when walking along these roads, as they have narrow gravel shoulders and moderate traffic.

At the end of Richter Road, continue west into the woods over a short boardwalk. Turn north along the Bark River, and at Hartling Road (CTH-EF), turn left, and cross the highway bridge diagonally to the river’s west side. Head northeast along the river’s west bank until you emerge onto Tamron Drive. Turn right and hike east a short distance back to Dorn Road.

Go north along Dorn Road for 0.4 miles, then leave it to travel west along the old Kettle Moraine Railway right-of-way. This section closely parallels the Bugline Trail and crosses CTH-VV and CTH-E along the way. Depart the Kettle Moraine Railway right-of-way just before it reaches the Oconomowoc River and head northeast to East Kilbourne Road. Take the road west, then north, pass under a railroad trestle, and arrive at the end of the segment. A blue-blazed spur trail leads west to a parking area.

![Map of the Hartland Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route from Centennial Park north towards the Loew Lake Segment. The map includes landmarks such as Beaver Lake, Lake Keesus, Merton Community Park, and various roads like CTH-K, CTH-EF, and CTH-VV. The trail follows the Bark River and the old Kettle Moraine Railway right-of-way.](page_342_image_1_v2.jpg)

**Monches Segment**
This segment traverses a mature maple forest,

parallels the Oconomowoc River, and highlights spring and early summer woodland wildflowers. Expect to see ephemeral flowers like Dutchman’s breeches, spring beauty, shooting star, marsh marigold, trillium, and jack-in-the-pulpit. The Oconomowoc River, a tributary of the Rock River, originates in Washington County. As it travels through Waukesha County, it links several glacial kettle lakes creating one of the most popular recreational resources in the state.

The forest you’ll walk through is named in honor of conservationist Carl Schurz, United States Secretary of the Interior from 1877 to 1881. He established the first U.S. Forest Reserves and helped lay the groundwork for the U.S. Forest Service and the National Park Service.

From the Trail access on East Kilbourne Road, quickly meet a short spur trail leading to a Leopold bench overlooking the Oconomowoc River. Continue heading east, then north, and soon you will arrive at a bridge over a picturesque cascading brook. Skunk cabbage and marsh marigolds inhabit the natural spring-fed environment. Continuing north, you’ll enjoy glimpses of the Oconomowoc River. Finally, cross a boardwalk and another bridge to the west side of the river. In early May, trout lilies carpet the steep hillside just north of the bridge.

Toward the end of the segment, climb the bank, a remnant of a glacial melt-water spillway (river). On top, the view across the meadow offers an excellent example of forest progression. A large sign memorializing Carl Schurz is just west of the segment’s endpoint at County Line Road (CTH-Q).

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Hartland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Cottonwood Ave and Cardinal Ave. Area info available from the Hartland Area Chamber of Commerce (262-367-7059, explorehartland.com). From the Centennial Park at CTH-K Trail access go east then south ~2 mi.

**CTH-K**: Convenience store. On Trail. At CTH-K and Merton Ave.

**Merton**: Tavern with food. From the Hartling Rd. (CTH-EF) Trail access go 1.0 mi east and 0.5 mi north on CTH-VV.

**North Lake**: Restaurant, convenience store, library. From the CTH-VV Trail access go 1.5 mi west.

**Monches**: Tavern with food. From the CTH-Q Trail access go south 0.1 mi on CTH-E.

> *I hiked mostly solo, I loved the quiet, the calm. The Trail was my confidante, my therapist. It took me to places I would have never thought to visit.*

> SUE STEINMANN, THOUSAND-MILER

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Washington County, Wisconsin, showing the Milwaukee River (Washington County) Segment, Kewaskum Segment, and West Bend Segment. The map includes local roads, highways (45, 41, 144, 33, 175), and landmarks like Kettle Moraine State Forest - Northern Unit and Ridge Run Park. An inset map shows Washington County's location within Wisconsin.](page_344_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
# Washington County

![Map of Washington County showing the Ice Age Trail route and segments. The map highlights the Cedar Lakes Segment, Slinger Segment, Pike Lake Segment, Holy Hill Segment, and Loew Lake Segment. Key locations include Slinger (an Ice Age Trail Community), Hartford, Polk Kames, Kettle Moraine State Forest (Pike Lake and Loew Lake Units), Glacier Hills County Park, Holy Hill, and Monches. The legend identifies the Ice Age Trail, connecting routes, other trails, county boundaries, and public or IATA land. A scale bar indicates distances from 0 to 4 miles. Map version 041023.](page_345_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Washington County

*Atlas Maps 83f–87f; Databook pages 85–88*

*Total miles: 45.1 (Trail 35.9; Connecting Route 9.2)*

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Washington County](page_346_image_2_v2.jpg)

The Ice Age Trail’s entire route through the county is within the margins of the Kettle Moraine. These ridges formed from sand and gravel deposited where the Green Bay and Lake Michigan lobes butted up against each other, often atop the Niagara Escarpment. To either side of the Kettle Moraine are drumlins and till plains left behind by each of the lobes. However, Wisconsin’s most distinctive geological landform is the glacier-formed moulin kame. Kames resulted from glacial streams flowing down through cracks or shafts (moulins) in the ice sheets that rose thousands of feet above our modern landscape. Several prominent kames, such as Holy Hill, Powder Hill, and a cluster known as the Polk Kames, dominate the narrow Kettle Moraine and offer panoramic hilltop views.

The Trail route in Washington County winds through the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Loew Lake Unit (KMSF-LLU) and Pike Lake Unit (KMSF-PLU), sandwiched between a trek to Holy Hill. Farther north, the route highlights two Ice Age Trail Communities, Slinger and West Bend.

Baruch Schleisinger Weil, an immigrant from France’s German Alsace region, founded Schleisingerville in 1845 after buying several thousand acres of land from the government. Weil soon built a home and general store, which served as the area’s primary trade center. By 1921, villagers voted to shorten “Schleisingerville” to “Slinger.” Today, this Ice Age Trail Community’s historic downtown offers delicious post-hike meals and retail opportunities.

West Bend, also an Ice Age Trail Community, is situated where the Milwaukee River turns east and flows into Ozaukee County. In 1845, the Wisconsin Territorial Legislature authorized a road connecting Fond du Lac with Milwaukee, including a rest stop. The project’s commissioners identified the bend in the river as an ideal location for overnight travelers and commerce and soon built West Bend’s first sawmill and gristmill. Today, West Bend’s charming downtown offers visitors plenty of dining, shopping, and lodging options. It also boasts outstanding trails, parks, and recreational spaces.

One park, Lizard Mound State Park, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. It contains 28 conical and linear effigy mounds.

. . .

Camping locations include a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) in the Alliance-owned Otten Preserve on the Kewaskum Segment and sites in the Kettle Moraine State Forest.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Washington/Ozaukee County Chapter, formed in 1987, maintains Ice Age Trail segments winding through their territory and seeks ways to get more blazed Trail miles. The Chapter promotes the Trail through local news articles, service club memberships, and Trail improvement events. They also sponsor regular hikes, hosting annual events like National Trails Day and a Fall Colors hike.

The Chapter’s “Meander the Mid-Moraine” hiking program recognizes hikers who have walked all Ice Age Trail miles hosted by Washington and Ozaukee counties. Start your Meandering at IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/washington-ozaukee-county-chapter to learn more about the Chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Pike Lake Unit (KMSF-PLU)**: 262-670-3400, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/pikelake

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU)**: 262-626-2116, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/kmn

**Washington County Convention & Visitors Bureau**: 262-677-5069, visitwashingtoncounty.com

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, West Bend**: visitwestbend.com; and wbachamber.org

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Slinger**: vi.slinger.wi.gov

![A tranquil view of a river winding through a lush, green forest in Ridge Run Park.](page_347_image_1_v2.jpg)

West Bend Segment: A tranquil view in Ridge Run Park.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Loew Lake Segment (Atlas Maps 83f, 84f)

**Summary:** The Loew Lake Segment is a 4.9-mile trail in Washington County (Atlas Map 83f, 84f), running from CTH-Q to Emerald Dr. Northern Trail Access.. This segment offers passage through the greenbelt between Washington and Waukesha counties.

### SNAPSHOT

* [Icon: mileage icon] [Icon: trail icon] **4.9 miles (4.5 IAT, 0.4 CR)**: CTH-Q to Emerald Dr. Northern Trail Access.
* [Icon: water icon] From the Oconomowoc River.
* [Icon: parking icon] At the Emerald Dr. DNR parking area.
* [Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: hunting icon] Portion of segment crossing private land on the west side of Emerald Dr. is closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times, and by law during nesting season, April 15 to July 31, in the Loew Lake Unit (KMSF-LLU).
* [Icon: spur trail icon] Two short spur trails.
* [Icon: roadwalk icon] Segment includes connecting-route roadwalk.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] **① CTH-Q Trail Access (43.19311, -88.34631)**: From Menomonee Falls take USH-41/45 north. Exit onto County Line Rd. (CTH-Q) and go west 11.5 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.1 mi north on CTH-K. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

[Icon: 2 icon] **② Emerald Dr. Northern Trail Access (43.23291, -88.31435)**: From Hartford at the intersection of STH-83 and STH-60, take STH-83 south 4.7 mi. At STH-167 turn left and go east 1.5 mi. At CTH-K turn right and go south 1.0 mi. At Donegal Rd. turn left and go east 1.5 mi. At Emerald Dr. turn right and go south 0.3 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the DNR parking area 0.5 mi south on Emerald Dr. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

**Additional Parking**: (i) DNR parking area on County Line Rd. (CTH-Q). (ii) Emerald Preserve parking area.

### THE HIKE

This segment offers passage through the greenbelt between Washington and Waukesha counties. It generally follows the rim of the Oconomowoc River valley and the winding Oconomowoc River. The valley was a glacial spillway where meltwater flowed south between the Green Bay and Lake Michigan glacial lobes.

The segment also travels through the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Loew Lake Unit (KMSF-LLU), a 1,090-acre area providing wildlife with various habitats, including 23-acre Loew Lake. The property is extremely popular with hunters and fishermen in all seasons.

From the Trail access near the CTH-K and CTH-Q intersection at the Washington/Waukesha county line, traverse a conifer grove before dropping down and briefly parallelling the Oconomowoc River. Leave the riverbank and continue north to a boardwalk at the edge of a marsh filled with skunk cabbage and cow parsnip. Follow several hedgerows adjacent to an open field and cut through another conifer grove before meeting Emerald Drive.

At Emerald Drive, turn right and go northeast along the road for 0.4 miles. Then, head east and northeast on an off-road section featuring distant views of the Holy Hill Shrine to the north. Just before meeting Emerald Drive again, enjoy a pause at a platform with benches built by the local chapter overlooking a spring. The spring feeds a small stream flowing southeastward and emptying into the Oconomowoc River.

![Map of the Loew Lake Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through the Kettle Moraine State Forest - Loew Lake Unit, including landmarks like Loew Lake, Hickey Lake, and the Oconomowoc River, with connections to the Monches and Holy Hill Segments.](page_349_image_1_v2.jpg)

Cross to the north side of Emerald Drive for an additional 0.1-mile walk. Exit the woods, cross Emerald Drive again to the south, and head east. Weave in and out of the woods, alongside meadows, and atop a steep-sided esker. Meet a horse trail going south, and soon after, you will see Loew Lake off to the right. Soon you'll meet a blue-blazed spur trail heading west to the DNR's Emerald Drive parking area. From this junction, continue north through a large meadow before climbing out of the river valley on a series of switchbacks to reach the segment's endpoint on Emerald Drive.

### AREA SERVICES

**Monches:** Tavern with food. From the CTH-K at CTH-Q Trail access go south 0.1 mi on CTH-E.

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **Hartland:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Cottonwood Ave and Cardinal Ave. Area info available from the Hartland Area Chamber of Commerce (262-367-7059, explorehartland.com). From the CTH-K at CTH-Q Trail access go south ~7 mi.

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Loew Lake Unit (KMSF-LLU):** On Trail. Access on Emerald Dr. (262-670-3400, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/loewlake).

**Hartford:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the Emerald Dr. northern Trail access go west and north ~9 mi.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Holy Hill Segment (Atlas Map 84f)

**Summary:** The Holy Hill Segment is a 6.8-mile trail in Washington County (Atlas Map 84f), running from Donegal Rd. to CTH-E. Especially popular during the fall color season, this segment showcases wooded, hilly terrain and deep kettles.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icon] **6.8 miles**: Donegal Rd. to CTH-E

* [Icon: water icon] At the Basilica complex.
* [Icon: picnic icon] At the Station Way Rd. picnic area.
* [Icon: restroom icon] At the Station Way Rd. picnic area and Basilica complex.
* [Icon: hunting icon] Portions of segment crossing private lands between Donegal Rd. and STH-167 (on Holy Hill Grounds), and Pleasant Hill Rd. and CTH-E are closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: coldcache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: road icon] Portions of the segment overlap Pleasant Hill Rd. and Glassgo Dr.
* [Icon: trail icon] Several short spur trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

* ① **Donegal Rd. Trail Access (43.23653, -88.32848)**: From the Menomonee Falls area take I-41/USH-45 north. Exit onto STH-167 and go west 6.0 mi. At St. Augustine Rd. turn left and go south 0.8 mi. At Emerald Dr. turn right and go west, curving south 0.7 mi. At Donegal Rd. turn right and go west 0.8 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Emerald Drive Preserve parking area 0.2 mi east on Donegal Rd.

* ② **CTH-E Trail Access (43.29535, -88.32985)**: From I-41 take Exit 64B and follow STH-60 west for 2.3 mi. At CTH-CC turn left and go south 2.0 mi. At CTH-E turn right and go west 1.4 mi. **No Parking.**

**Additional Parking**: (i) Carmel Rd. near St. Mary of the Hill Parish. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT. (ii) Station Way Rd. picnic area. The IAT follows the north edge of the picnic area. (iii) STH-167 roadside parking. A spur trail leads to the IAT. (iv) Shannon Rd. A blue-blazed spur trail leads to the IAT.

### THE HIKE

Especially popular during the fall color season, this segment showcases wooded, hilly terrain and deep kettles. The route offers views of the glacial kame Holy Hill and the shrine and monastery atop it at 1,350 feet above sea level. The kame formed where a glacial stream fell as a vertical shaft through the glacier, dropping debris into a pile on the glacier’s bed. Glacial erratics are a common trailside sight in this area. It’s estimated that at the time of the Wisconsin Glaciation, the thickness of the glacial ice extended another 1,000 feet above Holy Hill.

From the Trail access at Donegal Road to the STH-167 Trail access, traverse mostly forested landscape across the glacial terrain surrounding Holy Hill. Several spur trails offer vistas of the surrounding area. A blue-blazed spur trail north of Holy Hill leads to a parking area near St. Mary of the Hill Parish and a sizable glacial erratic perched at the top of a kame. At STH-167, continue north, crossing an intermittent stream on a bridge built as an Eagle Scout project. Wind through grasslands of a previous agricultural era with several stone fence remnants. Also, take note of the informational signs identifying tree species. Just south of the Shannon Road Trail access, a blue-blazed spur trail leads west to a DNR parking area on Shannon Road.

After crossing Shannon Road, you’ll pass through a pine grove and continue north along the edges of two fields. At Pleasant Hill Road, turn right and follow blazed telephone poles east. Depart Pleasant Hill Road and head north across a field along the edge of a small wetland. Climb two hills with a kettle pond in between and pass through a wetland on a long, elevated boardwalk.

Cross East Waterford Road and continue north and west, passing several deep kettles in a mature mixed forest. Then, follow the edges of farm fields and cross another before reaching Glassgo Drive. Follow this road northeast before departing off-road to the northwest on a short section leading to the segment’s end point on CTH-E.

![Map of the Holy Hill Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route, road crossings, and nearby landmarks like Holy Hill and IATA's Emerald Preserve.](page_351_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Holy Hill Shrine and Monastery:** Restaurant (seasonal), lodging (call ahead). On Trail (262-628-1838, holyhill.com).

**Glacial Hills County Park cabins:** Take STH-167 east 2.2 mi. At Friess Lake Rd. go 0.3 mi south to park’s entrance drive. Cabin rentals through Washington County Parks Department. See the county website for VRBO cabin reservation link (1664 Friess Lake Rd., 262-335-4445, washcoparks.com/parks/shelters/).

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Pike Lake Unit (KMSF-PLU):** Camping. On Trail. (262-670-3400, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/pikelake). General camping and three walk-in backpack campsites available. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). From the STH-167 Trail access go west and north ~6 mi.

**Hartford:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the STH-167 Trail access go west and north ~7 mi.
