iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.h2: Pike Lake Segment, Slinger Segment, and Cedar Lakes Segment (Atlas Maps 84f, 85f)
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.segment_name: Pike Lake Segment, Slinger Segment, and Cedar Lakes Segment
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Pike Lake Segment, Slinger Segment, and Cedar Lakes Segment (Atlas Maps 84f, 85f)

**Summary:** The Pike Lake Segment, Slinger Segment, and Cedar Lakes Segment: 1. [Icon: Access point 1 icon] **CTH-E Trail Access** (43.29535, -88.32985): From I-41 take Exit 64B and follow STH-60 west for 2.3 mi.

### [Icon: Pike Lake Segment icon] Pike Lake Segment—3.2 miles: CTH-E to STH-60

* [Icon: Water, picnic, toilet, and shower icons] [Icon: Tent, water, and toilet icons] KMSF-PLU campground [Icon: Campground icon].
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not permitted inside park buildings.**
* [Icon: Toilet and shower icons] At KMSF-PLU facilities.
* [Icon: Trail loop icon] KMSF-PLU trail network loops and spurs. A spur trail to three walk-in campsites.
* [Icon: Water icon] [Icon: Water icon] From Pike Lake.
* [Icon: Hiker and tent icons] Three walk-in campsites [Icon: Tent icon] at KMSF-PLU (see Area Services).

### [Icon: Slinger Segment icon] Slinger Segment—2.1 miles: STH-60 to Cedar Creek Rd.

* At Community Park.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: Wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### [Icon: Cedar Lakes Segment icon] Cedar Lakes Segment—2.4 miles: Cedar Creek Rd. to CTH-NN

* [Icon: No water icon] No reliable sources of water.
* [Icon: ColdCache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: Trail loop icon] A white-blazed loop trail and blue-blazed connecting trail.

![Map of Pike Lake, Slinger, and Cedar Lakes Segments](page_352_image_22_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. [Icon: Access point 1 icon] **CTH-E Trail Access** (43.29535, -88.32985): From I-41 take Exit 64B and follow STH-60 west for 2.3 mi. At CTH-CC turn left and go south 2.0 mi. At CTH-E turn right and go west 1.4 mi. **No Parking.**
2. [Icon: Access point 2 icon] **STH-60 Trail Access** (43.32489, -88.29933): **No Parking.** Instead, park at the KMSF-Pike Lake Unit Black Forest Nature Trail parking area on Powder Hill Rd.
3. [Icon: Access point 3 icon] **Cedar Creek Rd. Trail Access** (43.33904, -88.27299): **No Parking.** Instead, park at Slinger Community Park on Kettle Moraine Dr.

**4 CTH-NN Trail Access (43.35340, -88.27110):** From I-41 take Exit 66 and follow STH-144 north for 0.4 mi. At CTH-NN turn right and go east 0.4 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Kettle Moraine State Forest-Pike Lake Unit (KMSF-PLU) Swim Area on Kettle Moraine Rd. (ii) Roadside parking in Slinger.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Pike Lake Segment

**Summary:** The Pike Lake Segment: The segment passes through the family-friendly Kettle Moraine State Forest-Pike Lake Unit (KMSF-PLU), offering outstanding views from the Powder Hill observation tower.

![Map of the Pike Lake and Slinger segments of the Ice Age Trail, showing trail routes, road crossings, and local landmarks like Slinger Community Park and Powder Hill.](page_353_image_1_v2.jpg)

The segment passes through the family-friendly Kettle Moraine State Forest-Pike Lake Unit (KMSF-PLU), offering outstanding views from the Powder Hill observation tower. Most of the route follows the edge of a glacial ridge through heavily forested maple and beech woods. Pike Lake is a spring-fed kettle with a popular sandy beach; the segment reaches the swimming area parking lot 0.5 miles after entering the 446-acre KMSF-PLU.

From the Trail access on CTH-E, head north. First parallel and then follow quiet Glassgo Drive. When you arrive at

KMSF-PLU’s southern property boundary, posts with yellow bands mark the Trail’s route through the park.

Cross Kettle Moraine Road and head uphill to a junction with a spur trail on the right. It climbs 0.3 miles to the top of 1,350-foot Powder Hill, one of the larger kames in the area. A lookout tower on top offers spectacular 360-degree views. From the junction with the spur trail, continue to Powder Hill Road, joining the Black Forest Nature Trail along the way.

East of Powder Hill Road, pass a spur trail on the right leading to the Pike Lake Unit campground. Turn north to cross the east end of the Black Forest Nature Trail parking area. Travel north and east through a heavily wooded area and meet a short spur trail on the left leading to three walk-in backpack campsites.

Continue east to CTH-CC at a spot marked with an Ice Age Trail sign post on the west side of the road. Turn left and head north on CTH-CC to the segment’s endpoint at the CTH-CC and STH-60 intersection.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Slinger Segment

**Summary:** The Slinger Segment: This segment features Slinger, an Ice Age Trail Community.

This segment features Slinger, an Ice Age Trail Community. Enjoy the short trek along roads and sidewalks through the Village. Tour the local shops and restaurants while taking in the architecture of this historic center of trade.

From the Trail access at the CTH-CC and STH-60 intersection, head northeast on an abandoned road leading to Howard Avenue. Follow Howard Avenue north to Hartford Road, where the route heads east to Kettle Moraine Drive (STH-144).

At Kettle Moraine Drive (STH-144), head northeast through the village of Slinger. Cross East Washington Street and depart Kettle Moraine Drive (STH-144) at an Ice Age Trail kiosk. Enter Slinger Community Park, heading east, then north on a paved trail, crossing an 800-foot boardwalk before reaching the segment’s endpoint at Cedar Creek Road.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Cedar Lakes Segment

**Summary:** The Cedar Lakes Segment: This segment highlights several Polk Kames, the state’s second-largest kame cluster.

This segment highlights several Polk Kames, the state’s second-largest kame cluster. During winter, the north and east sides of the kames retain snow later than the surrounding landscape. The spring months present a scattered display of woodland and prairie wildflowers, including trillium, wood anemone, mayapple, violets, and jack-in-the-pulpit, as well as many ferns. The Trail also traverses agricultural fields, wetlands, and mixed forests of old oaks, sugar maple, white birch, and beech. The fall colors are spectacular.

From the Trail access on Cedar Creek Road, follow the road (caution: narrow shoulders and obstructed views) east for 0.4 miles, passing over USH 41/I-41. Depart Cedar Creek Road and head north along the edge of a farm field. Enter the woods and soon arrive at a junction with the 0.8-mile white-blazed Kame Loop trail. The Kame Loop trail curves around a prominent kame, allowing views of its west side; in spring, look for a large area of shooting stars on a hilltop north of the kame.

From the Kame Loop junction, head east and then north, passing a bench and informational sign on the east side of the kame. From the north junction with the white-blazed loop trail, skirt the eastern sides of a second kame. Volunteer-built puncheons and rock-lined water drainage structures highlight this portion of the segment.

Bend west and pass the north side of the northern kame and then meet a blue-blazed trail leading south to the white-blazed Kame Loop. Continue west and then north, passing through a mix of forest and farm fields before reaching the segment’s endpoint on Arthur Road (CTH-NN).

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Pike Lake Unit (KMSF-PLU)**: Camping. On Trail. (262-670-3400, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/pikelake). General camping and three walk-in backpack campsites available. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Slinger**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, library. From the STH-60 Trail access go 1.5 mi east on STH-60 or on Trail along Kettle Moraine Dr.

**Hartford**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the STH-60 Trail access go 2.5 mi west on STH-60. Area info available from the Hartford Chamber of Commerce (262-673-7002, hartfordchamber.org).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## West Bend Segment (Atlas Maps 85f, 86f)

**Summary:** The West Bend Segment is a 6.7-mile trail in Washington County (Atlas Map 85f, 86f), running from Paradise Dr. to CTH-D. This segment features the city of West Bend, an Ice Age Trail Community situated at the point where the Milwaukee River turns east and flows into Ozaukee County.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: segment icon] **West Bend Segment—6.7 miles**: Paradise Dr. to CTH-D

*Note: A reroute is planned south of Washington St. (STH-33/144). Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

![Snapshot icons and details](page_355_image_14_v2.jpg)
*   [Icon: water icon] [Icon: picnic icon] At Ridge Run Park.
*   [Icon: fishing icon] [Icon: swimming icon] From Silver Creek.
*   [Icon: ColdCache icon] Five ColdCache sites on segment.
*   [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (6-ft max) and under control at all times.
*   [Icon: gravel path icon] Portions overlap a gravel driveway and sidewalks. A small portion in Glacial Blue Hills Recreation Area is open to mountain biking.
*   [Icon: loop trail icon] Several spur trails and three white-blazed loop trails.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] **1 Paradise Dr. Trail Access (43.39815, -88.21099)**: From West Bend on USH-45, take Exit 63 and follow Paradise Dr. west for 1.0 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: parking icon 2] **2 CTH-D Trail Access (43.45550, -88.20845)**: From the West Bend area on USH-45, take Exit 73 and follow CTH-D west for 0.1 mi. **Parking**: Roadside.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Ridge Run Park on Wheat Ridge Ln. or University Dr. (ii) Washington St. (STH-33/144) commercial parking area near Culver’s. (iii) Glacial Blue Hills Recreation Area on Beaver Dam Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment features the city of West Bend, an Ice Age Trail Community situated at the point where the Milwaukee River turns east and flows into Ozaukee County. West Bend’s historic downtown offers visitors plenty of dining, shopping, and lodging options. It also boasts outstanding trails, parks, and recreational spaces. The Trail route takes advantage of the city’s green spaces. South of STH-33/STH-144, it traverses Ridge Run Park, with parking, water, skiing, picnic grounds, and several side trails. It travels north of STH-33/STH-144 through Glacial Blue Hills Recreation Area, with a parking lot, mountain biking, and hiking trails, including two white-blazed loop trails.

From the Trail access on Paradise Drive, meander north. Soon you’ll meet a short white-blazed loop trail arcing to the east. After you meet the other end of this loop, bend west and enter the heavily wooded property of the Girl Scouts’ Camp Silver Brook at the south end of Lucas Lake. Continue past an esker and then drop to a marshy area with a bridge over Silver Creek.

Climb a steep ridge and bend north, passing over lands protected by the Cedar Lakes Conservation Foundation. Then head back onto Girl Scout property, where glimpses of Lucas Lake are possible to the right.

Enter Ridge Run Park and curve past an artesian well and a small pond near a picnic area. Soon you’ll head north along picturesque Silver Creek and its chain of lily-filled ponds and lakes. Partially circle a ridge and then climb it. Turn east,

and exit Ridge Run Park, briefly following a driveway to connect with University Drive near its intersection with Chestnut Street. Head north, following sidewalks along University Drive for a short way.

To cross busy West Washington Street (STH-33/144) at a stop light, first head west a block to the crossing, then head east along the sidewalk on the other side of the road. You’ll pass a commercial area with most services. Depart the sidewalk at the Trail access area on the north side of the road. From here, head north, ascending a forested ridge.

Enter the scenic Glacial Blue Hills Recreation Area, traversing rugged terrain following a moraine and dip between ridges. Shortly after crossing Park Avenue, skirt a kettle and climb to the top of an esker, following the crest of the esker to Beaver Dam Road. Cross Beaver Dam Road and continue north to the segment’s end point at CTH-D.

![Map of the West Bend Segment of the Ice Age Trail showing the route through Ridge Run Park and Glacial Blue Hills Recreation Area.](page_356_image_1_v2.jpg)

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Lizard Mound State Park:** From the Glacial Blue Hills Recreation Area on Beaver Dam Rd., continue onto Jefferson St., turn right onto N. Main St., turn left onto Barton Ave. (STH-144) and travel 2.9 mi, turn right onto CTH-A (2121 CTH-A, West Bend, 262-626-2116, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/lizardmound).

This park is one of the most well-preserved Native American effigy mound sites in the nation. Its 28 extant conical and linear mounds represent spiritual and animal-like forms, including bird, panther or water spirit, and the eponymous lizard.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community icon] **West Bend:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical services. On Trail. Most services are located either 0.7 mi east from the Paradise Dr. Trail access parking area or along Washington St. (STH-33/144). For area info contact the West Bend Area Chamber of Commerce (262-338-2666, wbachamber.org).

![A photograph of the historic West Bend Theatre in downtown West Bend, Wisconsin. The theatre features a prominent vertical marquee spelling out "WEST BEND" in green neon letters. The marquee below lists "POLTERGEIST SAT 7PM SUN 12PM" and "OCTOBER MOVIES DEEP RED ZOMBIES X2 RE-ANIMATOR & NIGHT OF THE LIVING DEAD". In front of the theatre are flower planters and landscaping.](page_357_image_1_v2.jpg)
CAROLYN PRAHL.

West Bend Segment: A charming downtown view in an Ice Age Trail Community.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Kewaskum Segment (Atlas Map 86f)

**Summary:** The Kewaskum Segment: The segment begins by following the hummocky terrain of the Green Bay Lobe side of the Kettle Moraine.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Kewaskum Segment mileage and route summary icons] **Kewaskum Segment—5.5 miles (3.2 IAT, 2.3 CR):**
CTH-D to Eisenbahn State Trail.

*   [Icon: No water icon] No reliable sources of water.
*   [Icon: Camping icon] At nearby private campground.
*   [Icon: Dispersed Camping Area icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area DCA located on the Otten Preserve.
*   [Icon: Hunting season closure icon] Portion of segment crossing private land between Ridge Rd. and STH-45 is closed during gun deer season.
*   [Icon: Leashed dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Connecting route icon] Segment includes connecting road walk. Portion overlaps Friendly Dr., Prospect Dr., and briefly overlaps the multiuse Eisenbahn State Trail.

A short white-blazed loop Trail between Wildwood Rd. and Friendly Dr., loop trails at the Otten Preserve, a blue-blazed spur trail to a DCA, and the Eisenbahn State Trail extends north and south from the segment.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Parking access 1 icon] **① CTH-D Trail Access (43.45550, -88.20845):** From the West Bend area on USH-45 north, take Exit 73 and follow CTH-D west for 0.1 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

[Icon: Parking access 2 icon] **② Eisenbahn State Trail Access (43.49533, -88.21464):** From Kewaskum at the intersection of USH-45 and STH-28, take USH-45 south 1.8 mi. At the stoplight for CTH-H turn left and go east. The Eisenbahn State Trail intersects CTH-H immediately. The segment endpoint is 0.1 mi south on the Eisenbahn State Trail. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the McDonald’s lot on the northwest corner of USH-45 and CTH-H.

**Additional parking:** (i) Friendly Dr. roadside parking. (ii) Wildwood Rd. (iii) Ridge Rd. (iv) Sunburst Ski Area parking area on Prospect Dr.

### THE HIKE

The segment begins by following the hummocky terrain of the Green Bay Lobe side of the Kettle Moraine. Along the way, it highlights beech-maple woods filled with spring and summer wildflowers. Next, it transitions to a connecting route before traversing the 125-acre Alliance-owned Otten Preserve. You can see Kewaskum and Campbellsport to the north and the Dundee Kame and Kettle Moraine State Forest to the northeast. Keep an eye out for meadowlarks, bobolinks, turkeys, and several birds of prey. Gorgeous spring trillium displays and jack-in-the-pulpit fill the woods, along with summertime gooseberry and raspberry patches.

The segment also travels near the Sunburst Ski Hill area. The high drumlins of the Green Bay Lobe form most of the skyline to the west. Finally, it briefly follows the Eisenbahn State Trail, a state-owned multiuse recreation trail on the former C&NW Railroad right-of-way. Twelve of the 24 miles of the former rail corridor are maintained, extending from West Bend to Eden in Fond du Lac County. “Eisenbahn” is German for “iron road,” harkening back to this rail line’s heritage and original construction in 1871.

From the Trail access on CTH-D, head northeast along Friendly Drive. Depart Friendly Drive and head west, following a power line right-of-way over rolling hills. A boardwalk built by Alliance volunteers offers dry passage across a marshy area. Climb out of this low area and briefly leave the right-of-way to enter beech-maple woods and connect with a short white-blazed

loop trail. Continue west to a footbridge over a small ephemeral stream before reaching Wildwood Road.

Turn right and begin a 2.2-mile connecting route: first north on Wildwood Road, then left on Town Hall Road, and finally left on Ridge Road. At the Trail access on Ridge Road, head north into the Alliance’s Otten Preserve. Soon you’ll meet a spur trail heading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

![Map of the Milwaukee River Segment showing the trail route, connecting routes, and local landmarks like Sunburst Ski Hill and Otten Preserve.](page_359_image_1_v2.jpg)

After passing through the Preserve, continue east adjacent to the Sunburst Ski Hill property (covering a prominent kame). At Prospect Drive, turn left and follow the road north. Depart Prospect Drive east along a field edge, cross a series of boardwalks, and then skirt the southern edge of a local gravel and landscape business’s storage area.

Pass under USH-45 via an underpass built to accommodate the Ice Age Trail. *Note: Heavy rain may swamp the tunnel. Therefore, as conditions warrant, turn left and hike north 0.1 miles along USH-45 to its intersection with CTH-H, also signed as Badger Road. Cross USH-45 at the stoplight, turn right onto the Eisenbahn State Trail, and hike south 0.1 miles to where the Trail heads east.* East of USH-45, meet the Eisenbahn State Trail and head north briefly to the segment’s end point.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **West Bend:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical services. On Trail. Most services are located either 0.7 mi east from the Paradise Dr. Trail access parking area or along Washington St. (STH-33/144). For area info contact the West Bend Area Chamber of Commerce (262-338-2666, wbachamber.org). From the USH-45 Trail access go south ~6 mi.

**Sunburst Skil Hill**: Restaurant. On Trail. Open during winter (8355 Prospect Dr., Kewaskum, 262-626-8404, skisunburst.com).

**USH-45 Local Area**: Restaurant, convenience store. At USH-45 Trail crossing go north 0.2 mi.

**Kewaskum**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the USH-45 Trail access go north 1.8 mi on USH-45. Most services on USH-45. Area info available from the Kewaskum Area Chamber of Commerce (262-626-3336, kewaskum.org).

BRIAN JENSEN

![A scenic view of the Milwaukee River Segment of the Ice Age Trail during autumn. A dirt path leads through a grassy area towards a dense forest of trees with vibrant yellow and green foliage. In the background, rolling hills are visible under a blue sky filled with white, wispy clouds.](page_360_image_1_v2.jpg)

*Milwaukee River Segment: Fall hues set in along the Trail.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::washington-cou.parent_text:
## Milwaukee River Segment (Washington County) (Atlas Map 86f, 87f)

**Summary:** The Milwaukee River Segment (Washington County): Most of this segment crosses the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU).

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: segment icon] **Milwaukee River Segment (Washington County)—6.9 miles:**
Eisenbahn State Trail to Kettle Moraine Dr.

| [Icon: water pump icon] | From a hand-pump water well near Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Backpack Shelter 1 and at the nearby KMSF-NU New Fane Trails parking area. | [Icon: toilet icon]     | At KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 1 (please respect those who have reserved the shelter) and nearby KMSF-NU New Fane Trails parking area. |
| ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Icon: water drop icon] | From the Milwaukee River.                                                                                                                                       | [Icon: ColdCache icon]  | One ColdCache site on segment.                                                                                                      |
| [Icon: tent icon]       | At KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 1 (reservations required).                                                                                                          | [Icon: dog icon]        | Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.                                                                     |
| [Icon: parking icon]    | At nearby KMSF-NU New Fane Trails parking area.                                                                                                                 | [Icon: roadwalk icon]   | Segment includes a brief connecting route roadwalk.                                                                                 |
|                                           |                                                                                                                                                                 | [Icon: spur trail icon] | Spur trail to KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 1.                                                                                           |

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon] **Eisenbahn State Trail Access (43.49533, -88.21464):** From Kewaskum at the intersection of USH-45 and STH-28, take USH-45 south 1.8 mi. At the stoplight for CTH-H turn left and go east. The Eisenbahn State Trail intersects CTH-H immediately. The segment endpoint is 0.1 mi south on the Eisenbahn State Trail. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the McDonald’s lot on the northwest corner of USH-45 and CTH-H.

[Icon: 2 icon] **Kettle Moraine Dr. Trail Access (43.54587, -88.18053):** From Kewaskum at the intersection of STH-28 and USH-45, take STH-28 east 2.5 mi. At Kettle Moraine Dr. turn left and go north 2.0 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the New Fane Trails parking area on County Line Dr. A 0.5-mi spur trail leads from the northwest corner of the parking area to the IAT.

**Additional Parking:** (i) CTH-H. (ii) KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 1 parking area on CTH-H. (iii) STH-28. (iv) East Moraine Dr.

### THE HIKE

Most of this segment crosses the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU). The Kettle Moraine landscape dates back to the last Ice Age when the Green Bay Lobe and the Lake Michigan Lobe flowed into the area. As these lobes sideswiped each other and retreated, they created the ruggedly beautiful high-relief hummocky topography making up today’s landscape. It features numerous ridges, valleys, outwash plains, kettles, eskers, and kames.

The segment’s name recognizes the nearby Milwaukee River, which it crosses early before heading into the KMSF-NU. The river, approximately 100 miles long, stretches from Fond du Lac County to the Port of Milwaukee. Several tributaries converge about 60 miles northwest of Milwaukee to form the river near Campbellsport. Its zigzagging course eventually cuts through downtown Milwaukee.

From the Trail access at the Eisenbahn State Trail, head east, then south, skirting a wetland mitigation site. Cross a boardwalk, cut through woods, follow the edge of a field, and then enter a wooded area. Meander through the

forest, crossing a wet spot on a curving boardwalk. Travel below and alongside a ridge, gradually climbing it until you reach an open field offering long views of the surrounding fields and forested hills.

Cross Sandy Ridge Road and head north through another open field and up the side of a hill. Drop down into woods and course through a hummocky

area, passing grassy depressions and wetland openings as you enter the KMSF-NU and cross several farm fields.

At CTH-H, turn east and cross the Milwaukee River on the bridge using the multiuse path separate from traffic. Immediately after crossing the bridge, cross CTH-H and head north on Oak Drive until the Trail leads off-road to the east into the dense forest and hummocky terrain of the KMSF-NU. Cross the KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 1 parking area, then come to a trailside hand-pump water well on the left. Shortly after, meet a short spur trail leading to Backpack Shelter 1. The shelter, a lofty perch for enjoying the view, is dedicated to Raymond T. Zillmer. He envisioned the Kettle Moraine

![Map of the Milwaukee River Segment (Fond du Lac County) of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail route through the Kettle Moraine State Forest - Northern Unit, including landmarks like Backpack Shelter 1, the Milwaukee River, and various roads.](page_362_image_1_v2.jpg)

State Forest forming the nucleus for a much larger linear park that today is the Ice Age Trail.

Continue northward through the high-relief hummocky topography. The route highlights many trailside kettles and kames in a beautiful blend of deep

forest and sunny meadows. Cross STH-28, East Moraine Drive, and County Line Drive before reaching the segment’s terminus at Kettle Moraine Drive, just north of the Washington/Fond du Lac county line.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Trail Community logo] **West Bend:** See the West Bend Segment, p. 323. From the USH-45 Trail access go south ~6 mi.

**USH-45 Local Area:** Restaurant, convenience store. At USH-45 Trail crossing go north 0.2 mi.
**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Backpack Shelter 1:** Camping. On Trail. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Only one group (up to 10) per shelter per night is allowed.

**Kewaskum:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From the STH-28 Trail access go west 2.0 mi on STH-28. Most services on USH-45.

![Four hikers standing in a forest with yellow autumn leaves.](page_363_image_1_v2.jpg)
XIPING WANG

*Milwaukee River Segment: Hikers immersed in a blaze-yellow-colored woods.*

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Fond du Lac and Sheboygan Counties, highlighting the Milwaukee River (Fond du Lac County) Segment within the Kettle Moraine State Forest - Northern Unit. The map includes a legend for the Ice Age Trail, Connecting Route, Other Trail, County Boundary, and Public or IATA Land, along with a scale in miles. Key locations shown include Campbellsport, Dundee, and Greenbush.](page_364_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.parent_text:
# Fond du Lac & Sheboygan Counties

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Fond du Lac and Sheboygan Counties, showing the LaBudde Creek Segment, Greenbush Segment, and Parnell Segment. The map includes local roads, towns like Elkhart Lake, Glenbeulah, Plymouth, and Sheboygan Falls, and a locator map of Wisconsin.](page_365_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Fond du Lac & Sheboygan Counties

Atlas Maps 87f–91f; Databook pages 90–92
Total miles: 38.8 (Trail 30.3; Connecting Route 8.5)

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting the location of Fond du Lac and Sheboygan counties.](page_366_image_1_v2.jpg)

The Interlobate Moraine (known as the Kettle Moraine) formed when tongue-shaped glacial lobes, Green Bay and Lake Michigan, met and formed a valley on the ice surface.

South-flowing meltwater cascaded into crevasses and carried sand, gravel, and erratics, leaving a 120-mile-long series of kettles and ridges. The last remaining ice blocks eventually melted, creating kettles, many of which filled with water resulting in kettle lakes. The Ice Age Trail meanders within the Kettle Moraine topography through Fond du Lac and Sheboygan counties. Large and small wetlands intersperse the many forested uplands.

The Ice Age Trail in the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) highlights the formation of glacial features such as kettles, kames, and eskers. This State Forest unit contains 29,000 acres of rolling, wooded hills dotted with serene lakes. It offers year-round nature programs and hiking, biking, and equestrian and cross-country ski trails.

This area inspired Ray Zillmer to conceive of the Ice Age Trail. During the mid-1950s, he pursued government officials at all levels to recognize, preserve, and establish a 1,000-mile national park, featuring the glacial landscape that tells the story of the Ice Age.

. . .

Developed campgrounds and backpack shelters are available for camping along the Trail in the KMSF-NU. The LaBudde Creek Segment has a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA).

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Lakeshore Chapter was formed from the merger of the Door/Kewaunee County, Manitowoc County, and North Kettle Moraine chapters of the Alliance. Chapter members actively promote the Ice Age Trail in their region by hosting guided hikes along various segments. The Chapter also organizes many Trail improvement events along the segments in their care.

Hikers can become members of the “Hall of Kamers” by hiking the Northern Unit of the Kettle Moraine State Forest Ice Age Trail segments. Start your Hall of Kamers adventure by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/lakeshore-chapter to learn more about the chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Destination Lake Winnebago Region**: 920-923-3010, fdl.com

**Sheboygan County Chamber**: 920-457-9491, sheboygan.org

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU)**: 262-626-2116, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/kmn

![Milwaukee River Segment: An abundance of wildflowers spread across the landscape.](page_367_image_1_v2.jpg)

STEVE HERALY

*Milwaukee River Segment: An abundance of wildflowers spread across the landscape.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.h2: Milwaukee River Segment (Fond du Lac County) (Atlas Map 87f)
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.segment_name: Milwaukee River Segment (Fond du Lac County)
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.parent_text:
## Milwaukee River Segment (Fond du Lac County) (Atlas Map 87f)

**Summary:** The Milwaukee River Segment (Fond du Lac County) is a 4.3-mile trail in Fond du Lac & Sheboygan Counties (Atlas Map 87f), running from Kettle Moraine Dr. to Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Mauthe Lake Recreation Area. This segment parallels the East Branch of the Milwaukee River as it follows an irregular hummocky sand and gravel ridge – the Lake Michigan Lobe side of the Kettle Moraine.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 3 2L]
**4.3 miles**: Kettle Moraine Dr. to Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Mauthe Lake Recreation Area

[Icon: Water and picnic icons]
At the KMSF-NU New Fane Trails and Mauthe Lake Recreation Area.

[Icon: People icon]
At KMSF-NU New Fane Trails, Mauthe Lake Recreation Area and Backpack Shelter 2 (please respect those who have reserved the shelter).

[Icon: Backpacking icon]
At KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 2 [Icon: Shelter icon] (reservations required).

[Icon: Dog icon]
Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Car and tent icons]
At KMSF-NU Mauthe Lake Recreation Area [Icon: Orange triangle icon].

[Icon: Dotted line icon]
Portion briefly overlaps a snowmobile trail.

[Icon: Crossed arrows icon]
Spur trail to the KMSF-NU New Fane Trails parking area and spur trail to KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 2.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1]
**① Kettle Moraine Dr. Trail Access (43.54587, -88.18053)**: From Kewaskum at the intersection of STH-28 and USH-45, take STH-28 east 2.5 mi. At Kettle Moraine Dr. turn left and go north 2.0 mi. **No Parking**. Instead, park at the New Fane Trails parking area on County Line Dr. A 0.5-mi spur trail leads from the northwest corner of the parking area to the IAT.

[Icon: 2]
**② Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Mauthe Lake Recreation Area Trail Access (43.59388, -88.17642)**: From Kewaskum at the intersection of STH-28 and USH-45, take STH-28 east 0.4 mi. At CTH-S turn left and follow it through several turns for 6.1 mi. At CTH-GGG turn left and go north 1.0 mi. Turn left into Mauthe Lake Recreation Area and go west 0.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access near entrance station.

**Additional Parking**: Gross Rd. (unsigned) north of CTH-S.

### THE HIKE

This segment parallels the East Branch of the Milwaukee River as it follows an irregular hummocky sand and gravel ridge – the Lake Michigan Lobe side of the Kettle Moraine. It travels through the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU), offering quiet passage through dense forest. Ridges and hilltop clearings provide distant views. Opportunities abound for observing various wildlife, including birds, plants, and woodland flowers.

From the Trail access – where the Ice Age Trail crosses Kettle Moraine Drive about a quarter mile north of the Fond du Lac/Washington county line – head north. Soon, after passing over rolling hills and prairie grasses, you’ll meet a spur trail on the right. It leads to the KMSF-NU New Fane Trails, which make a loop, and a parking area 0.5 miles away.

From the New Fane Trails spur, continue through hardwood forests with some prairie clearings containing an abundance of wildflowers. Just before reaching CTH-DD, parallel a well-worn bike path.

After crossing CTH-DD, climb uphill and soon reach a clearing. Enjoy views of kames to the west from a bench. Continue on a forested hummocky ridge and

cross a snowmobile trail twice; at the second crossing, the Ice Age Trail and the snowmobile trail briefly overlap. A short distance farther, you’ll see a path to the snowmobile trail, which runs parallel to the Trail. Pay close attention to blazes to avoid wandering off the Trail.

Soon after crossing CTH-S, you’ll meet the southern end of a loop trail leading left to KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 2. Continue through hummocky, forested topography and meet the northern end of the loop trail.

Descend to cross wetland areas that can be muddy during rainy periods. After crossing a bridge over a small stream, follow a wide, grassy tread before reaching the segment’s terminus at the Mauthe Lake Recreation Area entrance road, just west of the Recreation Area entrance station. Before the terminus, the Trail passes a milkweed field; be on the lookout for monarch butterflies.

![Map of the Milwaukee River Segment and surrounding area, showing Mauthe Lake Recreation Area, KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 2, and trail connections.](page_369_image_1_v2.jpg)

### AREA SERVICES

**Kewaskum:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. From Kettle Moraine Dr. Trail access go south and west 4.5 mi or from Mauthe Lake Recreation Area go south and west ~8 mi.

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Backpack Shelter 2:** Camping. On Trail. Reservations required: (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Only one group (up to 10) per shelter per night is allowed. No water.

**KMSF-NU Mauthe Lake Recreation Area:** Camping. On Trail (262-626-4305; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

**KMSF-NU Long Lake Recreation Area:** Camping. From the Mauthe Lake Recreation Area go north and west ~8 mi.

![A hiker with a red backpack walks along a narrow, wooded ridgetop trail covered in fallen leaves.](page_370_image_1_v2.jpg)

AMY MELLOR

*Parnell Segment: A ridgetop view along the Parnell Esker.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::fond-du-lac-sh.parent_text:
## Parnell Segment (Atlas Maps 87f, 88f, 89f)

**Summary:** The Parnell Segment is a 13.9-mile trail in Fond du Lac & Sheboygan Counties (Atlas Map 87f, 88f, 89f), running from Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Mauthe Lake Recreation Area to STH-67. This long segment highlights remarkable glacial formations, including the world-famous Parnell Esker along the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage icons] **13.9 miles**: Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Mauthe Lake Recreation Area to STH-67

* [Icon: water tap icon] At the KMSF-NU Mauthe Lake Recreation Area, Butler Lake, the Parnell Observation Tower and nearby Long Lake Recreational Area.
* [Icon: water drop icon] From numerous lakes and streams.
* [Icon: hiker and tent icon] At KMSF-NU Backpack Shelters 3 and 4 [Icon: tent icon] (reservations required).
* [Icon: car and tent icon] At KMSF-NU Mauthe Lake Recreation Area and nearby Long Lake Recreational Area and private campground on Crooked Lake [Icon: tent icon].
* [Icon: picnic table icon] [Icon: hiker icon] At KMSF-NU Mauthe Lake Recreation Area and nearby Long Lake Recreation Area.
* [Icon: toilet icon] At KMSF-NU Mauthe Lake Recreation Area, Parnell Observation Tower parking area, KMSF-NU Backpack Shelters 3 and 4 (please respect those who have reserved the shelter) and nearby Long Lake Recreational Area.
* [Icon: globe icon] Three ColdCache sites on segment.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: dotted line icon] Spur trail to KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 3, spur trail to Backpack Shelter 4, Butler Lake Trail and Parnell Tower Trail.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: circled 1 icon] **① Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Mauthe Lake Recreation Area Trail Access (43.59388, -88.17642)**: From Kewaskum at the intersection of STH-28 and USH-45, take STH-28 east 0.4 mi. At CTH-S turn left and follow it through several turns for 6.1 mi. At CTH-GGG turn left and go north 1.0 mi. Turn left into Mauthe Lake Recreation Area and go west 0.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access near entrance station.

![circled 2 icon](page_371_image_14_v2.jpg) **② STH-67 Trail Access (43.72412, -88.09702)**: From Plymouth at intersection of STH-23 and STH-67, take STH-67 south then west 8.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) CTH-SS. (ii) Butler Lake Trail parking area on Butler Lake Rd. (iii) Parnell Observation Tower parking area on CTH-U.

### THE HIKE

This long segment highlights remarkable glacial formations, including the world-famous Parnell Esker along the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit. The Trail passes near Mauthe Lake, formerly named Moon Lake, which was protected by the Milwaukee Chapter of the Izaak Walton League in 1926. This initiative was a precursor to developing the Kettle Moraine State Forest and the Ice Age Trail. Ray Zillmer, founder of the Ice Age Trail Alliance, was a longtime member and president of the League.

Another lake featured in this segment is Butler Lake, which lies between two prominent eskers and is part of Butler Lake and Flynn’s Spring State Natural Area (SNA). Fed by nearby Flynn’s Spring, Butler Lake is surrounded on three sides by a bog. Various flora and fauna are present, including rare species such as the unicorn clubtail dragonfly and the swamp spreadwing damselfly.

The route also offers the opportunity to enjoy a bird’s-eye view of the region’s glacial features. From the Parnell Observation tower, there’s a great view of large moulin kames over a mile to the west and the hills on the western horizon are Green Bay Lobe drumlins. The top of this 60-foot wooden observation tower is the highest point of elevation in the

Kettle Moraine State Forest. It offers a panoramic view, and on a clear day one can see about 25 miles.

From the Trail access at the KMSF-NU Mauthe Lake Recreation Area entrance road, just west of the Recreation Area entrance station, head north and skirt the eastern edge of the Mauthe Lake campground. Be careful – it’s easy to get confused by the frequent side trails connecting the campground to the Ice Age Trail.

After crossing CTH-GGG, pass Forest Lake, with scenic views from the southern shore. Drop down for a couple of stream crossings, climb a hummock, and descend again to follow a small esker’s crest.

Cross CTH-SS and skirt the western side of Crooked Lake Wetlands State Natural Area. This area provides habitat for forest and plant communities and diverse nesting bird and waterfowl populations. Pass west of the three small Kellings Lakes and head into steep terrain with rocky tread, creating uncertain footing. A spur trail leads 0.6 miles west to KMSF-NU Backpack

![Map of the Milwaukee River Segment (Fond du Lac County) of the Ice Age Trail, showing trail route, roads, lakes, and landmarks like Mauthe Lake, Crooked Lake, and the Henry S. Reuss Ice Age Visitor Center.](page_372_image_2_v2.jpg)

Shelter 3. After the spur trail, traverse forests that include maple, birch, hickory, pine, and oak trees.

![Map of the Greenbush Segment showing the Parnell Esker, Butler Lake, and Parnell Observation Tower.](page_373_image_1_v2.jpg)

Cross CTH-F and soon meet the south end of the Butler Lake Trail, then a bridle trail, and then the north end of the Butler Lake Trail as you head toward Butler Lake. Climb to the crest of the four-mile Parnell Esker and follow it through Butler Lake and Flynn’s Spring State Natural Area (SNA). The esker, described on a geologic marker near the Butler Lake Trail parking area, ranges in height from 5 to 30 feet and runs southwest to northeast. (The Butler Lake Trail traverses the SNA and shares portions of the Ice Age Trail route.) At a high point on the esker, a bench overlooks scenic Butler Lake.

From the SNA, travel across an open prairie-like area and continue northeast through beautiful forested terrain going up, down, and around kettles, ridges, and hummocks. The landscape to the west contains a striking and world-famous collection of kames. Hills on the western horizon are drumlins. Occasionally, you may catch glimpses of these features to the left as you hike through the forest on your way to STH-67.

Cross CTH-U and soon meet the southern end of the Parnell Tower Trail on the right. Continue heading north. (For a short side trip, take the Parnell Tower Trail to the east and then north for the opportunity to climb the tower and enjoy the glacier-made landscape.) Just before the northern junction

of the Trail and the Parnell Tower loop trail, a short spur trail on the right leads to the visible KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 4.

Pass through progressively less hummocky forested terrain strewn with rocks and boulders. Cross a snowmobile/bridle trail twice and a power line right-of-way. Pay attention to Trail signage in these areas. Then, follow a wide, grassy path and reach the segment’s terminus in a clearing at STH-67.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Henry S. Reuss Ice Age Visitor Center:** From the Ice Age Trail at CTH-F go 1.5 mi west on CTH-F to STH-67. Continue straight on STH-67 0.7 mi to the Henry S. Reuss Ice Age Visitor Center (920-533-8322, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/kmn/naturecenter).
At the Center hikers can view exhibits to learn about the frozen history of Wisconsin and the gifts of the glacier. There is a great view of the landscape from the Center. Open hours vary depending on day and season. See website for details or call the Center.

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Mauthe Lake Recreation Area:** See Milwaukee River Segment (Fond du Lac County), p. 336.

**CTH-GGG Local Area:** Convenience store. Seasonal. (Pizza but no gasoline pumps).

**KMSF-NU Backpack Shelters 3 and 4:** Camping. On Trail. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Only one group (up to 10) per shelter per night is allowed.

**Dundee:** Restaurant, convenience store. From the CTH-F Trail crossing go 1.5 mi west on CTH-F.

**KMSF-NU Long Lake Recreation Area:** Camping. From the Butler Lake Trail parking area go 1.2 mi west on Butler Lake Rd. (920-533-8612; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

**Plymouth:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, medical service. From the STH-67 Trail access go east and south 8.3 mi.

![A young hiker tries out the hand-pumped well. MICAH LADOUSA](page_374_image_1_v2.jpg)

MICAH LADOUSA

*A young hiker tries out the hand-pumped well.*

## Greenbush Segment (Atlas Map 89f)

**Summary:** The Greenbush Segment is a 8.5-mile trail in Fond du Lac & Sheboygan Counties (Atlas Map 89f), running from STH-67 to CTH-P. This segment highlights the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) as it winds around deep kettles and over hummocks that form the Green Bay Lobe side of the Kettle Moraine.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: distance icons] **8.5 miles**: STH-67 to CTH-P

*Note: A reroute is planned immediately south of STH-23. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.*

| Feature                                                                      | Description                                                                                                                                                              | Feature                                      | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| [Icon: water icon]                                         | From hand-pumped well 0.2 mi north of Kettle Moraine State Forest- Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Backpack Shelter 5, at Greenbush Trails and Old Plank Rd. Trail parking area. | [Icon: dog icon]           | Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.                                                                                                                                                          |
| [Icon: shelter icon]                                       | At KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 5 (reservations required).                                                                                                                   | [Icon: multi-use icon]     | The Old Plank Rd. Trail is open to biking, horseback riding and snowmobiling.                                                                                                                                            |
| [Icon: picnic icon] [Icon: parking icon] | At KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 5 (please respect those who have reserved the shelter), Greenbush Trails/Picnic Area and Old Plank Rd. Trail parking area.                   | [Icon: trail network icon] | KMSF-NU Greenbush Trails network, blue-blazed spur trail to Greenbush and other short spur trails, including a spur trail to KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 5. The Old Plank Rd. Trail extends east and west from the segment. |

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **STH-67 Trail Access (43.72412, -88.09702):** From Plymouth at intersection of STH-23 and STH-67, take STH-67 south then west 8.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

[Icon: 2] **CTH-P Trail Access (43.79691, -88.03511):** From Plymouth at the intersection of STH-23 and STH-67, take STH-23 west 2.0 mi. At CTH-P turn right and go north 2.3 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking**: (i) Greenbush picnic area on Kettle Moraine Dr. (ii) Greenbush Trails on Kettle Moraine Dr. (iii) Old Plank Rd. Trail parking area on Plank Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights the Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) as it winds around deep kettles and over hummocks that form the Green Bay Lobe side of the Kettle Moraine. It also features the Kettle Moraine Red Oaks State Natural Area, a mature second-growth southern dry-mesic forest originating in 1889. Composed of red oak, basswood, sugar maple, and white ash, it is one of the richest oak forests in the region. It contains more than 100 species of trees, shrubs, and herbs, with its understory attracting rare birds such as the Acadian flycatcher, red-shouldered hawk, and cerulean warbler. And various plant species thrive in this shady area, such as ferns, orchids, trillium, and mayapples. This hardwood forest, as currently preserved, will evolve into an old-growth forest.

The segment passes 1.6 miles east of Greenbush, home of the Wade House Historic Site (see Points of Interest). The Trail also briefly shares a short piece of the Old Plank Road Trail, a 19th-century historic stagecoach route linking Sheboygan and Fond du Lac.

From the Trail access on STH-67, head north. Initially, you’ll travel across relatively flat outwash before ascending hummocky terrain. As the Trail approaches the KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 5, you’ll have the opportunity for a side trip. A sign points to a brief hike to the east takes you to the Greenbush Kettle, one of the most symmetrical deep depressions in the area.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail showing the Greenbush Segment and connections to LaBudde Creek and Parnell Segments. The map includes landmarks such as Glenbeulah, Wade House, Kettle Moraine State Forest–Northern Unit, and various trail features like shelters, picnic areas, and parking.](page_376_image_1_v2.jpg)

Just north of the kettle is a spur trail heading east to KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 5. You’ll also pass a hand-pumped well and cross a picnic area as you travel north toward Kettle Moraine Drive.

Before you reach Kettle Moraine Drive and just before the Greenbush Outdoor Group Camp, a short unsigned spur trail leads left to the campground’s parking area. This area is a jumping-off point for the Greenbush Trails, a system of loops for mountain biking, hiking, and cross-country skiing. The deep forest near the Greenbush Trails is an excellent place to see the hooded warbler, a rare migratory bird.

Cross Kettle Moraine Drive, continuing along hummocky topography. After you cross CTH-A, the Trail enters the Kettle Moraine Red Oaks State Natural Area (described above). As you pass through the woods, you’ll meet a blue-blazed spur trail to the left leading 1.6 miles to the village of Greenbush.

Just south of STH-23, you’ll cross a snowmobile/equestrian trail and soon arrive at Old Plank Road Trail, a paved and partially grass-covered path paralleling STH-23. Follow it 0.3 miles east. It is. Depart this path at the point where the Trail crosses underneath STH-23.

Follow a roller coaster serpentine course, ascending, descending, and curving around kettles and ridges. Finally, you’ll cross quiet Ridge Road and CTH-S and head northeast toward the segment’s terminus at the Trail access kiosk and the parking area at CTH-P.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Wade House:** In Greenbush south of STH-23 and accessible from the Ice Age Trail via a 1.6-mi spur trail (W7965 WI-23, Greenbush, 920-526-3271, wadehouse.wisconsinhistory.org).

The Wade House, an 1860s New England–style stagecoach inn, served travelers of the Civil War era. From the Wade House, a short walk will take you to the Wesley W. Jung Carriage Museum, which houses the state’s largest collection of fine carriages and pioneer wagons. Also on site is the reconstructed historic Herrling Sawmill. The working water-powered mill portrays a vital component of the 19th century frontier settlement. There is a fee. Hours vary by season.

### AREA SERVICES

**Kettle Moraine State Forest-Northern Unit (KMSF-NU) Long Lake Recreation Area:** See Parnell Segment, p. 339. From the STH-67 Trail access go west and south 7.0 mi.

**Plymouth:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, medical service. From the STH-67 Trail access go 8.3 mi east then north on STH-67. Or from the STH-23 Trail access go east ~4 mi on STH-23 and CTH-C. Area info available from the Plymouth Chamber of Commerce (888-693-8263, plymouthwisconsin.com).

**KMSF-NU Backpack Shelter 5:** Camping. On Trail. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). Only one group (up to 10) per shelter per night is allowed.

**KMSF-NU Greenbush Outdoor Group Camp:** Camping (group camping only). Within the Greenbush Trails Area on Kettle Moraine Dr. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com).

**Glenbeulah:** Restaurant, convenience store, camping. From CTH-P Trail access go 0.5 mi west on CTH-P.

**Elkhart Lake:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the CTH-P Trail access go west and north 4.0 mi.

## LaBudde Creek Segment (Atlas Map 90f)

**Summary:** The LaBudde Creek Segment: This segment features a wetland complex, undulating grassland, and forest upland as it highlights the 426-acre LaBudde Creek State Fishery Area.

### SNAPSHOT

4.9 miles (3.7 IAT, 1.3 CR): Garton Rd. to CTH-MM

[Icon: water icon]
From LaBudde Creek.
[Icon: globe icon]
One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: DCA icon]
At a Dispersed Camping Area DCA south of CTH-MM.
[Icon: dog icon]
Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: tent icon]
At nearby Plymouth Rock and Broughton Sheboygan Marsh campgrounds ▲ (see Area Services).
[Icon: road icon]
Portions overlap roads.

[Icon: picnic icon]
At nearby Fireman’s Park and Beach in Elkhart Lake.
[Icon: trail icon]
Side trail to LaBudde Creek Channel, a white-blazed loop trail, and a blue-blazed spur trail leading to a DCA.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1 icon]
① **Garton Rd. Trail Access** (43.81873, -87.99813): From Elkhart Lake at the intersection of STH-67 and CTH-A, take STH-67 south 1.3 mi. At Garton Rd, turn left and go east 0.1 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Garton Rd. parking area 0.3 mi east on Garton Rd.

[Icon: 2 icon]
② **CTH-MM Trail Access** (43.86210, -87.97626): From Elkhart Lake at the intersection of STH-67 and CTH-A, take CTH-A east 1.6 mi. At Little Elkhart Lake Rd., turn left and go north 2.0 mi. At CTH-MM turn right and go east 0.3 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Ridge Creek Rd. parking area.

**Additional Parking:** (i) CTH-A. (ii) Keystone Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment features a wetland complex, undulating grassland, and forest upland as it highlights the 426-acre LaBudde Creek State Fishery Area. This property is home to several species of game birds, including ruffed grouse, ring-necked pheasant, and woodcock, as well as a variety of songbirds such as yellow-throated and yellow warblers, catbirds, woodpeckers, and brown thrashers.

The route offers a side trip opportunity just north of the Garton Road parking area, where a path leads to the LaBudde Channel. This glacial meltwater channel formed along the eastern edge of the Kettle Moraine and the western edge of the Lake Michigan Lobe.

From the Trail access at Garton Road, head east and north through scenic upland brush and grasslands/wetlands with cedars and colorful sumacs. Cross a short boardwalk and then a longer one before entering a forest containing aspen, pine, and cedar trees, followed by mature hardwoods.

*At Badger Road, follow a connecting route: Take Badger Road 0.3 miles east, then turn north and follow Little Elkhart Lake Road for 0.3 miles.*

Then the Trail heads west and into the woods. A gradual downhill brings you to the intersection of Little Elkhart Lake Road and CTH-A. Follow CTH-A east for 0.1 miles and turn north, heading northeast to cross an open grassland. Next, skirt the edge of a mature hardwood forest and cut through more upland brush.

At Keystone Road, follow the road west 0.2 miles, then turn north, heading through a wetland and along a row of pine trees with views of LaBudde Creek to the east.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail segment showing LaBudde Creek State Fishery Area, Ridge Creek Rd, and connections to Walla Hi and Greenbush segments.](page_379_image_1_v2.jpg)

At CTH-FF, follow a connecting route: Take CTH-FF east for 0.2 miles and turn north to follow Ridge Creek Road for 0.5 miles.

The Trail resumes as it heads through a prairie, quickly meeting the south end of a white-blazed trail. Continuing ahead, you’ll cut into a patch of woods, pass an old fieldstone wall, and curve to the north through more woods before meeting the north end of the white-blazed trail.

Then the Trail turns sharply to the right and heads north. Cross LaBudde Creek and meet a short blue-blazed spur trail leading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Continuing, head north through mostly fields and prairies to the segment’s terminus on CTH-MM.

### AREA SERVICES

**Private campground**: From the Garton Rd. at STH-67 Trail access go 1.6 mi south on STH-67, then left on Lando St. for 0.1 mi.

**Elkhart Lake**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library. From the Garton Rd. at STH-67 Trail access go 1.5 mi north on STH-67. Area info available from the Elkhart Lake Chamber of Commerce (920-876-2922).

**Broughton Sheboygan Marsh County Park**: Restaurant, camping. From the Garton Rd. at STH-67 Trail access go 1.3 mi north on STH-67. At CTH-J turn left and go 1.8 mi northwest to CTH-SR. Park entrance is on the left (920-876-2535, county vendor website: www.threeguysandagrill.com/pages/our-marsh-campground).

**Plymouth:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, medical service. From the Garton Rd. at STH-67 Trail access go south ~6 mi.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Manitowoc County, showing segments including Tisch Mills, East Twin River, Mishicot, Point Beach, Two Rivers, Dunes, and Manitowoc. The map includes a locator map of Wisconsin, a north arrow, and various highway markers (43, 10, 147, 42, V, B, BB).](page_380_image_1_v2.jpg)

![Map of Manitowoc County showing the Ice Age Trail. The Walla Hi Segment is shown in the southwest, near Walla Hi County Park and Highway 57/32. A connecting route (unmarked) leads north through Valders and Lower Cato Falls County Park. The Manitowoc Segment is shown in the northeast, ending at the city of Manitowoc on Lake Michigan. Major roads shown include I-43, US-151, and US-10. A legend indicates Ice Age Trail Community, Ice Age Trail, Connecting Route (unmarked), Other Trail, County Boundary, and Public or IATA Land. A scale bar shows 0 to 5 miles.](page_381_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Manitowoc County

*Atlas Maps 91f–98f; Databook pages 94–100*
*Total miles: 72.9 (Trail 31.7; Connecting Route 41.3)*

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting the location of Manitowoc County and the Ice Age Trail segments within it.](page_382_image_2_v2.jpg)

The Green Bay Lobe and the Lake Michigan Lobe covered Manitowoc County during the Wisconsin Glaciation. The Niagara Escarpment divided the two lobes. In the county’s southwestern corner lies the Kettle Moraine, with its hummocky terrain. Drumlins near Valders have a north-south trend, indicating the direction in which the ice sheets flowed. The soils here are reddish brown and rich in silt and clay from the Lake Michigan Lobe advancements.

In the northern part of the county, the East Twin River valley occupies a channel that formed along the ice margin. The West Twin River is part of an early drainage outlet of Glacial Lake Oshkosh. Shoreline remnants of ancient Lake Nipissing, a higher-level postglacial ancestor of Lake Michigan existing 5,000 years ago, can be seen along the Point Beach Segment.

Glacial debris formed the eskers, erratics, kettles, and drumlins scattered across the county. Exposed dolomite, its soil removed by scraping glaciers, dots the countryside. A few miles east of the Trail is Two Creeks Buried Forest, a unit of the Ice Age National Scientific Reserve preserving a 14,000-year-old spruce forest knocked down by the glacier.

The name Manitowoc is derived from the Ojibwa word meaning “place of the good spirit.” The area was explored in the 1670s by French trappers, in 1795 the Northwest Fur Company established a trading post on the site, and by 1836, white settlers had established a permanent settlement. The area soon developed as a lumbering, shipping, and shipbuilding center. By the late 19th century, dairying and manufacturing also fueled the economy.

The county is considered a premier birding area because of its position on the lakeshore migratory bird route.

The cities of Manitowoc and Two Rivers are an Ice Age Trail Community, highlighting their rich maritime histories and providing an opportunity to walk along the shore of Lake Michigan.

. . .

The East Twin River and Tisch Mills segments host Dispersed Camping Areas (DCAs) for long-distance, multiday hikers.

**ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION**

The Lakeshore Chapter formed from the merger of the Door/Kewaunee County, Manitowoc County, and North Kettle Moraine chapters of the Alliance.

Chapter members actively promote the Ice Age Trail in their region by hosting guided hikes along various segments. The Chapter also organizes many Trail improvement events along the segments in their care.

Hikers can become members of the "Hall of Kamers" by hiking the Northern Unit of the Kettle Moraine State Forest Ice Age Trail segments. Start your Hall of Kamers adventure by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/lakeshore-chapter to learn more about the chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Manitowoc Area Visitor and Convention Bureau**: 920-686-3070, manitowoc.info

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Manitowoc**: visitmanitowoc.com

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Two Rivers**: two-rivers.org

![Walla Hi Segment: Glorious fall colors saturate the Trail.](page_383_image_1_v2.jpg)
CHERYL BARARETT

Walla Hi Segment: Glorious fall colors saturate the Trail.

Manitowoc County | 351

## Walla Hi Segment (Atlas Map 91f)

**Summary:** The Walla Hi Segment is a 2.3-mile trail in Manitowoc County (Atlas Map 91f), running from Lax Chapel Rd. to Mueller Rd. at S. Cedar Lake Rd.. This segment travels along an easement and through the 160-acre Walla Hi County Park, showcasing the best glacial landscape features: kettles, moraines, and erratics.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage icon] **2.3 miles**: Lax Chapel Rd. to Mueller Rd. at S. Cedar Lake Rd.

![water icon](page_384_image_11_v2.jpg) From Lake Michigan and Molash Creek.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: picnic icon] At Walla Hi County Park.

[Icon: overlapping trails icon] Portions overlap Walla Hi hiking trails and small portions overlap equestrian trails and Mueller Rd.

[Icon: camping icon] [Icon: HA icon] At nearby Manitowoc County Expo Center grounds (see Area Services).

[Icon: trail network icon] Walla Hi County Park hiking and equestrian trail network.

[Icon: wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **Lax Chapel Rd. Trail Access (43.90268, -87.96949)**: From Elkhart Lake at the intersection of STH-67 and CTH-A, take STH-67 north 5.5 mi. At STH-32/57 turn right and go east 2.6 mi. At Lax Chapel Rd. turn left and go north 0.4 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Walla Hi County Park on Mueller Rd.

[Icon: 2] **Mueller Rd. at S. Cedar Lake Rd. Trail Access (43.90639, -87.94223)**: From Elkhart Lake at the intersection of STH-67 and CTH-A, take STH-67 north 5.5 mi. At STH-32/57 turn right and go east (stay on STH-32) 4.2 mi. At S. Cedar Lake Rd., turn left and go north 1.0 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at Walla Hi County Park main parking area 0.6 mi west and south on Mueller Rd.

### THE HIKE

This segment travels along an easement and through the 160-acre Walla Hi County Park, showcasing the best glacial landscape features: kettles, moraines, and erratics. It packs in beauty and challenge over a short distance as the Trail navigates almost 200 feet in elevation change. It travels hummocky, high-relief topography through land heavily forested with oak and beech trees. In places, the ground cover is thick and diverse, with wildflowers and large moss-covered erratics.

From the Trail access on Lax Chapel Road, head east along an easement overlooking farm fields. To the south of the Trail is a sand and gravel pit, and you may hear equipment operating during work hours. Soon you’ll reach a magnificent handcrafted stone staircase built by Crew volunteers. Hike 80 feet down “Slab Hill” as you head toward Walla Hi County Park.

Shortly after the stairs, take a serpentine trek up a wooded hillside and enter Walla Hi County Park. You’ll almost immediately meet a horse trail after reaching the top of the hill, quickly followed by a second. The park contains several hiking and horse trails, but the intersections are well marked with signs, yellow blazes, and park maps.

Soon, on your left, you’ll meet the western end of a white-blazed loop trail that features other parts of the park and provides an alternate route to the park’s main parking area. From the junction with the white-blazed trail, traverse the oak and beech forest to reach a high point. Enjoy the woods’ stillness while

resting on a nearby bench. Upon descending the rocky terrain, you’ll meet the eastern end of the white-blazed loop trail.

Cross a small clear stream on a covered bridge, another gem in the delightful landscape. After the bridge, a wide grassy area leads 200 yards to the Walla Hi County Park main parking area.

![Map of the Walla Hi Segment area showing trail path, mileage, and nearby roads like Mueller Rd and S. Cedar Lake Rd.](page_385_image_1_v2.jpg)

Continuing north from the grassy area, progress through a more open area, then return to the woods. Briefly share a park service road as you head toward Mueller Road near the Walla Hi County Park entrance sign. Continue on Mueller Road east 0.3 miles to the segment’s terminus at South Cedar Lake Road.

### AREA SERVICES

Kiel: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, medical service. From Walla Hi County Park go east on Mueller Rd. 0.3 mi, south on S. Cedar Lake Rd. 1.0 mi and west on STH-32 (becomes STH-32/57) 4.6 mi. Restaurant on Lax Chapel Rd south of STH-32/57.

Elkhart Lake: See LaBudde Creek Segment, p. 346. From Walla Hi County Park go east, south, west and south ~12 mi.

> I am extremely grateful to the Trail for pushing my boundaries. It’s easy to hike only where and when I’m comfortable, but the Trail pushed me beyond that. For example, it gave me the experience of camping alone. To start out it was out of necessity to save money. I was terrified, but since then I have camped by myself many times. Now, I love the time alone and being able to enjoy the quiet, the night sky, or a book and a fire before setting out to hike again the next morning.

SARAH DRISCOLL, THOUSAND-MILER

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.parent_text:
## Manitowoc Segment (Atlas Map 95f)

**Summary:** The Manitowoc Segment: This segment highlights Manitowoc, an Ice Age Trail Community filled with lots to see and do.

### SNAPSHOT

![Snapshot icons](page_386_image_8_v2.jpg)

**2 1 7.5 miles**: Rapids Rd. (CTH-R) at Broadway St. to STH-42 at Taylor St.

* [Icon: Water icon] [Icon: Information icon] At Henry Schuette Park (Upper parking area), Union Park, Lighthouse Park, the Manitowoc Chamber of Commerce, and the Spirit of the Rivers Monument and Wayside.
* [Icon: Picnic icon] [Icon: Restroom icon] At Henry Schuette Park (upper parking area), Union Park, and Lighthouse Park.
* [Icon: Camping icon] At nearby Manitowoc County Expo Center grounds (see Area Services).
* [Icon: No hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.
* [Icon: Dog on leash icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: Trail surface icon] Much of the segment is on sidewalks and multiuse recreation trails.
* [Icon: Connecting trail icon] The Mariners Trail extends north from the segment.
* [Icon: Wheelchair accessible icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

![Map of the Manitowoc Segment showing the trail route through Manitowoc Rapids and Manitowoc, including Henry Schuette Park, Union Park, and Lighthouse Park along the Manitowoc River and Lake Michigan shoreline.](page_386_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

① **Rapids Rd. (CTH-R) at Broadway St. Trail Access (44.09747, -87.70069):** From I-43 take Exit 149 and follow USH-151 east (north) for 0.2 mi. At S. Rapids Rd. (CTH-R) turn left and go north 1.8 mi to Broadway St. **Parking:** Roadside.

② **STH-42 at Taylor St. Trail Access (44.12581, -87.61477):** From I-43 take Exit 152 and follow USH-10 (Waldo Blvd.) east 4.3 mi. At STH-42 (Memorial Dr.) roundabout, take STH-42 north 1.9 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Aurora Medical Center complex. An Ice Age Trail sign is on the northeast corner of the clinic parking area.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Henry Schuette Park. (ii) Mariners Trail parking areas along Maritime Dr. and Memorial Dr. (STH-42).

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights Manitowoc, an Ice Age Trail Community filled with lots to see and do. Grand views of Lake Michigan immediately foster a vacation state of mind. Satisfy your post-hike cravings at numerous dining establishments, including breweries, coffee shops, and wine bars. Locate souvenirs and one-of-a-kind items in well-curated shops.

The segment travels through Schuette Park and past the Eternal Light Veterans Memorial, Rahr-West Art Museum, and the Wisconsin Maritime Museum, all worth exploring. The latter offers interactive educational exhibits exploring Wisconsin’s naval history and the Great Lakes. In addition, it includes a tour of a World War II submarine, the USS Cobia. Manitowoc residents are proud that the Manitowoc Shipbuilding Company is the only inland shipyard to build submarines for the U.S. Navy in World War II.

![Map of the Manitowoc area showing the Ice Age Trail route, Mariners Trail, Aurora Medical Center, and West of the Lake Gardens along the shore of Lake Michigan.](page_387_image_1_v2.jpg)

Lake Michigan

The hike also features the Manitowoc Breakwater Light, with its 165-year history guiding ships in and out of Manitowoc Harbor. During the summer, you may see the SS Badger as it sets out across Lake Michigan, ferrying cars and people to Ludington, Michigan.

The route also shares the Mariners Trail, which connects the cities of Manitowoc and Two Rivers on sidewalks and paved blacktop along Memorial Drive (STH-42). With the waves and shoreline of Lake Michigan to the east, the Mariners Trail boasts the longest continuous scenic view of Lake Michigan in Wisconsin. Along the way, several waysides offer parking areas and trailside benches, and some even have free telescopes to give you a closer look at passing ships. Along the route of the Mariners Trail, the segment passes West of the Lake Gardens, which are open seasonally to the public with free admission. Situated on six acres next to Lake Michigan, the gardens offer a peaceful respite featuring ornate plantings and lovely fountains.

The segment also passes the “Spirit of the Rivers.” This monument, a trio of 10-foot-tall bronze figures carrying a birch bark canoe between two navigable waters, celebrates the culture and history of the area’s

earliest inhabitants and Manitowoc’s rich maritime history. It is the work of internationally recognized artist and Manitowoc native R.T. “Skip” Wallen.

From the Trail access at the intersection of Rapids Road (CTH-R) and Broadway Street, head east on Broadway Street. At the entrance road for Lower Henry Schuette Park, encountered shortly after crossing the Manitowoc River bridge, turn left and head north along the road to the park’s lower parking area.

Depart the parking area and meander through the park, mainly along the Manitowoc River, on the park’s gravel roads and footpaths. Note: Most of the facilities for Schuette Park are in the upper parking area, which is slightly off the segment route. The upper parking area is on Broadway Street, just southeast of the intersection of Broadway Street and the Lower Henry Schuette Park entrance. An access trail leads from the lower parking area to the upper parking area. Connect with Clay Pit Road and head southeast to its intersection with South 21st Street.

Then, take the following route through the city of Manitowoc: Head north on South 21st Street, which changes to Revere Drive as it as it crosses the river. At the Eternal Light Veterans Memorial, continue straight on North 18th Street (Revere Drive turns right). Turn right and head east on Michigan Avenue, which becomes Huron Street. Then, turn right and head south on North 11th Street (which turns into North Water Street). Turn left and head east on Park Street, passing the Rahr-West Art Museum at the intersection with North 8th Street. At the Park Street and North 7th Street intersection, turn right and walk southeast through Union Park to the North 6th Street and State Street intersection. Head south on North 6th Street.

At the end of North 6th Street, head south across Maritime Drive and through the Wisconsin Maritime Museum’s parking lot. At the edge of the lot, pick up a paved path and head east along the river. Follow the path as it curves north to follow the Lake Michigan shoreline. At this curve, the Manitowoc River empties into Lake Michigan. You can also see the Manitowoc Breakwater Light situated on the north breakwater. Continuing north, you’ll pass behind a hotel and arrive at the Manitowoc Marina, the official southern terminus of the Mariners Trail.

Join the Mariners Trail and continue heading northeast. You’ll pass Lighthouse Park with water, restrooms, picnic tables, and a small beach. Continue past West of the Lake Gardens, the Manitowoc Chamber of Commerce building, and the Spirit of the River Monument Wayside. The segment ends at the stoplight at the Taylor Street and Memorial Drive (STH-42) intersection.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Manitowoc**: Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on USH-151, USH-10 and STH-42. For area info, contact the City of Manitowoc Tourism (visitmanitowoc.com).

**Manitowoc County Expo Center**: Camping. From the Rapids Rd./Broadway St. Trail access, take Rapids Rd. south 1.0 mi. At Custer St. go west. At Vista Rd. go south 0.4 mi to the grounds (920-683-4378, co.manitowoc.wi.us/expo). Public camping available nonevent weekends and weekdays. RV with electric/water sites and tent gravel pads. Contact the main office for details.

![A vibrant pollinator garden with various flowers in the foreground and Lake Michigan in the background.](page_389_image_1_v2.jpg)

JILL WERNER

*Manitowoc Segment: Lake Michigan provides a colorful pollinator garden its backdrop.*

**Manitowoc County** 357
