## Dunes Segment and Two Rivers Segment (Atlas Maps 95f, 96f)

**Summary:** The Dunes Segment and Two Rivers Segment covers approximately 5.7 miles in Manitowoc County (Atlas Map 95f, 96f). It comprises the Dunes Segment (2.7 mi, STH-42 at Taylor St. to Columbus St.), the Two Rivers Segment (3.0 mi, Columbus St. to Park Rd.). ③ **Park Rd. Trail Access (44.15759, -87.54547):** From Two Rivers at the intersection of Washington St. and 22nd St. (along STH-42), take 22nd St. east until it ends. *Note: 22nd St. turns right at Neshotah Park across from the water tower.* At Neshotah Rd. turn left and go north 0.1 mi.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Dunes Segment icon] **Dunes Segment—2.7 miles**: STH-42 at Taylor St. to Columbus St.

[Icon: Water and Restroom icon] At Aurora Medical Center.

[Icon: Hunting icon] All trails in Woodland Dunes Preserve are closed during gun deer and muzzleloader hunting seasons.

[Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are permitted on the Ice Age Trail through Woodland Dunes but are not permitted on other trails through the preserve.**

![Sidewalk icon](page_390_image_16_v2.jpg) A short portion is on sidewalks and follows paved surfaces on the Aurora Medical Center complex property.

![Trail network icon](page_390_image_16_v2.jpg) Woodland Dunes Preserve trail network.

[Icon: Two Rivers Segment icon] **Two Rivers Segment—3.0 miles**: Columbus St. to Park Rd.

[Icon: Water and Restroom icon] At nearby Zander Park, Coast Guard Park, and Neshotah Park. Porta-potty at Harbor View Park.

[Icon: Picnic and Playground icon] At nearby Zander Park, Coast Guard Park, and at Neshotah Park.

[Icon: Camping icon] At a nearby private campground ▲.

[Icon: No hunting icon] Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.

[Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Sidewalk icon] Much of the segment is on sidewalks.

![Trail icon](page_390_image_8_v2.jpg) South Breakwater Trail.

![Wheelchair icon](page_390_image_5_v2.jpg) Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

[Icon: Map of Dunes and Two Rivers Segments]

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

![Parking icon 1](page_390_image_7_v2.jpg) **① STH-42 at Taylor St. Trail Access (44.12581, -87.61477)**: From I-43 take Exit 152 and follow USH-10 (Waldo Blvd.) east 4.3 mi. At STH-42 (Memorial Dr.) roundabout, take STH-42 north 1.9 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park at the Aurora Medical Center complex. An Ice Age Trail sign is on the northeast corner of the clinic parking area.

[Icon: Parking icon 2] **② Columbus St. Trail Access (44.14442, -87.59022)**: Parking: Roadside.

③ **Park Rd. Trail Access (44.15759, -87.54547):** From Two Rivers at the intersection of Washington St. and 22nd St. (along STH-42), take 22nd St. east until it ends. *Note: 22nd St. turns right at Neshotah Park across from the water tower.* At Neshotah Rd. turn left and go north 0.1 mi. At Park Rd. turn right and go east 0.3 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

**Additional Parking:** (i) Woodland Dunes Nature Center and Preserve on Goodwin Rd. (ii) Neshotah Park on Zlatnik Dr.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.parent_text:
## Dunes Segment

**Summary:** The Dunes Segment: This segment features the 1,200-acre Woodland Dunes Nature Center and Preserve, an oasis of marshland, swamps, sandy meadows, and wooded ridges.

This segment features the 1,200-acre Woodland Dunes Nature Center and Preserve, an oasis of marshland, swamps, sandy meadows, and wooded ridges. It represents a "tension zone" between two distinct areas of both northern and southern species of plants and birds, making it one of the premier birding areas in the country. The Woodland Dunes State Natural Area is within the Preserve, with shoreline remnants of postglacial Lake Nipissing seen as ridges and swales. White birch, aspen, beech, and hemlock trees populate many of these ridges.

The Woodland Dunes Nature Center offers educational nature programs, and the Preserve has miles of nature trails.

[Icon: Map of the Dunes Segment in Two Rivers, showing the trail route, Lake Michigan, and local streets.]

From the Trail access at the intersection of Taylor Street and Memorial Drive (STH-42), cross cautiously at the stoplight and head north on Taylor Street. At Lake View Avenue, turn left into the Aurora Medical Center complex and veer left between the medical center's hospital and a small stream. Cross a bridge and then turn right, heading toward an Ice Age Trail sign at the northeast corner of the medical center's clinic parking area.

North of the clinic parking area, enter a wooded area, cross a utility corridor (former railroad right-of-way), and enter the Woodland Dunes Nature Center and Preserve. The Trail heads northeast and shares the southeast part of the Preserve's Trillium Trail for a time.

Toward the segment's end, cross 10th Street, bearing slightly to the left. Exit the Preserve and reach the segment's endpoint at the 12th Street and Columbus Street intersection.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.parent_text:
## Two Rivers Segment

**Summary:** The Two Rivers Segment: This segment highlights Two Rivers, an *Ice Age Trail Community* brimming with seaside-like charm.

This segment highlights Two Rivers, an *Ice Age Trail Community* brimming with seaside-like charm. Enjoy 50 acres of groomed sand at Neshota Beach, where beachcombers search for sea glass. Famished hikers will delight in authentic supper clubs and homey restaurants serving delicious meals. Finally, plan a stop at the Washington House (free admission, donations welcomed), home of the original ice cream sundae. This old hotel has beautiful murals and a ballroom on the second floor; antiques, restored period rooms, and interesting collections are also on display.

The segment also offers two quick side trips. One is to the Historic Rodgers Street Fishing Village (see Points of Interest). Another is along the blue-blazed South Breakwater Trail, connecting Mariners Trail and the South Breakwater on Lake Michigan. It features interpretive signage conveying the harbor’s history and a 100-foot-long mural depicting fish of Lake Michigan.

From the Trail access at 12th Street and Columbus Street in Two Rivers, take the following route through the city: head east on 12th Street. You’ll pass the free Historic Farm Museum, located at the corner of 12th and Monroe Street, which is part of the Two Rivers Historical Society.

Arriving at Washington Street (STH-42), you can take a side trip. To explore the blue-blazed South Breakwater Trail (mentioned above), cross Washington Street (STH-42) and follow Lake Street to connect with it.

To remain on the route, turn left and head north at Washington Street (STH-42), crossing the West Twin River. At East River Road, turn right and go northeast. Then, at Jefferson Street, turn left and go north to 17th Street. The historic Washington House (mentioned above) is on the southeast corner of this intersection.

Turn right on 17th Street and go east across the East Twin River. At East Street, there’s an opportunity for another side trip. Turning left and heading north will take you to the Rogers Street Fishing Village.

However, turn right on East Street and continue south to remain on the route. Turn left on Harbor Street and then right on 16th Street. 16th Street turns into Zlatnik Drive and continues into Neshotah Park. At 22nd Street, turn right, go east briefly, then left onto Neshotah Road. At Park Road, turn right and continue to the segment’s endpoint at the end of Park Road.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Rogers Street Fishing Village:** From the intersection of 17th St. and East St., go northeast on East St. then north on Jackson St. (2102 Jackson St., Two Rivers, 920-793-5905, rogersstreet.com).

The historic Rogers Street Fishing Village is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The museum village and historic park showcase more than 170 years of commercial fishing with the Great Lakes Coast Guard Shipwreck Exhibit and many other historic artifacts, including the climbable and symbolic 1886 North Pier Lighthouse.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo]
**Manitowoc:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on USH-151, USH-10 and STH-42. For area info, contact the City of Manitowoc Tourism (visitmanitowoc.com). From the STH-42 Trail access go south ~4 mi.

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo]
**Two Rivers:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on Washington St. (STH-42). Area info available from the City of Two Rivers (920-793-5523, two-rivers.org).

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.parent_text:
## Point Beach Segment (Atlas Map 96f)

**Summary:** The Point Beach Segment is a 10.0-mile trail in Manitowoc County (Atlas Map 96f), running from Park Rd. to Lakeshore Rd.. This segment traverses a remarkable swath of public land, a state forest, and two state natural areas, protecting both the ancient and current shorelines of Lake Michigan.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: mileage icon] **10.0 miles**: Park Rd. to Lakeshore Rd.

[Icon: water and toilet icon] At Point Beach State Forest (PBSF) entrance station, campground/nature center, indoor group camp, and outdoor group campground. Pit toilet (no water) is also available near the PBSF Ice Age walk-in campsite (please respect those who have reserved the campsite).

[Icon: water source icon] From Lake Michigan and Molash Creek.

[Icon: campsite icon] At PBSF walk-in Ice Age campsite [Icon: tent icon with number 4] (reservations required).

[Icon: campground icon] At PBSF campground and nearby private campground.

[Icon: picnic area icon] At PBSF campground/nature center area.

[Icon: geocache icon] Four ColdCache sites on segment.

[Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. **Dogs are not permitted inside park buildings.**

[Icon: ski and bike icon] Portions overlap park road, bike and XC trails. Hike off to the side of ski trails when groomed.

[Icon: trail network icon] [Icon: trail network icon] PBSF and School Forest trail networks.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: parking icon 1] ****Park Rd. Trail Access (44.15759, -87.54547):**** From Two Rivers at the intersection of Washington St. and 22nd St. (along STH-42), take 22nd St. east until it ends. Note: 22nd St. turns right at Neshotah Park across from the water tower. At Neshotah Rd. turn left and go north 0.1 mi. At Park Rd. turn right and go north 0.3 mi. **Parking:** Roadside.

[Icon: parking icon 2] ****Lakeshore Rd. Trail Access (44.23978, -87.52848):**** From Two Rivers take STH-42 and follow it north for ~ 6 mi. At CTH-V turn right and go east 1.8 mi. At Lakeshore Rd. turn left and go north 0.2 mi. **Parking:** Roadside on the east side of the road.

****Additional Parking:**** (i) Viceroy Rd. (CTH-VV). (ii) Point Beach State Forest (PBSF), east and north of the entrance station. (iii) PBSF Red Pine Trail parking area on the west side of Sandy Bay Rd. (CTH-O).

### THE HIKE

This segment traverses a remarkable swath of public land, a state forest, and two state natural areas, protecting both the ancient and current shorelines of Lake Michigan. The 3,000-acre Point Beach State Forest (PBSF) offers 6 miles of sandy beach, family campsites, and large group cabins.

The Point Beach Ridges State Natural Area features a topography of 11 alternating ridges and swales paralleling the present Lake Michigan shoreline. Formed through the prolonged lowering of glacial Lake Nippissing, the ridges and swales are old beaches deposited during the last 8,000 years. Except for a strip of dunes and beach along the lake, the area is forested with conifers and hardwoods.

The Nipissing Swamp State Natural Area protects northeast Wisconsin’s largest northern hardwood swamp remnant. Prominent before the cutover era, it is less common in today’s landscape. The swamp sits in a large wetland basin, once a lagoon formed behind a large sand bar across the mouth of glacial Lake Nipissing. Remnants of the old Nipissing Beach remain as sand barrens and dunes along the upland transition on the east side of the swamp.

A short side trip near the segment’s terminus features the Rawley Point Lighthouse, operated and maintained by the U.S. Coast Guard since 1853. The light atop this steel tower, rising 113 feet above the lake, is one of the largest and brightest on the Great Lakes. It is visible up to 19 miles away. Lake Michigan’s most famous shipwreck, the *Rouse Simmons*, called the “Christmas Tree Ship,” lies off Rawley Point. In 1912, the ship, bound for Chicago with a cargo of evergreens, sank in a storm.

From the Trail access at the end of Park Road, head briefly northeast and then southeast to the beach. At an Ice Age Trail marker, turn north, and follow the shoreline for 2.0 miles. Along the way, you may encounter a small, intermittent creek requiring a ford. Continue to another Trail marker at the top of the beach. This second Trail marker can be difficult to spot; if you reach the mouth of Molash Creek, which always requires a ford, you’ve gone too far. Note: There are no other

![Map of the northern portion of the Point Beach Segment, showing the trail route through Point Beach State Forest, past the Outdoor Group Camp and Rawley Point Lighthouse, towards the Mishicot Segment.](page_394_image_2_v2.jpg)

![Map of the southern portion of the Point Beach Segment, showing the trail route from Neshotah Park along Park Road to the beach, then north along the Lake Michigan shoreline through Point Beach Ridges State Natural Area towards Molash Creek.](page_394_image_1_v2.jpg)

trail markers or signage along the beach walk.

From the Trail marker, head west (inland), crossing dunes atop another cordwalk, and then head briefly northwest to connect with PBSF’s scenic Molash Creek Trail. Next, head southwest, cross a swale on a short boardwalk, then again head northwest. Enjoy views of Molash Creek and the surrounding wetlands along the way.

As you near Sandy Bay Road (CTH-O), meet PBSF’s Rawley Point Bike

Trail and follow it north across a bridge over Molash Creek. Depart the bike path and head east in a mixed conifer/hardwood forest until you intersect the Green Loop of PBSF’s Ridges Trail.

As you follow a series of PBSF’s trails, you’ll traverse ancient beach ridges and swales left behind from Lake Nipissing, the predecessor of Lake Michigan. Then, paying attention to Trail signage, travel generally north through this unique area’s mixed forests, with occasional glimpses of and openings to the dunes.

At the intersection with the Green Loop, take it south, then north until transitioning onto the Blue Loop. Meet a spur trail leading to a walk-in campsite with a bench, a fire ring, and a picnic table. A pit toilet and a kayak campsite are nearby.

Continue on the Blue Loop, turning left, away from the lake, and soon reach a junction where the Trail switches onto the Red Loop and follows it north. Leave the Red Loop to follow a narrow, winding tread on a ridge between marshy swales under a red-pine canopy to PBSF’s entrance road. For a short side trip, head east on the entrance road to a trail leading to Rawley Point Lighthouse (mentioned previously).

Turn left (west) on the entrance road, cross Sandy Bay Road (CTH-O), and head for the northeast corner of the Trail access parking area for PBSF’s Red Pine Trail. From here, you’ll travel through the woods, intersecting paths within the Red Pine Trail system. At one of these trail junctions, a wide, grassy trail leads 0.2 miles east to the PBSF group campsite.

Continuing north, at a signed boundary, leave PBSF and enter the Manitowoc Public School District’s Rahr Memorial School Forest, a 300-acre parcel with a network of hiking trails. Navigate the forest, carefully observing Ice Age Trail signage. Cross CTH-V and meander to the segment’s endpoint on Lakeshore Road.

A side trip to the School Forest’s 0.3-mile-long curving boardwalk is north and west of the CTH-V Trail access. The structure preserves a delicate swamp/wetland ecosystem full of trees, wildflowers, and wildlife, including frogs, turtles, ducks, and salamanders.

### AREA SERVICES

**Point Beach State Forest (PBSF):** Camping, summer concession stand. On Trail. (920-794-7480, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/pointbeach). General camping and remote walk-in Ice Age backpack and kayak campsites available. Reservations required (888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). *Note: For large groups, there is an outdoor group camp and group cabins available for rent.*

[Icon: Two Rivers logo] **Two Rivers:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on Washington St. (STH-42). Area info available from the City of Two Rivers (920-793-5523, two-rivers.org). From the Point Beach State Forest entrance road go south ~5 mi.

**Mishicot:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Main St. (STH-147) and Randolph St. (CTH-V). For additional information visit mishicot.org. From Point Beach State Forest Trail entrance road go north and west 8.5 mi.

**Rahr Memorial School Forest:** On Trail (11617 Sandy Bay Rd., Two Rivers, 920-686-4777, manitowocpublicschools.org/services/school_forest). An outdoor education facility for the Manitowoc Public School District and area community. Buildings can be rented by organized groups. The School Forest has 4.5 mi of trails accessing a variety of habitats and is open to the public during daylight hours.

## Mishicot Segment and East Twin River Segment (Atlas Map 97f)

**Summary:** The Mishicot Segment and East Twin River Segment: 1. [Icon: circled 1] **CTH-V at Woodlawn Dr. Trail Access (44.24039, -87.63020):** From I-43 take Exit 164 and follow STH-147 south for 8.0 mi to the village of Mishicot.

### [Icon: hiker icon] [Icon: number 3 icon] Mishicot Segment – 2.9 miles: CTH-V at Woodlawn Dr. to Princl Rd.

* [Icon: water icon] [Icon: water icon] From the East Twin River.
* [Icon: park icon] [Icon: park icon] At Mishicot Village Park.
* [Icon: coldcache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: deer icon] Portion of segment crossing private land between CTH-B and Princl Rd. is closed during gun deer season.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: road icon] About one-third of the segment is on sidewalks, roads or urban paths.

### [Icon: hiker and number icon] East Twin River Segment—1.4 miles: Rockledge Rd. to Hillview Rd.

* [Icon: water icon] From the East Twin River.
* [Icon: tent icon] At a Dispersed Camping Area [Icon: DCA icon].
* [Icon: coldcache icon] One ColdCache site on segment.
* [Icon: dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: farm road icon] Portions overlap farm access roads.
* [Icon: spur trail icon] A blue-blazed spur trail to the DCA.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

1. [Icon: circled 1] **CTH-V at Woodlawn Dr. Trail Access (44.24039, -87.63020):** From I-43 take Exit 164 and follow STH-147 south for 8.0 mi to the village of Mishicot. At Randolph St. (CTH-V) turn left and go east 0.6 mi to Woodlawn Dr. **Parking:** Roadside.
2. [Icon: circled 2] **Princl Rd. Trail Access (44.25406, -87.65417):** **Parking:** Roadside.
3. [Icon: circled 3] [Icon: Rd. icon] **Rockledge Rd. Trail Access (44.26102, -87.66088):** No Parking. Instead, park roadside at Princl Rd. or at the Hillview Rd. parking area.
4. [Icon: circled 4] **Hillview Rd. Trail Access (44.27684, -87.66614):** From Mishicot at the intersection of STH-147, CTH-V, and CTH-B, take CTH-B north 2.4 mi. At Hillview Rd. turn left and go west 1.1 mi. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.parent_text:
## Mishicot Segment

**Summary:** The Mishicot Segment: This segment travels through Mishicot, a charming village founded in 1847.

This segment travels through Mishicot, a charming village founded in 1847. According to local lore, white settler Daniel Smith respectfully named the settlement after his friend, Chief Mishicot, leader of a Potawatomi band living in the area. The route offers a scenic Riverwalk and a covered bridge over the East Twin River.

From the Trail Access in Mishicot, at the Woodlawn Dr. and CTH-V (Randolph Street) intersection, head south on Woodlawn Dr., then head west on Washington St. Cross Main St. (STH-147) and head south across a bridge to the Mishicot Village Park. Curve right on a path down to the Mishicot Riverwalk along the south side of the East Twin River.

At Rockway Street, turn right and head northeast, crossing the East Twin River on a picturesque covered bridge. Head briefly east at Randolph Street (CTH-V) before turning north onto Oak Street.

![Map of the East Twin River Segment and surrounding area in Manitowoc County, showing trail routes, connecting routes to Tisch Mills and Point Beach segments, and local roads near Mishicot.](page_397_image_1_v2.jpg)

Depart Oak Street to the left and head northwest on Pit Road (a gravel road). Look for Trail signage after about 0.3 miles on the right, where the segment leaves the road, climbs a thirty-foot esker, and runs along its top. A Leopold bench offers a woodsy rest stop. Cross CTH-B and zigzag along the edges of farm fields until turning north, passing a wooded area, and reaching the segment’s endpoint on Princl Road.

*To reach the East Twin River Segment, follow a 0.7-mile connecting route by heading north on Princl Road 0.2 miles then west on Rockledge Road 0.5 miles.*

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.parent_text:
## East Twin River Segment

**Summary:** The East Twin River Segment: This segment courses through East Twin River’s riparian zone and highlights towering northern white cedars.

This segment courses through East Twin River’s riparian zone and highlights towering northern white cedars. It offers scenic river views while five boardwalks over a seasonally wet floodplain keep hikers’ feet dry.

From the Trail access on Rockledge Road, head north in a corridor of pine, oak, and silver maple, following the west side of the East Twin River. At a farm road, turn right and cross to the east side of the river on an old bridge. Meander north in the forest corridor near the edge of a farm field. Reach a split-log cedar bench overlooking a giant silver maple (Thompson Family Maple) standing near the river. Shortly after passing the bench, meet a blue-blazed trail leading west to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Leave the field edge and continue through the forest, enjoying scenic river views. Cross the fifth boardwalk before you reach Hillview Road. Head west on Hillview Road to the segment’s endpoint at a parking area.

### AREA SERVICES

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Alliance logo] [FIGURE] **Two Rivers:** See Dunes Segment and Two Rivers Segment, p. 358. From the CTH-V at Woodlawn Dr. Trail access go south and east ~8 mi.

**Mishicot:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Main St. (STH-147) and Randolph St. (CTH-V). For additional information visit mishicot.org.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::manitowoc-coun.parent_text:
## Tisch Mills Segment (Atlas Map 98f)

**Summary:** The Tisch Mills Segment is a 2.7-mile trail in Manitowoc County (Atlas Map 98f), running from CTH-B to Nuclear Rd.. This segment highlights the Tisch Mills Creek and East Twin River as it straddles two counties.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Distance and Map icon]  **2.7 miles (1.8 IAT, 0.9 CR)**: CTH-B to Nuclear Rd.

[Icon: Water icon]  From Tisch Mills Creek and the East Twin River.

[Icon: Hunting season closure icon]  Portion of segment crossing private land between Mill Ln. and Nuclear Rd. is closed during gun deer season.

[Icon: Camping icon]  At a Dispersed Camping Area DCA east of the Tisch Mills Creek crossing.

[Icon: Leashed dogs icon]  Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Campground icon]  At nearby Maple View campground.

[Icon: ColdCache icon]  One ColdCache site on segment.

[Icon: Spur trail icon]  A blue-blazed spur trail west of Tisch Mills Creek and a blue-blazed spur trail to the DCA.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Parking access 1 icon]  **1 CTH-B Trail Access (44.32395, -87.64428)**: From Two Rivers take STH-42 and follow it north for ~ 12.5 mi. At CTH-BB turn left and go west 5.0 mi. At CTH-B turn left and go south 0.2 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park roadside at the CTH-BB Trail access.

[Icon: Parking access 2 icon]  **2 Nuclear Rd. Trail Access (44.34193, -87.61159)**: From Kewaunee at the intersection of STH-42 and STH-29, take STH-42 south 9.3 mi. At Nuclear Rd. turn right and go west 3.5 mi. **No Parking.** Instead, park roadside at the north end of Mill Ln. in Tisch Mills.

**Additional Parking**: (i) CTH-BB roadside parking. (ii) Mill Ln. roadside parking.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights the Tisch Mills Creek and East Twin River as it straddles two counties. In Manitowoc County, the route features the 40.5-acre Alliance-owned Weber’s Woods Preserve, where wildflowers grow under a wooded canopy, an oasis in the surrounding farmland. In Kewaunee County, the Trail follows the west side of the East Twin River — the front of the Two Rivers Moraine — offering views of waterfowl and wildflowers in a vast wetland. *Note: Before hiking the section in Kewaunee County, check with the Alliance’s Lakeshore Chapter for Trail conditions. In spring and after heavy rainfall, expect flooding.*

From the Trail access on CTH-B, head east. (Look for a brown Carsonite post with the Ice Age Trail logo and blaze on an elevation above the road.) Enter the Alliance’s Weber’s Woods Preserve, continuing east along an agricultural field property line. Enjoy a densely wooded area where the route makes good use of boardwalks spanning wet areas. However, there’s no bridge across Tish Mills Creek. Instead, a wooden sign suggests, “Wading would be safer than walking on rocks.” (But before you need to decide whether to wade or not, you’ll meet a blue-blazed spur trail leading north to CTH-BB, where you can turn right and cross the creek on a bridge. After the creek crossing, rejoin the Trail as it emerges from the forest onto CTH-BB.) If you choose to ford the creek, east of the creek crossing you’ll meet a spur trail to the right leading to a Dispersed Camping Area (DCA) for long-distance, multiday hikers. Continue uphill and soon pass a memorial to the Weber family before emerging onto CTH-BB.

![Map of the Tisch Mills Segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the route from Weber's Woods to Nuclear Road, including connecting routes and local roads.](page_399_image_1_v2.jpg)

Turn right and head east 0.7 miles on a connecting route through the unincorporated village of Tisch Mills. Then head north 0.2 miles on Mill Lane, which parallels the west side of the East Twin River. Back on Trail, continue north, following the west bank of the East Twin River, to the segment’s endpoint on Nuclear Road.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Two Creeks Buried Forest:** From Kewaunee, take STH-42 south 10.5 mi to the junction with CTH-BB at the Kewaunee County line. Park in the lot immediately east of the intersection and south of the restaurant. Walk southeast into the natural area (dnr.wi.gov/topic/Lands/naturalareas/index.asp?SNA=50).

Two Creeks Buried Forest is an Ice Age National Scientific Reserve Unit. It is world-famous among geologists because it provides a unique, precise record of glacial advances and retreats during the Wisconsin Glaciation. The layers of glacial till and buried forest can be seen on the side of the steep bluff along the lakeshore due to wave erosion. Some features may be harder to see during the summer months because of ground cover. Wood from the forest has been radiocarbon-dated to 11,850 years ago. Removal of any material is strictly prohibited.

### AREA SERVICES

**Mishicot:** Restaurant, convenience store, lodging, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Main St. (STH-147) and Randolph St. (CTH-V). For additional information visit mishicot.org. From the Tisch Mills Trail access on CTH-B go west and south ~6 mi.

****Tisch Mills:**** Restaurant, convenience store. Store is at the intersection of CTH-B/CTH-AB/CTH-BB.

![Map of the Ice Age Trail in Kewaunee and Door Counties. The map shows the trail route through the Sturgeon Bay Segment and Forestville Segment, largely following the Ahnapee State Trail. Key landmarks include Potawatomi State Park, Sturgeon Bay, Maplewood, Forestville, and Algoma. An inset map of Wisconsin highlights the location of Kewaunee and Door Counties. The map also includes a compass rose and the Ice Age Trail Alliance website address.](page_400_image_1_v2.jpg)

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::kewaunee-door-.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::kewaunee-door-.parent_text:
# Kewaunee & Door Counties

![Map of Kewaunee and Door Counties showing the Ice Age Trail segments. The map highlights the Kewaunee River Segment and the Tisch Mills Segment. It also shows the Ahnapee State Trail, Lake Michigan, and towns like Algoma, Casco, Luxemburg, Kewaunee, and Tisch Mills. A legend indicates the Ice Age Trail Community, Ice Age Trail, Connecting Route (unmarked), Other Trail, County Boundary, and Public or IATA Land, along with a scale in miles.](page_401_image_1_v2.jpg)

# Kewaunee & Door Counties

*Atlas Maps 98f–105f; Databook pages 102–105*
*Total miles: 70.3 (Trail 36.3; Connecting Route 34.0)*

![Map of Wisconsin highlighting Kewaunee and Door counties](page_402_image_1_v2.jpg)

Door County’s shape is due to the 400-million-year-old dolomite rock at its base, known as the Niagara Escarpment. The erosion-resistant rock extends in a broad arc from the east side of Horicon Marsh and Lake Winnebago through Door County, Michigan, and Ontario and forms the crest of Niagara Falls. Blocks of Niagara dolomite, carried by glacial ice, are scattered through the counties to the south.

Door County is the location of the most recent Ice Age event along the Ice Age Trail. As the front of the Green Bay Lobe slowly melted northward, and the Niagara Escarpment bound its waters to the east, Glacial Lake Oshkosh formed in a large area of today’s Fox River Valley.

Then as the Green Bay Lobe melted back, successively lower outlets of the lake opened, creating what are now the Manitowoc, West Twin, Kewaunee, and Ahnapee rivers. And finally, Sturgeon Bay.

In what is today Potawatomi State Park, beaches made during higher levels of Lake Michigan can be seen along the Trail. These ancient shorelines of Lake Algonquin (11,000 years ago), and Lake Nipissing (5,000 years ago), rise as much as 20 to 60 feet above modern-day Lake Michigan.

Sturgeon Bay, an Ice Age Trail Community, was named for the long, fish-shaped bay it borders. The first house built along the waterfront prompted the town’s rapid development. By 1862, it was home to more than 200 residents and 3 sawmills. Eventually, Sturgeon Bay became a center of stone quarrying, with five quarries shipping limestone to many ports throughout the region for harbor improvements.

The canal completed in 1880 linked Sturgeon Bay to Lake Michigan, providing a safe pass for ships previously forced to travel through the dangerous Porte des Morts (Death’s Door) Strait. The new passage quickly attracted thousands of vessels, and Sturgeon Bay became a center of maritime traffic and shipbuilding.

Sturgeon Bay’s dining and drinking scene will provide plenty of sustenance to hikers, from greasy spoons to sushi spots and everything in between. In addition, a vibrant downtown and plenty of retail options cater to numerous tastes and interests.

The Ice Age Trail shares the route of the multiuse Ahnapee State Trail in Kewaunee and Door counties. The route passes through drumlins, farmlands, wetlands, and forests while following parts of the Kewaunee River Valley and

Ahnapee River Valley. After making its way through Sturgeon Bay, the Ice Age Trail reaches its eastern terminus at the official terminus marker in Potawatomi State Park.

### ALLIANCE CHAPTER INFORMATION

The Lakeshore Chapter formed from a merger of the Door/Kewaunee County, Manitowoc County, and North Kettle Moraine chapters of the Alliance. Chapter members actively promote the Ice Age Trail in their region by hosting guided hikes along various segments. The Chapter also organizes many Trail improvement events along the segments in their care.

Hikers can become members of the “Hall of Kamers” by hiking the Northern Unit of the Kettle Moraine State Forest Ice Age Trail segments. Start your Hall of Kamers adventure by visiting: IceAgeTrail.org/hiking-awards.

Visit IceAgeTrail.org/lakeshore-chapter to learn more about the chapter.

### COUNTY INFORMATION

**Door County Visitor Bureau**: 920-743-4456 or 800-527-3529, doorcounty.com

[Icon: Ice Age Trail Community logo] **Ice Age Trail Community, Sturgeon Bay**: 920-743-6246, sturgeonbay.net

![A large brown mammoth sculpture stands in a landscaped area near a body of water.](page_403_image_1_v2.jpg)

PHOTOGRAPHER UNKNOWN

Sturgeon Bay Segment: A Trail Community proudly touts its Ice Age Trail connection.

![Map of the Kewaunee River Segment showing trail path, access points, and local landmarks.](page_404_image_1_v2.jpg)

## Kewaunee River Segment

**Summary:** The Kewaunee River Segment: This segment highlights the 28-mile-long Kewaunee River as it follows the Ahnapee State Trail (AST), a crushed limestone, multiuse rail-trail converted for recreational use in 1975.

(Atlas Maps 100f, 101f)

![Snapshot summary box for the Kewaunee River Segment.](page_404_image_15_v2.jpg)

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: Icon] **12.5**: Ahnapee State Trail (AST) at Sunset Rd. to Miller St. (CTH-E) at Milwaukee St. (STH-42)

[Icon: Water icon] At Bruemmer County Park and Zoo and nearby Harbor Park and Father Marquette Memorial Park.

[Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.

[Icon: Picnic icon] At Bruemmer County Park and Zoo and nearby Harbor Park and Father Marquette Memorial Park.

[Icon: Multi-use trail icon] The AST is open to horseback riding, snowmobiling and biking.

[Icon: Fishing icon] From the Kewaunee River.

[Icon: Trail connection icon] The AST extends west from the segment.

[Icon: Camping icon] At nearby private campgrounds [Icon: Triangle icon].

[Icon: Accessibility icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

[Icon: Restroom icon] At Sunset Rd. Trail access, Bruemmer County Park and Zoo, Harbor Park and Father Marquette Memorial Park.

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: Parking icon 1] **1 Ahnapee State Trail at Sunset Rd. Trail Access (44.53060, -87.65048)**: From Kewaunee at the intersection of Milwaukee St. (STH-42) and Ellis St. (STH-29), take Ellis St. (STH-29) west 1.0 mi. At the CTH-C/STH-29 fork, veer right onto CTH-C (Ellis St.) and continue northwest 1.4 mi. At CTH-F turn right and go northwest 4.8 mi. At CTH-A turn left and go west 3.1 mi. At Sunset Rd. turn right and go east 0.4 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access at Harold Reckelberg Park.

[Icon: Parking icon 2] **2 Miller St. (CTH-E) at Milwaukee St. (STH-42) Trail Access (44.46031, -87.50353)**: From Kewaunee at the intersection of Milwaukee St. (STH-42) and Ellis St. (STH-29), take Milwaukee St. (STH-42) north 0.1 mi. **Parking**: Adjacent to Trail Access at Clock Corner Park.

**Additional Parking**: (i) CTH-C western Trail access. (ii) Bruemmer Park and Zoo. (iii) CTH-C eastern Trail access. (iv) Harbor Park.

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights the 28-mile-long Kewaunee River as it follows the Ahnapee State Trail (AST), a crushed limestone, multiuse rail-trail converted for recreational use in 1975. The river begins near Frog Station in the northwest part of the county. It flows southeast, emptying into Lake Michigan at the city of Kewaunee. Kewaunee is located in Kewaunee County, on the western shore of Lake Michigan.

The AST’s namesake, the Ahnapee & Western Railway (A&W), served major industries in Algoma, Sturgeon Bay, and Casco Junction dating back to its origin in 1892. The railway

![Map of the Kewaunee River segment of the Ice Age Trail, showing the trail following the Ahnapee State Trail, passing through C.D. "Buzz" Besadny State Fish and Wildlife Area, and ending in Kewaunee near Lake Michigan.](page_405_image_1_v2.jpg)

connected to Green Bay and Kewaunee at Casco Junction via the Green Bay & Western Railroad, where steam and diesel-powered locomotives hauled lumber, shipbuilding materials, dairy products, petroleum products, and other commodities. During World War II, the A&W transported German prisoners of war to Door County to work the fruit harvest season. In the early 1970s, costs and alternative routes of shipping forced A&W to abandon the line.

The segment also passes Bruemmer Park & Zoo. The small free zoo, on an eight-acre site within the 30-acre county park, features animals native to northeastern Wisconsin and a few exotic animals. The park offers wooded trails, a playground, restrooms, and a picnic area with grills.

From the Trail access at the gravel parking lot of Harold Reckelberg Park, located on Sunset Road in Casco Junction, head southeast toward Kewaunee on the AST. You’ll pass through primarily wooded, rolling terrain and follow the Kewaunee River, although the river is only sometimes visible. The Trail intersects numerous county roads, crosses the Kewaunee River and Scarboro Creek on three refurbished wooden trestle bridges, and passes by and through the various tracts of the C.D. “Buzz” Besadny State Fish and Wildlife Area.

When the AST crosses CTH-F, head southwest on CTH-F to take advantage of two side trips. First, an immediate left off of CTH-F takes you into Bruemmer Park & Zoo (mentioned above). To explore the C.D. “Buzz” Besadny Anadromous Fish Facility, continue following CTH-F southwest (see Points of Interest).

Back on Trail, the route travels through wetlands and woods. Shortly after crossing River Road (CTH-E), the rail-trail splits; one path heads left (south-east) toward STH-42, and the other heads right (south) toward Kewaunee. Follow the split south to the segment’s endpoint at the AST trailhead in Kewaunee (featuring the world’s tallest grandfather clock) at the south-west corner of the intersection of Miller Street (CTH-E) and Milwaukee Street (STH-42).

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**C.D. “Buzz” Besadny Anadromous Fish Facility:** From the Ice Age Trail at the CTH-F intersection, head southwest on CTH-F for 0.5 mi then north a short distance on Ransom Moore Ln. (N3884 Ransom Moore Ln., Kewaunee, 920-388-1025, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/Fishing/hatcheries/cdbesadny.html).

The Fish Facility collects eggs from spawning trout and salmon. The fertilized eggs are shipped to hatcheries, raising the fish for stocking many of the Lake Michigan tributaries. Visitors can take a self-guided tour. Educational displays contain basic information on trout and salmon life cycles, facility operations, and other related subjects. Visitors can watch trout and salmon swim up the Kewaunee River during spawning. Underwater windows offer views of the fish jumping and splashing their way up a fish ladder to large holding ponds. The Fish Facility grounds are open spring through fall during daylight hours. Facility building hours vary, call or visit website for details.

### AREA SERVICES

**Kewaunee:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services on Milwaukee St. (STH-42), downtown at Milwaukee St. (STH-42) and Ellis St. (STH-29) or north of the Kewaunee River Bridge on Main St. (STH-42). Area information available from the Kewaunee Chamber of Commerce (920-388-4822, Kewaunee.org)

PHOTOGRAPHER UNKNOWN

![A vivid summer day on the Ahnapee State Trail, showing a wooden bridge with metal railings crossing over a calm river under a blue sky with white clouds.](page_407_image_1_v2.jpg)

Kewaunee Segment: A vivid summer day on the Ahnapee State Trail.

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::kewaunee-door-.parent_text:
## Forestville Segment (Atlas Maps 102f, 103f, 104f)

**Summary:** The Forestville Segment is a 9.8-mile trail in Kewaunee & Door Counties (Atlas Map 102f, 103f, 104f), running from Birch St. (CTH-S) to CTH-H. This segment features the well-used Ahnapee State Trail (AST), a 30-mile former railroad bed traveling from Sturgeon Bay south to Algoma and Casco in Kewaunee County.

### SNAPSHOT

[Icon: 9.8 miles: Birch St. (CTH-S) to CTH-H] **9.8 miles**: Birch St. (CTH-S) to CTH-H

* [Icon: Water icon] From the Ahnapee River and Forestville Flowage/Mill Pond.
* [Icon: Camping icon] At nearby Timber Trails and Ahnapee River Trails campgrounds ▲ (see Area Services).
* [Icon: Restroom icon] At Blahnik Heritage Park, Forestville Dam County Park and town hall in Maplewood.
* [Icon: Fishing icon] At Forestville Dam County Park.
* [Icon: Parking icon] On the AST just north of CTH-M, at Blahnik County Park, at Forestville Dam County Park and on STH-42 just north of the CTH-H Trail access.
* [Icon: Dog icon] Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times.
* [Icon: Multi-use trail icon] The AST is open to horseback riding, snowmobiling and biking.
* [Icon: Trail network icon] Trail network at Blahnik Heritage Park.
* [Icon: Wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

![Map of Forestville Segment showing the Ahnapee State Trail, Blahnik Heritage Park, and various road crossings.](page_408_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **Birch St. (CTH-S) Trail Access** (44.61767, -87.45462): From Algoma at the intersection of STH-42 and CTH-S (Navarino St.), take CTH-S west, north, and northwest 0.8 mi. Parking: Roadside on Birch St. (CTH-S).

[Icon: 2] **CTH-H Trail Access** (44.74786, -87.47987): From Sturgeon Bay take STH-42 south 8.0 mi to CTH-H in Maplewood. Parking: Park in the parking area on STH-42 just north of CTH-H.

Additional Parking: (i) CTH-M. (ii) Blahnik County Park on Washington Rd. (iii) Forestville Dam County Park on Main St. (CTH-J).

### THE HIKE

This segment features the well-used Ahnapee State Trail (AST), a 30-mile former railroad bed traveling from Sturgeon Bay south to Algoma and Casco in Kewaunee County. This hardpacked limestone multiuse path follows the meanders of the Ahnapee River along farm fields, meadows, marshes, bogs, and grasslands. This vibrant habitat,

![Map of the Ahnapee State Trail segment from Forestville to Maplewood, showing the trail route, road crossings, and the Ahnapee River. An inset arrow points north to the Sturgeon Bay Segment.](page_409_image_1_v2.jpg)

attracting unusual and colorful birds, deer, fox, and other native wildlife, is along the Great Wisconsin Birding and Nature Trail (wisconsinbirds.org/trail).

The AST started in the 1890s as the Ahnapee & Western Railway. It transported lumber and dairy products until the early 1970s, when it was abandoned and later converted into a recreational trail.

North of Forestville, the Trail passes through an area burned by the Great Peshtigo Fire, the most devastating forest fire in American history. On October 8, 1871, in two hours, the fire destroyed more than 1.25 million acres and killed more than a thousand people.

From the Trail access on Birch Street (CTH-S), head north. The AST parallels the west side of the Ahnapee River and crosses numerous county roads, including CTH-M and West Wilson Road. You’ll pass Blahnik Heritage Park at the Washington Road intersection and, soon after, cross the river.

The Trail crosses Main Street west of Forestville and briefly overlaps West Avenue (Mill Road) before passing Forestville Dam County Park on the left. Continue north, passing through the Peshtigo Fire area. Finally, reach the segment’s endpoint in the village of Maplewood on CTH-H near the intersection of the AST and STH-42.

### AREA SERVICES

| Town                                                        | Services                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| ----------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Algoma                                                      | Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, lodging, camping, library, medical service. Area info available from the Algoma Area Chamber of Commerce (920-487-2041, algomachamber.org). |
| Forestville                                                 | Restaurant, convenience store, library. From the Main St. (CTH-J) Trail access take CTH-J east 0.3 mi.                                                                                    |
| Maplewood                                                   | Restaurant. On Trail. From |
| [Icon: Trail Community logo] Sturgeon Bay | Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Green Bay Rd. (STH-45/57) and at Michigan St and 3rd Ave. Area info available from the Sturgeon Bay Visitor Center (920-743-6246, sturgeonbay.net). From From CTH-H/STH-42 go north \~9 mi.                                                                                                                      |

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::kewaunee-door-.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::kewaunee-door-.parent_text:
## Sturgeon Bay Segment (Atlas Maps 104f, 105f)

**Summary:** The Sturgeon Bay Segment is a 14.0-mile trail in Kewaunee & Door Counties (Atlas Map 104f, 105f), running from CTH-H to Ice Age Trail Eastern Terminus in Potawatomi State Park. This segment highlights downtown Sturgeon Bay, an Ice Age Trail Community, and Bay View Park on the Sturgeon Bay Canal.

### SNAPSHOT

![Snapshot icons](page_410_image_1_v2.jpg) **14.0 miles**: CTH-H to Ice Age Trail Eastern Terminus in Potawatomi State Park

**Note**: A reroute is planned at the northern end of Potawatomi State Park. Check with the Ice Age Trail Alliance (800-227-0046, IceAgeTrail.org) for details.

*   At Cherry Blossom Park, Otumba Park, and various locations in Potawatomi State Park (PSP). Picnic areas also available along Ahnapee State Trail.

[Icon: Water icon] From Sturgeon Bay.

*   At PSP and nearby private campground.

[Icon: Parking icon] On STH-42 just north of the CTH-H Trail access, at the S. Neenah Ave. Trail access, Cherry Blossom Park, Otumba Park and PSP.

[Icon: ColdCache icon] Two ColdCache sites on segment.

*   Dogs must be leashed (8-ft max) and under control at all times. Dogs are **not permitted inside park buildings at PSP or on groomed ski trails.**

*   Portions overlap the multiuse Ahnapee State Trail. Other portions overlap sidewalks and roads and with PSP trails open to biking and skiing. Hike off to the side of ski trails when groomed.

[Icon: Biking icon] PSP trail network.

[Icon: Wheelchair icon] Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.

![Map of the Sturgeon Bay Segment showing the trail route from Maplewood along the Ahnapee State Trail, crossing Stony Creek and various roads like Idlewild Rd., Maple Rd., and Tagge Rd.](page_410_image_2_v2.jpg)

![Map of the Ice Age Trail's Eastern Terminus in Potawatomi State Park and its route through Sturgeon Bay. The map includes trail mileages, parking areas, campsites, and local landmarks such as Sawyer Harbor, Otumba Park, and the Cardy Paleo-Indian Camp Archaeological Site. An inset map provides a detailed view of the trail's path through the city streets of Sturgeon Bay, connecting to the Ahnapee State Trail.](page_411_image_1_v2.jpg)

### TRAIL ACCESS AND PARKING

[Icon: 1] **CTH-H Trail Access (44.74786, -87.47987):** From Sturgeon Bay take STH-42 south 8.0 mi to CTH-H in Maplewood. **Parking:** Park in the parking area on STH-42 just north of CTH-H.

[Icon: 2] **Ice Age Trail Eastern Terminus in Potawatomi State Park Trail Access (44.87465, -87.43231):** From Sturgeon Bay take STH-57/42 southwest to Park Dr. (CTH-PD). Turn right and go north 2.4 mi to Potawatomi State Park (PSP). Follow the park’s entrance drive (N. Norway Rd.) 3.4 mi to the Ice Age Trail eastern terminus and Old Ski Hill Overlook. **Parking:** Adjacent to Trail Access.

**Additional Parking:** (i) S. Neenah Ave. (ii) Cherry Blossom Park. (iii) Bayview Park. (iv) Otumba Park. (v) Duluth Ave. (vi) Potawatomi State Park parking areas along Shoreline Rd..

### THE HIKE

This segment highlights downtown Sturgeon Bay, an Ice Age Trail Community, and Bay View Park on the Sturgeon Bay Canal. Since 1882, Great Lakes freighters have traveled through the canal to Lake Michigan. This route allows them to bypass the tip of the Door County peninsula, which requires passage through “Death’s Door.” Not only is the canal safer, but it also reduces the travel distance from Green Bay to Milwaukee and Chicago by 150 miles.

Bedrock outcrops of the Niagara Escarpment peek out among cedar, birch, maple, and pine forests. In the southern portion of the Potawatomi State Park (PSP), the segment’s route overlaps the park’s Hemlock Trail. A segment highlight is climbing a set of rock steps in Niagara dolomite constructed by Ice Age Trail Alliance volunteers. The segment ends, as does the Trail itself, at the Old Ski Hill Overlook, which offers expansive views of Green Bay (the body of water).

From the Trail access on CTH-H in Maplewood, head northeast. The Trail shares the Ahnapee State Trail (AST) route. This wide, hardpacked limestone multiuse recreational trail, formerly the Ahnapee & Western Railway’s railbed, crosses the Maplewood Swamp’s southern extent. In this unique peat swamp, waterlogged soil prevents dead vegetation from fully decomposing, creating a spongy layer of peat. Continue northeast, passing Stony Creek Swamp and crossing the creek itself. Dark swamp water creeps within a foot of the raised trail bed as it cuts through these swamps. From here, the Trail bends gently through forests, orchards, and farmland, sometimes under cover of overhanging trees and sometimes in openings.

Cross South Neenah Avenue and head left on a gravel path. Arrive at an AST trailhead parking area and follow its driveway back to South Neenah Avenue. Head north along South Neenah Avenue. Depart South Neenah Avenue and head west and north on a path that skirts and weaves through an industrialized area between South Neenah Avenue and Lansing Road.

At Yew Street, head west briefly, then take Lansing Road north a short distance before heading west off-road, following the southern perimeter of Cherry Blossom Park, a grassy neighborhood park. Continue beyond the park and through the adjoining neighborhood.

Turn north on South Hudson Avenue and cross West Walnut Drive to South Hudson Court. Follow the Trail under STH-42/STH-57 to a sidewalk paralleling Green Bay Road. Follow the sidewalk north and then east to the intersection of Green Bay Road and Lansing Road. Cross to the north side of Green Bay Road. Caution: this is a dangerous road crossing. From here, for an interesting side

trip, head north on Lansing Road to view the Cardy Paleo-Indian Camp archaeological site (see Points of Interest).

Back on Trail, continue east on the sidewalk along Green Bay Road, which curves north and becomes Madison Avenue. A downhill view across building rooftops features shipyards, dry docks, towering cranes, and a historic steel bridge.

At the intersection of Madison Avenue and Larch Street, angle northwest through Bay View Park and pass Woolly the Mammoth, an 8-foot-tall, 14-foot-long steel and concrete sculpture created by Carl Vanderheyden. Then, head west along the Canal walkway to Juniper Street. Follow it west to Joliet Avenue, passing Otumba Park. Take Joliet Avenue north and Hickory Street west and head north on North Duluth Avenue.

Follow North Duluth Avenue to its endpoint and head east into Potawatomi State Park. A nearby informational sign shows a map of the park with marked trails. The Trail soon turns north along the shoreline. You’ll travel through Picnic Area 4, and at the north end of it, join the Hemlock Trail (asphalt to Shoreline Road). Continue north between Sturgeon Bay and Shoreline Road. Cross Shoreline Road and angle west into the woods toward the park campground, climbing up dolomite steps constructed by Ice Age Trail Alliance volunteers and following switchbacks.

Near the campground, leave Hemlock Trail, turn sharply north on a downhill slope, and soon cross Shoreline Road again. Continuing north between the bay and the road, pass the campground’s South and North Camp Areas and join Tower Trail, enjoying bay vistas along the way.

Continue north on the Tower Trail and pass a junction with a looping path within the Tower Trail’s network. Rock outcroppings begin to appear, and the route turns inland, heading west through a gap in an outcropping and climbing to Shoreline Road.

Cross Shoreline Road a final time and head inland, climbing steeply into a forested area and eventually emerging onto North Norway Road and crossing it. Travel through the woods to the Old Ski Hill Overlook parking lot. Here, the eastern terminus is marked. Look for an official terminus marker (similar to that found at the western terminus) affixed to a large rock on the north side of the parking area near the bluff. Enjoy the expansive views to the west.

### POINTS OF INTEREST

**Cardy Paleo-Indian Camp Archaeological Site:** From the Ice Age Trail at the intersection of Green Bay Rd. and Lansing Rd., head north on Lansing Rd. then turn left at Spruce St. and walk west 0.2mi. (near 322 W. Spruce St., Sturgeon Bay).

The Cardy Paleo-Indian Camp archaeological site, listed on the National Register of Historic Places, is considered one of Wisconsin’s most significant finds. The Cardy site, marked by a kiosk and plaque, preserves the remains of a campsite used by Indigenous people at the end of the Ice Age. An extensive dig in 2003 unearthed spear points, tools, a fire pit, and other artifacts. Archeologists believe Indigenous people lived and worked in this area 11,000 years ago near the shore of Glacial Lake Algonquin and within walking distance of the receding continental ice sheet. This camp is unusual for its far north location. Glacial Lake Algonquin occupied the Lake Michigan and Lake Huron basins at the end of the Ice Age. It would have been about 25 ft higher than Green Bay is today.

### AREA SERVICES

**Maplewood:** Restaurant. On Trail. From the CTH-H Trail access go west 0.2 mi on CTH-H.

[Icon: Trail Community logo]
**Sturgeon Bay:** Restaurant, grocery store, convenience store, general shopping, lodging, camping, library, medical service. On Trail. Most services located on Green Bay Rd. (STH-45/57) and at Michigan St and 3rd Ave. Area info available from the Sturgeon Bay Visitor Center (920-743-6246, sturgeonbay.net).

**Potawatomi State Park (PSP):** Camping. On Trail. (920-746-2890, dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/parks/potawatomi; reservations: 888-947-2757, wisconsin.goingtocamp.com). There are several other state parks in Door County.

> *I hereby avow and attest that I have walked every inch of this Trail from the glittering waters of the Saint Croix River to the new terminus overlooking Green Bay. In between those two points, I have placed one foot in front of the other over 2.5 million times, over hills and valleys, through boot-sucking bogs and fens, on scorched hilltops and flooded wetlands, atop drumlins, eskers, kames and moraines. I have been hailed on, rained on, blasted by subzero windchills, baked by 100-degree temperatures, feasted on by flies, mosquitoes, and ticks... I have traversed beaver dams, rickety bridges, log walks, ice-covered hills, washouts, shallow rivers, and mile upon mile of terrain featuring roots, water, mud, and rocks.*

BROCK JANSEN, THOUSAND-MILER

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::glossary::7427.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::glossary::7427.parent_text:
# Glossary

**Barrens** Areas where pine and stunted oaks grow. Barrens made up 12% of the state’s original landscape. Found in prairie-like areas with sandy, infertile soil. Animals that inhabit barrens include whitetail deer, grouse, prairie chicken, red-headed woodpecker, and timber wolf.

**Boardwalk** An elevated footpath built with wooden planks that enable hikers to cross wet, fragile, or marshy landscape.

**Bog** A wetland of spongy ground or peat, often with tamaracks and sphagnum moss.

**Continental Glaciation** The formation, movement, recession and related effects of colossal, nearly continent-sized ice sheets. Though common during the Pleistocene (or most recent) Ice Age, the only ice sheets that today approach the enormity of those existing during the Ice Age are in Antarctica and Greenland. Continental glaciation sculpted a quarter of the Earth’s landmass and dramatically changed the Earth’s climate, oceans, plants, and animals.

**Dells/Dalles** A gorge cut by torrents of meltwater released by a melting glacier or draining of glacial lakes. Some dramatic examples: the Dells of the Eau Claire, the Wisconsin Dells, and the Dalles of St. Croix.

**Dispersed Camping Area (DCA)** A minimally developed camping area for long-distance hikers. To help increase camping opportunities for Ice Age Trail long-distance hikers, the Ice Age Trail Alliance and its partners are working to establish DCAs, especially in areas (i.e., the southern two-thirds of the Trail) where convenient camping options are otherwise limited for long-distance hikers. DCAs are not “campgrounds” or even “campsites” in the traditional sense; instead, they are typically nothing more than a cleared area where hikers may legally camp for a night. Use of DCAs is restricted to those on long-distance, multiday hikes.

**Dolomite** A rock similar to limestone consisting largely of calcium magnesium carbonate.

**Driftless Area** The southwestern quarter of Wisconsin is unglaciated or shows no signs of past glacial activity. It is a landscape deeply cut by ancient streams into narrow, angular valleys and several-hundred-million-years-old ridges. The best place along the Trail to see the Driftless Area is Dane County between Mineral Point Rd. and Table Bluff Rd., west of the end moraine.

**Drumlin** An elongated, teardrop-shaped hill. These streamlined hills were sculpted in the direction of the glacial ice movement. They often occur in groups known as swarms. Because drumlins generally form miles behind, or up-ice, from an end moraine, they are rare along the Trail. The Farmington Drumlins, in Waupaca County, is the largest swarm of drumlins along the existing segments of the Trail. A small group of drumlins is in Door County between Maplewood and Sturgeon Bay. STH-60, between Columbus and Hartford, and I-94, between Madison and Sussex, cross one of the largest drumlin swarms in the world.

**End Moraine** A type of moraine formed at the outer edge of a glacier or glacial lobe where it paused or stopped. Prominent end moraines along the Trail can be witnessed at Prairie Moraine County Park in Dane County, Devil’s Lake State Park in Sauk County, and the range of hills north and east of Antigo in Langlade County. End moraines define the general route of the Trail.

**Ephemeral Ponds (also Vernal Ponds)** Small isolated wetland depressions or pools of water that dry occasionally or are temporary. They can be deep or shallow with vegetation around them and occur in habitats such as sandhill, natural pinelands, dry prairies, and other related communities. Lacking in fish, they are often home to insect species such as mosquitoes and mayflies and natal amphibians such as frogs and toads.

**Erratics** Boulders carried long distances by the glaciers and deposited when the glacier melted. They tend to be smooth and rounded. Erratics can be found along the entire Trail, except where it traverses parts of the Driftless Area. Large, famous erratics along the Trail are in Walworth, Waupaca, and Langlade counties.

**Esker** A sinuous rounded ridge of sand and gravel deposited by the streams that flowed through tunnels at the base of the glacier. The Parnell Esker in the Kettle Moraine State Forest’s Northern Unit is the most notable example along the Trail. Other excellent eskers are in Polk and Taylor counties.

**Extinct Glacial Lake** A glacial lake that drained, often catastrophically, when a glacier or glacial lobe melted back. Extinct Glacial Lake Wisconsin’s lakebed remains visible in Adams and Juneau counties. Much of the Fox Valley was for a time under Glacial Lake Oshkosh.

**Fen** An area of low, flat marshy land where decomposing plants accumulate, forming peat.

**Ford** A shallow place in a river or stream where one can cross by wading.

**Hummocky** Describing hilly, knob-and-kettle topography.

**Ice Age National Scientific Reserve Unit** Areas administered by the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources to protect, preserve, and interpret the outstanding examples of glaciation in Wisconsin. There are nine units (Interstate State Park, Chippewa Moraine, Mill Bluff, Devil’s Lake, Cross Plains, Horicon Marsh, Campbellsport Drumlins, Kettle Moraine, and Two Creeks Buried Forest) and all but three (Mill Bluff, Horicon Marsh, and Campbellsport Drumlins) are on or near established Ice Age Trail segments.

**Ice Sheet** A large continental glacier that is not confined by underlying topography. The northeastern quarter of North America was covered more than a dozen times by the Laurentide Ice Sheet during the Ice Age, between 10,000 and 2.5 million years ago. Today, ice sheets are found only in polar regions such as Greenland and Antarctica.

**Ice-Walled Lake Plains** Mesa-like hills that were once lakes on a melting glacier. Streams flowing on the glacier deposited loads of sediment into these lakes. When the surrounding glacier had completely melted, the lake bottoms became the hilltops. Ice-walled lake plains are showcased at the Chippewa Moraine National Scientific Reserve in Chippewa County.

**Kame** A conical hill composed primarily of water-rounded sand and cobbles that were deposited by streams that flowed downward through shafts in the glacial ice. The Kettle Moraine contains the largest and most important kame fields in the world, particularly between Dundee and the Parnell Tower, near Slinger and at Holy Hill. Kames are intriguing because of their shape and the way they were formed, not because of their size.

**Karner Blue Butterfly** (*Lycaeides melissa samuelis*) On the federal endangered species list. Wisconsin has the largest remaining population. It is slightly larger than

Glossary

a postage stamp with a wingspan of approximately 1 inch. It is seen in the beginning of June and again in August. This butterfly feeds exclusively on wild lupine, a bright blue flower, found in dry, sandy soil, partly shaded meadows, and oak savannas in central and northwestern Wisconsin.

**Kettle** A surface depression formed by large, detached blocks of melting ice that were buried with sand and gravel. As the ice melted, the other material collapsed, leaving a crater-like depression. Some kettles are more than 100 feet deep. Kettles can be found in many places along the Trail.

**Kettle Moraine** Also called the Interlobate Moraine, the Kettle Moraine is a series of ridges, 120 miles long and only a few miles wide, in eastern Wisconsin. The combined action and deposits of the Green Bay and Lake Michigan lobes of the continental ice sheet formed the Kettle Moraine. The Kettle Moraine is the birthplace of the Ice Age Trail and the subject of the first published study of interlobate glaciation in 1878.

**Leopold Bench** A 33- or 48-inch-long bench seen along the Trail originally designed by conservationist Aldo Leopold. He built and used the bench when he lived in central Wisconsin while writing *A Sand County Almanac*. Design plans can be found by entering “Leopold Bench” into an internet search engine.

**Lobe** A tongue-like extension of an ice sheet. Six major lobes during the late Wisconsin Glaciation covered portions of Wisconsin. These lobes were the Superior, Chippewa, Wisconsin Valley, Langlade, Green Bay, and Lake Michigan lobes. The Des Moines Lobe extended slightly into western Polk County.

**Mammoth** An extinct species of elephant with hairy skin and long tusks curving upward that roamed North America, Europe, and Asia. It is the Ice Age Trail mascot.

**Marl** An earthy material rich in carbonate minerals, clays, and silt. A marl lake is a type of alkaline lake whose bottom sediments include large marl deposits.

**Moraine** A ridge formed by unsorted gravel, sand, and boulders carried by the glacier and deposited at the outer edge, or front, of the glacier. Some are only 10 feet high, while others rise 250 to 300 feet. Moraines can be found in many places along the Trail.

**Moulin** A vertical shaft through a glacier that often extended all the way to the bed, occurring where the ice is relatively thin and where the glacier is at its melting point, so water could coexist with ice.

**Outwash Plain** A sandy plain formed when glacial meltwater streams in front of glaciers spread over a very wide, flat area. The water swept the sand into both glaciated and unglaciated areas. Between Hancock and Plover, I-39 crosses part of a vast outwash plain. Another example is the Antigo Flats of Langlade County, visible along the Trail from the Harrison Hills of Lincoln County.

**Perched Wetland** A wetland that exists due to the presence of a low-permeability soil layer at or near the ground surface that holds water. The confining layer may maintain the wetland water level above the regional water table, resulting in a “perched” wetland.

**Pitted Outwash** An area of outwash that is dimpled with kettles. These areas were formed by meltwater-carried blocks of ice that were deposited with sand and gravel and later melted in place, leaving kettles.

**Portage** A route to carry a boat overland to get from one body of water to another or to avoid a water obstacle. Two well-known historic portage routes along the Trail are in the city of Portage on the land between the Fox and Wisconsin Rivers and at Grandfather Falls in Lincoln County.

**Potable Water** Water that is safe to drink or to use for food preparation.

**Pothole** A smooth bowl carved into bedrock by the grinding action of stones whirling around in a river eddy. Many potholes were formed by torrents of glacial meltwater during the Ice Age. The best place to see these along the Trail is near the western terminus in Interstate State Park. These potholes were formed when the St. Croix River was much deeper than today. Small potholes at Devil’s Lake State Park formed before the Ice Age.

**Proglacial** In front of, at, or immediately beyond the margin of a glacier or ice sheet. A proglacial lake is formed either by the damming action of a moraine during the retreat of a melting glacier, by a glacial ice dam, or by meltwater trapped in a depression.

**Puncheon** A footpath built with wooden planks. The structure rests on horizontal sills that are in direct contact with the ground. A puncheon is built when conditions (rocks and boulders or deep muck) are not ideal for a boardwalk.

**Riparian Zone** The area of thick vegetation that runs along the bank of a river. It is characterized by shrubs, vines, trees and grasses. Important to a watershed, these zones help maintain streams and rivers in their natural state. During heavy rains they help prevent flooding by slowing the flow of water both into the river and along the river banks. In addition, they act as a buffer between the land and the water where chemicals such as fertilizers and pesticides applied to the land are absorbed through many of the river area’s plant roots.

**Sedge Meadow** A wetland that is dry in late summer and composed mostly of sedges. Sedges are plants that look like grasses but feel rough when stroked.

**State Ice Age Trail Area (SIATA)** A property owned by the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (DNR) and managed for the Ice Age Trail.

**Swale** A hollow or depression at the beginning of a valley that often has wet soils.

**Terminal Moraine** A type of end moraine where a glacier or glacial lobe reached its maximum extent and melted back.

**Thru-Hike** To hike an entire long-distance trail, such as the Ice Age Trail, end to end as a continuous journey.

**Till Plain** An extensive flat plain that forms when a sheet of ice becomes detached from the main body of a glacier and melts in place, depositing the sediments it carried.

**Troad** A former extraction route created for nontrail purposes that has been adopted as a recreational trail. Extraction activities include logging, agriculture, and mining.

**Tunnel Channel** Created by a fast moving river under a glacier that carves a valley. After the glacier has melted, the valley often contains a series of lakes. Prominent tunnel channels can be seen along the Trail in the New Hope Segment in Portage County and the Straight River Segment in Polk County.

**Wisconsin Glaciation** A period of the Earth’s history at the end of the Pleistocene Ice Age, between 10,000 and 75,000 years ago. All glacial lobes and landforms described in the Guidebook occurred or were created during the last part of the Wisconsin Glaciation, unless otherwise noted.

Glossary

Index

*I have a stubborn streak, and the more resistance I heard from people telling me that as a woman I should never hike alone, the more I wanted to prove that as a woman I was capable of completing the Ice Age Trail on my own, and that I could do it safely and intelligently. I never want a woman to feel so discouraged that she doesn’t try.*

AMY “ICE AGE AMY” BAYER, THOUSAND-MILER

Index

*How does one decide to hike the entire Ice Age Trail? I like to think there were many things in our lives that brought us to this quest: a love of the outdoors; a search for a low-cost activity that is also good for our health; a longing to do something epic.*

DAWN THAYER, THOUSAND-MILER

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::key-to-segment.parent_text:
## Elevation & Ruggedness

| Symbol                                                                 | Description                                                                                                                                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| [Icon: Elevation symbol: mountain with number 3]     | This segment is: 1 (mostly flat) through 5 (very hilly).                                                                                                                 |
| [Icon: Ruggedness symbol: hiking boot with number 2] | Factoring in signage, maintenance and/or layout challenges; water hazards or crossings; remoteness and logging, this segment is: 1 (not rugged) through 5 (very rugged). |

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::key-to-segment.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::key-to-segment.h1: KEY TO SEGMENT SNAPSHOT SYMBOLS (See also map symbol key, opposite. See pages xxvii–xxix for more information about symbols.)
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::key-to-segment.parent_text:
## Water

| Symbol                                                                      | Description                                                                                                                                              |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Icon: Potable water symbol: faucet]                      | Drinking water is available on this segment from a pump or spigot with potable water. Assume water source is unavailable early fall through late spring. |
| [Icon: Natural water source symbol: water drop with dots] | Drinking water is available on this segment from a natural source; filtration required.                                                                  |
| [Icon: No water symbol: crossed-out faucet]               | Drinking water is NOT available on this segment.                                                                                                         |

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::camping::ameni.parent_text:
## Amenities and Activities

| Symbol                                                           | Description                                                                                                                                   |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Icon: Picnic area symbol: picnic table]       | Picnic areas are available on or near the segment.                                                                                            |
| [Icon: Child-friendly symbol: child on swing]  | Child-friendly amenities like playgrounds and/or swim areas are available on or near the segment.                                             |
| [Icon: Restroom symbol: male and female icons] | Restrooms are available on or near the segment. Assume facility is unavailable/closed early fall through late spring.                         |
| [Icon: ColdCaching symbol: globe icon]         | ColdCaching site is on the segment. ColdCaching is a family activity in which participants seek out natural features along the Ice Age Trail. |

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::camping::hunti.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::camping::hunti.parent_text:
## Hunting & Dogs

| Symbol                                                          | Description                                                                                                                        |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Icon: No hunting symbol: crossed-out hunter] | Hikers will not have any interaction with hunting on this segment.                                                                 |
| [Icon: Hunting season symbol: hunter icon]    | Segment crosses private land and portions or the full segment may be closed to hikers during hunting season(s).                    |
| [Icon: Dogs permitted symbol: dog icon]       | In general, dogs are permitted but must be leashed and under control. Some segments have special regulations for hiking with dogs. |

iat-trail-guidebook-2023::camping::share.doc_title: Ice Age National Scenic Trail Official Guidebook
iat-trail-guidebook-2023::camping::share.parent_text:
## Shared, Spur & Accessible Trails

| Symbol                                                             | Description                                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| [Icon: Shared trail symbol: dashed lines]        | Portions of the segment overlap biking, snowmobiling or groomed cross-country skiing trails or roads and/or sidewalks. |
| [Icon: Spur trail symbol: crossed lines]         | Other hiking trails (spurs, loops or lollipops) are present off the main segment route.                                |
| [Icon: Accessible trail symbol: wheelchair icon] | Portions of this segment may be suitable for those using wheelchairs or similar devices.                               |
